- [x] **Investment Techniques** = Systematic methodologies: Fundamental, Technical, Quantitative, Sentiment.
- [x] **Modern Portfolio Theory (Markowitz, 1952):** Diversification reduces *portfolio* risk below the weighted average of individual risks.
- [x] **Portfolio Expected Return:** $E[R_p] = \sum w_i E[R_i]$ (no correlation terms).
- [x] **Portfolio Variance (2-asset):** $\sigma_p^2 = w_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + w_2^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2 w_1 w_2 \rho \sigma_1 \sigma_2$ (correlation terms DO appear).
- [x] **CAPM Equation:** $E[R_i] = R_f + \beta_i (E[R_m] - R_f)$.
- [x] **Beta** = $\text{Cov}(R_i, R_m) / \text{Var}(R_m)$. $\beta = 1$ = market-neutral, $>1$ = aggressive, $<1$ = defensive, $<0$ = hedge.
- [x] **Sharpe Ratio** = $(E[R_p] - R_f) / \sigma_p$ — uses **total risk**.
- [x] **Treynor Ratio** = $(E[R_p] - R_f) / \beta_p$ — uses **systematic risk only**.
- [x] **Jensen's Alpha** = Actual Return − CAPM-predicted return. $\alpha > 0$ = outperformance.
- [x] **Time Value of Money:** $FV = PV(1+r)^n$, $PV = FV / (1+r)^n$, $PV_{\text{perpetuity}} = C / r$.
- [x] **NPV** > 0 ⇒ Accept project. **IRR** = discount rate making NPV = 0.
- [x] **Efficient Frontier:** Curve of portfolios maximising return for given risk; *upper* half is the "efficient" region.
- [x] **CML** = Capital Market Line (uses $\sigma$, mixes R_f with tangent portfolio).
- [x] **SML** = Security Market Line (uses $\beta$, plots individual assets).
- [x] **Diversification works only if** $\rho < 1$. The lower the $\rho$ (especially negative), the greater the risk reduction.
- [x] **Correlation range:** $-1 \le \rho \le 1$. Use this to *verify* a numerical answer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Sentence Takeaway for the Exam Hall:**
> *"Investment Techniques are the rulebook of financial decision-making — Master CAPM, MPT, and the portfolio variance formula; the rest of Module 1 is commentary on these three pillars."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Asset Types - Real Estate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET602 - FINANCIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Asset Types - Real Estate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 1: ASSET TYPES — REAL ESTATE

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real Estate (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> Real estate refers to the **land** together with any **permanent improvements** attached to it, whether natural or man-made, including residential, commercial, and industrial properties. In financial engineering, real estate is classified as a **tangible, alternative, income-generating asset class** that combines characteristics of both fixed-income and equity instruments.

From a financial engineering perspective, real estate is a **physical asset** (as opposed to financial assets like stocks and bonds) that:

- Generates **periodic cash flows** through rental income (similar to bonds).
- Appreciates (or depreciates) in **capital value** over time (similar to equities).
- Is **location-bound** and **heterogeneous** — no two parcels are identical.
- Carries high **illiquidity** but offers powerful **leverage** through mortgage financing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Real Estate Matters in Financial Engineering**
> Real estate constitutes roughly **60–70% of global household wealth**. Modern financial engineering has transformed it from a purely physical asset into a **securitized, tradable, and risk-managed** instrument through REITs, MBS (Mortgage-Backed Securities), CMBS, and real estate derivatives.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Fruit Tree" Analogy 🍎

Imagine you are a financial planner advising a friend between two investments:

| Feature | Buying Stocks 🍇 | Buying a Rental Apartment 🏠 |
|---|---|---|
| **Tangible?** | Paper certificate | Physical building + land |
| **Income?** | Dividends (variable) | Rent (relatively stable) |
| **Liquidity** | Sell in seconds on an exchange | Selling takes **3–6 months** |
| **Leverage** | Margin (limited) | Mortgage (up to 80% LTV) |
| **Inflation Hedge** | Weak (cash flows fixed) | Strong (rents rise with inflation) |
| **Risk** | Market volatility | Tenant default + vacancy |

**The Intuition:** Real estate is like a **fruit tree** 🌳 — you plant it (purchase), it produces fruit every season (rental income), and the tree itself grows thicker and taller over the years (capital appreciation). But unlike a stock certificate you can sell in 1 second, you cannot uproot and transport the tree — its value is locked to its **location, soil, and climate**. This *rootedness* is the defining feature that distinguishes real estate from every other financial asset.

---

## 1.3 The Five Defining Characteristics of Real Estate

> [!NOTE]
> **The "SLCIL" Mnemonic (Memory Aid for KTU Exam)**

1. **S — Scarcity**: Land is finite. The supply curve is essentially vertical in the long run.
2. **L — Location Specificity**: "The three most important things in real estate are location, location, location." Value is hyper-local.
3. **C — Capital Intensity**: Requires large upfront capital; financing is the norm.
4. **I — Illiquidity**: Transaction costs are high (5–10%); search time is long.
5. **L — Leverage Amplification**: Mortgages allow control of a large asset with a small down payment, magnifying both gains and losses.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Real Estate Assets

### A. By Physical Use

- **Residential**: Apartments, villas, single-family homes, condominiums.
- **Commercial (CRE)**: Office buildings, retail malls, hotels, restaurants.
- **Industrial**: Warehouses, manufacturing plants, logistics centers, data centers.
- **Land**: Raw undeveloped land, agricultural land, timberland.
- **Special Purpose**: Hospitals, schools, religious buildings, sports stadiums.

### B. By Investment Vehicle

- **Direct Ownership**: Buying the physical property outright.
- **Indirect Ownership**:
  - **REITs (Real Estate Investment Trusts)**
  - **Real Estate Mutual Funds**
  - **MBS / CMBS** (Mortgage/Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities)
  - **Real Estate ETFs**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Focus Area**
> In PCIET602, the emphasis is on **REITs** (as securitized instruments) and **direct ownership** (as a financial-engineering valuation problem). Both are examinable.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Real Estate Valuation

Real estate valuation rests on three classical approaches, recognized by KTU and globally (IVS — International Valuation Standards):

### Pillar 1: Income Capitalization Approach
> Used for **income-producing** properties (commercial, rental residential).

The **present value** of all future expected net operating incomes (NOI), plus the resale value (terminal value), is the property's intrinsic value.

### Pillar 2: Sales Comparison Approach
> Compares the subject property to **recently sold comparable properties** ("comps") in the same micro-market.

### Pillar 3: Cost Approach
> Value = **Land Value + Replacement Cost of Improvements − Depreciation**.

This is most useful for **new, unique, or special-purpose** properties.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — Real Estate Finance (PCIET602)**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Net Operating Income (NOI)** | $\text{NOI} = \text{PGI} - \text{Vacancy Loss} - \text{Operating Expenses}$ | All in ₹/year |
| 2 | **Capitalization Rate (Cap Rate)** | $r_{cap} = \dfrac{\text{NOI}}{\text{Property Value}}$ | Dimensionless (%) |
| 3 | **Direct Cap Value (Income Approach)** | $V = \dfrac{\text{NOI}}{r_{cap}}$ | ₹ |
| 4 | **Gross Rent Multiplier (GRM)** | $\text{GRM} = \dfrac{\text{Property Price}}{\text{Gross Annual Rent}}$ | Years (number) |
| 5 | **Effective Gross Income (EGI)** | $\text{EGI} = \text{PGI} \times (1 - \text{Vacancy Rate})$ | ₹/year |
| 6 | **Debt Service Coverage Ratio (DSCR)** | $\text{DSCR} = \dfrac{\text{NOI}}{\text{Annual Debt Service}}$ | Ratio (≥ 1.2 typical) |
| 7 | **Loan-to-Value Ratio (LTV)** | $\text{LTV} = \dfrac{\text{Loan Amount}}{\text{Property Value}}$ | % (≤ 80% typical) |
| 8 | **Mortgage Payment (EMI)** | $M = P \cdot \dfrac{r(1+r)^n}{(1+r)^n - 1}$ | ₹/month |
| 9 | **Cash-on-Cash Return** | $\text{CoC} = \dfrac{\text{Annual Pre-Tax Cash Flow}}{\text{Total Cash Invested}}$ | % |
| 10 | **After-Tax Cash Flow (ATCF)** | $\text{ATCF} = \text{NOI} - \text{DS} - \text{Tax}$ | ₹/year |
| 11 | **Levered IRR** | $0 = \sum_{t=0}^{N} \dfrac{\text{CF}_t}{(1+\text{IRR})^t}$ | % |
| 12 | **Real Estate Risk Premium** | $\text{RERP} = r_{\text{RE}} - r_f$ | % |
| 13 | **One-Percent Rule (Quick Filter)** | $\text{Monthly Rent} \geq 1\% \cdot \text{Property Price}$ | Sanity check |
| 14 | **Cap Rate Decomposition** | $r_{cap} = r_f + \text{RP} + \text{LP} + \text{RR}$ | Risk-free + Risk + Liquidity + Recapture |
| 15 | **Gordon Cap Rate (Terminal)** | $r_{\text{exit}} = r_{cap,\text{market at exit}}$ | Used in IRR |

> **Legend:** PGI = Potential Gross Income, DS = Debt Service, RP = Risk Premium, LP = Liquidity Premium, RR = Recapture Rate, $r$ = periodic interest rate, $n$ = number of periods, $P$ = loan principal, $r_f$ = risk-free rate.

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The pipe character `|` is avoided inside math expressions (e.g., absolute value is written as $\vert x \vert$ to preserve the markdown table structure).

---

## 2.3 Real Estate in the Risk-Return Framework

Real estate occupies a unique position in the **Capital Market Line / Efficient Frontier**:

| Asset Class | Expected Return | Volatility (σ) | Liquidity | Inflation Hedge |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Treasury Bills | 4% | 0% | Highest | ❌ |
| Corporate Bonds | 7% | 5% | High | ❌ |
| Large-cap Stocks | 10% | 16% | High | Partial |
| **Real Estate (Direct)** | **8–12%** | **10–14%** | **Low** | **✅ Strong** |
| REITs | 9% | 18% | High | ✅ |
| Gold | 6% | 15% | Medium | ✅ |

> **Why real estate returns are "asymmetric"**: You participate in long-term appreciation but rental income cushions short-term drawdowns. This is the **diversification benefit** that real estate adds to a portfolio.

---

## 2.4 The Cap Rate Decomposition (Engineering the Discount Rate)

The cap rate is **not arbitrary** — it is a built-up rate that reflects all the risks of a specific property:

$$r_{cap} = r_f + \text{Property Risk Premium} + \text{Liquidity Premium} + \text{Recapture Rate}$$

- **$r_f$**: Risk-free rate (e.g., 10-year G-Sec yield = **6.5%** in 2024 India).
- **Property Risk Premium**: Reflects tenant quality, lease length, location volatility.
- **Liquidity Premium**: Penalty for the illiquid nature of real estate.
- **Recapture Rate**: Annual return *of* capital (depreciation of building component over its useful life).

> **KTU Insight**: When RBI raises interest rates, $r_f$ rises, pushing $r_{cap}$ up, which *reduces* property values (since $V = \text{NOI}/r_{cap}$). This is the **interest-rate transmission mechanism** to real estate prices.

---

## 2.5 REITs — The Financial-Engineering Revolution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **REIT (Real Estate Investment Trust) — KTU 2024 Definition**
> A REIT is a company that owns, operates, or finances income-producing real estate. Modeled on mutual funds, it pools investor capital, provides professional management, and is mandated to distribute ≥ **90% of taxable income** as dividends to maintain pass-through tax status.

**Why REITs matter for financial engineers**:
- They convert **illiquid real estate** into **liquid equity securities** tradable on stock exchanges.
- They unlock the **Markowitz diversification** benefit by allowing tiny positions.
- They enable **leverage** through corporate debt, magnifying equity returns.

**Indian Context**: SEBI introduced REITs in 2014. As of 2024, listed Indian REITs include **Embassy Office Parks REIT, Mindspace Business Parks REIT, Brookfield India Real Estate Trust, and Nexus Select Trust** — all predominantly **commercial office** assets.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: From NOI to Property Value (Gordon-Income Model)

> **Problem Setup:** A property generates a **stable, perpetual NOI**. Find its intrinsic value.

**Step 1 — Start with the perpetuity formula**

For a perpetuity paying cash flow $C$ forever, the present value is:

$$V = \dfrac{C}{r}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute real estate variables**

Replace $C$ with NOI (the annual net rental income) and $r$ with $r_{cap}$ (the capitalization rate, which serves as the discount rate for real estate):

$$V = \dfrac{\text{NOI}}{r_{cap}}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the cap rate (inverse form)**

Rearranging the same equation to express the cap rate:

$$r_{cap} = \dfrac{\text{NOI}}{V}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**

The cap rate is the **unlevered yield** of a property. A buyer paying price $V$ and receiving NOI annually earns exactly $r_{cap}$ per year on equity (ignoring debt). Hence, **higher NOI** or **lower price** ⇒ **higher cap rate** ⇒ **better yield for buyer**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cap Rate ↔ Value Inverse Relationship**
> If market cap rates rise from 7% to 9% (due to interest rate hikes), a property with NOI = ₹1,00,00,000 drops in value from ₹14.29 Cr to ₹11.11 Cr — a **22% loss** in capital value. This is the leverage risk of real estate.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Mortgage EMI Formula (Amortization)

> **Problem Setup:** A loan of principal $P$ is amortized over $n$ monthly periods at monthly interest rate $r$. Find the constant monthly payment $M$.

**Step 1 — Set up the loan balance equation**

Let $B_t$ be the balance after period $t$. Each period: interest accrues, payment is made, balance reduces:

$$B_t = B_{t-1}(1+r) - M$$

**Step 2 — Solve the linear difference equation**

The general solution of $B_t = (1+r)B_{t-1} - M$ with initial condition $B_0 = P$ and terminal condition $B_n = 0$ gives:

$$B_t = P(1+r)^t - M \cdot \dfrac{(1+r)^t - 1}{r}$$

**Step 3 — Apply boundary condition $B_n = 0$**

Setting $B_n = 0$:

$$0 = P(1+r)^n - M \cdot \dfrac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for M**

$$M \cdot \dfrac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r} = P(1+r)^n$$

$$\boxed{M = P \cdot \dfrac{r(1+r)^n}{(1+r)^n - 1}}$$

This is the **standard amortizing loan payment formula**, identical for home loans, auto loans, and CRE mortgages.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Full Real-Estate Investment Analysis

> **Case Study: "Sunrise Apartments", a 10-unit rental building in Kochi**

**Given Data:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Purchase Price | ₹5,00,00,000 (₹5 Cr) |
| Gross Annual Rent (PGI) | ₹60,00,000 |
| Vacancy Rate | 5% |
| Operating Expenses | ₹15,00,000/year |
| Down Payment (Equity) | ₹1,50,00,000 (30%) |
| Loan Amount (P) | ₹3,50,00,000 (70%) |
| Interest Rate | 9% per annum (monthly $r = 0.75\%$) |
| Loan Tenure | 20 years ($n = 240$ months) |

---

**Step A — Compute Effective Gross Income (EGI)**

$$\text{EGI} = \text{PGI} \times (1 - \text{Vacancy Rate})$$

$$\text{EGI} = 60{,}00{,}000 \times (1 - 0.05) = 60{,}00{,}000 \times 0.95$$

$$\text{EGI} = ₹57{,}00{,}000$$

---

**Step B — Compute Net Operating Income (NOI)**

$$\text{NOI} = \text{EGI} - \text{Operating Expenses}$$

$$\text{NOI} = 57{,}00{,}000 - 15{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{NOI} = ₹42{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

---

**Step C — Compute Cap Rate (going-in yield)**

$$r_{cap} = \dfrac{\text{NOI}}{V} = \dfrac{42{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}00{,}000}$$

$$r_{cap} = 0.084 = 8.4\%$$

This is the **unlevered yield** of the property.

---

**Step D — Compute Monthly Mortgage Payment (EMI)**

Using $P = 3,50,00,000$, $r = 0.0075$ (monthly), $n = 240$:

$$M = 3{,}50{,}00{,}000 \cdot \dfrac{0.0075 \cdot (1.0075)^{240}}{(1.0075)^{240} - 1}$$

First, compute $(1.0075)^{240}$:

$$(1.0075)^{240} = e^{240 \cdot \ln(1.0075)} = e^{240 \cdot 0.007472} = e^{1.7933} \approx 6.0092$$

Now substitute:

$$M = 3{,}50{,}00{,}000 \cdot \dfrac{0.0075 \cdot 6.0092}{6.0092 - 1}$$

$$M = 3{,}50{,}00{,}000 \cdot \dfrac{0.045069}{5.0092}$$

$$M = 3{,}50{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.008999$$

$$\boxed{M \approx ₹3{,}14{,}965 \text{ per month}}$$

Annual Debt Service (DS):

$$\text{DS} = 3{,}14{,}965 \times 12 = ₹37{,}79{,}580$$

---

**Step E — Compute DSCR (Lender's Safety Check)**

$$\text{DSCR} = \dfrac{\text{NOI}}{\text{DS}} = \dfrac{42{,}00{,}000}{37{,}79{,}580} = 1.111$$

> **Warning Flag:** DSCR < 1.20 means the property is **risky for the lender**. The borrower should either inject more equity or negotiate better loan terms.

---

**Step F — Compute Pre-Tax Cash Flow (Levered Cash Flow)**

$$\text{Pre-Tax CF} = \text{NOI} - \text{DS}$$

$$\text{Pre-Tax CF} = 42{,}00{,}000 - 37{,}79{,}580$$

$$\text{Pre-Tax CF} = ₹4{,}20{,}420 \text{ per year}$$

---

**Step G — Compute Cash-on-Cash Return (Levered Yield)**

$$\text{CoC} = \dfrac{\text{Pre-Tax CF}}{\text{Cash Invested}} = \dfrac{4{,}20{,}420}{1{,}50{,}00{,}000}$$

$$\text{CoC} = 0.0280 = 2.80\%$$

> **Insight:** The CoC is **lower** than the cap rate (8.4%) because debt service "eats" most of the NOI. Cash-on-cash is the **real yield on equity** — but the equity investor also gains leverage on capital appreciation.

---

**Step H — Compute Levered Return on Equity Including Appreciation**

Assume property appreciates at 5% per year. After 1 year:

$$\text{Property Value End of Year 1} = 5{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 1.05 = ₹5{,}25{,}00{,}000$$

Capital Gain = ₹25,00,000.

Total Return to Equity (Year 1):

$$\text{Total Return} = \text{Pre-Tax CF} + \text{Capital Gain} = 4{,}20{,}420 + 25{,}00{,}000 = ₹29{,}20{,}420$$

Levered Total Return on Equity:

$$\text{Return on Equity} = \dfrac{29{,}20{,}420}{1{,}50{,}00{,}000} = 19.47\%$$

> **Conclusion:** Levered equity return (**19.47%**) is **2.3×** the unlevered property yield (**8.4%**). This is the magic — and danger — of financial leverage in real estate.

---

## 3.4 Sensitivity Matrix — How Variables Affect Returns

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Matrix for Exam Memorization**

| Variable ↓ / Metric → | Cap Rate | Levered CoC | Property Value | DSCR |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Vacancy | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ |
| ↑ Operating Expenses | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ |
| ↑ Interest Rate | — | ↓ | ↓ (via higher $r_{cap}$) | ↓ |
| ↑ Property Appreciation | — | — | ↑ | — |
| ↑ LTV (more debt) | — | ↑ (initially) | — | ↓ (more debt service) |
| ↑ Rental Income | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Real Estate Investment Process Flow


## 4.3 Diagram 3: Real Estate Cash-Flow Waterfall (Levered)

```mermaid

    A[GROSS POTENTIAL INCOME PGI] --> B[minus Vacancy and Credit Loss]
    B --> C[EFFECTIVE GROSS INCOME EGI]
    C --> D[minus Operating Expenses OPEX]
    D --> E[NET OPERATING INCOME NOI]
    E --> F[minus Annual Debt Service DS]
    F --> G[PRE TAX CASH FLOW PTCF]
    G --> H[minus Income Tax]
    H --> I[AFTER TAX CASH FLOW ATCF]
    I --> J[plus Principal Paydown]
    J --> K[plus Capital Appreciation]
    K --> L[TOTAL RETURN TO EQUITY]

    E --> M[CAP RATE equals NOI divided by Value]
    F --> N[DSCR equals NOI divided by DS]

```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 🔹 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**Q: Define "Capitalization Rate" in real estate finance and explain its significance.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Capitalization Rate (Cap Rate)** is defined as the ratio of a property's annual **Net Operating Income (NOI)** to its current market value:

$$r_{cap} = \dfrac{\text{NOI}}{V}$$

It is expressed as a percentage and represents the **unlevered yield** an investor would earn if the property were purchased entirely with cash. Significance:

1. **Valuation Tool**: It directly converts NOI into property value via $V = \text{NOI}/r_{cap}$.
2. **Risk Indicator**: A higher cap rate implies higher perceived risk or weaker market.
3. **Market Benchmark**: Allows comparison of properties across geographies.
4. **Macro Sensitivity**: Rises with interest rates, falls with strong rental demand.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is a REIT? List any two advantages of investing in REITs over direct property ownership.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Real Estate Investment Trust (REIT)** is a listed company that owns, operates, or finances income-producing real estate, and is legally required to distribute **at least 90% of its taxable income** as dividends to enjoy pass-through tax status.

**Advantages over Direct Ownership:**

1. **Liquidity**: REIT units trade on stock exchanges — can be sold in seconds, unlike direct property which takes 3–6 months.
2. **Diversification**: A single REIT unit gives exposure to a portfolio of properties, reducing idiosyncratic risk.
3. **Lower Capital Requirement**: Investors can participate with as little as a few hundred rupees, vs. crores for direct purchase.
4. **Professional Management**: Eliminates the operational burden of being a landlord.

---

## 🔹 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️**
> Common reasons students LOSE MARKS in real estate problems:
> 1. **Confusing NOI with cash flow** — NOI is *before* debt service; cash flow is *after*.
> 2. **Mixing up cap rate and cash-on-cash return** — Cap rate is unlevered, CoC is levered.
> 3. **Forgetting vacancy in EGI calculation** — Always compute EGI = PGI × (1 − vacancy rate) before subtracting OPEX.
> 4. **Unit mismatch in EMI** — Use *monthly* rate and *monthly* periods; convert annual DS to monthly carefully.
> 5. **Not stating formulas before substitution** — Always write the symbolic formula first, then plug in numbers.

---

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Q: (a)** A commercial property has the following characteristics:

- Purchase Price = **₹8,00,00,000**
- Gross Rental Income = **₹96,00,000 per year**
- Vacancy and Credit Loss = **8%**
- Operating Expenses = **₹24,00,000 per year**

**Compute the following:**
(i) Effective Gross Income (EGI)
(ii) Net Operating Income (NOI)
(iii) Going-in Cap Rate
(iv) The GRM (Gross Rent Multiplier)

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Effective Gross Income (EGI):**

$$\text{EGI} = \text{PGI} \times (1 - \text{Vacancy Rate})$$

$$\text{EGI} = 96{,}00{,}000 \times (1 - 0.08) = 96{,}00{,}000 \times 0.92$$

$$\boxed{\text{EGI} = ₹88{,}32{,}000}$$

---

**(ii) Net Operating Income (NOI):**

$$\text{NOI} = \text{EGI} - \text{OPEX}$$

$$\text{NOI} = 88{,}32{,}000 - 24{,}00{,}000$$

$$\boxed{\text{NOI} = ₹64{,}32{,}000}$$

---

**(iii) Going-in Cap Rate:**

$$r_{cap} = \dfrac{\text{NOI}}{\text{Value}} = \dfrac{64{,}32{,}000}{8{,}00{,}00{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{r_{cap} = 0.0804 = 8.04\%}$$

---

**(iv) Gross Rent Multiplier (GRM):**

$$\text{GRM} = \dfrac{\text{Property Price}}{\text{Gross Annual Rent (PGI)}}$$

$$\text{GRM} = \dfrac{8{,}00{,}00{,}000}{96{,}00{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{\text{GRM} = 8.33 \text{ years}}$$

> **Interpretation:** A GRM of 8.33 years is reasonable for a commercial property in a Tier-1 Indian city (typical range: 7–12 years).

---

**Q: (b)** A residential property is purchased for **₹1,20,00,000**. The buyer pays **25% as down payment** and finances the rest with a **15-year mortgage at 8.5% annual interest rate** (monthly compounding).

**Compute:**
(i) Monthly EMI
(ii) Total interest paid over the life of the loan
(iii) DSCR if the property's NOI is **₹9,60,000 per year**

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Loan Principal: $P = ₹1,20,00,000 \times 0.75 = ₹90,00,000$
- Annual Rate: 8.5% → Monthly Rate $r = 0.085/12 = 0.007083$
- Tenure: 15 years → $n = 15 \times 12 = 180$ months

---

**(i) Monthly EMI:**

$$M = P \cdot \dfrac{r(1+r)^n}{(1+r)^n - 1}$$

First, compute $(1.007083)^{180}$:

$$(1.007083)^{180} = e^{180 \times \ln(1.007083)} = e^{180 \times 0.007058} = e^{1.2705} \approx 3.5620$$

Substitute:

$$M = 90{,}00{,}000 \cdot \dfrac{0.007083 \times 3.5620}{3.5620 - 1}$$

$$M = 90{,}00{,}000 \cdot \dfrac{0.025230}{2.5620}$$

$$M = 90{,}00{,}000 \times 0.009848$$

$$\boxed{M \approx ₹88{,}633 \text{ per month}}$$

---

**(ii) Total Interest Paid:**

$$\text{Total Amount Paid} = M \times n = 88{,}633 \times 180 = ₹1,59{,}53{,}940$$

$$\text{Total Interest} = \text{Total Paid} - \text{Principal}$$

$$\text{Total Interest} = 1{,}59{,}53{,}940 - 90{,}00{,}000$$

$$\boxed{\text{Total Interest} = ₹69{,}53{,}940}$$

> **Insight:** The borrower pays **77% of the original principal as interest** over 15 years — illustrating the true cost of leverage.

---

**(iii) DSCR:**

$$\text{Annual Debt Service} = 88{,}633 \times 12 = ₹10{,}63{,}596$$

$$\text{DSCR} = \dfrac{\text{NOI}}{\text{DS}} = \dfrac{9{,}60{,}000}{10{,}63{,}596}$$

$$\boxed{\text{DSCR} = 0.903}$$

> **Critical Evaluation:** DSCR < 1.0 — the property **cannot cover its debt service from rental income alone**. The owner must fund the shortfall from other income sources. The lender will likely **reject this loan** until either rent increases, more equity is injected, or a longer tenure is negotiated.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Alternative]`

**Q: (a) Explain the three classical approaches to real estate valuation. Under what conditions is each approach most appropriate?**

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Real estate valuation is performed using three internationally recognized methods (per **IVS — International Valuation Standards**):

---

**1. Income Capitalization Approach**

**Method:** Value = Present Value of expected future net cash flows (rents) plus terminal resale value.

$$V = \sum_{t=1}^{N} \dfrac{\text{NOI}_t}{(1+r_{cap})^t} + \dfrac{\text{Terminal Value}}{(1+r_{cap})^N}$$

**When to use:**
- Income-producing properties: rental apartments, office buildings, malls, warehouses.
- Properties held for **investment** rather than owner-occupation.
- When a reliable **rental market** exists for comparable properties.

---

**2. Sales Comparison Approach (Market Approach)**

**Method:** Adjust prices of 3–5 recently sold **comparable properties** for differences in size, age, location, condition, and timing to estimate the subject's value.

$$V = P_{comp} \times \prod_{i} \text{Adjustment}_i$$

**When to use:**
- **Residential** properties (owner-occupied homes, plots).
- Active markets with **frequent, recent transactions**.
- Stable, non-volatile markets where "comps" are reliable.

---

**3. Cost Approach**

**Method:**

$$V = \text{Land Value} + \text{Replacement Cost of Improvements} - \text{Depreciation}$$

**When to use:**
- **New, unique, or special-purpose** properties (schools, hospitals, airports, religious buildings).
- Properties in **early construction** stages.
- When market data is unavailable or unreliable (e.g., remote industrial sites).
- For **insurance valuation** purposes.

---

**Comparison Summary Table:**

| Feature | Income Approach | Sales Comparison | Cost Approach |
|---|---|---|---|
| Best for | Investment property | Residential / Land | Special-purpose |
| Data needed | NOI, cap rate | Comparables | Land + construction cost |
| Reliability | High (commercial) | High (residential) | Medium |
| KTU exam focus | **★★★★★** | ★★★ | ★★ |

> **KTU Tip:** In exam, when asked to "value a property," default to the **Income Approach** if the property is described as rental/income-producing.

---

**Q: (b)** A real estate developer is evaluating two investment opportunities with the following cash flows (in ₹ Lakhs):

| Year | Project A (₹L) | Project B (₹L) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | -100 | -100 |
| 1 | +30 | +10 |
| 2 | +35 | +25 |
| 3 | +40 | +40 |
| 4 | +45 | +60 |
| 5 | +20 | +80 |

**Compute the IRR for each project. Which should the developer choose? Discuss the limitations of IRR as a project selection criterion.**

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

We solve the IRR equation for each project:

$$0 = \sum_{t=0}^{N} \dfrac{\text{CF}_t}{(1+\text{IRR})^t}$$

---

**Project A — Trial and Error:**

At $r = 25\%$:

$$\dfrac{30}{1.25} + \dfrac{35}{1.5625} + \dfrac{40}{1.9531} + \dfrac{45}{2.4414} + \dfrac{20}{3.0518}$$

$$= 24.00 + 22.40 + 20.48 + 18.43 + 6.55 = 91.86 \text{ (less than 100)}$$

At $r = 22\%$:

$$\dfrac{30}{1.22} + \dfrac{35}{1.4884} + \dfrac{40}{1.8158} + \dfrac{45}{2.2153} + \dfrac{20}{2.7027}$$

$$= 24.59 + 23.51 + 22.03 + 20.31 + 7.40 = 97.84$$

At $r = 20\%$:

$$= 25.00 + 24.31 + 23.15 + 21.70 + 8.04 = 102.20 \text{ (greater than 100)}$$

Interpolating between 20% and 22%:

$$\text{IRR}_A \approx 20\% + \dfrac{102.20 - 100}{102.20 - 97.84} \times 2\% = 20\% + 0.83\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{IRR}_A \approx 20.8\%}$$

---

**Project B — Trial and Error:**

At $r = 20\%$:

$$\dfrac{10}{1.20} + \dfrac{25}{1.44} + \dfrac{40}{1.728} + \dfrac{60}{2.0736} + \dfrac{80}{2.4883}$$

$$= 8.33 + 17.36 + 23.15 + 28.94 + 32.15 = 109.93$$

At $r = 25\%$:

$$\dfrac{10}{1.25} + \dfrac{25}{1.5625} + \dfrac{40}{1.9531} + \dfrac{60}{2.4414} + \dfrac{80}{3.0518}$$

$$= 8.00 + 16.00 + 20.48 + 24.58 + 26.21 = 95.27$$

Interpolating:

$$\text{IRR}_B \approx 20\% + \dfrac{109.93 - 100}{109.93 - 95.27} \times 5\% = 20\% + 3.53\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{IRR}_B \approx 23.5\%}$$

---

**Decision:**

By **IRR alone**, the developer should select **Project B** (23.5% > 20.8%).

---

**Limitations of IRR:**

1. **Multiple IRR Problem**: With non-conventional cash flows (multiple sign changes), IRR can be non-unique or undefined.
2. **Reinvestment Assumption**: IRR implicitly assumes intermediate cash flows are reinvested *at the IRR* — often unrealistic. **NPV assumes reinvestment at the cost of capital**, which is more conservative.
3. **Scale Ignorance**: IRR is a *rate*, not a *value*. A small project with high IRR may add less wealth than a large project with lower IRR.
4. **Mutually Exclusive Projects**: IRR can mislead when comparing projects of different sizes or timing.
5. **No Absolute Wealth Measure**: IRR does not tell you *how much* wealth is created, only the rate.

> **Better Practice:** For mutually exclusive projects of different scales, use **NPV** or **Incremental IRR** (IRR on the *incremental* cash flow of the larger project over the smaller).

---

## 🔹 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST 📝

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "RENT-CAP-LOC-LEV" Final Summary**

✅ **Real Estate** is a tangible, illiquid, location-bound asset combining bond-like income with equity-like appreciation.

✅ **The "SLCIL" mnemonic** captures its five defining features: Scarcity, Location, Capital intensity, Illiquidity, Leverage.

✅ **Three valuation methods**: Income, Sales Comparison, Cost — pick based on property type and data availability.

✅ **NOI** is the master metric: $\text{NOI} = \text{EGI} - \text{OPEX}$, where $\text{EGI} = \text{PGI} \times (1 - \text{Vacancy Rate})$.

✅ **Cap Rate** = NOI / Value; **Value** = NOI / Cap Rate. Inverse relationship with interest rates.

✅ **GRM** = Price / PGI (used in residential); **Cap Rate** = NOI / Value (used in commercial).

✅ **Mortgage EMI**: $M = P \cdot \dfrac{r(1+r)^n}{(1+r)^n - 1}$ — always use *monthly* $r$ and *monthly* $n$.

✅ **DSCR** = NOI / Debt Service — must be ≥ 1.20 for a safe loan; < 1.0 = property cannot service debt.

✅ **Cash-on-Cash Return** = Annual Pre-Tax CF / Cash Invested — measures levered equity yield.

✅ **Leverage amplifies** both gains and losses; total equity return can be 2–3× the unlevered property yield.

✅ **REITs** convert illiquid real estate into liquid, diversified, dividend-paying securities — must distribute ≥ 90% of taxable income.

✅ **Cap Rate Decomposition**: $r_{cap} = r_f + \text{RP} + \text{LP} + \text{RR}$ — explains what builds the discount rate.

✅ **Indian Context**: Embassy, Mindspace, Brookfield, Nexus Select are major listed REITs (all commercial-office focused).

✅ **Exam Pitfall**: Distinguish NOI (before debt) from Cash Flow (after debt); Cap Rate (unlevered) from CoC (levered).

✅ **For IRR problems**: Always show formula → substitution → trial-and-error → interpolation steps.

✅ **Real estate provides**: Income + Appreciation + Inflation Hedge + Diversification + Leverage — a unique four-way payoff unmatched by any other asset class.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Securities
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET602 - FINANCIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Securities

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Securities

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology):**
> A **Security** is a fungible, tradable financial instrument that holds monetary value and represents a contractual obligation or ownership right between an issuer and a holder. Securities are classified under financial assets and are governed by regulatory frameworks such as the **Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI)** in the Indian context and the **Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC)** globally.

Mathematically, a security is a stochastic claim on future cash flows, where the present value is determined by the discounting of expected future payoffs at a rate commensurate with the risk involved.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The IOU Receipt":**
> Imagine you lend ₹1,000 to your friend for one year, and your friend gives you a signed receipt promising to return ₹1,100 after one year. That signed paper is essentially a **security** — a documented proof of a financial promise. If your friend is a large corporation instead, that receipt is called a **bond**. If instead of lending money, you buy a small slice of your friend's pizza shop hoping to share future profits, that slice is called a **stock (equity)**. So, securities are simply **tradable financial promises** — pieces of paper (now digital) that represent money owed, ownership, or bets on future outcomes.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Classification

The primary classification parameters used by KTU examiners include:

- **Issuer Type**: Corporate, Government, Supranational
- **Claim Priority**: Senior debt, Subordinated debt, Equity
- **Maturity Profile**: Money market (≤ 1 year), Capital market (> 1 year)
- **Risk Class**: Risk-free, Low-risk, High-risk
- **Standardized Trading Unit**: Lot size = **1 share** (equity) or **₹1,000 face value** (bonds in India)

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Module 1 specifically emphasizes the distinction between **Primary Securities** (direct claim instruments like stocks and bonds) and **Derivative Securities** (whose value is *derived* from an underlying asset).

## 1.4 Broad Taxonomy of Securities

| Category | Sub-Type | Example | Risk Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Equity Securities** | Common Stock, Preferred Stock | Infosys share, TCS share | High |
| **Debt Securities** | Bonds, Debentures, T-Bills | Government of India G-Sec | Low–Medium |
| **Derivative Securities** | Futures, Options, Swaps | Nifty Futures Contract | Very High |
| **Hybrid Securities** | Convertible Bonds, Preference Shares | Compulsorily Convertible Debentures | Medium–High |
| **Money Market Instruments** | Commercial Paper, Certificate of Deposit | 90-day Commercial Paper | Low |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: The Risk-Return Tradeoff

The fundamental principle governing all securities is the **Risk-Return Tradeoff**:
> *Higher expected returns are required to compensate investors for bearing higher risk.*

This is mathematically formalized in finance through:
1. **Time Value of Money (TVM)** — A rupee today is worth more than a rupee tomorrow.
2. **Risk Premium Theory** — Risk-averse investors demand a premium over the risk-free rate.
3. **No-Arbitrage Principle** — Two securities with identical payoffs must have identical prices.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of Security Pricing

**Step 1: Identify the Cash Flow Stream**
Every security generates a stream of future cash flows. The pricing process begins by enumerating these cash flows $\{C_1, C_2, ..., C_n\}$ at discrete time points.

**Step 2: Determine the Appropriate Discount Rate**
The discount rate $r$ must reflect:
- The risk-free rate ($R_f$)
- A risk premium ($\beta \times (R_m - R_f)$) for systematic risk
- A liquidity premium for illiquid securities

**Step 3: Discount the Future Cash Flows**
The intrinsic value $V_0$ is computed as the present value of all future expected cash flows.

**Step 4: Compare Market Price to Intrinsic Value**
- If $P_{market} < V_0$ → Security is **undervalued** → Buy signal
- If $P_{market} > V_0$ → Security is **overvalued** → Sell signal
- If $P_{market} = V_0$ → Security is **fairly valued**

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas constitute the **high-frequency examination items** for the KTU 2024 ESE and internal assessments.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bond Price (Coupon Bond) | $P = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{C}{(1+r)^t} + \dfrac{F}{(1+r)^n}$ | $C$ = Coupon, $F$ = Face Value, $n$ = Periods, $r$ = YTM | ₹ (Rupees) |
| 2 | Zero-Coupon Bond Price | $P = \dfrac{F}{(1+r)^n}$ | $F$ = Face Value, $r$ = YTM, $n$ = Periods | ₹ |
| 3 | Perpetuity Price | $P = \dfrac{C}{r}$ | $C$ = Constant Coupon, $r$ = Discount Rate | ₹ |
| 4 | Gordon Growth Model (DDM) | $P_0 = \dfrac{D_1}{r - g}$ | $D_1$ = Next Dividend, $r$ = Required Return, $g$ = Growth | ₹ |
| 5 | Current Yield | $CY = \dfrac{C}{P_0} \times 100$ | $C$ = Annual Coupon, $P_0$ = Current Price | % |
| 6 | Yield to Maturity (Approx.) | $YTM \approx \dfrac{C + \dfrac{F - P_0}{n}}{\dfrac{F + P_0}{2}}$ | All bond variables | % |
| 7 | CAPM | $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i (E(R_m) - R_f)$ | $R_f$ = Risk-free rate, $\beta$ = Beta, $R_m$ = Market return | % |
| 8 | Holding Period Return | $HPR = \dfrac{P_1 - P_0 + D}{P_0}$ | $P_1$ = End Price, $D$ = Dividend | % |
| 9 | Forward Price | $F_0 = S_0 \times e^{rT}$ (continuous) or $S_0(1+r)^T$ (discrete) | $S_0$ = Spot, $T$ = Time | ₹ |
| 10 | Sharpe Ratio | $SR = \dfrac{R_p - R_f}{\sigma_p}$ | $R_p$ = Portfolio return, $\sigma_p$ = Std. Dev. | Dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Rule:** In the Gordon Growth Model, the constraint $r > g$ **MUST** hold; otherwise, the model produces a meaningless negative or infinite price.

## 2.4 Engineering & Real-World Utility

In financial engineering practice, these security-pricing models are deployed in:
- **Corporate Treasury Management**: Pricing corporate bond issuances
- **Algorithmic Trading**: Real-time arbitrage detection
- **Risk Management**: Computing Value-at-Risk (VaR) for portfolios
- **Pension Fund Management**: Liability-driven investment (LDI) strategies
- **Venture Capital & Startup Valuation**: Using modified DDM and DCF techniques

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Bond Pricing Formula (Discounted Cash Flow Method)

A coupon-paying bond promises a stream of fixed coupon payments $C$ at the end of each period for $n$ periods, plus the face value $F$ repaid at maturity.

**Step 1:** Consider a single future cash flow $C_t$ received at time $t$. The present value $PV$ of this single future cash flow, discounted at rate $r$, is given by the time value of money equation.

$$
PV_1 = \frac{C_1}{(1+r)^1}
$$

**Step 2:** For two periods, the present value of $C_2$ is obtained by dividing by $(1+r)^2$ because the money could have been reinvested for two periods at rate $r$.

$$
PV_2 = \frac{C_2}{(1+r)^2}
$$

**Step 3:** Generalize this logic for $n$ periods. The total present value of the bond is the sum of the present values of all coupon payments plus the present value of the face value repaid at maturity $n$.

$$
P_0 = \frac{C_1}{(1+r)^1} + \frac{C_2}{(1+r)^2} + \cdots + \frac{C_n}{(1+r)^n} + \frac{F}{(1+r)^n}
$$

**Step 4:** Recognize the geometric series structure. The first $n$ terms form a finite annuity, and the last term is a single lump-sum discount.

$$
P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C}{(1+r)^t} + \frac{F}{(1+r)^n}
$$

**Step 5:** Apply the geometric series summation identity $\sum_{t=1}^{n} x^t = \frac{x(1 - x^n)}{1 - x}$ to obtain the closed-form annuity expression. Let $x = \frac{1}{1+r}$.

$$
P_0 = C \cdot \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r} + \frac{F}{(1+r)^n}
$$

**Final Bond Pricing Equation:**

$$
\boxed{\,P_0 = C \cdot \left[ \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r} \right] + \frac{F}{(1+r)^n}\,}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Gordon Growth Model (Dividend Discount Model)

The Gordon Growth Model assumes dividends grow at a constant perpetual rate $g$, and the required rate of return is $r$.

**Step 1:** Define the dividend stream. If the current dividend is $D_0$, then next year's dividend $D_1 = D_0(1+g)$, year 2 dividend $D_2 = D_0(1+g)^2$, and so on.

$$
D_t = D_0 (1+g)^t
$$

**Step 2:** Discount each dividend back to the present at the required rate of return $r$.

$$
P_0 = \frac{D_0(1+g)}{1+r} + \frac{D_0(1+g)^2}{(1+r)^2} + \cdots
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $D_1 = D_0(1+g)$ to write the series in terms of the next dividend $D_1$.

$$
P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{D_1 (1+g)^{t-1}}{(1+r)^t}
$$

**Step 4:** Factor out $\frac{D_1}{1+r}$ from the series to recognize the geometric progression with common ratio $\frac{1+g}{1+r}$.

$$
P_0 = \frac{D_1}{1+r} \cdot \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \left( \frac{1+g}{1+r} \right)^{t}
$$

**Step 5:** Apply the infinite geometric series formula $\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} x^t = \frac{1}{1-x}$ which is valid only when $\left| \frac{1+g}{1+r} \right| < 1$, i.e., $r > g$.

$$
P_0 = \frac{D_1}{1+r} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1+g}{1+r}}
$$

**Step 6:** Simplify the denominator by finding a common denominator of $(1+r)$.

$$
1 - \frac{1+g}{1+r} = \frac{(1+r) - (1+g)}{1+r} = \frac{r-g}{1+r}
$$

**Step 7:** Substitute this simplification back into the price equation.

$$
P_0 = \frac{D_1}{1+r} \cdot \frac{1+r}{r-g} = \frac{D_1}{r-g}
$$

**Final Gordon Growth Equation:**

$$
\boxed{\,P_0 = \frac{D_1}{r - g}\,}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)

The CAPM emerges from the **Single-Index Model** of William Sharpe (1964), which expresses the return on any security as a function of market return.

**Step 1:** Express security return as a linear function of market return. The term $\alpha$ is the firm-specific intercept, and $\beta$ measures sensitivity to the market.

$$
R_i = \alpha_i + \beta_i R_m + \epsilon_i
$$

**Step 2:** Under equilibrium conditions, the expected residual $\epsilon_i$ is zero, so we take expectations on both sides.

$$
E(R_i) = \alpha_i + \beta_i E(R_m)
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the no-arbitrage condition. The market portfolio $M$ must satisfy $E(R_m) = R_f + (E(R_m) - R_f) \cdot 1$, which means $\alpha_m = R_f(1 - \beta_m)$ where $\beta_m = 1$.

**Step 4:** Use the diversification argument. In a well-diversified portfolio, all idiosyncratic risk ($\epsilon_i$) vanishes, leaving only systematic (market) risk priced via $\beta$.

**Step 5:** Substitute and rearrange to obtain the final CAPM relationship.

$$
E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \big[ E(R_m) - R_f \big]
$$

**Final CAPM Equation:**

$$
\boxed{\,E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \cdot \big[ E(R_m) - R_f \big]\,}
$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A 10-year corporate bond has a face value of ₹1,000, an annual coupon rate of **8%**, and the current Yield to Maturity is **10%**. Compute the bond price.

**Solution:**

Given: $F = 1000$, $C = 0.08 \times 1000 = 80$, $n = 10$, $r = 0.10$

$$
P_0 = 80 \cdot \left[ \frac{1 - (1.10)^{-10}}{0.10} \right] + \frac{1000}{(1.10)^{10}}
$$

Compute $(1.10)^{-10} = 0.3855$

$$
P_0 = 80 \cdot \left[ \frac{1 - 0.3855}{0.10} \right] + 1000 \cdot 0.3855
$$

$$
P_0 = 80 \cdot (6.1446) + 385.54 = 491.57 + 385.54 = 877.11
$$

**Bond Price** = **₹877.11** (discount bond since price < face value)

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Taxonomy of Securities

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FINANCIAL SECURITIES] --> B[PRIMARY SECURITIES]
    A --> C[DERIVATIVE SECURITIES]
    A --> D[HYBRID SECURITIES]

    B --> B1[Equity Securities]
    B --> B2[Debt Securities]
    B --> B3[Money Market Instruments]

    B1 --> B1a[Common Stock]
    B1 --> B1b[Preferred Stock]
    B1 --> B1c[ADRs and GDRs]

    B2 --> B2a[Government Bonds]
    B2 --> B2b[Corporate Bonds]
    B2 --> B2c[Municipal Bonds]
    B2 --> B2d[Debentures]

    B3 --> B3a[Treasury Bills]
    B3 --> B3b[Commercial Paper]
    B3 --> B3c[Certificate of Deposit]
    B3 --> B3d[Repurchase Agreements]

    C --> C1[Futures Contracts]
    C --> C2[Options Contracts]
    C --> C3[Swaps]
    C --> C4[Forwards]

    C1 --> C1a[Stock Futures]
    C1 --> C1b[Index Futures]
    C1 --> C1c[Commodity Futures]

    C2 --> C2a[Call Options]
    C2 --> C2b[Put Options]

    C3 --> C3a[Interest Rate Swaps]
    C3 --> C3b[Currency Swaps]

    D --> D1[Convertible Bonds]
    D --> D2[Preference Shares with Warrants]
    D --> D3[Equity Warrants]
    D --> D4[Real Estate Investment Trusts REITs]
```

## 4.2 Primary vs Secondary Market Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Issuer[ISSUER: Company or Government] -->|Issues new securities| PrimaryMkt[PRIMARY MARKET]
    PrimaryMkt -->|IPO, FPO, Rights Issue| Investor[INVESTOR: Retail and Institutional]
    Investor -->|Trades existing securities| SecondaryMkt[SECONDARY MARKET]
    SecondaryMkt -->|Stock Exchanges BSE, NSE| Investor
    SecondaryMkt -->|Price Discovery| MarketPrice[Market Price Signal]
    MarketPrice -->|Feedback| Issuer
    PrimaryMkt -->|Capital Raised| IssuerCapital[Issuer Capital Account]
    Investor -->|Dividends, Coupons| CashFlow[Cash Flow Stream]
    CashFlow -->|Reinvests| SecondaryMkt
```

## 4.3 Risk-Return Pyramid Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Bottom[Sovereign Bonds - Lowest Risk, Lowest Return]
    Bottom --> Level2[Investment Grade Corporate Bonds]
    Level2 --> Level3[High Yield Junk Bonds]
    Level3 --> Level4[Preferred Stock]
    Level4 --> Level5[Blue Chip Common Stock]
    Level5 --> Level6[Small Cap and Mid Cap Stocks]
    Level6 --> Level7[Derivatives Options and Futures]
    Level7 --> Top[Crypto Assets and Penny Stocks - Highest Risk, Highest Expected Return]
```

## 4.4 Security Pricing Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start: Identify Security] --> Identify[Step 1: Identify Cash Flow Type]
    Identify --> Decision1{Is it a bond?}
    Decision1 -->|Yes| BondPath[Bond Path]
    Decision1 -->|No| Decision2{Is it equity with dividends?}
    Decision2 -->|Yes| EquityPath[Equity DDM Path]
    Decision2 -->|No| Decision3{Is it a derivative?}
    Decision3 -->|Yes| DerivPath[Derivative Path]
    Decision3 -->|No| HybridPath[Hybrid Security Path]

    BondPath --> Discount[BondPath: Discount coupons and face value]
    EquityPath --> DDMCalc[EquityPath: Apply Gordon Growth Model]
    DerivPath --> BlackScholes[DerivativePath: Apply Black Scholes or Binomial Model]
    HybridPath --> Combo[HybridPath: Decompose into bond plus option component]

    Discount --> IntrinsicValue[Compute Intrinsic Value V0]
    DDMCalc --> IntrinsicValue
    BlackScholes --> IntrinsicValue
    Combo --> IntrinsicValue

    IntrinsicValue --> Compare{Compare V0 to Market Price}
    Compare -->|V0 greater than P| Buy[BUY Decision]
    Compare -->|V0 less than P| Sell[SELL Decision]
    Compare -->|V0 equals P| Hold[HOLD Decision]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a security. Distinguish between a primary security and a derivative security with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **security** is a tradable financial instrument representing a contractual right to receive future cash flows, ownership, or any form of monetary value. Securities are issued by corporations, governments, or financial institutions and are regulated by market authorities.

- **Primary Security**: A direct claim on the issuer's assets or income. Example: A Government of India 10-year G-Sec bond. The holder has a direct creditor relationship with the issuer.
- **Derivative Security**: A contract whose value is *derived* from an underlying asset. Example: A Nifty 50 Index Futures contract. The holder does not have a direct claim on the issuer of underlying stocks.

> **Marking Scheme (1 + 1 + 1)**: Definition (1M), Primary with example (1M), Derivative with example (1M).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is Yield to Maturity (YTM)? Why is it called the internal rate of return of a bond?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Yield to Maturity (YTM)** is the annualized rate of return an investor would earn if a bond is held until its maturity date, assuming all coupon payments are reinvested at the same rate.

It is called the **internal rate of return** because YTM is the discount rate $r$ that equates the present value of all future coupon payments and the face value repayment to the bond's current market price. Mathematically, YTM solves:

$$
P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C}{(1+r)^t} + \frac{F}{(1+r)^n}
$$

> **Marking Scheme (1 + 2)**: Definition (1M), IRR justification with equation (2M).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with a typical split of **7 marks per sub-part**. Students must answer **ONE full question** from the choice.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**A) A 5-year corporate bond has a face value of ₹10,000 and pays an annual coupon of 9%. The current Yield to Maturity is 11%. Calculate: (7 marks)**

**(i)** The current market price of the bond.
**(ii)** The current yield of the bond.

**(B) A stock is expected to pay a dividend of ₹12 next year. The required rate of return is 14% and the constant growth rate of dividends is 5%. Using the Gordon Growth Model, compute: (7 marks)**

**(i)** The intrinsic value of the stock today.
**(ii)** The expected price after 4 years.

---

**Complete Model Solution:**

#### Part A(i): Bond Price Calculation

**Given:** $F = 10{,}000$, $C = 0.09 \times 10{,}000 = 900$, $n = 5$, $r = 0.11$

**Step 1:** Compute $(1.11)^{-5}$

$$
(1.11)^{-5} = \frac{1}{(1.11)^5} = \frac{1}{1.6851} = 0.5935
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the bond pricing formula.

$$
P_0 = 900 \cdot \left[ \frac{1 - 0.5935}{0.11} \right] + 10{,}000 \cdot 0.5935
$$

$$
P_0 = 900 \cdot (3.6959) + 5{,}935 = 3{,}326.32 + 5{,}935 = 9{,}261.32
$$

**Bond Price = ₹9,261.32** (Discount bond, since $P_0 < F$)

#### Part A(ii): Current Yield

$$
CY = \frac{C}{P_0} = \frac{900}{9{,}261.32} = 0.0972 = 9.72\%
$$

#### Part B(i): Intrinsic Value via DDM

**Given:** $D_1 = 12$, $r = 0.14$, $g = 0.05$

Since $r > g$ is satisfied, we apply the formula:

$$
P_0 = \frac{D_1}{r - g} = \frac{12}{0.14 - 0.05} = \frac{12}{0.09} = 133.33
$$

**Intrinsic Value = ₹133.33 per share**

#### Part B(ii): Expected Price After 4 Years

The dividend in year 5 (i.e., next dividend relative to year 4) is $D_5 = D_1 (1+g)^4$.

$$
D_5 = 12 \times (1.05)^4 = 12 \times 1.2155 = 14.586
$$

$$
P_4 = \frac{D_5}{r - g} = \frac{14.586}{0.09} = 162.07
$$

**Expected Price at end of Year 4 = ₹162.07**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(A) Explain the Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) with its assumptions. A stock has a beta of 1.4, the risk-free rate is 7%, and the expected market return is 13%. Calculate the expected return on the stock using CAPM. (7 marks)**

**(B) A company issues a perpetual bond (perpetuity) paying ₹100 annually. If the required rate of return is 12.5%, what is the maximum price an investor should pay? Justify using the perpetuity formula. (7 marks)**

---

**Complete Model Solution:**

#### Part A: CAPM Analysis

**Assumptions of CAPM:**
1. Investors are rational and risk-averse.
2. Single-period investment horizon.
3. Homogeneous expectations among investors.
4. No taxes or transaction costs.
5. Investors can borrow and lend at the risk-free rate.
6. Markets are in equilibrium.

**Calculation:**

**Given:** $\beta = 1.4$, $R_f = 7\%$, $E(R_m) = 13\%$

$$
E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \cdot [E(R_m) - R_f]
$$

$$
E(R_i) = 7\% + 1.4 \times (13\% - 7\%) = 7\% + 1.4 \times 6\% = 7\% + 8.4\% = 15.4\%
$$

**Expected Return = 15.4%**

**Interpretation:** Since beta > 1, the stock is more volatile than the market and thus commands a higher expected return.

#### Part B: Perpetuity Valuation

**Given:** $C = 100$, $r = 0.125$

The perpetuity formula is derived from the annuity formula as $n \to \infty$:

$$
P = \lim_{n \to \infty} \left[ C \cdot \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r} \right] = \frac{C}{r}
$$

**Calculation:**

$$
P = \frac{100}{0.125} = 800
$$

**Maximum Price = ₹800**

**Justification:** A perpetuity has no maturity, so the bondholder receives ₹100 forever. The present value of this infinite stream, discounted at 12.5%, is ₹800. Any price above ₹800 would yield a return lower than the required 12.5%, making the bond unattractive.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Securities Problems:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in Discounting**: Students often write $(1+r)^n$ in the numerator instead of the denominator. Always confirm direction of discounting.
> 2. **Forgetting Face Value Repayment**: In bond pricing, the face value $F$ is repaid at maturity in *one lump sum* in addition to the last coupon — do not double count.
> 3. **Gordon Growth Constraint Violation**: If $r \le g$, the DDM model is invalid. Mention this explicitly if applicable.
> 4. **Beta Misinterpretation**: A beta of 1.0 means the stock moves with the market; > 1 means more volatile; < 1 means less volatile. Many students confuse the direction.
> 5. **Units and Percentage Confusion**: Express $r$ and $g$ as decimals (e.g., 0.10) inside formulas, and convert to % only in the final answer.
> 6. **Perpetuity Misapplication**: The perpetuity formula is only for infinite-life instruments. Do not use it for finite-maturity bonds.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1: Securities**

- A **security** is a tradable financial claim with monetary value.
- **Primary securities** = direct claims (stocks, bonds). **Derivatives** = derived claims (futures, options).
- **Equity = Ownership**; **Debt = Creditorship**; **Hybrid = Mix of both**.
- **Bond Price Formula** (Most Important): $P_0 = \sum \frac{C}{(1+r)^t} + \frac{F}{(1+r)^n}$
- **Gordon Growth Model**: $P_0 = \dfrac{D_1}{r - g}$ — valid **only** when $r > g$.
- **CAPM**: $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i [E(R_m) - R_f]$ — defines expected return via systematic risk.
- **Current Yield** measures annual income; **YTM** measures total annualized return assuming held to maturity.
- **Perpetuity**: $P = \dfrac{C}{r}$ — infinite-life bond, no face value repayment.
- Higher **Beta** = Higher systematic risk = Higher required return.
- **Discount bond**: $P_0 < F$ (YTM > Coupon Rate); **Premium bond**: $P_0 > F$ (YTM < Coupon Rate).
- The **No-Arbitrage Principle** ensures market efficiency — identical payoffs must have identical prices.
- SEBI regulates securities markets in India; SEC in the United States.
- **Money market** instruments mature in ≤ 1 year; **Capital market** instruments mature in > 1 year.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Commodities
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET602 - FINANCIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Commodities

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Commodities

In the rigorous language of **Financial Engineering** as adopted by the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, a **Commodity** is defined as a basic, raw, or primary physical good that is interchangeable in nature across producers and standardized for trading on formal exchanges. Commodities form one of the foundational **asset classes** in global financial markets, alongside equities, fixed income, and currencies.

Formally, a commodity is a fungible economic good that:
- Possesses **uniform quality** across producers (fungibility),
- Is **physically deliverable** (or cash-settled on equivalent benchmarks),
- Has its price determined by global **supply and demand dynamics**, and
- Is traded on regulated **commodity exchanges** under standardized contracts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Commodities in Financial Engineering refer **NOT** merely to physical goods but to the *financial instruments* (futures, forwards, options, swaps) built on these underlying physical assets. The exam treats commodities as an *asset class* for pricing, hedging, and portfolio diversification.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you go to a vegetable market. Whether you buy wheat from Farmer A in Punjab or Farmer B in Madhya Pradesh, the wheat is graded by the same quality parameters (protein content, moisture, foreign matter). You don't really care *whose* wheat you get, only that it meets a **standard grade**. This interchangeability is what makes wheat a commodity.

Now, instead of taking physical delivery of 100 tonnes of wheat (which would require storage, transport, insurance), you can sign a paper contract today promising to *buy or sell* that wheat at a fixed price in 3 months. This contract — a **commodity derivative** — is the financial engineering perspective on commodities.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of commodities as the "raw ingredients" of the global economy — like flour, sugar, gold bars, and crude oil. Just as a baker needs flour to make bread, industries need commodities as basic inputs. Financial engineers then build "recipes" (derivatives) on top of these raw ingredients to manage price risk.

## 1.3 Classification of Commodities

Commodities are broadly classified into two major categories under the KTU syllabus framework:

### 1.3.1 Hard Commodities
These are commodities that are **extracted or mined** from the earth.

| Sub-Category | Examples | Key Exchange Traded |
|--------------|----------|---------------------|
| **Precious Metals** | Gold, Silver, Platinum, Palladium | MCX, COMEX, LBMA |
| **Base Metals** | Copper, Aluminium, Zinc, Lead, Nickel | LME, MCX |
| **Energy Commodities** | Crude Oil, Natural Gas, Coal, Heating Oil | NYMEX, ICE, MCX |

### 1.3.2 Soft Commodities (Agricultural & Livestock)
These are commodities that are **grown or raised**.

| Sub-Category | Examples | Key Exchange Traded |
|--------------|----------|---------------------|
| **Cereals & Grains** | Wheat, Rice, Maize, Barley | CBOT, NCDEX |
| **Oilseeds** | Soybean, Rapeseed, Mustard, Crude Palm Oil | CBOT, BMD, NCDEX |
| **Softs** | Sugar, Coffee, Cocoa, Cotton, Rubber | ICE, NYBOT, MCX |
| **Livestock & Dairy** | Live Cattle, Lean Hogs, Milk | CME |

> [!NOTE]
> **India-Specific:** Multi Commodity Exchange of India (**MCX**) handles Bullion, Base Metals, and Energy. National Commodity & Derivatives Exchange (**NCDEX**) handles agricultural commodities. These are the most relevant Indian exchanges for KTU exam questions.

## 1.4 Key Characteristics of Commodities

A commodity must satisfy the following characteristics to be exchange-traded:

1. **Fungibility:** Any one unit of the commodity is identical to and interchangeable with any other unit of the same grade.
2. **Durability:** Must be storable for a reasonable period (this excludes perishables like fresh vegetables).
3. **Standardization:** Quality, quantity, and delivery specifications are predefined by the exchange.
4. **Homogeneity:** Uniform characteristics irrespective of producer.
5. **Marketability:** Sufficient supply and demand to support an active futures market.
6. **Price Volatility:** Prices fluctuate due to weather, geopolitics, currency movements — this creates the *need* for hedging.
7. **Physical Deliverability:** Either through actual delivery or cash settlement on a benchmark price.

## 1.5 Standard Metrics Used in Commodity Markets

- **Tick Size:** Smallest permissible price movement (e.g., **₹1 per barrel** for crude oil on MCX).
- **Lot Size:** Standardized contract quantity (e.g., **100 barrels** for crude oil).
- **Quotation Unit:** Price quoted per standardized unit (per kg, per quintal, per barrel).
- **Expiry Date:** Last date of the contract, after which physical delivery or cash settlement occurs.
- **Initial Margin:** Upfront collateral (typically **5%–10%** of contract value) the trader must deposit.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Spot vs. Forward/Futures Market for Commodities

The two fundamental markets for commodities are:

### 2.1.1 Spot Market (Cash Market)
- **Definition:** Market where commodities are traded for **immediate delivery and immediate payment**.
- **Settlement:** T+1 or T+2 (immediate physical delivery or cash settlement).
- **Price:** Called the **Spot Price** $S_0$.
- **Use:** For immediate consumption/processing needs.

### 2.1.2 Forward/Futures Market
- **Definition:** Market where contracts are traded for **delivery at a future date** at a price agreed today.
- **Settlement:** On a specified future date.
- **Price:** Called the **Forward Price** $F_0$ or **Futures Price** $f_0$.
- **Use:** Hedging future price risk and speculation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Focus Point:** The difference between *Forward Contracts* and *Futures Contracts* is often a 7-mark question. Forwards are **OTC, customized, with default risk**, while Futures are **exchange-traded, standardized, with clearing house guarantee and daily mark-to-market (MTM)**.

## 2.2 The Cost-of-Carry Model (Core Pricing Theory)

The most fundamental pricing relationship for commodity futures is the **Cost-of-Carry Model**. It states:

> *"The futures price equals the spot price compounded by the cost of holding the physical commodity (storage, insurance, financing) net of any income earned (convenience yield, dividends)."*

### 2.2.1 Components of Cost of Carry

| Symbol | Component | Description | Direction |
|--------|-----------|-------------|-----------|
| $S_0$ | Spot Price | Current market price | Base |
| $r$ | Risk-free rate | Financing cost (opportunity cost of capital) | Adds (+) |
| $u$ | Storage cost | Warehousing, insurance, spoilage | Adds (+) |
| $y$ | Convenience yield | Benefit of physically holding the commodity | Subtracts (−) |

### 2.2.2 Intuition Behind Cost of Carry

When you buy a commodity in the spot market and hold it until time $T$:
- You pay $S_0$ today,
- You **tie up capital** that could have earned $r$ (financing cost),
- You pay for **storage and insurance** (cost $u$),
- But you **gain flexibility** because physically holding inventory has value during shortages (convenience yield $y$).

The futures price must reflect these holding costs/benefits, otherwise **arbitrage** would arise.

## 2.3 Market States: Contango and Backwardation

### 2.3.1 Contango
- **Definition:** When futures prices are **higher** than spot prices, and the futures curve is **upward-sloping**.
- **Reason:** Cost of carry ($r + u$) exceeds convenience yield ($y$). Holding the physical commodity is expensive.
- **Visual:** $F_0 > S_0$ and $F_1 < F_2 < F_3 < \ldots$

### 2.3.2 Backwardation
- **Definition:** When futures prices are **lower** than spot prices, and the futures curve is **downward-sloping**.
- **Reason:** Convenience yield ($y$) exceeds cost of carry ($r + u$). Holding physical inventory is highly valuable (e.g., during scarcity).
- **Visual:** $F_0 < S_0$ and $F_1 > F_2 > F_3 > \ldots$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** "Contango is the *normal* market state for storable commodities because net cost of carry is usually positive. Backwardation signals *supply tightness* or *high convenience value*."

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Industry

Commodity financial engineering is critical for:
- **Airlines** hedging jet fuel (crude oil derivatives) to lock in ticket prices.
- **Steel manufacturers** hedging iron ore and coking coal costs.
- **Bakeries** hedging wheat and sugar to stabilize bread pricing.
- **Jewellers** hedging gold price risk.
- **Power producers** hedging natural gas and coal costs.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 ESE — Memorize This Table.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables / Units |
|---|--------------|----------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Cost-of-Carry (No Income)** | $F_0 = S_0 \cdot e^{rT}$ | $S_0$: Spot Price; $r$: Risk-free rate (decimal); $T$: Years to maturity |
| 2 | **Cost-of-Carry (With Storage)** | $F_0 = S_0 \cdot e^{(r+u)T}$ | $u$: Storage cost rate (decimal) |
| 3 | **Cost-of-Carry (Full Model)** | $F_0 = S_0 \cdot e^{(r+u-y)T}$ | $y$: Convenience yield (decimal) |
| 4 | **Annualized Basis** | $b = \dfrac{F_0 - S_0}{S_0}$ | Basis expressed as a fraction |
| 5 | **Contango Check** | $F_0 > S_0 \Rightarrow b > 0$ | Positive basis = Contango |
| 6 | **Backwardation Check** | $F_0 < S_0 \Rightarrow b < 0$ | Negative basis = Backwardation |
| 7 | **Margin Call Amount** | $M_c = (P_t - P_{t-1}) \cdot Q$ | $P_t$: Current price; $P_{t-1}$: Previous settlement; $Q$: Lot size |
| 8 | **Hedge Ratio (Minimum Variance)** | $h^* = \rho \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_S}{\sigma_F}$ | $\rho$: Correlation; $\sigma_S$: Std. dev. of spot; $\sigma_F$: Std. dev. of futures |
| 9 | **Number of Futures Contracts** | $N = h^* \cdot \dfrac{Q_S}{Q_F}$ | $Q_S$: Spot exposure; $Q_F$: Lot size of futures |
| 10 | **Payoff of Long Forward** | $\Pi = S_T - K$ | $S_T$: Spot at maturity; $K$: Forward price |
| 11 | **Payoff of Short Forward** | $\Pi = K - S_T$ | Same variables as above |
| 12 | **Forward Value at Time $t < T$** | $f_t = (F_0 - S_0) \cdot e^{r(T-t)}$ | $t$: Current time |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Cost-of-Carry Futures Price Formula

We will rigorously derive the relationship $F_0 = S_0 \cdot e^{(r+u-y)T}$ using the **No-Arbitrage Argument**.

### Setup of the Arbitrage Argument

Suppose today (time $t=0$) the spot price is $S_0$ and the futures price for delivery at time $T$ is $F_0$. Assume the following:
- Annualized risk-free rate: $r$
- Annualized storage cost rate: $u$
- Annualized convenience yield rate: $y$
- The commodity is storable.

### Strategy A: "Buy and Hold" Strategy

1. Borrow $S_0$ at rate $r$ today.
2. Buy 1 unit of the commodity at spot price $S_0$.
3. Store the commodity for $T$ years, paying storage cost at rate $u$.
4. At time $T$, deliver the commodity against the futures contract and receive $F_0$.

### Strategy B: "Cash and Carry" Short Position

1. Short-sell the commodity today, receiving $S_0$.
2. Invest $S_0$ at the risk-free rate $r$ for $T$ years.
3. At time $T$, withdraw the maturity amount: $S_0 \cdot e^{rT}$.
4. Use this amount to buy the commodity in the spot market and deliver against the short position.

### Cost-Benefit Analysis

To prevent arbitrage, the **net cost of Strategy A** must equal the **net proceeds of Strategy B**.

Total cost of buy-and-hold (Strategy A):

$$
\text{Total Cost}_A = S_0 \cdot e^{uT} - y \cdot S_0 \cdot T_{\text{adj}}
$$

Total proceeds at time $T$ from short cash-and-carry (Strategy B):

$$
\text{Total Proceeds}_B = S_0 \cdot e^{rT}
$$

In continuous-time, with storage cost paid continuously and convenience yield compounded continuously:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_0 &= S_0 \cdot e^{(r + u - y)T}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation

**Step 1:** Write the future value of the spot purchase price plus storage costs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FV of Spot Purchase} &= S_0 \cdot e^{rT} \\
\text{FV of Storage Costs} &= S_0 \cdot u \cdot \int_0^T e^{r(T-s)} \, ds = S_0 \cdot u \cdot \dfrac{e^{rT} - 1}{r}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Subtract the present value of convenience yield benefits earned during the holding period:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PV of Convenience Yield} &= S_0 \cdot y \cdot \int_0^T e^{r(T-s)} \, ds = S_0 \cdot y \cdot \dfrac{e^{rT} - 1}{r}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Equate the futures price $F_0$ to the net future cost of physical holding:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_0 &= S_0 \cdot e^{rT} + S_0 \cdot u \cdot \dfrac{e^{rT} - 1}{r} - S_0 \cdot y \cdot \dfrac{e^{rT} - 1}{r}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Simplify by factoring $S_0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_0 &= S_0 \left[ e^{rT} + (u - y) \cdot \dfrac{e^{rT} - 1}{r} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** In the continuous-compounding limit (where $u$ and $y$ are also continuously compounded), this reduces to the elegant form:

$$
\boxed{F_0 = S_0 \cdot e^{(r + u - y)T}}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Problem)

### Problem Statement

The spot price of gold is **₹58,000 per 10 grams**. The annualized risk-free rate is **6%**, storage cost is **0.5% per annum**, and convenience yield is **0.1% per annum**. Compute the **3-month futures price** of gold.

### Given Data

- $S_0 = 58{,}000$
- $r = 0.06$
- $u = 0.005$
- $y = 0.001$
- $T = \dfrac{3}{12} = 0.25$ years

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Calculate the net cost of carry rate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(r + u - y) &= 0.06 + 0.005 - 0.001 \\
&= 0.064
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $(r + u - y) \cdot T$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(r + u - y) \cdot T &= 0.064 \times 0.25 \\
&= 0.016
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the exponential factor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e^{0.016} &= 1 + 0.016 + \dfrac{0.016^2}{2} + \dfrac{0.016^3}{6} + \ldots \\
&\approx 1.016129
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by spot price:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_0 &= 58{,}000 \times 1.016129 \\
&\approx \text{₹}58{,}935.48
\end{aligned}
$$

### Interpretation

The 3-month futures price of gold is **approximately ₹58,935.48 per 10 grams**. Since $F_0 > S_0$, the gold market is in **Contango** (a normal upward-sloping futures curve).

## 3.3 Worked Example: Hedge Ratio Calculation

### Problem Statement

A flour mill needs to hedge its exposure of **10,000 quintals of wheat** to be purchased in 3 months. The wheat futures contract lot size is **10 quintals**. The standard deviation of spot wheat price is **₹80/quintal**, the standard deviation of wheat futures price is **₹100/quintal**, and the correlation coefficient between spot and futures is **0.92**. Calculate the optimal hedge ratio and number of contracts.

### Given Data

- Spot exposure: $Q_S = 10{,}000$ quintals
- Futures lot size: $Q_F = 10$ quintals
- $\sigma_S = 80$
- $\sigma_F = 100$
- $\rho = 0.92$

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Compute the optimal hedge ratio $h^*$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h^* &= \rho \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_S}{\sigma_F} \\
&= 0.92 \cdot \dfrac{80}{100} \\
&= 0.92 \cdot 0.8 \\
&= 0.736
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the number of futures contracts:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= h^* \cdot \dfrac{Q_S}{Q_F} \\
&= 0.736 \cdot \dfrac{10{,}000}{10} \\
&= 0.736 \cdot 1000 \\
&= 736 \text{ contracts}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Interpretation

The mill should **short 736 wheat futures contracts** to optimally hedge its purchase exposure. This **Long Spot / Short Futures** position locks in a price close to the current spot rate, eliminating most of the variance caused by wheat price fluctuations.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Commodity Classification Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[COMMODITIES ASSET CLASS] --> B[HARD COMMODITIES]
    A --> C[SOFT COMMODITIES]

    B --> B1[PRECIOUS METALS]
    B --> B2[BASE METALS]
    B --> B3[ENERGY]

    B1 --> B1a[GOLD]
    B1 --> B1b[SILVER]
    B1 --> B1c[PLATINUM]

    B2 --> B2a[COPPER]
    B2 --> B2b[ALUMINIUM]
    B2 --> B2c[ZINC]
    B2 --> B2d[NICKEL]

    B3 --> B3a[CRUDE OIL]
    B3 --> B3b[NATURAL GAS]
    B3 --> B3c[COAL]

    C --> C1[CEREALS AND GRAINS]
    C --> C2[OILSEEDS]
    C --> C3[SOFTS]
    C --> C4[LIVESTOCK]

    C1 --> C1a[WHEAT]
    C1 --> C1b[RICE]
    C1 --> C1c[MAIZE]

    C2 --> C2a[SOYBEAN]
    C2 --> C2b[MUSTARD]
    C2 --> C2c[CRUDE PALM OIL]

    C3 --> C3a[SUGAR]
    C3 --> C3b[COFFEE]
    C3 --> C3c[COCOA]
    C3 --> C3d[COTTON]
    C3 --> C3e[RUBBER]

    C4 --> C4a[LIVE CATTLE]
    C4 --> C4b[LEAN HOGS]
    C4 --> C4c[MILK]
```

## 4.2 Spot vs. Futures Pricing Architecture (Cost-of-Carry Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[SPOT MARKET TODAY] -- Cash S0 --> BUY[BUY COMMODITY]
    BUY -- Storage u --> HOLD[STORE FOR T YEARS]
    HOLD -- Convenience y --> MAT[MATURITY AT TIME T]
    MAT -- Deliver --> FUT[FUTURES PRICE F0 RECEIVED]

    FUT -- Compare --> ARB[ARBITRAGE CHECK]
    ARB -- F0 greater than S0 e to the power rT --> CONT[CONTANGO]
    ARB -- F0 less than S0 e to the power rT --> BACK[BACKWARDATION]
    ARB -- F0 equals S0 e to the power rT --> EQ[NO ARBITRAGE EQUILIBRIUM]

    S0 -. Alternative .-> SHORT[SHORT SELL COMMODITY]
    SHORT -. Invest at r .-> GROW[GROWTH TO S0 e to the power rT]
    GROW -. Buy back at ST .-> MAT
```

## 4.3 Hedging Mechanism Architecture (Long Spot, Short Futures)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EXP[INDUSTRY EXPOSURE TO COMMODITY PRICE RISK] --> HE[HEDGING ENGINE]
    HE --> HR[HEDGE RATIO CALCULATION]
    HR --> N[NUMBER OF FUTURES CONTRACTS]

    N --> ACT[HEDGE ACTION]
    ACT --> L1[BUY SPOT COMMODITY]
    ACT --> L2[SELL FUTURES CONTRACT]

    L1 --> RES1[GAIN IF SPOT PRICE RISES]
    L2 --> RES2[LOSS IF FUTURES PRICE RISES]

    RES1 --> NET[NET POSITION]
    RES2 --> NET

    NET --> OUT[PRICE RISK NEUTRALIZED OR HEDGED]
```

## 4.4 Commodity Derivative Product Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    UC[UNDERLYING COMMODITY] --> FWD[FORWARD CONTRACT OTC]
    UC --> FUT[FUTURES CONTRACT EXCHANGE TRADED]
    UC --> OPT[OPTIONS ON FUTURES]
    UC --> SWP[COMMODITY SWAPS]

    FWD --> F1[CUSTOMIZED DEFAULT RISK]
    FUT --> F2[STANDARDIZED CLEARING HOUSE]
    OPT --> O1[RIGHT NOT OBLIGATION]
    SWP --> S1[EXCHANGE OF CASH FLOWS]
```
