The horizontal gap between bars shrinks — this visually proves that there is a **theoretical ceiling** to compounding returns, beyond which additional frequency yields negligible gain. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Explain the concept of Time Value of Money. Why is a rupee received today worth more than a rupee received after one year?* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The Time Value of Money (TVM) is the financial principle asserting that money available at the present time is worth more than the identical sum in the future due to its earning potential. A rupee received today is worth more than a rupee received after one year for two fundamental reasons: **(1) Opportunity Cost** — the current rupee can be invested today to earn a return, growing its value; **(2) Inflation** — the general rise in prices erodes the purchasing power of future money. Additionally, future receipts carry **risk** — there is uncertainty that the money will actually be received as promised. *(Examiner's Note: Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for opportunity cost, 1 mark for inflation/risk. Total: 3 marks.)* ### Question A2 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Differentiate between an Ordinary Annuity and an Annuity Due with a suitable example for each.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** An **Ordinary Annuity** consists of equal payments made at the **end** of each compounding period, while an **Annuity Due** consists of equal payments made at the **beginning** of each period. *Example of Ordinary Annuity:* Equated Monthly Instalments (EMIs) on a home loan, paid at the end of each month. *Example of Annuity Due:* Annual insurance premiums paid in advance at the start of each policy year, or lease rentals paid at the beginning of each rental month. Each annuity due payment has one additional period to compound compared to an ordinary annuity, making its present and future values mathematically larger. *(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for examples. Total: 3 marks.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Regulation:** Part B Module 2 questions carry **14 marks** and offer an internal choice between two questions of equal difficulty. Both questions typically contain a 7-mark sub-part and a 7-mark sub-part, totalling 14 marks. --- ### Question B1 — Option A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 | Module 2 Internal Choice Q1]** **(a)** A finance engineer deposits **₹50,000** in a bank today. The bank offers an interest rate of **9% per annum compounded semi-annually**. Calculate the amount she will receive after **6 years**. Also, compute the effective annual rate (EAR). *(7 Marks)* **(b)** Mr. Krishnan wins a lottery and is offered two alternative payout options: - **Option X:** Receive ₹12,00,000 as a lump sum today. - **Option Y:** Receive ₹4,00,000 at the end of each year for the next 4 years. Assuming the prevailing market discount rate is **10% per annum**, determine which option Mr. Krishnan should choose using the present value criterion. *(7 Marks)* --- ### **Model Solution for B1(a)** **Given:** PV = ₹50,000; nominal rate = 9% p.a.; compounding = semi-annually (so m = 2); n = 6 years. **Step 1:** Convert nominal rate to periodic rate. $$r = \frac{0.09}{2} = 0.045 \quad \text{(4.5\% per half-year)}$$ **Step 2:** Compute total number of compounding periods. $$N = m \times n = 2 \times 6 = 12 \quad \text{periods}$$ **Step 3:** Apply the Future Value formula. $$\begin{aligned} FV &= PV \cdot (1 + r)^N \\ &= 50{,}000 \cdot (1 + 0.045)^{12} \\ &= 50{,}000 \cdot (1.045)^{12} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4:** Evaluate the exponent numerically. $$(1.045)^{12} = 1.69588$$ **Step 5:** Compute final amount. $$FV = 50{,}000 \times 1.69588 = ₹84{,}794 \quad (\text{rounded})$$ **Step 6:** Compute the Effective Annual Rate. $$\begin{aligned} EAR &= \left(1 + \frac{r_{\text{nom}}}{m}\right)^m - 1 \\ &= \left(1 + \frac{0.09}{2}\right)^2 - 1 \\ &= (1.045)^2 - 1 \\ &= 1.092025 - 1 \\ &= 0.092025 \\ &= 9.2025\% \text{ per annum} \end{aligned}$$ **Final Answer:** Mr. Krishnan's deposit matures to **₹84,794**, and the bank's **EAR is 9.2025%**. *(7 Marks: 4 for FV, 2 for EAR computation, 1 for unit/finalization.)* --- ### **Model Solution for B1(b)** **Step 1:** Compute PV of Option X (already given as lump sum). $$PV_X = ₹12{,}00{,}000$$ **Step 2:** Compute PV of Option Y using the ordinary annuity formula. $$\begin{aligned} PV_Y &= PMT \cdot \frac{1 - (1 + r)^{-n}}{r} \\ &= 4{,}00{,}000 \cdot \frac{1 - (1.10)^{-4}}{0.10} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3:** Evaluate the discount factor. $$(1.10)^{-4} = \frac{1}{(1.10)^4} = \frac{1}{1.4641} = 0.6830$$ **Step 4:** Compute the annuity factor. $$\frac{1 - 0.6830}{0.10} = \frac{0.3170}{0.10} = 3.170$$ **Step 5:** Compute PV of Option Y. $$PV_Y = 4{,}00{,}000 \times 3.170 = ₹12{,}68{,}000$$ **Step 6:** Compare and conclude. $$PV_Y = ₹12{,}68{,}000 > PV_X = ₹12{,}00{,}000$$ **Conclusion:** Mr. Krishnan should choose **Option Y**, as it has a higher present value (₹12,68,000) compared to Option X (₹12,00,000), making it the financially superior alternative by ₹68,000. *(7 Marks: 3 for annuity factor, 2 for PV computation, 2 for comparative conclusion.)* --- ### Question B2 — Option B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 | Module 2 Internal Choice Q2]** **(a)** Define a perpetuity. A preferred stock pays a fixed annual dividend of **₹75** per share indefinitely. If the required rate of return is **12%**, calculate the maximum price an investor should pay for this stock today. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** An engineering firm has the option to invest in two mutually exclusive machines: - **Machine Alpha:** Requires an initial investment of ₹8,00,000 and generates annual net cash inflows of ₹2,50,000 for 5 years. - **Machine Beta:** Requires an initial investment of ₹10,00,000 and generates annual net cash inflows of ₹3,10,000 for 6 years. Using the Net Present Value (NPV) method at a discount rate of **11%**, recommend which machine the firm should acquire. *(7 Marks)* --- ### **Model Solution for B2(a)** **Definition (2 Marks):** A **perpetuity** is a perpetual stream of equal cash flows that continues indefinitely into the future (n → ∞). Examples include preferred stock dividends, consol bonds, and permanent endowments. **Numerical Solution (5 Marks):** **Given:** PMT = ₹75; r = 12% = 0.12. **Step 1:** State the perpetuity formula. $$PV_{\text{perp}} = \frac{PMT}{r}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute the values. $$\begin{aligned} PV_{\text{perp}} &= \frac{75}{0.12} \\ PV_{\text{perp}} &= 625 \end{aligned}$$ **Conclusion:** The maximum price an investor should pay for this preferred stock is **₹625 per share**. *(7 Marks: 2 for definition, 5 for numerical computation.)* --- ### **Model Solution for B2(b)** **Machine Alpha — NPV Computation:** **Step 1:** Compute PV of inflows (ordinary annuity, n = 5, r = 11%). $$PVA_{\text{alpha}} = 2{,}50{,}000 \cdot \frac{1 - (1.11)^{-5}}{0.11}$$ **Step 2:** Evaluate the discount factor. $$(1.11)^5 = 1.68506$$ $$(1.11)^{-5} = 0.59345$$ **Step 3:** Compute the annuity present value factor. $$\frac{1 - 0.59345}{0.11} = \frac{0.40655}{0.11} = 3.6959$$ **Step 4:** Compute PV of inflows. $$PVA_{\text{alpha}} = 2{,}50{,}000 \times 3.6959 = ₹9{,}23{,}975$$ **Step 5:** Compute NPV. $$NPV_{\text{alpha}} = 9{,}23{,}975 - 8{,}00{,}000 = ₹1{,}23{,}975$$ **Machine Beta — NPV Computation:** **Step 1:** Compute PV of inflows (ordinary annuity, n = 6, r = 11%). $$PVA_{\text{beta}} = 3{,}10{,}000 \cdot \frac{1 - (1.11)^{-6}}{0.11}$$ **Step 2:** Evaluate the discount factor. $$(1.11)^6 = 1.87041$$ $$(1.11)^{-6} = 0.53464$$ **Step 3:** Compute the annuity present value factor. $$\frac{1 - 0.53464}{0.11} = \frac{0.46536}{0.11} = 4.2305$$ **Step 4:** Compute PV of inflows. $$PVA_{\text{beta}} = 3{,}10{,}000 \times 4.2305 = ₹13{,}11{,}455$$ **Step 5:** Compute NPV. $$NPV_{\text{beta}} = 13{,}11{,}455 - 10{,}00{,}000 = ₹3{,}11{,}455$$ **Comparative Conclusion:** | Machine | Initial Investment | PV of Inflows | NPV | |---|---|---|---| | Alpha | ₹8,00,000 | ₹9,23,975 | ₹1,23,975 | | Beta | ₹10,00,000 | ₹13,11,455 | **₹3,11,455** | **Final Recommendation:** The firm should acquire **Machine Beta**, as it yields a substantially higher NPV (₹3,11,455) compared to Machine Alpha (₹1,23,975), creating ₹1,87,480 more value for the shareholders. *(7 Marks: 3 for Alpha NPV, 3 for Beta NPV, 1 for recommendation.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert annual rate to periodic rate** when compounding is semi-annual or quarterly. *(Loss: up to 2 marks per question)* > 2. **Confusing Ordinary Annuity with Annuity Due timing.** Always confirm whether payments occur at the beginning or end of the period. *(Loss: up to 3 marks)* > 3. **Incorrect identification of PV vs FV direction.** Read the problem carefully: "What is it worth today?" = PV. "What will it grow to?" = FV. *(Loss: 2–3 marks)* > 4. **Dropping the negative sign in NPV problems.** Initial investment is an outflow and must be subtracted from the present value of inflows. *(Loss: 1 mark)* > 5. **Rounding errors.** Use at least 4–5 decimal places during intermediate steps; round only the final answer. > 6. **Writing `(1+r)^-n` as `(1-r)^n`** — a catastrophic algebraic error that yields nonsense values. > 7. **Failure to state the formula before substituting.** Examiners award partial marks for stating the correct formula even if numerical errors follow. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Master These Before Entering the KTU Examination Hall** - **Single Sum:** $FV = PV(1+r)^n$ and $PV = FV(1+r)^{-n}$ are the foundational pair. Memorize them on day one. - **Annuity Ordinary:** $PVA = PMT \cdot \left[\dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}\right]$ and $FVA = PMT \cdot \left[\dfrac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r}\right]$. - **Annuity Due:** Always equals the ordinary annuity value multiplied by $(1 + r)$. This is the universal shift factor. - **Perpetuity:** The ultimate limit case. $PV_{\text{perp}} = \dfrac{PMT}{r}$. - **Growing Perpetuity:** $PV = \dfrac{PMT}{r - g}$, valid only when $r > g$. - **Effective Annual Rate:** $EAR = \left(1 + \dfrac{r_{\text{nom}}}{m}\right)^m - 1$. Always compute EAR before comparing financial products. - **Continuous Compounding:** $FV = PV \cdot e^{r \cdot n}$. Remember that $e \approx 2.71828$. - **Cash Flow Diagrams:** Always sketch the timeline. It eliminates 90% of "ordinary vs due" confusion. - **NPV Rule:** A project with **NPV > 0** is financially viable; the higher the NPV among mutually exclusive projects, the better the choice. - **Rule of 72:** A quick mental shortcut: years to double your money $\approx \dfrac{72}{\text{interest rate \%}}$. Useful for sanity-checking compound interest results. - **Always state the formula before substituting** — a KTU examiner's golden rule for partial mark allocation. - **Sign Convention:** Cash inflows are positive; cash outflows (investments) are negative. - **Compounding Frequency Hierarchy:** Annual < Semi-annual < Quarterly < Monthly < Daily < Continuous. Higher frequency → higher EAR for the same nominal rate. --- *End of Module 2 — Time Value of Money Notes | KTU 2024 Scheme | PCIET602 Financial Engineering*

topics.title: Investment and Maturity Period, Doubling Period, Present Value of Annuity and Future Value of Annuity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET602 - FINANCIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Investment and Maturity Period, Doubling Period, Present Value of Annuity and Future Value of Annuity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Investment Period
The **Investment Period** is the duration of time for which a sum of money (principal) remains deployed in a financial asset, scheme, or project, continuously earning a return at the agreed rate of interest.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** The length of time (in years, months, or days) between the date of investment and the date of withdrawal or redemption, during which the capital is at work in the chosen instrument.

### 1.2 Maturity Period
The **Maturity Period** is the pre-agreed tenure of a financial instrument after which the principal along with the accumulated interest/returns becomes due and payable to the investor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** The *Investment Period* refers to the *actual duration* the money is kept invested (which may be cut short), whereas the *Maturity Period* is the *contractual/fixed tenure* of the instrument (e.g., a 5-year FD has a maturity period of 5 years).

### 1.3 Doubling Period
The **Doubling Period** is the exact time required for a given principal to grow to twice its original value at a specified compound interest rate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of 72** is the famous heuristic approximation: Doubling Period (years) ≈ **72 ÷ Rate of Interest (%)**.

### 1.4 Annuity — The Conceptual Foundation
An **Annuity** is a sequence of equal payments made at equal intervals of time (monthly, quarterly, annually). Two primary valuations exist:

- **Future Value of Annuity (FVA):** The accumulated worth of all payments at a future date, after compounding.
- **Present Value of Annuity (PVA):** The lump-sum equivalent *today* of all future annuity payments, after discounting.

There are two timing variants:
- **Ordinary Annuity (Arrear):** Payments occur at the *end* of each period (e.g., loan EMIs, post-paid rentals).
- **Annuity Due (Advance):** Payments occur at the *beginning* of each period (e.g., insurance premiums, hostel rent paid in advance).

### 1.6 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Mango Tree"**
> Imagine you plant a mango sapling today (Investment starts). The tree is guaranteed to fruit exactly after 5 years — that 5-year contract is the **Maturity Period**. If the gardener promises a specific yield, you can compute how long it takes for the tree's value to double — the **Doubling Period**. Now, suppose instead of one sapling, you plant a new sapling every monsoon and harvest fruits every season — that recurring stream of harvest payments is an **Annuity**. The *future value* tells you how much fruit-wealth you'll accumulate by year 10; the *present value* tells you what that future harvest is worth in today's money.

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
- Annual compounding frequency is the default in KTU problems unless specified.
- **ln(2) ≈ 0.6931** — the natural logarithm of 2, used in exact doubling-period derivation.
- **Rule of 69.3** is the precise continuous-compounding analog of the Rule of 72.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Investment Period vs. Maturity Period — Structural Logic

| Parameter | Investment Period | Maturity Period |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Actual time money is deployed | Contractual/fixed tenure |
| Flexibility | May vary (early withdrawal allowed) | Fixed at the time of contract |
| Computation | End date − Start date | Defined in the instrument |
| Example | You withdraw FD after 3 yrs from a 5-yr FD | A 10-year PPF account |

### 2.2 Doubling Period — Why It Matters

The doubling period answers a fundamental question: *"How long until my money doubles?"* It is derived by setting Future Value = 2 × Principal.

### 2.3 Annuity Logic — Why and How

- **Why PV?** A rupee received tomorrow is worth less than a rupee today (opportunity cost). Discounting collapses a future stream into today's terms.
- **Why FV?** Compounding accumulates periodic savings into a future corpus (e.g., retirement planning).
- **How?** Both rely on the geometric progression sum formula: $\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}(1+i)^k = \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Variables | Annuity Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| Doubling Period (Exact) | $t = \dfrac{\ln(2)}{\ln(1+i)} = \dfrac{0.6931}{\ln(1+i)}$ | $t$ = years, $i$ = annual rate | — |
| Doubling Period (Rule of 72) | $t \approx \dfrac{72}{r\%}$ | $r\%$ = annual rate in percent | — |
| FV of Ordinary Annuity | $F = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$ | $A$ = periodic payment, $n$ = no. of periods | End-of-period |
| FV of Annuity Due | $F_d = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i} \cdot (1+i)$ | Same as above | Begin-of-period |
| PV of Ordinary Annuity | $P = A \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$ | $A$ = periodic payment | End-of-period |
| PV of Annuity Due | $P_d = A \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i} \cdot (1+i)$ | Same as above | Begin-of-period |
| Sinking Fund Payment | $A = F \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | $F$ = target future value | — |
| Capital Recovery (Loan EMI) | $A = P \cdot \dfrac{i}{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}$ | $P$ = loan principal | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Convention:** In KTU problems, the rate $i$ must always be expressed per period matching the payment frequency. If the annual rate is $r\%$ and payments are monthly, then $i = \dfrac{r/12}{100}$ and $n = 12 \times (\text{years})$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Financial Utility

- **Project Appraisal:** Engineers compute PV of cash inflows vs. PV of outflows to decide project viability (NPV/IRR analysis).
- **Equipment Replacement:** Capital recovery formula determines equivalent annual cost of machinery.
- **Loan Structuring:** EMI calculations directly use the PV-of-annuity formula.
- **Retirement Planning:** FVA tells engineers how much to invest periodically to build a target corpus.
- **Depreciation & Sinking Funds:** Industries build replacement reserves via sinking-fund annuity logic.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Doubling Period (Exact)

**Starting principle:** We seek the time $t$ such that the future value equals twice the principal.

$$F = P(1+i)^t$$

**Set the condition:** $F = 2P$ (the investment must double)

$$2P = P(1+i)^t$$

**Divide both sides by $P$:**

$$2 = (1+i)^t$$

**Take natural logarithm on both sides:**

$$\ln(2) = t \cdot \ln(1+i)$$

**Isolate $t$:**

$$t = \dfrac{\ln(2)}{\ln(1+i)} = \dfrac{0.6931}{\ln(1+i)}$$

This is the **exact doubling period** under annual compounding.

### 3.2 Rule of 72 — Approximate Derivation

Using the Taylor expansion $\ln(1+i) \approx i - \dfrac{i^2}{2} + \ldots$ For small $i$ (in decimal), $\ln(1+i) \approx i$.

$$t \approx \dfrac{0.6931}{i}$$

Converting $i$ to percent ($i = r/100$):

$$t \approx \dfrac{0.6931}{r/100} = \dfrac{69.31}{r\%} \approx \dfrac{72}{r\%}$$

The Rule of 72 adds a small correction factor (≈3.7%) that empirically improves accuracy in the practical range $r = 5\%$ to $20\%$.

### 3.3 Derivation of Future Value of Ordinary Annuity

Consider an annuity payment $A$ made at the **end** of each period for $n$ periods, with interest rate $i$ per period.

**Step 1 — Identify each payment's future value at time $n$:**

- Payment at end of period 1 compounds for $(n-1)$ periods: $A(1+i)^{n-1}$
- Payment at end of period 2 compounds for $(n-2)$ periods: $A(1+i)^{n-2}$
- Payment at end of period $k$ compounds for $(n-k)$ periods: $A(1+i)^{n-k}$
- Payment at end of period $n$ compounds for $0$ periods: $A(1+i)^{0} = A$

**Step 2 — Sum the geometric series:**

$$F = A(1+i)^{n-1} + A(1+i)^{n-2} + \ldots + A(1+i)^{1} + A(1+i)^{0}$$

$$F = A \sum_{k=0}^{n-1}(1+i)^k$$

**Step 3 — Apply geometric series sum formula** (first term $a=1$, common ratio $r=(1+i)$, number of terms $n$):

$$F = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{(1+i) - 1}$$

$$\boxed{F = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}}$$

### 3.4 Derivation of Future Value of Annuity Due

In an annuity due, each payment occurs **one period earlier** than in an ordinary annuity. Hence every payment earns one additional period of interest.

$$F_d = F \cdot (1+i)$$

$$\boxed{F_d = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i} \cdot (1+i)}$$

### 3.5 Derivation of Present Value of Ordinary Annuity

The PV of an annuity is the sum of present values of all future payments.

**Step 1 — Discount each payment back to time 0:**

$$P = \dfrac{A}{1+i} + \dfrac{A}{(1+i)^2} + \ldots + \dfrac{A}{(1+i)^n}$$

$$P = A \sum_{k=1}^{n}(1+i)^{-k}$$

**Step 2 — Apply geometric series formula:**

$$P = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{-1}\left[1 - (1+i)^{-n}\right]}{1 - (1+i)^{-1}}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the denominator** $1 - (1+i)^{-1} = \dfrac{(1+i) - 1}{(1+i)} = \dfrac{i}{(1+i)}$

$$P = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{-1}\left[1 - (1+i)^{-n}\right]}{(1+i)^{-1} \cdot i}$$

$$\boxed{P = A \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}}$$

### 3.6 Derivation of Present Value of Annuity Due

Each payment in an annuity due is received one period sooner, so the PV is multiplied by $(1+i)$:

$$\boxed{P_d = A \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i} \cdot (1+i)}$$

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Demonstration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example:** Find the amount after 5 years if ₹1,000 is deposited annually in a savings scheme at 10% p.a. compounded annually. Compare ordinary vs. annuity due.

**Given:** $A = 1000$, $i = 0.10$, $n = 5$

**(a) Ordinary Annuity:**

$$F = 1000 \cdot \dfrac{(1.10)^5 - 1}{0.10}$$

**Compute $(1.10)^5$ step-by-step:**

$$(1.10)^2 = 1.21$$

$$(1.10)^3 = 1.21 \times 1.10 = 1.331$$

$$(1.10)^4 = 1.331 \times 1.10 = 1.4641$$

$$(1.10)^5 = 1.4641 \times 1.10 = 1.61051$$

**Substitute back:**

$$F = 1000 \cdot \dfrac{1.61051 - 1}{0.10} = 1000 \cdot \dfrac{0.61051}{0.10} = 1000 \cdot 6.1051 = ₹6,105.10$$

**(b) Annuity Due:**

$$F_d = 6105.10 \times 1.10 = ₹6,715.61$$

The due annuity exceeds the ordinary annuity by exactly ₹610.51 (= one period's interest on ₹6,105.10), confirming the structural relationship.

**(c) Doubling Period Check at 10%:**

$$t = \dfrac{0.6931}{\ln(1.10)} = \dfrac{0.6931}{0.09531} \approx 7.27 \text{ years}$$

Using Rule of 72: $t \approx 72/10 = 7.2$ years — close agreement.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Concept Map — Time Value of Money Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Time Value of Money] --> nodeB[Single Cash Flow]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Annuity Stream]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Investment Period]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Maturity Period]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Doubling Period]
    nodeF --> nodeF1[Exact Formula]
    nodeF --> nodeF2[Rule of 72 Approximation]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Future Value of Annuity]
    nodeC --> nodeH[Present Value of Annuity]
    nodeG --> nodeG1[Ordinary Annuity End of Period]
    nodeG --> nodeG2[Annuity Due Start of Period]
    nodeH --> nodeH1[Ordinary Annuity End of Period]
    nodeH --> nodeH2[Annuity Due Start of Period]
    nodeG1 --> nodeApp1[Sinking Fund Reserve]
    nodeG2 --> nodeApp2[Insurance Premium Accumulation]
    nodeH1 --> nodeApp3[Loan EMI Calculation]
    nodeH2 --> nodeApp4[Lease Rental Valuation]
```

### 4.2 Cash Flow Timeline — Ordinary Annuity vs. Annuity Due

```mermaid
gantt
    title Annuity Cash Flow Timeline (n = 4 periods)
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section Ordinary Annuity End of Period
    Payment 1 :a1, 1, 1d
    Payment 2 :a2, 2, 1d
    Payment 3 :a3, 3, 1d
    Payment 4 :a4, 4, 1d
    section Annuity Due Start of Period
    Payment 1 :b1, 0, 1d
    Payment 2 :b2, 1, 1d
    Payment 3 :b3, 2, 1d
    Payment 4 :b4, 3, 1d
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Doubling Period Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart([Principal P and Rate i]) --> nodeCompute[Compute ln of 1 plus i]
    nodeCompute --> nodeDivide[Divide 0.6931 by ln of 1 plus i]
    nodeDivide --> nodeOutput1[Exact Doubling Period t]
    nodeStart --> nodeRule72[Apply Rule of 72]
    nodeRule72 --> nodeOutput2[Approximate Doubling Period t approx]
    nodeOutput1 --> nodeCompare{Compare both estimates}
    nodeOutput2 --> nodeCompare
    nodeCompare --> nodeDecision[Decision Threshold within 5 percent]
    nodeDecision --> nodeReport([Report Final Doubling Time])
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the terms: (i) Investment Period, (ii) Maturity Period, and (iii) Doubling Period. State the Rule of 72.*

**Model Answer:**
(i) **Investment Period:** The actual duration for which a sum of money remains deployed in a financial asset earning interest.
(ii) **Maturity Period:** The pre-fixed contractual tenure of a financial instrument after which the principal and accumulated interest are repaid.
(iii) **Doubling Period:** The time required for a sum of money to grow to twice its original value at a given rate of compound interest.
**Rule of 72:** Doubling Period (in years) ≈ 72 ÷ Rate of Interest (in % per annum). It is a quick mental-math approximation valid in the rate range 5%–20%.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between Present Value of Annuity and Future Value of Annuity. State one engineering application for each.*

**Model Answer:**
| Aspect | Future Value of Annuity | Present Value of Annuity |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Accumulated value of periodic payments at a future date | Today's lump-sum equivalent of future periodic payments |
| Operation Used | Compounding | Discounting |
| Formula | $F = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$ | $P = A \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$ |
| Application | Retirement corpus accumulation via SIPs | Computing equivalent annual cost of capital equipment / Loan EMI valuation |

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Internal Choice Rule:** Answer any ONE full question from the choice given. Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Show all working — marks are awarded for intermediate steps.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Modified]*

**(a)** A sum of ₹50,000 is invested at 9% p.a. compounded annually. Calculate:
(i) The exact doubling period using the logarithmic formula.
(ii) Verify using the Rule of 72.
*(7 Marks)*

**(b)** An engineer deposits ₹20,000 at the end of every year into a fund that yields 8% p.a. compounded annually.
(i) Find the accumulated amount after 12 years.
(ii) How much additional interest is earned compared to a simple accumulation (no compounding)?
*(7 Marks)*

##### **Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

**Given:** $P = 50{,}000$, $i = 0.09$, target $F = 1{,}00{,}000$.

**(i) Exact Doubling Period:**

$$t = \dfrac{\ln(2)}{\ln(1+i)} = \dfrac{0.6931}{\ln(1.09)}$$

**Compute $\ln(1.09)$:**

$$\ln(1.09) = 0.08618$$

**Substitute:**

$$t = \dfrac{0.6931}{0.08618} = 8.043 \text{ years}$$

**Therefore, the exact doubling period is ≈ 8.04 years.**

**(ii) Rule of 72 verification:**

$$t \approx \dfrac{72}{9} = 8.0 \text{ years}$$

The Rule of 72 gives 8.0 years, while the exact value is 8.04 years — an error of only 0.04 years (≈0.5%), confirming its accuracy.

##### **Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Given:** $A = 20{,}000$, $i = 0.08$, $n = 12$.

**(i) Future Value of Ordinary Annuity:**

$$F = 20{,}000 \cdot \dfrac{(1.08)^{12} - 1}{0.08}$$

**Compute $(1.08)^{12}$ step-by-step:**

$$(1.08)^2 = 1.1664$$

$$(1.08)^4 = (1.1664)^2 = 1.36049$$

$$(1.08)^8 = (1.36049)^2 = 1.85093$$

$$(1.08)^{12} = (1.08)^8 \times (1.08)^4 = 1.85093 \times 1.36049 = 2.51817$$

**Substitute:**

$$F = 20{,}000 \cdot \dfrac{2.51817 - 1}{0.08} = 20{,}000 \cdot \dfrac{1.51817}{0.08} = 20{,}000 \times 18.9771$$

$$F = ₹3{,}79{,}542$$

**(ii) Simple Accumulation (no compounding):**

$$F_{\text{simple}} = 20{,}000 \times 12 = ₹2{,}40{,}000$$

**Additional interest from compounding:**

$$\Delta F = 3{,}79{,}542 - 2{,}40{,}000 = ₹1{,}39{,}542$$

**This is the interest earned due to the compounding effect across 12 years.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**(a)** A company takes a loan of ₹10,00,000 from a bank at 12% p.a. compounded annually, to be repaid in equal annual instalments over 8 years (ordinary annuity).
(i) Calculate the annual EMI.
(ii) Find the total interest paid over the loan tenure.
*(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Compare the present value of an ordinary annuity with that of an annuity due of ₹50,000 per year for 6 years at 10% p.a. What is the premium (extra PV) gained by paying at the beginning of each period instead of the end?
*(7 Marks)*

##### **Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

**Given:** $P = 10{,}00{,}000$, $i = 0.12$, $n = 8$.

**(i) Capital Recovery Formula:**

$$A = P \cdot \dfrac{i}{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}$$

$$A = 10{,}00{,}000 \cdot \dfrac{0.12}{1 - (1.12)^{-8}}$$

**Compute $(1.12)^{-8}$:**

$$(1.12)^2 = 1.2544$$

$$(1.12)^4 = (1.2544)^2 = 1.5735$$

$$(1.12)^8 = (1.5735)^2 = 2.4760$$

$$(1.12)^{-8} = \dfrac{1}{2.4760} = 0.40388$$

**Substitute:**

$$A = 10{,}00{,}000 \cdot \dfrac{0.12}{1 - 0.40388} = 10{,}00{,}000 \cdot \dfrac{0.12}{0.59612}$$

$$A = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.20130 = ₹2{,}01{,}303$$

**Annual EMI = ₹2,01,303 (approx).**

**(ii) Total Amount Paid:**

$$\text{Total} = 2{,}01{,}303 \times 8 = ₹16{,}10{,}424$$

**Total Interest:**

$$I = 16{,}10{,}424 - 10{,}00{,}000 = ₹6{,}10{,}424$$

##### **Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

**Given:** $A = 50{,}000$, $i = 0.10$, $n = 6$.

**(i) PV of Ordinary Annuity:**

$$P = 50{,}000 \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1.10)^{-6}}{0.10}$$

**Compute $(1.10)^6$:**

$$(1.10)^2 = 1.21$$

$$(1.10)^3 = 1.331$$

$$(1.10)^6 = (1.331)^2 = 1.771561$$

$$(1.10)^{-6} = \dfrac{1}{1.771561} = 0.56447$$

**Substitute:**

$$P = 50{,}000 \cdot \dfrac{1 - 0.56447}{0.10} = 50{,}000 \cdot \dfrac{0.43553}{0.10} = 50{,}000 \times 4.3553 = ₹2{,}17{,}765$$

**(ii) PV of Annuity Due:**

$$P_d = P \times (1+i) = 2{,}17{,}765 \times 1.10 = ₹2{,}39{,}542$$

**Premium Gained:**

$$\Delta P = 2{,}39{,}542 - 2{,}17{,}765 = ₹21{,}777$$

**The annuity due commands a PV premium of ₹21,777 due to receiving each payment one period earlier.**

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks:**
> 1. **Rate-period mismatch (–2 marks):** Using $i = 0.10$ directly for monthly payments. Always convert: monthly $i = r/12$.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert percent to decimal (–1 mark):** Plugging $i = 10$ instead of $0.10$ in the formula.
> 3. **Confusing ordinary annuity with annuity due (–2 marks):** EMI/loan problems are almost always ordinary (arrear). Insurance premiums and rent-in-advance are annuity due.
> 4. **Skipping intermediate steps in (1+i)^n computation (–1 mark):** Always show $(1.08)^2$, $(1.08)^4$, $(1.08)^8$ steps — KTU examiners reward explicit working.
> 5. **In Doubling Period problems:** Writing only the Rule of 72 without the exact logarithmic formula loses full credit unless explicitly asked for the approximation.
> 6. **Final unit omission (–0.5 mark):** Always state the final answer with ₹ symbol or in years explicitly.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Investment Period = Actual time deployed**; **Maturity Period = Contractual fixed tenure**.
- **Doubling Period exact:** $t = \dfrac{0.6931}{\ln(1+i)}$ ; **approximate:** $t \approx \dfrac{72}{r\%}$.
- **FV of Ordinary Annuity:** $F = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$.
- **FV of Annuity Due:** $F_d = F \cdot (1+i)$.
- **PV of Ordinary Annuity:** $P = A \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$.
- **PV of Annuity Due:** $P_d = P \cdot (1+i)$.
- **Capital Recovery (EMI):** $A = P \cdot \dfrac{i}{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}$.
- **Sinking Fund Deposit:** $A = F \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$.
- **Always match rate units with payment frequency** — annual rate must be divided by periods-per-year.
- **End-of-period payments = Ordinary Annuity**; **Beginning-of-period = Annuity Due**.
- **Annuity Due value > Ordinary Annuity value** (for same $A$, $i$, $n$).
- **Geometric series foundation** underlies all annuity derivations.
- **Engineering applications:** Loan EMI, equipment replacement, sinking funds, project NPV, retirement planning.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equated Monthly Instalment (EMI) Calculations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET602 - FINANCIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Equated Monthly Instalment (EMI) Calculations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Equated Monthly Instalment (EMI)**: A fixed, predetermined monetary payment made by a borrower to a lender at a specified date each calendar month. EMIs are the standard amortisation mechanism used in retail lending products such as home loans, vehicle loans, personal loans, and educational loans, wherein the borrower repays both the principal and the interest accrued over a fixed loan tenure through equal periodic payments.

Mathematically, an EMI is the **annuity payment** that exactly amortises (i.e., reduces to zero) a present loan principal $P$ over $n$ compounding periods at a periodic interest rate $r$, computed under the **present value of an ordinary annuity** framework.

The loan is said to be **fully amortised** when the cumulative sum of all EMIs paid equals the original principal borrowed plus the total interest charged, leaving a final outstanding balance of **zero**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Milk Bottle" Intuition

Imagine a leaking milk bottle (the **outstanding principal**) that you have promised to return empty to the lender on the last day of the month. Each day, milk leaks out (this is the **interest** accumulating on the unpaid balance). You, as the borrower, commit to paying a fixed sum every month (the **EMI**). Two things happen with each payment:

- A portion refills the "interest leak" — this is the **interest component** of that month's EMI.
- The remainder actually reduces the milk level — this is the **principal component**.

Early in the loan, the bottle is nearly full, so the leak is large; most of your EMI goes to "buying back the leaked milk" (interest). By the end of the tenure, the bottle is almost empty, the leak is small, and almost the entire EMI goes towards emptying it (principal repayment).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 2)**: EMI calculations are a direct application of the **time value of money (TVM)** principle. Mastery of the standard EMI formula, amortisation schedules, effective vs. flat interest rate comparisons, and prepayment impact analysis is essential for both Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit / Standard |
|---|---|---|
| $P$ | Principal loan amount (Present Value) | Currency (₹, $, etc.) |
| $r$ | Periodic (monthly) interest rate | Decimal per month |
| $R$ | Annual nominal interest rate | % per annum |
| $n$ | Total number of monthly instalments | Months |
| $E$ | Equated Monthly Instalment | Currency per month |
| $A$ | Total amount paid over loan life | Currency |
| $I_{total}$ | Total interest payable | Currency |
| $B_k$ | Outstanding balance after $k$th payment | Currency |

The critical conversion relationship is:

$$r = \frac{R}{12 \times 100}$$

where $R$ is the **quoted annual rate in percentage** and the denominator accounts for the fact that there are **12 months** in a year and the percentage is converted to a decimal by dividing by **100**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Logic — Why the EMI Formula Works

The EMI is derived from the **Present Value of an Ordinary Annuity** equation. The lender disburses a lump sum $P$ today (the loan), and receives $n$ equal future payments $E$ at the end of each month. The present value of those $n$ payments, discounted at the periodic rate $r$, must equal the loan amount $P$.

The structural logic, broken into the core "why" and "how":

- **Why fixed payments?** Because the loan is amortised using the *reducing balance method*, where interest is recalculated every month on the **declining principal**. As principal falls, the interest portion falls, and the principal portion rises — but the total payment remains identical by design.
- **How is it kept constant?** By mathematically forcing the sum of the discounted monthly payments to exactly equal the original principal at the loan's stated interest rate.
- **Why the exponent $n$?** Because each rupee paid in month $k$ is worth less today than a rupee paid today; the compounding factor $(1+r)^n$ accounts for the time value of money over the full tenure.
- **Boundary condition**: At $k = n$, the outstanding balance $B_n$ must equal **zero** (full amortisation). This is the constraint that uniquely solves for $E$.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the formulas below. These are the only equations needed to solve any EMI problem that appears in the KTU ESE.**

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Monthly Rate Conversion | $r = \dfrac{R}{12 \times 100}$ | Convert annual % rate to monthly decimal rate |
| 2 | Standard EMI Formula | $E = \dfrac{P \cdot r \cdot (1+r)^{n}}{(1+r)^{n} - 1}$ | Core formula for EMI on reducing balance |
| 3 | Total Amount Payable | $A = E \times n$ | Sum of all instalments over the loan tenure |
| 4 | Total Interest Payable | $I_{total} = A - P = (E \times n) - P$ | Total cost of borrowing |
| 5 | Interest Component (k-th month) | $I_k = B_{k-1} \cdot r$ | Interest charged on previous month's balance |
| 6 | Principal Component (k-th month) | $\text{PR}_k = E - I_k$ | Portion of EMI that reduces the principal |
| 7 | Outstanding Balance after k payments | $B_k = P(1+r)^{k} - E \cdot \dfrac{(1+r)^{k} - 1}{r}$ | Remaining loan amount after $k$ instalments |
| 8 | Alternative Balance Form | $B_k = E \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-(n-k)}}{r}$ | Cleaner closed-form using remaining periods $(n-k)$ |
| 9 | Flat-rate Equivalent EMI | $E_{flat} = \dfrac{P + (P \cdot R_{flat} \cdot t / 100)}{n}$ | Used in the **flat-rate method** (not reducing) |
| 10 | Effective Annual Rate (from monthly $r$) | $\text{EAR} = (1+r)^{12} - 1$ | True yearly cost under monthly compounding |
| 11 | Outstanding Tenure after prepayment | $n' = \dfrac{-\ln\!\left(1 - \dfrac{B_{k} \cdot r}{E}\right)}{\ln(1+r)}$ | New remaining months after partial prepayment at month $k$ |
| 12 | Prepayment Tenure Reduction (closed form) | $\Delta n = n - n'$ | Months saved by making a lump-sum prepayment |

### 2.3 Reducing Balance vs. Flat Rate — Critical Distinction

KTU examiners frequently set 14-mark questions that test the difference between these two methods. Many students lose marks by treating them as equivalent.

> [!WARNING]
> **Reducing Balance Method** (Standard, RBI-mandated for floating-rate loans): Interest is computed each month on the **outstanding principal only**. The EMI derived from Formula #2 applies here. This is the **correct, fair** method.

> [!WARNING]
> **Flat Rate Method** (Often used in vehicle/consumer durable loans): Interest is computed on the **original principal** for the entire tenure, ignoring the fact that principal is being repaid. This makes the loan *appear* cheaper but is actually more expensive in real terms.

The **Effective Annual Rate (EAR)** for a flat-rate loan, when expressed as a reducing-balance equivalent, is roughly **double** the flat rate for medium-tenure loans. The KTU board expects students to compute and compare both.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Finance Utility

- **Banking & NBFC Sector**: EMI is the foundational cash-flow model for **₹20+ lakh crore** Indian retail credit market.
- **FinTech & Loan Origination Systems**: Every digital lending app (Lendingkart, MoneyTap, etc.) uses the EMI formula in real-time underwriting engines.
- **Corporate Treasury**: Working capital term loans and equipment financing are structured as EMIs.
- **Actuarial Science**: EMI structures underpin microfinance, pension annuity products, and insurance premium financing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Standard EMI Formula

We start from the **Present Value of an Ordinary Annuity** identity. The lender disburses $P$ at $t = 0$ and receives $E$ at the end of each of the next $n$ months. Equating the two sides:

$$P = \frac{E}{(1+r)^{1}} + \frac{E}{(1+r)^{2}} + \frac{E}{(1+r)^{3}} + \cdots + \frac{E}{(1+r)^{n}}$$

Factor out $E$ and recognise the geometric series:

$$P = E \sum_{k=1}^{n} (1+r)^{-k}$$

Apply the standard finite geometric series closed form $\sum_{k=1}^{n} x^{k} = \dfrac{x(1-x^{n})}{1-x}$ with $x = (1+r)^{-1}$:

$$P = E \cdot \frac{(1+r)^{-1} \cdot \left[1 - (1+r)^{-n}\right]}{1 - (1+r)^{-1}}$$

Simplify the denominator by writing $1 - (1+r)^{-1} = \dfrac{r}{1+r}$:

$$P = E \cdot \frac{(1+r)^{-1} \cdot \left[1 - (1+r)^{-n}\right]}{(1+r)^{-1} \cdot r}$$

The $(1+r)^{-1}$ terms cancel beautifully:

$$P = E \cdot \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}$$

Multiply both sides by $r$ and isolate $E$:

$$E = P \cdot \frac{r}{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}$$

Rationalise the denominator by multiplying numerator and denominator by $(1+r)^{n}$:

$$E = P \cdot \frac{r \cdot (1+r)^{n}}{(1+r)^{n} - 1}$$

This is the **canonical EMI formula**. Each algebraic transition is now annotated in the table below for the analytical matrix.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Step-by-Step Justification

| Step | Mathematical Action | Logical / Financial Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Write $P$ as sum of $n$ discounted EMIs | The loan equals the present value of all future payments |
| 2 | Factor $E$ out of the sum | EMI is constant by design — pull it out |
| 3 | Identify the series as geometric with ratio $(1+r)^{-1}$ | Each payment is discounted one period further than the previous |
| 4 | Apply closed-form geometric sum | Allows the infinite sum to collapse into a finite expression |
| 5 | Simplify $1 - (1+r)^{-1} = \dfrac{r}{1+r}$ | Algebraic manipulation to remove the nested fraction |
| 6 | Cancel the $(1+r)^{-1}$ factor | Numerator and denominator share the same term |
| 7 | Multiply both sides by $r$ | Isolate the term containing $E$ |
| 8 | Rationalise denominator using $(1+r)^{n}$ | Convert negative exponent form into a "growth" form |
| 9 | Final expression $E = P \cdot \dfrac{r(1+r)^{n}}{(1+r)^{n} - 1}$ | EMI is principal scaled by an annuity factor that depends only on $r$ and $n$ |

### 3.3 Derivation of the Outstanding Balance Formula

We want the balance $B_k$ immediately **after** the $k$th EMI is paid. Two valid approaches:

**Approach A — Recursive (intuitive):**
The balance evolves month by month as:

$$B_k = B_{k-1}(1+r) - E$$

with $B_0 = P$. Unrolling this recursion:

$$B_k = P(1+r)^{k} - E \cdot \frac{(1+r)^{k} - 1}{r}$$

**Approach B — Remaining-Annuity (cleaner):**
$B_k$ equals the present value (at the end of month $k$) of the **remaining** $(n-k)$ instalments:

$$B_k = E \cdot \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-(n-k)}}{r}$$

Verification: Setting $k = n$ gives $B_n = E \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^0}{r} = 0$ ✓ (loan fully paid).
Setting $k = 0$ gives $B_0 = E \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r} = P$ ✓ (initial principal).

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Full KTU-Style Solution)

**Problem**: A loan of **₹5,00,000** is taken at an annual interest rate of **12% p.a.**, compounded monthly, for a tenure of **5 years**. Compute: (a) the EMI, (b) the total interest paid, (c) the outstanding balance after **2 years (24 months)**, and (d) the principal and interest split for the **first month** and the **36th month**.

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters:**

$$P = 5{,}00{,}000, \quad R = 12\%, \quad t = 5 \text{ years}, \quad n = 60 \text{ months}$$

**Step 2 — Convert the annual rate to a monthly rate:**

$$r = \frac{R}{12 \times 100} = \frac{12}{1200} = 0.01 \text{ per month}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the EMI using the standard formula:**

$$E = \frac{5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.01 \times (1.01)^{60}}{(1.01)^{60} - 1}$$

Compute $(1.01)^{60}$ step by step:

$$(1.01)^{10} \approx 1.10462$$

$$(1.01)^{20} = (1.10462)^2 \approx 1.22019$$

$$(1.01)^{40} = (1.22019)^2 \approx 1.48886$$

$$(1.01)^{60} = (1.01)^{40} \times (1.01)^{20} \approx 1.48886 \times 1.22019 \approx 1.81670$$

Substitute back:

$$E = \frac{5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.01 \times 1.81670}{1.81670 - 1} = \frac{5{,}000 \times 1.81670}{0.81670} = \frac{9083.50}{0.81670}$$

$$\boxed{E \approx ₹11{,}122 \text{ per month}}$$

**Step 4 — Total amount payable and total interest:**

$$A = E \times n = 11{,}122 \times 60 = ₹6{,}67{,}320$$

$$I_{total} = A - P = 6{,}67{,}320 - 5{,}00{,}000 = ₹1{,}67{,}320$$

**Step 5 — Outstanding balance after 24 months using the remaining-annuity form:**

$$B_{24} = E \cdot \frac{1 - (1.01)^{-36}}{0.01}$$

Compute $(1.01)^{-36}$:

$$(1.01)^{36} = (1.01)^{20} \times (1.01)^{16} \approx 1.22019 \times (1.01)^{16}$$

$$(1.01)^{16} = (1.01)^{8} \times (1.01)^{8} \approx (1.08286)^2 \approx 1.17257$$

$$(1.01)^{36} \approx 1.22019 \times 1.17257 \approx 1.43077$$

$$(1.01)^{-36} \approx \frac{1}{1.43077} \approx 0.69892$$

$$B_{24} = 11{,}122 \times \frac{1 - 0.69892}{0.01} = 11{,}122 \times \frac{0.30108}{0.01} = 11{,}122 \times 30.108$$

$$B_{24} \approx ₹3{,}34{,}861$$

**Step 6 — Interest and principal split for the 1st month:**

$$I_1 = B_0 \cdot r = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.01 = ₹5{,}000$$

$$\text{PR}_1 = E - I_1 = 11{,}122 - 5{,}000 = ₹6{,}122$$

**Step 7 — Interest and principal split for the 36th month (first compute $B_{35}$):**

$$(1.01)^{35} = \frac{(1.01)^{36}}{1.01} \approx \frac{1.43077}{1.01} \approx 1.41660$$

$$B_{35} = 11{,}122 \times \frac{1 - (1.01)^{-25}}{0.01} = 11{,}122 \times \frac{1 - 0.77976}{0.01}$$

$$B_{35} \approx 11{,}122 \times 22.024 \approx ₹2{,}44{,}951$$

$$I_{36} = B_{35} \times 0.01 \approx ₹2{,}450$$

$$\text{PR}_{36} = 11{,}122 - 2{,}450 = ₹8{,}672$$

**Observation**: Notice that by month 36, the interest portion has nearly halved (from ₹5,000 to ₹2,450) while the principal portion has grown from ₹6,122 to ₹8,672 — perfectly illustrating the "milk bottle" intuition. This is a key KTU-style observation expected in 14-mark answers.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 EMI Computation Flow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Inputs: Principal P, Annual Rate R, Tenure t years] --> B[Convert Annual Rate to Monthly: r = R / 1200]
    B --> C[Compute Number of Months: n = t x 12]
    C --> D[Compute Annuity Factor: F = r times 1+r power n]
    D --> E[Compute Denominator: Dden = 1+r power n minus 1]
    E --> F[Compute EMI: E = P times F / Dden]
    F --> G[Compute Total Payable: A = E times n]
    G --> H[Compute Total Interest: Itotal = A minus P]
    H --> I[Generate Amortisation Schedule Month by Month]
    I --> J[Loop: For k = 1 to n]
    J --> K[Interest Component: Ik = Bk-1 times r]
    K --> L[Principal Component: PRk = E minus Ik]
    L --> M[New Balance: Bk = Bk-1 minus PRk]
    M --> N{k equals n}
    N -->|No| J
    N -->|Yes| O[Output: EMI, Total Interest, Schedule, Final Balance = 0]
```

### 4.2 Component Evolution Across Loan Tenure — Schematic Trend

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STG1[Early Tenure Months 1 to 12]
        A1[EMI E] --> B1[Interest Component LARGE]
        A1 --> C1[Principal Component SMALL]
    end
    subgraph STG2[Mid Tenure Months 24 to 36]
        A2[EMI E] --> B2[Interest Component DECLINING]
        A2 --> C2[Principal Component RISING]
    end
    subgraph STG3[Late Tenure Months 48 to 60]
        A3[EMI E] --> B3[Interest Component SMALL]
        A3 --> C3[Principal Component LARGE]
    end
    STG1 --> STG2 --> STG3
```

### 4.3 Loan Lifecycle State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Disbursed: Lender credits P to borrower
    Disbursed --> Amortising: Month 1 begins
    Amortising --> Amortising: Each month pay EMI = Interest + Principal
    Amortising --> PartialPrepayment: Optional lump-sum payment
    PartialPrepayment --> Amortising: Balance and tenure recalculated
    Amortising --> FullyPaid: Outstanding balance reaches zero
    FullyPaid --> [*]: Loan closure certificate issued
```

### 4.4 EMI Computation Decision Tree (Flat vs. Reducing)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Loan Quote Received from Lender] --> B{Is rate quoted as FLAT or REDUCING?}
    B -->|FLAT| C[Compute EMI Flat using formula with original P]
    B -->|REDUCING| D[Compute EMI Reducing using standard formula]
    C --> E[Convert to Effective Annual Rate EAR]
    D --> F[EAR equals 1 plus r power 12 minus 1]
    E --> G[Compare EARs to make informed decision]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Choose loan with lower EAR]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Define the term *Equated Monthly Instalment (EMI)*. Mention any **two** situations in personal finance where EMI-based repayment is used.

**Model Answer:**
An Equated Monthly Instalment (EMI) is a fixed monetary amount paid by a borrower to a lender every month on a specified date, designed to amortise both the principal and interest of a loan over an agreed tenure. The EMI is computed such that the present value of all future EMIs, discounted at the loan's periodic interest rate, exactly equals the original principal disbursed.

Two situations where EMI repayment is used:
1. **Home loans / Housing finance** — typically 15 to 30 year tenure with reducing balance.
2. **Vehicle loans** — usually 5 to 8 year tenure, sometimes offered on a flat-rate basis.

*(Other valid examples: personal loans, education loans, consumer durable financing, gold loans.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Distinguish between the **flat-rate method** and the **reducing balance method** of computing interest on a loan. Which method results in a higher effective cost to the borrower?

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Flat-Rate Method | Reducing Balance Method |
|---|---|---|
| Interest calculated on | Original principal for full tenure | Outstanding (declining) principal each period |
| EMI tends to be | Lower (looks attractive) | Higher (but fairer) |
| Effective cost | **Higher** (true APR ≈ 1.8× to 2× flat rate) | **Lower** (matches quoted rate) |
| Regulatory status | Permitted with disclosure | Standard for floating-rate retail loans |

The **flat-rate method** results in a **higher effective cost** to the borrower, because the interest continues to be charged on amounts that have already been repaid, even though those amounts are no longer outstanding.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 2 Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** A bank offers a personal loan of **₹3,00,000** at an interest rate of **14% per annum**, compounded monthly, repayable in **4 years** through EMIs.
Compute:
(i) The monthly EMI.
(ii) The total interest payable over the loan tenure.
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Construct a complete amortisation schedule for the **first 3 months** and verify that the outstanding balance after 48 months is **zero** (within rounding). Also compute the interest and principal components for the **last (48th) month**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Step 1: Identify parameters.**

$$P = 3{,}00{,}000, \quad R = 14\%, \quad t = 4 \text{ years}, \quad n = 48 \text{ months}$$

**Step 2: Monthly rate conversion.**

$$r = \frac{14}{12 \times 100} = \frac{14}{1200} = 0.01166\overline{6} \text{ per month}$$

**Step 3: Compute $(1+r)^{n} = (1.011667)^{48}$.**

Using successive squaring:
$$(1.011667)^{2} \approx 1.02343$$
$$(1.011667)^{4} \approx 1.04747$$
$$(1.011667)^{8} \approx 1.09723$$
$$(1.011667)^{16} \approx 1.20392$$
$$(1.011667)^{32} \approx 1.44943$$
$$(1.011667)^{48} = (1.011667)^{32} \times (1.011667)^{16} \approx 1.44943 \times 1.20392 \approx 1.74531$$

**Step 4: Apply the EMI formula.**

$$E = \frac{3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.011667 \times 1.74531}{1.74531 - 1} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.020361}{0.74531}$$

$$E = \frac{6108.30}{0.74531} \approx ₹8{,}196 \text{ per month}$$

**Step 5: Total amount and total interest.**

$$A = 8196 \times 48 = ₹3{,}93{,}408$$

$$I_{total} = 3{,}93{,}408 - 3{,}00{,}000 = ₹93{,}408$$

---

**Part (b) — Amortisation Schedule (First 3 months):**

For month 1:
$$I_1 = 3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.011667 = ₹3{,}500$$
$$\text{PR}_1 = 8{,}196 - 3{,}500 = ₹4{,}696$$
$$B_1 = 3{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}696 = ₹2{,}95{,}304$$

For month 2:
$$I_2 = 2{,}95{,}304 \times 0.011667 \approx ₹3{,}445$$
$$\text{PR}_2 = 8{,}196 - 3{,}445 = ₹4{,}751$$
$$B_2 = 2{,}95{,}304 - 4{,}751 = ₹2{,}90{,}553$$

For month 3:
$$I_3 = 2{,}90{,}553 \times 0.011667 \approx ₹3{,}390$$
$$\text{PR}_3 = 8{,}196 - 3{,}390 = ₹4{,}806$$
$$B_3 = 2{,}90{,}553 - 4{,}806 = ₹2{,}85{,}747$$

**Verification that $B_{48} = 0$:**

The amortisation schedule is constructed such that the EMI $E$ is exactly the annuity payment that fully amortises $P$ over $n$ periods. By the **fundamental amortisation identity**, $B_n = 0$ is guaranteed by construction. Numerically, computing $B_{48}$ using the remaining-annuity form:

$$B_{48} = 8196 \times \frac{1 - (1.011667)^{0}}{0.011667} = 8196 \times 0 = ₹0 \text{ ✓}$$

**Last (48th) month — Interest and Principal split:**

By definition, $B_{47}$ is the balance after 47 payments. The final payment must clear this balance plus one month's interest. So:

$$I_{48} = B_{47} \times 0.011667$$

To find $B_{47}$, use the remaining-annuity formula with 1 period remaining:

$$B_{47} = 8196 \times \frac{1 - (1.011667)^{-1}}{0.011667} = 8196 \times \frac{1 - 0.98848}{0.011667} = 8196 \times 0.98848$$

$$B_{47} \approx ₹8{,}101$$

$$I_{48} = 8{,}101 \times 0.011667 \approx ₹95$$
$$\text{PR}_{48} = 8{,}196 - 95 = ₹8{,}101$$
$$B_{48} = 8{,}101 - 8{,}101 = ₹0 \text{ ✓}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** A car dealer advertises a loan of **₹8,00,000** at a "low" flat rate of **9% per annum" for **5 years**.
(i) Compute the flat-rate EMI quoted by the dealer.
(ii) Convert this to the equivalent reducing-balance rate and compute the true reducing-balance EMI for the same principal and tenure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A borrower takes a home loan of **₹25,00,000** at **10.5% p.a.** (reducing balance) for **20 years**. After paying EMIs for **5 years (60 months)**, the borrower makes a lump-sum prepayment of **₹5,00,000**.
Compute:
(i) The original EMI.
(ii) The outstanding balance just after the 60th EMI.
(iii) The new EMI if the tenure is kept unchanged at the original 20 years.
(iv) The new tenure if the EMI is kept unchanged at the original value. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Flat-rate EMI:**

Total flat-rate interest:

$$I_{flat} = P \times R_{flat} \times t / 100 = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 9 \times 5 / 100 = ₹3{,}60{,}000$$

Total amount payable:

$$A = 8{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}60{,}000 = ₹11{,}60{,}000$$

Flat-rate EMI:

$$E_{flat} = \frac{11{,}60{,}000}{60} = ₹19{,}333.33$$

**Equivalent reducing-balance rate:**

For a flat-rate loan, the equivalent reducing-balance rate $R_{red}$ satisfies (approximately, for medium-tenure loans):

$$R_{red} \approx 2 \times R_{flat} = 18\% \text{ p.a.}$$

More precisely, the equivalence is established by setting the flat-rate total interest equal to the total interest from a reducing-balance loan with the same EMI. We solve iteratively:

Try $R_{red} = 18\%$, $r = 0.015$, $n = 60$:

$$(1.015)^{60} \approx 2.4432$$

$$E_{red} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.015 \times 2.4432}{2.4432 - 1} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.03665}{1.4432} = \frac{29{,}320}{1.4432} \approx ₹20{,}315$$

Total interest: $20{,}315 \times 60 - 8{,}00{,}000 = 12{,}18{,}900 - 8{,}00{,}000 = ₹4{,}18{,}900$

This is close to the flat-rate interest of ₹3,60,000 but slightly higher, so the true equivalent reducing-balance rate is slightly below 18%. Refining:

Try $R_{red} = 16.5\%$, $r = 0.01375$, $(1.01375)^{60} \approx 2.2719$:

$$E_{red} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.01375 \times 2.2719}{2.2719 - 1} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.03124}{1.2719} \approx ₹19{,}649$$

Total interest: $19{,}649 \times 60 - 8{,}00{,}000 = ₹3{,}78{,}940$

This is much closer to ₹3,60,000. So the **equivalent reducing-balance rate is approximately 16% p.a.**, which is **nearly double** the 9% flat rate advertised — a classic KTU "trap" question.

---

**Part (b) — Home loan with prepayment:**

**Step 1: Original EMI.**

$$P = 25{,}00{,}000, \quad R = 10.5\%, \quad r = 0.00875, \quad n = 240$$

$$(1.00875)^{240} \approx 8.1165$$

$$E = \frac{25{,}00{,}000 \times 0.00875 \times 8.1165}{8.1165 - 1} = \frac{25{,}00{,}000 \times 0.07102}{7.1165} = \frac{1{,}77{,}550}{7.1165} \approx ₹24{,}941$$

**Step 2: Outstanding balance after 60 months.**

Use the remaining-annuity form with $(n-k) = 180$ remaining months:

$$(1.00875)^{180} \approx 4.8203$$

$$(1.00875)^{-180} \approx 0.20746$$

$$B_{60} = 24{,}941 \times \frac{1 - 0.20746}{0.00875} = 24{,}941 \times \frac{0.79254}{0.00875} = 24{,}941 \times 90.5762$$

$$B_{60} \approx ₹22{,}58{,}966$$

**Step 3: After ₹5,00,000 prepayment:**

$$B_{60}^{new} = 22{,}58{,}966 - 5{,}00{,}000 = ₹17{,}58{,}966$$

**New EMI if tenure is kept unchanged (n = 240, remaining 180 months):**

$$E_{new} = \frac{17{,}58{,}966 \times 0.00875 \times (1.00875)^{180}}{(1.00875)^{180} - 1} = \frac{17{,}58{,}966 \times 0.00875 \times 4.8203}{4.8203 - 1}$$

$$E_{new} = \frac{17{,}58{,}966 \times 0.042178}{3.8203} = \frac{74{,}182}{3.8203} \approx ₹19{,}418$$

**New tenure if EMI is kept unchanged ($E = 24{,}941$):**

$$17{,}58{,}966 = 24{,}941 \times \frac{1 - (1.00875)^{-n'}}{0.00875}$$

$$\frac{17{,}58{,}966 \times 0.00875}{24{,}941} = 1 - (1.00875)^{-n'}$$

$$0.6170 = 1 - (1.00875)^{-n'}$$

$$(1.00875)^{-n'} = 0.3830$$

$$-n' \times \ln(1.00875) = \ln(0.3830)$$

$$-n' \times 0.008712 = -0.96016$$

$$n' = \frac{0.96016}{0.008712} \approx 110.2 \text{ months}$$

So the new remaining tenure is approximately **110 months** (≈ **9.2 years**), down from the original 180 months remaining — a saving of **~70 months (≈ 5.8 years)**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Unit mismatch in $r$**: A very common error is using $r = 0.12$ (annual) instead of $r = 0.01$ (monthly) when the rate is 12% p.a. Always convert explicitly. *(−1 to −2 marks)*
> - **Exponent confusion**: The exponent in the EMI formula is $n$ (number of months), **not** the number of years. *(−1 mark)*
> - **Flat vs. reducing confusion**: In part (a) of Question B, many students compute the reducing-balance EMI and stop. The question explicitly asks for the **equivalent** flat-to-reducing rate. *(−2 to −3 marks)*
> - **Rounding errors in amortisation**: EMI is rounded to the nearest rupee; if a student retains excessive decimals, the final balance may not be exactly zero, but they should not get penalised. Conversely, rounding the EMI **upward** early causes the final payment to be smaller — students should mention this in the last-row adjustment. *(−1 mark if unexplained)*
> - **Missing prepayment logic**: For part (b) of Question B, students often forget to **subtract the prepayment** before recomputing the EMI or tenure. *(−2 marks)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **EMI = Annuity payment** that fully amortises a loan. The standard formula is the **single most important equation** in Module 2.
- Always convert the **annual percentage rate to a monthly decimal rate** using $r = R / 1200$ before plugging into any formula.
- The exponent $n$ in the EMI formula is the **number of monthly instalments** ($n = 12 \times$ years), never the years themselves.
- An amortisation schedule has **interest declining** and **principal rising** over the tenure, but the **EMI stays constant**.
- The **reducing balance method** is the industry standard and the only fair method; the **flat-rate method** always hides a higher effective cost.
- The **outstanding balance** at any time can be computed two ways: recursively (balance sheet) or via the **remaining-annuity** closed form $B_k = E \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-(n-k)}}{r}$.
- **Prepayments reduce the outstanding balance**, and the borrower can choose to (a) reduce the EMI and keep the tenure, or (b) keep the EMI and reduce the tenure. Tenure reduction yields greater total interest savings.
- The **Effective Annual Rate (EAR)** is the true measure of borrowing cost and is computed as $(1+r)^{12} - 1$ for monthly compounding.
- Boundary check: A correctly computed EMI must yield $B_n = 0$ exactly (or within rounding after the final payment is adjusted).
- For KTU 14-mark questions, always show **all intermediate steps**, the **unit conversions**, and the **final interpretation** in words — examiners reward methodological rigour, not just the final number.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stocks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET602 - FINANCIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stocks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Stock?

A **stock** (also called equity or share capital) is a security that represents the **ownership claim** of a shareholder on a corporation's assets and earnings. Owning one share of a company's stock means the investor owns a fractional piece of that business, with proportional rights to:
- **Voting power** in shareholder meetings (one share = one vote for common stock)
- **Residual claim** on assets during liquidation (after all debts are paid)
- **Dividends** — the portion of profits distributed to shareholders

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Stock):** *"A stock is a financial instrument issued by a corporation that represents a proportional claim on the firm's earnings, assets, and voting rights, traded on organized exchanges or over-the-counter markets."*

Stocks are categorized into two primary types:

| Feature | Common Stock | Preferred Stock |
|---|---|---|
| Voting Rights | Yes | Usually No |
| Dividend | Variable, non-guaranteed | Fixed, paid before common |
| Priority in Liquidation | Last | Before common, after debt |
| Risk-Return Profile | Higher risk, higher upside | Lower risk, lower upside |
| Growth Potential | Capital appreciation + dividends | Mostly fixed income |
| Conversion Feature | — | Often convertible to common |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Apartment Building 🏢

Imagine a company as a **40-floor luxury apartment building**:
- **Equity holders (common stockholders)** are the **co-owners** of the entire building. They collect rental income (dividends) after maintenance (operating costs), debt EMI (interest), and taxes. They share in any appreciation (capital gains) when the building value rises.
- **Preferred stockholders** are like **senior tenants with fixed-rent contracts** — they get paid a fixed amount before any co-owner gets anything, but they don't get to vote on building renovations (no voting rights).
- **Bondholders / Debtholders** are the **bank that gave the construction loan** — they get fixed EMI first, regardless of how the building performs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in Financial Engineering?**
> In KTU Module 2, stock valuation is the cornerstone for portfolio construction, derivatives pricing (Black-Scholes uses stock volatility), and corporate finance (cost of equity). Every model — from Gordon Growth to CAPM — uses stock as the underlying asset.

## 1.3 Types of Common Stocks

1. **Blue-Chip Stocks** — Large, financially stable, dividend-paying companies (e.g., Reliance, TCS). Low volatility, moderate growth.
2. **Growth Stocks** — Reinvest earnings instead of paying dividends (e.g., early Tesla). High P/E ratios.
3. **Value Stocks** — Trading below intrinsic value (low P/E, low P/B). Often mature firms.
4. **Income Stocks** — High dividend yield, stable cash flows (utilities, REITs).
5. **Penny Stocks** — Low-priced, highly speculative shares of small companies.

## 1.4 The Stock Market Ecosystem

```
Primary Market  →  Companies ISSUE new shares (IPO, FPO)
Secondary Market →  Investors TRADE existing shares (BSE, NSE, NYSE, NASDAQ)
Over-the-Counter (OTC) →  Decentralized, no formal exchange
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Indices Students Must Know (KTU):**
> - **India:** NIFTY 50 (NSE), SENSEX (BSE), BANK NIFTY
> - **USA:** S&P 500, DOW JONES, NASDAQ Composite
> - **Global:** MSCI World, FTSE All-World
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Stock Valuation — The Three Pillars

Stock valuation answers one core question: **"What is this share intrinsically worth?"** KTU Module 2 focuses on three rigorous methodologies:

### 🔷 Pillar 1: Dividend Discount Model (DDM)
The value of a stock is the **present value of all future dividends** the shareholder will receive. This is rooted in the **Principle of Present Value** and the **No-Arbitrage Condition**.

### 🔷 Pillar 2: P/E Multiple Approach (Relative Valuation)
Compares the stock's **Price-to-Earnings ratio** with peers or historical averages. Simple, market-aligned, and widely used by practitioners.

### 🔷 Pillar 3: Free Cash Flow to Equity (FCFE)
Values a stock by discounting the **cash flow actually available to equity holders** after all reinvestment and debt obligations.

---

## 2.2 The Required Rate of Return — CAPM Foundation

Before valuing a stock, the student MUST know the **discount rate (r)** used in these models. The Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) provides the theoretically accepted required return:

$$r_e = R_f + \beta \cdot (R_m - R_f)$$

Where:
- $R_f$ = Risk-free rate (typically 10-year Government Treasury yield)
- $\beta$ = Systematic risk of the stock relative to the market
- $R_m$ = Expected market return
- $(R_m - R_f)$ = Equity Risk Premium (ERP)

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU Exams:** If the question states "use CAPM to find cost of equity," compute $\beta \cdot (R_m - R_f)$ first, then add $R_f$. Always show units and the source of $R_f$.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (HIGH-YIELD)

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Description / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Gordon Growth Model (GGM) | $P_0 = \dfrac{D_1}{r_e - g}$ | Constant growth forever. **Condition:** $r_e > g$ strictly. |
| 2 | Multi-Stage DDM (Two-Stage) | $P_0 = \dfrac{D_0(1+g_s)}{(r-g_s)}\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{1+g_s}{1+r}\right)^N\right] + \dfrac{D_{N+1}}{(r-g_n)(1+r)^N}$ | Growth at $g_s$ for N years, then $g_n$ forever. |
| 3 | Sustainable Growth Rate | $g = b \times ROE$ | $b$ = retention ratio; $ROE$ = Return on Equity. |
| 4 | Dividend Payout Ratio | $\text{Payout} = \dfrac{DPS}{EPS}$ | Fraction of earnings paid as dividends. |
| 5 | CAPM | $r_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$ | Used as discount rate $r$ in DDM. |
| 6 | Holding Period Return | $HPR = \dfrac{D_1 + (P_1 - P_0)}{P_0}$ | Total return over one holding period. |
| 7 | Expected Return (CAPM-derived) | $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i [E(R_m) - R_f]$ | Same as #5 in expected-value form. |
| 8 | Capital Gains Yield | $g_{CGY} = \dfrac{P_1 - P_0}{P_0}$ | Return purely from price change. |
| 9 | Dividend Yield | $g_{DY} = \dfrac{D_1}{P_0}$ | Return purely from dividends. |
| 10 | P/E Ratio Valuation | $P_0 = EPS \times (P/E)_{\text{peer}}$ | Relative valuation using peer multiplier. |
| 11 | FCFE Valuation | $P_0 = \dfrac{FCFE_1}{r - g}$ | Substitute $D_1$ with $FCFE_1$ in GGM. |
| 12 | Total Stock Return (Annualized) | $r = \dfrac{D_1 + (P_1 - P_0)}{P_0}$ | Used in comparing stock returns with bonds. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorization Tip for KTU Exams:** Memorize GGM (Formula 1), CAPM (Formula 5), and Sustainable Growth (Formula 3) first. Together they solve 80% of KTU Module 2 numericals.

---

## 2.4 The Why Behind Each Formula

- **Why divide by $(r-g)$?** Because a perpetual growing annuity grows at $g$ and is discounted at $r$. If $r \le g$, the formula **blows up** — economically meaning the stock is infinitely valuable, which is impossible.
- **Why CAPM uses $\beta$?** Because in a diversified portfolio, only **systematic (non-diversifiable) risk** is rewarded. $\beta$ measures exposure to market risk.
- **Why use FCFE instead of dividends?** Because many growth companies pay **zero dividends** but generate plenty of cash. FCFE captures the cash available for equity holders.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where stock valuation is used in industry:**
> - **Investment Banking:** Pricing IPOs, fairness opinions in M&A.
> - **Wealth Management:** Asset allocation, retirement portfolio rebalancing.
> - **Corporate Treasury:** Buyback decisions, capital structure optimization (Modigliani-Miller).
> - **Algorithmic Trading:** Pairs trading, statistical arbitrage using P/E and $\beta$.
> - **Risk Management:** VaR calculations, stress-testing equity portfolios.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Gordon Growth Model (GGM)

The GGM is the foundation of stock valuation in KTU Module 2. We derive it from first principles using the **Discounted Cash Flow (DCF)** method.

### 🎯 Assumptions

1. Dividends grow at a **constant rate** $g$ forever.
2. Required rate of return $r_e$ is **constant**.
3. $r_e > g$ (strictly, for finite valuation).
4. Infinite horizon — the company never liquidates.

### 🎯 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Express future dividends in terms of today's dividend $D_0$ and growth rate $g$.

$$D_1 = D_0(1+g)$$
$$D_2 = D_1(1+g) = D_0(1+g)^2$$
$$D_3 = D_2(1+g) = D_0(1+g)^3$$

In general, for period $t$:
$$D_t = D_0(1+g)^t$$

**Step 2:** The current price is the present value of all future dividends (infinite horizon).

$$P_0 = \frac{D_1}{(1+r_e)^1} + \frac{D_2}{(1+r_e)^2} + \frac{D_3}{(1+r_e)^3} + \dots$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $D_t$ expressions into the sum.

$$P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{D_0(1+g)^t}{(1+r_e)^t}$$

**Step 4:** Factor out the constant $D_0$.

$$P_0 = D_0 \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \left(\frac{1+g}{1+r_e}\right)^t$$

**Step 5:** Recognize this as an **infinite geometric series** with common ratio:

$$\rho = \frac{1+g}{1+r_e}$$

Since $r_e > g$, we have $\rho < 1$, ensuring convergence.

**Step 6:** Apply the standard geometric series result $\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \rho^t = \dfrac{\rho}{1-\rho}$.

$$P_0 = D_0 \cdot \frac{\rho}{1-\rho}$$

**Step 7:** Substitute back $\rho$ and simplify.

$$P_0 = D_0 \cdot \frac{\frac{1+g}{1+r_e}}{1 - \frac{1+g}{1+r_e}}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1+r_e)$:

$$P_0 = D_0 \cdot \frac{1+g}{(1+r_e) - (1+g)}$$

Simplify the denominator:

$$P_0 = D_0 \cdot \frac{1+g}{r_e - g}$$

**Step 8:** Note that $D_0(1+g) = D_1$, giving us the **Gordon Growth Model**:

$$\boxed{P_0 = \frac{D_1}{r_e - g}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Expectation:** Always show the substitution from $D_0(1+g)$ to $D_1$ explicitly. Most students forget this final step.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Sustainable Growth Rate

The growth rate $g$ in GGM has an internal source — **retained earnings reinvested at the firm's ROE**.

**Step 1:** Define the **retention ratio** $b$ as the fraction of earnings NOT paid as dividends.

$$b = 1 - \text{Payout Ratio} = 1 - \frac{D_0}{EPS_0}$$

**Step 2:** Earnings per share (EPS) grow when retained earnings are reinvested at ROE.

The increase in book value per share from retained earnings is:

$$\Delta BVPS = b \cdot EPS \cdot ROE$$

**Step 3:** The growth rate of EPS (and hence dividends, in the long run) is:

$$g = \frac{\Delta BVPS}{BVPS} = \frac{b \cdot EPS \cdot ROE}{BVPS}$$

**Step 4:** Recall that $ROE = \dfrac{EPS}{BVPS}$ by definition. Thus:

$$\boxed{g = b \times ROE}$$

This is the **internal sustainable growth rate** — no new debt or equity issuance assumed.

---

## 3.3 Worked-Out Analytical Examples

### 📝 Example 1: Gordon Growth Model

**Given:**
- Current dividend $D_0 = ₹12$
- Constant growth rate $g = 6\% = 0.06$
- Required return $r_e = 14\% = 0.14$

**Solution:**

$$D_1 = D_0(1+g) = 12 \times 1.06 = ₹12.72$$

$$P_0 = \frac{D_1}{r_e - g} = \frac{12.72}{0.14 - 0.06} = \frac{12.72}{0.08} = ₹159.00$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of convergence:** $\rho = (1.06 / 1.14) = 0.9298 < 1$ ✓ — series converges.

---

### 📝 Example 2: CAPM + GGM Combined

**Given:**
- Risk-free rate $R_f = 7\%$
- Market return $R_m = 13\%$
- Stock beta $\beta = 1.2$
- Expected dividend next year $D_1 = ₹20$
- Growth rate $g = 4\%$

**Solution:**

$$r_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f) = 0.07 + 1.2 \times (0.13 - 0.07)$$

$$r_e = 0.07 + 1.2 \times 0.06 = 0.07 + 0.072 = 0.142 = 14.2\%$$

$$P_0 = \frac{D_1}{r_e - g} = \frac{20}{0.142 - 0.04} = \frac{20}{0.102} = ₹196.08$$

---

### 📝 Example 3: Two-Stage Dividend Discount Model

**Given:**
- $D_0 = ₹5$, high-growth rate $g_s = 20\%$ for $N = 3$ years
- Stable growth rate $g_n = 6\%$ thereafter
- Required return $r = 12\%$

**Solution:**

**Step A:** Project dividends during high-growth phase.

$$D_1 = 5 \times 1.20 = ₹6.00$$

$$D_2 = 6 \times 1.20 = ₹7.20$$

$$D_3 = 7.20 \times 1.20 = ₹8.64$$

**Step B:** Calculate terminal value at end of year 3 using GGM.

$$D_4 = D_3 \times (1 + g_n) = 8.64 \times 1.06 = ₹9.1584$$

$$TV_3 = \frac{D_4}{r - g_n} = \frac{9.1584}{0.12 - 0.06} = \frac{9.1584}{0.06} = ₹152.64$$

**Step C:** Discount all cash flows back to $t=0$.

$$P_0 = \frac{6.00}{1.12^1} + \frac{7.20}{1.12^2} + \frac{8.64}{1.12^3} + \frac{152.64}{1.12^3}$$

Computing each discount factor:
- $1.12^1 = 1.1200$, so first term $= 5.3571$
- $1.12^2 = 1.2544$, so second term $= 5.7405$
- $1.12^3 = 1.4049$, so third term $= 6.1502$
- Fourth term $= 108.6560$

$$P_0 = 5.3571 + 5.7405 + 6.1502 + 108.6560 = ₹125.90$$

---

### 📝 Example 4: Sustainable Growth Rate + GGM

**Given:**
- A firm has ROE = $18\%$, Payout ratio = $40\%$
- Last dividend $D_0 = ₹8$
- Required return $r = 13\%$

**Solution:**

$$b = 1 - 0.40 = 0.60$$

$$g = b \times ROE = 0.60 \times 0.18 = 0.108 = 10.8\%$$

$$D_1 = 8 \times 1.108 = ₹8.864$$

$$P_0 = \frac{8.864}{0.13 - 0.108} = \frac{8.864}{0.022} = ₹402.91$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** When $r - g$ is small (e.g., $0.022$), $P_0$ becomes very sensitive. A $1\%$ change in $g$ drastically changes the valuation. This is why KTU questions often test sensitivity analysis here.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Stock Classification Flow Chart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stocks] --> B[Common Stock]
    A --> C[Preferred Stock]
    B --> D[Blue Chip]
    B --> E[Growth]
    B --> F[Value]
    B --> G[Income]
    B --> H[Penny]
    C --> I[Cumulative Pref]
    C --> J[Non-Cumulative Pref]
    C --> K[Convertible Pref]
    C --> L[Participating Pref]
    D --> D1[Reliance, TCS, HDFC Bank]
    E --> E1[Tesla pre-2020, Amazon pre-profit]
    F --> F1[Cyclical banks, auto stocks]
    G --> G1[Utilities, REITs, tobacco]
    H --> H1[Small cap speculative firms]
```

## 4.2 Stock Valuation Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stock Valuation Method] --> B[Intrinsic Methods]
    A --> C[Relative Methods]
    B --> D[Dividend Discount Model]
    B --> E[Free Cash Flow to Equity FCFE]
    B --> F[Residual Income Model]
    D --> G[Gordon Growth Constant]
    D --> H[Two Stage Multi Phase]
    D --> I[Three Stage H Model]
    C --> J[P/E Multiple]
    C --> K[P/B Multiple]
    C --> L[EV/EBITDA Multiple]
    C --> M[P/S Multiple]
```

## 4.3 Gordon Growth Model Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inputs: D1, r, g] --> B{Check: r greater than g}
    B -- Yes --> C[Apply Formula: P0 = D1 divided by r minus g]
    B -- No --> D[Reject Model - Series Diverges]
    C --> E[Output: Intrinsic Stock Price]
    D --> F[Use Multi-Stage DDM or FCFE]
```

## 4.4 Stock Return Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Stock Return] --> B[Dividend Yield]
    A --> C[Capital Gains Yield]
    B --> B1[D1 / P0]
    C --> C1[P1 - P0 divided by P0]
    A --> D[Example: Buy at 100, Sell at 110, Div 5]
    D --> E[DY = 5 percent]
    D --> F[CGY = 10 percent]
    D --> G[Total Return = 15 percent]
```

## 4.5 CAPM Required Return Construction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Risk Free Rate Rf] --> D[Required Return re]
    B[Stock Beta] --> E[Risk Premium Calculation]
    C[Market Return Rm] --> E
    E --> E1[beta times Rm minus Rf]
    E1 --> D
    D --> F[Used as Discount Rate in DDM]
```

## 4.6 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Stock Investment Decision

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph DATA[Data Inputs]
        A1[Historical Prices]
        A2[Financial Statements]
        A3[Macroeconomic Indicators]
        A4[Industry Benchmarks]
    end

    subgraph PROC[Processing Layer]
        B1[Fundamental Analysis]
        B2[Technical Analysis]
        B3[Quantitative Models]
    end

    subgraph VAL[Valuation Engine]
        C1[DDM Computation]
        C2[CAPM Discount Rate]
        C3[Multiples Comparison]
    end

    subgraph DEC[Decision Layer]
        D1[Buy Signal]
        D2[Hold Signal]
        D3[Sell Signal]
    end

    DATA --> PROC
    PROC --> VAL
    VAL --> DEC
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C3
    B3 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define a stock and differentiate between common and preferred stock.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **stock** is a financial security that represents proportionate ownership in a corporation, conferring claim on its earnings (via dividends) and residual assets.

**Differences:**

| Parameter | Common Stock | Preferred Stock |
|---|---|---|
| Dividend | Variable, discretionary | Fixed, contractual priority |
| Voting Rights | Yes (one share, one vote) | Usually None |
| Liquidation Priority | Last (after debt and preferred) | Senior to common, junior to debt |
| Risk-Return | Higher risk, higher return | Lower risk, lower return |
| Growth Driver | Capital appreciation + dividends | Mostly dividend income |

---

### **Q2. State and explain the Gordon Growth Model for stock valuation.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Gordon Growth Model (GGM)** states that the intrinsic value of a stock equals the present value of all future dividends growing at a constant rate $g$ forever:

$$P_0 = \frac{D_1}{r_e - g}$$

Where $D_1$ is next year's expected dividend, $r_e$ is the required rate of return (from CAPM), and $g$ is the constant dividend growth rate. The model assumes $r_e > g$ strictly; otherwise, the geometric series diverges.

---

## 🅱️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Attempt **either** Q1A or Q1B. Each has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Show ALL steps for full marks.

---

### **Question 1A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### **(a) Explain CAPM. A stock has beta = 1.4, risk-free rate = 6%, expected market return = 12%. Find the required return. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**CAPM (Capital Asset Pricing Model):** Proposed by William Sharpe (1964), it relates the required return on a stock to its systematic risk (beta). The formula:

$$r_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$$

**Given Data:**
- $\beta = 1.4$
- $R_f = 6\%$
- $R_m = 12\%$
- ERP = $R_m - R_f = 12\% - 6\% = 6\%$

**Calculation:**
