$$r_e = 6\% + 1.4 \times (12\% - 6\%)$$

$$r_e = 6\% + 1.4 \times 6\%$$

$$r_e = 6\% + 8.4\% = \boxed{14.4\%}$$

**Interpretation:** The stock's required return is 14.4% because its beta is greater than 1, meaning it carries **more systematic risk** than the market portfolio.

---

#### **(b) Using GGM, find the current price of a stock with D₀ = ₹10, g = 5%, and required return = 12%. What is the dividend yield and capital gains yield? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute next year's dividend.

$$D_1 = D_0(1+g) = 10 \times 1.05 = ₹10.50$$

**Step 2:** Apply GGM.

$$P_0 = \frac{D_1}{r_e - g} = \frac{10.50}{0.12 - 0.05} = \frac{10.50}{0.07} = ₹150.00$$

**Step 3:** Dividend Yield.

$$\text{DY} = \frac{D_1}{P_0} = \frac{10.50}{150} = 0.07 = 7\%$$

**Step 4:** Capital Gains Yield.

$$\text{CGY} = g = 5\%$$

**Step 5:** Verify — Total return equals required return.

$$r_e = 7\% + 5\% = 12\% \checkmark$$

---

### **Question 1B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **(a) Discuss the Two-Stage Dividend Discount Model with a numerical illustration. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Two-Stage DDM** accommodates firms with an initial period of **abnormally high (or low) growth**, followed by a **stable long-term growth** phase. It is useful for valuing growth firms that eventually mature.

**Formula:**

$$P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{D_{t}}{(1+r)^t} + \frac{TV_N}{(1+r)^N}$$

where the terminal value is:

$$TV_N = \frac{D_{N+1}}{r - g_n}$$

**Numerical Illustration:**
- $D_0 = ₹4$, $g_s = 15\%$ (high growth) for $N = 3$ years
- $g_n = 5\%$ (stable growth thereafter)
- Required return $r = 11\%$

**Stage 1 — Project and discount high-growth dividends:**

$$D_1 = 4 \times 1.15 = ₹4.60, \quad D_2 = 4.60 \times 1.15 = ₹5.29, \quad D_3 = 5.29 \times 1.15 = ₹6.08$$

**Stage 2 — Terminal value at end of year 3:**

$$D_4 = 6.08 \times 1.05 = ₹6.384$$

$$TV_3 = \frac{6.384}{0.11 - 0.05} = \frac{6.384}{0.06} = ₹106.40$$

**Stage 3 — Total present value:**

$$P_0 = \frac{4.60}{1.11} + \frac{5.29}{1.11^2} + \frac{6.08}{1.11^3} + \frac{106.40}{1.11^3}$$

$$P_0 = 4.14 + 4.29 + 4.45 + 77.81 = \boxed{₹90.69}$$

---

#### **(b) What is the sustainable growth rate? A firm has ROE = 20%, retention ratio = 60%, and last dividend = ₹6. If required return is 13%, find the stock price using GGM. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Sustainable Growth Rate (SGR):** The rate at which a firm can grow using only **internal financing** (retained earnings), without issuing new debt or equity.

$$g = b \times ROE$$

where $b$ is the retention ratio.

**Given:**
- $ROE = 20\% = 0.20$
- $b = 60\% = 0.60$
- $D_0 = ₹6$
- $r = 13\% = 0.13$

**Step 1:** Compute $g$.

$$g = 0.60 \times 0.20 = 0.12 = 12\%$$

**Step 2:** Compute $D_1$.

$$D_1 = 6 \times 1.12 = ₹6.72$$

**Step 3:** Apply GGM.

$$P_0 = \frac{6.72}{0.13 - 0.12} = \frac{6.72}{0.01} = \boxed{₹672.00}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Forgetting to compute $D_1$** from $D_0$ — Students plug $D_0$ into GGM directly. This loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Not verifying $r > g$** — If $r \le g$, the GGM is invalid. Show this check explicitly.
> 3. **Mixing up dividend yield and capital gains yield** — DY uses $D_1/P_0$; CGY always equals $g$ in GGM.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Stock = Ownership claim** with voting rights, residual assets, and dividends.
- ✅ **Common vs Preferred:** Voting/Residual vs Fixed/Priority.
- ✅ **GGM:** $P_0 = D_1/(r_e - g)$ — requires $r_e > g$ strictly.
- ✅ **CAPM:** $r_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$ — supplies the discount rate.
- ✅ **SGR:** $g = b \times ROE$ — internal growth only.
- ✅ **Two-Stage DDM:** Sum of high-growth dividends + discounted terminal value.
- ✅ **Holding Period Return:** $\text{HPR} = [D_1 + (P_1 - P_0)] / P_0$.
- ✅ **Dividend Yield** = $D_1/P_0$; **Capital Gains Yield** = $g$ (in GGM).
- ✅ **Total Return = DY + CGY = r_e** (must always reconcile).
- ✅ **Sensitivity Alert:** When $r - g$ is small, small changes in $g$ produce large valuation swings.
- ✅ **KTU Exam Tip:** Always verify $\rho = (1+g)/(1+r) < 1$ before applying GGM.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Valuation of Stocks and Intrinsic Value of Stock
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET602 - FINANCIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Valuation of Stocks and Intrinsic Value of Stock

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Stock Valuation** is the process of determining the theoretical fair value (Intrinsic Value) of a company's share by estimating the present value of its expected future cash flows (primarily dividends) discounted back to the present at an appropriate required rate of return ($K_e$). In the KTU 2024 Finance framework, it is the mathematical bridge between a firm's earnings power and its market price.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intrinsic Value ($P_0$)**: The "true" worth of a stock based on fundamental analysis, independent of current market sentiment. It is what an investor *should* pay.
> **Market Value ($P_m$)**: The actual price at which the stock trades in the market. It reflects supply, demand, and psychology.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The Mango Orchard)
Imagine you are buying a **mango orchard**. The orchard will produce mangoes (dividends) for 50 years. You would never pay more than the *total present value of all future mango harvests*, minus the costs of running the orchard, discounted to today's rupees.

A stock is exactly this orchard. Instead of mangoes, it produces **cash dividends**. Instead of soil, it has **earnings power**. Instead of weather risk, it has **business risk (β)**. 

- If the market price ($P_m$) > Intrinsic Value ($P_0$) → **Stock is Overvalued (SELL signal)**
- If the market price ($P_m$) < Intrinsic Value ($P_0$) → **Stock is Undervalued (BUY signal)**
- If $P_m \approx P_0$ → **Stock is at Fair Value (HOLD)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: Stock valuation sits at the heart of **Equity Research, Portfolio Management, and Mergers & Acquisitions**. Mastery of the Dividend Discount Model (DDM) and the Constant Growth (Gordon) Model is mandatory for Module 2 internal assessments.

### 1.3 Key Terminology
| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Intrinsic Value | $P_0$ | Today's fair price per share |
| Expected Dividend | $D_1, D_t$ | Cash paid to shareholders next period |
| Required Rate of Return | $K_e$ | Minimum return investor demands |
| Constant Growth Rate | $g$ | Perpetual annual dividend growth |
| Beta | $\beta$ | Stock's sensitivity to market risk |
| Risk-Free Rate | $R_f$ | Return on government bonds |
| Market Return | $R_m$ | Expected return on the broad market |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logic of Stock Valuation
Stock valuation rests on **one foundational principle**: *A stock is worth the present value of all future cash flows the investor will receive.* For common stockholders, those cash flows are:

1. **Periodic Dividends** ($D_t$) — distributed during the life of the investment.
2. **Terminal Sale Price** ($P_n$) — the price at which the stock is eventually sold.

Therefore:
$$P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{D_t}{(1 + K_e)^t} + \frac{P_n}{(1 + K_e)^n}$$

Where:
- $P_0$ = Present Intrinsic Value
- $D_t$ = Dividend expected at the end of year $t$
- $K_e$ = Cost of Equity (Required Rate of Return)
- $n$ = Number of years the stock is held
- $P_n$ = Expected selling price at year $n$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why discount by $K_e$?** Because money received in the future is worth less than money received today (time value of money). $K_e$ compensates the investor for both the **time** and the **risk** of holding equity.

### 2.2 The Cost of Equity ($K_e$) — CAPM Approach
The most academically rigorous method to determine $K_e$ is the **Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)**:
$$K_e = R_f + \beta \cdot (R_m - R_f)$$

- $R_f$ = Risk-free return (typically 10-year Government Securities yield)
- $\beta$ = Stock's systematic risk measure
- $(R_m - R_f)$ = Equity Risk Premium (ERP)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Model | Formula | Applicability / Condition |
|:-:|:------|:--------|:--------------------------|
| 1 | Zero Growth (Perpetual) | $P_0 = \dfrac{D}{K_e}$ | Constant dividends forever, $g = 0$ |
| 2 | Constant Growth (Gordon) | $P_0 = \dfrac{D_1}{K_e - g}$ | Dividends grow at constant $g$ forever |
| 3 | Gordon Growth (Alt. form) | $P_0 = \dfrac{D_0(1+g)}{K_e - g}$ | When only $D_0$ is known |
| 4 | Two-Stage Growth | $P_0 = \displaystyle\sum_{t=1}^{n}\dfrac{D_0(1+g_1)^t}{(1+K_e)^t} + \dfrac{D_{n+1}}{(K_e - g_2)(1+K_e)^n}$ | High growth for $n$ years, then stable $g_2$ |
| 5 | P/E Approach | $P_0 = EPS \times \dfrac{P}{E}$ | Used as a market-comparison sanity check |
| 6 | CAPM (Cost of Equity) | $K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$ | Determines the appropriate discount rate |
| 7 | Expected Return (in terms of Growth) | $K_e = \dfrac{D_1}{P_0} + g$ | Re-arranged Gordon identity (used in $K_e$ back-solving) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary / Validity Conditions (Board-Exam Tested!)**:
> 1. $K_e > g$ **MUST** hold. If $K_e \le g$, the model breaks (denominator zero or negative → infinite/negative price).
> 2. $\beta > 0$ for normal equities; $\beta < 1$ = defensive stock, $\beta > 1$ = aggressive stock.
> 3. $g$ must be less than the long-run nominal GDP growth rate of the economy (sustainability constraint).
> 4. $D_1 = D_0(1+g)$ — always use **next year's** dividend in the Gordon numerator.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Finance Engineering
- **Equity Research Analysts** use these models to issue BUY/SELL/HOLD ratings.
- **Investment Bankers** rely on them during IPO pricing and M&A fairness opinions.
- **Portfolio Managers** benchmark index inclusions/exclusions using intrinsic-value gaps.
- **Algorithmic Trading** systems use them to flag statistical arbitrage opportunities.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Constant Growth Model (Gordon Growth Model)

**Starting Premise**: Dividends grow at a constant rate $g$ in perpetuity.
- $D_1 = D_0(1+g)$
- $D_2 = D_0(1+g)^2$
- $D_t = D_0(1+g)^t$

**Step 1 — Write the general present-value formula for an infinite series:**

$$P_0 = \frac{D_1}{(1+K_e)^1} + \frac{D_2}{(1+K_e)^2} + \frac{D_3}{(1+K_e)^3} + \dots$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the geometric dividend pattern:**

$$P_0 = \frac{D_0(1+g)}{(1+K_e)} + \frac{D_0(1+g)^2}{(1+K_e)^2} + \frac{D_0(1+g)^3}{(1+K_e)^3} + \dots$$

**Step 3 — Factor out the first term:**

$$P_0 = \frac{D_0(1+g)}{(1+K_e)} \left[ 1 + \frac{(1+g)}{(1+K_e)} + \left(\frac{(1+g)}{(1+K_e)}\right)^2 + \dots \right]$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the inner bracket as an infinite geometric series.** For a series $\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} r^t = \dfrac{1}{1-r}$ provided $\vert r \vert < 1$.
Here, $r = \dfrac{1+g}{1+K_e}$, and since $K_e > g$, the condition holds.

**Step 5 — Apply the geometric series identity:**

$$P_0 = \frac{D_0(1+g)}{(1+K_e)} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1+g}{1+K_e}}$$

**Step 6 — Simplify the denominator:**

$$1 - \frac{1+g}{1+K_e} = \frac{(1+K_e) - (1+g)}{1+K_e} = \frac{K_e - g}{1+K_e}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute back and cancel $(1+K_e)$ terms:**

$$P_0 = \frac{D_0(1+g)}{(1+K_e)} \cdot \frac{1+K_e}{K_e - g} = \frac{D_0(1+g)}{K_e - g}$$

**Step 8 — Final Gordon Growth Identity:**

$$\boxed{P_0 = \frac{D_1}{K_e - g}}$$

This completes the derivation. The intuition: a stock is worth its **next dividend** divided by the **spread between required return and growth**.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Standard)

**Problem (Module 2 — Typical 14-Mark Question)**:
A company has just paid a dividend of ₹5 per share. The dividend is expected to grow at a constant rate of 8% per year indefinitely. The company's beta is 1.2, the risk-free rate is 7%, and the market risk premium is 6%.

**Required**: Calculate the intrinsic value of the stock using CAPM and the Gordon Growth Model.

---

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step A — Compute the Cost of Equity ($K_e$) using CAPM:**

$$K_e = R_f + \beta \cdot (R_m - R_f)$$

$$K_e = 7\% + 1.2 \times 6\%$$

$$K_e = 7\% + 7.2\%$$

$$K_e = 14.2\%$$

**Step B — Compute next year's expected dividend ($D_1$):**

$$D_1 = D_0 \cdot (1 + g) = 5 \cdot (1 + 0.08)$$

$$D_1 = 5 \cdot 1.08 = \text{₹}5.40$$

**Step C — Apply the Gordon Growth Model:**

$$P_0 = \frac{D_1}{K_e - g}$$

$$P_0 = \frac{5.40}{0.142 - 0.08}$$

$$P_0 = \frac{5.40}{0.062}$$

$$P_0 = \text{₹}87.10 \text{ per share}$$

**Step D — Interpretation**:
If the current market price is, say, ₹75, the stock is **undervalued by ₹12.10** → Investment recommendation: **BUY**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensitivity Insight (KTU Frequently Asked)**: A small change in $g$ dramatically alters $P_0$. For instance, if $g$ rises to 9%, $P_0 = \dfrac{5.40}{0.142 - 0.09} = \dfrac{5.40}{0.052} = \text{₹}103.85$. **Always double-check** that $K_e > g$ before plugging into the denominator.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Valuation Model to Use?

| Scenario | Best-Fit Model | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Mature, blue-chip company (e.g., ITC, HUL) | **Constant Growth (Gordon)** | Stable, predictable dividend growth |
| Utility companies with no growth | **Zero Growth Model** | $D$ is a perpetuity; $g \approx 0$ |
| High-growth tech startup (early stage) | **Two-Stage or Multi-Stage** | Growth fades after some years |
| Negative or zero dividend payers | **P/E or FCFE Model** | DDM fails when $D = 0$ |
| Cyclical companies | **Normalized Earnings Model** | Smooth out boom-bust cycles |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Stock Valuation

The diagram below maps the **decision workflow** a financial analyst follows when valuing an equity security, from raw input data to the final BUY/SELL/HOLD recommendation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[StepA_InputData] --> B[StepB_RiskFreeRate]
    A --> C[StepC_StockBeta]
    A --> D[StepD_MarketReturn]
    A --> E[StepE_DividendHistory]
    A --> F[StepF_GrowthAssumption]
    
    B --> G[StepG_CostOfEquityCAPM]
    C --> G
    D --> G
    
    E --> H[StepH_ProjectDividends]
    F --> H
    
    G --> I[StepI_DiscountRateReady]
    H --> J[StepJ_CashFlowsReady]
    
    I --> K{StepK_GrowthPattern}
    J --> K
    
    K -->|ZeroGrowth| L[StepL_ZeroGrowthModel]
    K -->|ConstantGrowth| M[StepM_GordonGrowthModel]
    K -->|MultiStage| N[StepN_TwoStageModel]
    
    L --> O[StepO_IntrinsicValueP0]
    M --> O
    N --> O
    
    O --> P{StepP_CompareMarketPrice}
    P -->|PmGreater| Q[StepQ_SELL_OVERVALUED]
    P -->|PmLesser| R[StepR_BUY_UNDERVALUED]
    P -->|PmApproxEqual| S[StepS_HOLD_FAIRVALUE]
    
    style A fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style O fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style Q fill:#d62728,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style R fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style S fill:#9467bd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Concept Map — Hierarchy of Stock Valuation Models

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ConceptA_StockValuation] --> B[ConceptB_DDMApproach]
    A --> C[ConceptC_RelativeApproach]
    A --> D[ConceptD_AssetApproach]
    
    B --> E[ConceptE_ZeroGrowth]
    B --> F[ConceptF_ConstantGrowth]
    B --> G[ConceptG_TwoStageGrowth]
    B --> H[ConceptH_MultiStage]
    
    E --> I[ConceptI_DoverKe]
    F --> J[ConceptJ_D1overKeminusg]
    G --> K[ConceptK_SumPVplusTerminal]
    H --> L[ConceptL_PiecewisePV]
    
    C --> M[ConceptM_PEratio]
    C --> N[ConceptN_PBratio]
    C --> O[ConceptO_EVEBITDA]
    
    D --> P[ConceptP_LiquidationValue]
    D --> Q[ConceptQ_BookValue]
    
    style A fill:#1f77b4,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#ff7f0e,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#2ca02c,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram**: A student should be able to verbally trace the flow from `StepA_InputData` all the way to `StepS_HOLD_FAIRVALUE` during a viva. Examiners frequently test this *process knowledge* — not just the formula.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Intrinsic Value of a stock. How does it differ from Market Value?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
- **Intrinsic Value** ($P_0$) is the true worth of a share determined through fundamental analysis by discounting expected future dividends at the required rate of return. **(1.5 marks)**
- It is based on the company's **cash-generating ability**, not on crowd sentiment.
- **Market Value** is the price at which the stock currently trades on the exchange; it reflects demand-supply and market psychology. **(1 mark)**
- **Key Difference**: Intrinsic Value is an *estimated* theoretical value; Market Value is an *observed* real price. When they diverge, investment opportunities arise. **(0.5 mark)**

---

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the assumptions of the Gordon Growth Model.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
1. Dividends grow at a **constant rate ($g$)** forever. **(1 mark)**
2. The growth rate $g$ is **less than** the required return $K_e$ (i.e., $K_e > g$). **(1 mark)**
3. The cost of equity ($K_e$) **remains constant** over the stock's life. **(0.5 mark)**
4. The company is a **going concern** with stable financial leverage and no extraordinary capital injections. **(0.5 mark)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Note**: KTU 2024 ESE Module-end exam mandates an **internal choice**. Below, both alternatives are provided with complete model solutions.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(a)** Explain the Constant Growth Model of stock valuation. Derive the formula for intrinsic value. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A stock pays a current dividend of ₹12. The required rate of return is 15% and the constant growth rate is 6%. Calculate the intrinsic value. If the market price is ₹100, should you buy the stock? **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Theory + Derivation (7 Marks)**:

The Constant Growth Model (Gordon Growth Model) assumes that dividends of a company grow at a uniform rate $g$ in perpetuity. **(1 mark)**

The general present-value expression is:

$$P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{D_0(1+g)^t}{(1+K_e)^t}$$

Factoring out $D_0(1+g)/(1+K_e)$ and recognizing the infinite geometric series with common ratio $r = (1+g)/(1+K_e)$:

$$\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} r^t = \frac{1}{1-r} \quad \text{for} \quad \vert r \vert < 1$$

Since $K_e > g$, the convergence condition holds. **(2 marks)**

Substituting and simplifying:

$$P_0 = \frac{D_0(1+g)}{(1+K_e)} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1+g}{1+K_e}} = \frac{D_0(1+g)}{K_e - g}$$

Since $D_1 = D_0(1+g)$:

$$\boxed{P_0 = \frac{D_1}{K_e - g}}$$

**Key assumptions**:
- $g$ is constant in perpetuity **(1 mark)**
- $K_e$ is constant and $K_e > g$ **(1 mark)**
- Company maintains its capital structure (constant financial risk) **(1 mark)**

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)**:

**Step 1**: Calculate $D_1$:
$$D_1 = D_0(1+g) = 12 \times (1 + 0.06) = 12 \times 1.06 = \text{₹}12.72 \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 2**: Apply Gordon's Model:
$$P_0 = \frac{D_1}{K_e - g} = \frac{12.72}{0.15 - 0.06} = \frac{12.72}{0.09} = \text{₹}141.33 \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}$$

**Step 3**: Investment Decision:
- Intrinsic Value $P_0 =$ ₹141.33
- Market Price $P_m =$ ₹100
- Since $P_m < P_0$, the stock is **undervalued** by ₹41.33 per share. **(1 mark)**
- **Recommendation**: **BUY** the stock. **(1 mark)**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Alternative Choice]`
**(a)** Discuss the Zero Growth Model of stock valuation. Under what conditions is it preferred? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A firm is expected to pay dividends of ₹8, ₹10, ₹12, and ₹14 over the next four years. After year 4, dividends will grow at 5% forever. The required return is 12%. Find the intrinsic value. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Theory (7 Marks)**:

The Zero Growth Model is a special case of the Gordon Model where $g = 0$. Dividends are assumed to remain **constant in perpetuity** (no growth). **(2 marks)**

The intrinsic value is simply the dividend capitalized at the required return:

$$P_0 = \frac{D}{K_e}$$

**Derivation**: Setting $g=0$ in Gordon's model yields $P_0 = D_1 / K_e = D / K_e$. **(2 marks)**

**Preferred under the following conditions**: **(3 marks)**
1. **Mature preference shares** paying a fixed dividend with no growth prospect.
2. **Government bonds** or fixed-income instruments with perpetual coupon payments.
3. **Established utility companies** with regulated returns and saturated markets (e.g., mature electricity distributors).
4. As a **conservative benchmark** to compare against growth assumptions.

**Limitations**: Unrealistic for most growth-oriented firms; ignores earnings reinvestment; highly sensitive to $K_e$ estimates.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)**:

This is a **Two-Stage Growth Model** problem.

**Step 1**: Compute the present value of explicit dividends (Years 1–4): **(3 marks)**

$$PV(D_1) = \frac{8}{(1.12)^1} = \frac{8}{1.12} = \text{₹}7.143$$

$$PV(D_2) = \frac{10}{(1.12)^2} = \frac{10}{1.2544} = \text{₹}7.972$$

$$PV(D_3) = \frac{12}{(1.12)^3} = \frac{12}{1.4049} = \text{₹}8.542$$

$$PV(D_4) = \frac{14}{(1.12)^4} = \frac{14}{1.5735} = \text{₹}8.897$$

$$\text{Sum of PVs (Years 1-4)} = 7.143 + 7.972 + 8.542 + 8.897 = \text{₹}32.554$$

**Step 2**: Compute the terminal value at end of Year 4 ($P_4$) using Gordon: **(2 marks)**

$$P_4 = \frac{D_5}{K_e - g} = \frac{14 \times (1+0.05)}{0.12 - 0.05} = \frac{14.70}{0.07} = \text{₹}210.00$$

**Step 3**: Discount $P_4$ back to present: **(1 mark)**

$$PV(P_4) = \frac{210}{(1.12)^4} = \frac{210}{1.5735} = \text{₹}133.46$$

**Step 4**: Total Intrinsic Value: **(1 mark)**

$$P_0 = 32.554 + 133.46 = \text{₹}166.01 \text{ per share}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**:
> 1. **Mistake**: Using $D_0$ instead of $D_1$ in the Gordon numerator → **Lose 1 mark**.
> 2. **Mistake**: Forgetting to discount the terminal value $P_n$ back to $t=0$ → **Lose 2 marks** (this is the #1 error in two-stage questions).
> 3. **Mistake**: Writing $K_e - g$ without specifying units (decimal vs. %) → **Lose 0.5 mark**.
> 4. **Mistake**: Recommending BUY/SELL without explicitly comparing $P_0$ vs $P_m$ → **Lose 0.5 mark** on interpretation.
> 5. **Mistake**: Ignoring the convergence condition ($K_e > g$) → Examiner may deduct **0.5 mark** for theoretical incompleteness.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Intrinsic Value** = Present Value of all future expected dividends discounted at $K_e$.
- ✅ **Zero Growth**: $P_0 = D / K_e$ — used for preference shares and perpetuities.
- ✅ **Constant Growth (Gordon)**: $P_0 = D_1 / (K_e - g)$ — only if $K_e > g$.
- ✅ **Two-Stage Growth**: Discount explicit dividends year-by-year + discounted terminal Gordon value.
- ✅ **CAPM**: $K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$ — the gold standard for cost of equity.
- ✅ **P/E Approach**: $P_0 = EPS \times (P/E \text{ ratio})$ — relative valuation sanity check.
- ✅ **Investment Rule**: $P_m < P_0 \Rightarrow$ BUY; $P_m > P_0 \Rightarrow$ SELL; $P_m \approx P_0 \Rightarrow$ HOLD.
- ✅ **Convergence Condition**: Always verify $K_e > g$ **before** applying Gordon's formula.
- ✅ **Sensitivity**: $P_0$ is highly sensitive to $g$ and $K_e$ — small changes cause large valuation swings.
- ✅ **Limitations**: DDM fails for non-dividend-paying firms (use FCFE or relative valuation instead).
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Trend**: 14-mark questions often combine **theory (7 marks) + numerical (7 marks)** — practice both together!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capital Budgeting Process - Investment Criteria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET602 - FINANCIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Capital Budgeting Process - Investment Criteria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Capital Budgeting** is the systematic process by which a firm evaluates and selects long-term investment projects that are expected to generate cash flows over multiple years. The **Investment Criteria** refers to the standardized quantitative techniques (such as NPV, IRR, Payback Period, Profitability Index, and ARR) used to accept or reject proposed capital projects based on their financial viability and alignment with the firm's wealth maximization objective.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The primary goal of capital budgeting is **Wealth Maximization** (i.e., maximizing the market value of shares) — *not* profit maximization. Every investment criterion must ultimately be tested against this benchmark.

### 1.2 The Capital Budgeting Process (Phases)

The capital budgeting process is a multi-stage decision framework:

1. **Identification of Investment Opportunities** – Generating ideas for new projects, expansion, or replacement of assets.
2. **Evaluation of Projects** – Forecasting cash flows and applying investment criteria.
3. **Selection of the Best Project** – Ranking projects using the chosen criteria.
4. **Capital Allocation & Implementation** – Allocating funds and executing the project.
5. **Performance Review (Post-Audit)** – Comparing actual vs. expected performance.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "Buying a House"**
> Think of capital budgeting as deciding whether to **buy a house**. You don't just look at the purchase price; you consider:
> - **Total cost** (initial investment),
> - **Future rental income / resale value** (future cash flows),
> - **How long it will take to recover your down payment** (Payback Period),
> - **The effective annual return on your money** (IRR),
> - **Net value added after discounting future income to today** (NPV).
>
> Just as a smart buyer would never purchase a house without calculating these metrics, a rational firm should never commit crores of rupees to a project without applying capital budgeting criteria.

### 1.4 Classification of Investment Criteria

Investment criteria are classified into two broad categories:

| Category | Criteria | Basis |
|---|---|---|
| **Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Methods** | NPV, IRR, PI, Discounted Payback | Time value of money |
| **Non-Discounted (Traditional) Methods** | Payback Period, Accounting Rate of Return (ARR) | Ignores time value of money |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Criteria

#### A. Net Present Value (NPV)

**Concept:** NPV translates all future cash inflows and outflows of a project into a single lump-sum value **today (Year 0)**. A project is accepted if NPV ≥ 0.

- **Why NPV works:** Money available today is worth more than the same amount in the future due to its earning potential.
- **How it works:** Each future cash flow is discounted back to the present using the firm's cost of capital (discount rate, *k*), then summed. The initial investment is subtracted.

#### B. Internal Rate of Return (IRR)

**Concept:** IRR is the discount rate at which the **NPV of a project becomes exactly zero**. In other words, it is the project's "break-even" rate of return.

- **Why IRR is popular:** It expresses the answer as a percentage, which is easy to compare with the firm's cost of capital.
- **Decision Rule:** Accept the project if IRR > Cost of Capital (*k*).
- **Pitfall:** IRR assumes intermediate cash flows are reinvested at the IRR itself (an unrealistic assumption in many cases).

#### C. Profitability Index (PI)

**Concept:** PI is the ratio of the **present value of future cash inflows** to the **initial investment**. It is also called the Benefit-Cost Ratio.

- **Why it matters:** When capital is rationed (limited), PI is more useful than NPV because it ranks projects by value created per rupee invested.
- **Decision Rule:** Accept if PI > 1.

#### D. Discounted Payback Period

**Concept:** It is the time (in years) required to recover the initial investment from the **discounted** future cash flows.

- **Why it exists:** It corrects the major flaw of the regular payback by incorporating the time value of money.

### 2.2 Non-Discounted (Traditional) Criteria

#### E. Payback Period

**Concept:** The time required for cumulative cash inflows to equal the initial investment.

- **Decision Rule:** Accept if Payback < Target/Standard period set by management.

#### F. Accounting Rate of Return (ARR)

**Concept:** Measures the return on a project using **accounting profit** (not cash flow) relative to the average investment.

- **Formula variation 1 (on average investment):** 
- **Formula variation 2 (on initial investment):**

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.1 — Master Formula Sheet for Investment Criteria**
> 
> **Notation:** $CF_t$ = Cash flow in year *t*; $k$ = Cost of capital (discount rate); $C_0$ = Initial investment; $n$ = Project life; $NPV_k$ = NPV at discount rate $k$.

| # | Criterion | Mathematical Formula | Decision Rule | Accept / Reject |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $\displaystyle NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+k)^t}$ | NPV $\geq 0$ | Accept |
| 2 | **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** | $\displaystyle \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+IRR)^t} = 0$ | IRR $> k$ | Accept |
| 3 | **Profitability Index (PI)** | $\displaystyle PI = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+k)^t}}{C_0}$ | PI $> 1$ | Accept |
| 4 | **Discounted Payback** | Solve $\sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{CF_t}{(1+k)^t} = 0$ | $T < \text{Target}$ | Accept |
| 5 | **Payback Period** | $\displaystyle T = \text{Year before full recovery} + \frac{\text{Unrecovered cost}}{\text{CF in next year}}$ | $T < \text{Target}$ | Accept |
| 6 | **ARR (on avg. investment)** | $\displaystyle ARR = \frac{\text{Average Annual Profit}}{\text{Average Investment}} \times 100$ | ARR $>$ Target Return | Accept |
| 7 | **ARR (on initial investment)** | $\displaystyle ARR = \frac{\text{Average Annual Profit}}{\text{Initial Investment}} \times 100$ | ARR $>$ Target Return | Accept |

### 2.4 Practical Utility in Engineering Project Decisions

For B.Tech graduates entering project management roles in industry, these criteria are applied to:

- **Plant & Machinery procurement** (replacement decisions)
- **R&D investments** (new product development)
- **Capacity expansion** (adding a new production line)
- **Technology upgrades** (e.g., Industry 4.0 automation)
- **Real estate and infrastructure** (warehouses, factories)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Examples

### 3.1 Example 1: Computation of NPV, PI, and IRR

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> A firm is evaluating a project requiring an initial investment of **₹5,00,000**. The project is expected to generate cash inflows of **₹1,50,000 per year for 5 years**. The cost of capital is **10%**. Calculate NPV and PI. Should the project be accepted?

#### Step 1: Identify the Cash Flow Stream

| Year | Cash Flow (₹) | Notation |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | -5,00,000 | $C_0$ |
| 1 | +1,50,000 | $CF_1$ |
| 2 | +1,50,000 | $CF_2$ |
| 3 | +1,50,000 | $CF_3$ |
| 4 | +1,50,000 | $CF_4$ |
| 5 | +1,50,000 | $CF_5$ |

#### Step 2: Recognize this as an Annuity

Since the cash inflow is identical for 5 years, we use the **Present Value of an Annuity Factor (PVAF)**:

$$
PVAF_{(k=10\%, n=5)} = \frac{1 - (1+k)^{-n}}{k}
$$

#### Step 3: Compute PVAF explicitly

$$
PVAF = \frac{1 - (1.10)^{-5}}{0.10}
$$

Evaluate $(1.10)^{-5}$:

$$
(1.10)^{-5} = \frac{1}{(1.10)^5}
$$

Computing $(1.10)^5$:

$$
(1.10)^5 = 1.10 \times 1.10 \times 1.10 \times 1.10 \times 1.10
$$

$$
= 1.21 \times 1.21 \times 1.10
$$

$$
= 1.4641 \times 1.10 = 1.61051
$$

Therefore:

$$
(1.10)^{-5} = \frac{1}{1.61051} \approx 0.62092
$$

Substitute back:

$$
PVAF = \frac{1 - 0.62092}{0.10} = \frac{0.37908}{0.10} = 3.7908
$$

#### Step 4: Compute the Present Value of Cash Inflows

$$
PV_{inflows} = CF \times PVAF = 1{,}50{,}000 \times 3.7908
$$

$$
PV_{inflows} = 5{,}68{,}620
$$

#### Step 5: Calculate NPV

$$
NPV = PV_{inflows} - Initial\ Investment
$$

$$
NPV = 5{,}68{,}620 - 5{,}00{,}000 = ₹68{,}620
$$

#### Step 6: Calculate Profitability Index

$$
PI = \frac{PV_{inflows}}{C_0} = \frac{5{,}68{,}620}{5{,}00{,}000} = 1.1372
$$

#### Step 7: Decision

- NPV = **₹68,620** (positive → **Accept**)
- PI = **1.1372** (> 1 → **Accept**)

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Insight:** Even a small NPV of ₹68,620 over 5 years translates into lakhs of value when scaled across a firm's portfolio of projects.

---

### 3.2 Example 2: Computation of IRR (using Interpolation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> For the same project above, calculate the IRR. Use trial values of 12% and 14% for interpolation.

#### Step 1: Calculate NPV at k = 12%

$$
PVAF_{(12\%,5)} = \frac{1 - (1.12)^{-5}}{0.12}
$$

Compute $(1.12)^5$:

$$
(1.12)^2 = 1.2544
$$

$$
(1.12)^4 = (1.2544)^2 = 1.5735
$$

$$
(1.12)^5 = 1.5735 \times 1.12 = 1.7623
$$

Therefore:

$$
(1.12)^{-5} = \frac{1}{1.7623} = 0.5674
$$

$$
PVAF_{12\%} = \frac{1 - 0.5674}{0.12} = \frac{0.4326}{0.12} = 3.6048
$$

$$
PV_{inflows} = 1{,}50{,}000 \times 3.6048 = 5{,}40{,}720
$$

$$
NPV_{12\%} = 5{,}40{,}720 - 5{,}00{,}000 = ₹40{,}720
$$

#### Step 2: Calculate NPV at k = 14%

$$
PVAF_{(14\%,5)} = \frac{1 - (1.14)^{-5}}{0.14}
$$

Compute $(1.14)^5$:

$$
(1.14)^2 = 1.2996
$$

$$
(1.14)^4 = (1.2996)^2 = 1.6890
$$

$$
(1.14)^5 = 1.6890 \times 1.14 = 1.9254
$$

Therefore:

$$
(1.14)^{-5} = \frac{1}{1.9254} = 0.5194
$$

$$
PVAF_{14\%} = \frac{1 - 0.5194}{0.14} = \frac{0.4806}{0.14} = 3.4331
$$

$$
PV_{inflows} = 1{,}50{,}000 \times 3.4331 = 5{,}14{,}965
$$

$$
NPV_{14\%} = 5{,}14{,}965 - 5{,}00{,}000 = ₹14{,}965
$$

#### Step 3: Apply the Interpolation Formula

$$
IRR = L\% + \frac{NPV_L}{NPV_L - NPV_H} \times (H\% - L\%)
$$

where $L$ = lower rate (12%), $H$ = higher rate (14%).

$$
IRR = 12\% + \frac{40{,}720}{40{,}720 - 14{,}965} \times (14\% - 12\%)
$$

$$
IRR = 12\% + \frac{40{,}720}{25{,}755} \times 2\%
$$

$$
IRR = 12\% + 1.5811 \times 2\% = 12\% + 3.16\% = 15.16\%
$$

#### Step 4: Decision

Since **IRR (15.16%) > Cost of Capital (10%)**, the project is **accepted**.

---

### 3.3 Example 3: Payback Period & Discounted Payback

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> A project requires an initial investment of **₹4,00,000** and yields the following cash inflows:
> Year 1: ₹1,00,000; Year 2: ₹1,50,000; Year 3: ₹1,00,000; Year 4: ₹1,00,000.
> Cost of capital: 10%. Compute (a) Payback Period, (b) Discounted Payback Period.

#### Part (a): Payback Period

| Year | Cash Flow (₹) | Cumulative Cash Flow (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1,00,000 | 1,00,000 |
| 2 | 1,50,000 | 2,50,000 |
| 3 | 1,00,000 | 3,50,000 |
| 4 | 1,00,000 | 4,50,000 |

Cumulative cash flow equals ₹4,00,000 **between Year 3 and Year 4**.

$$
Payback = 3 + \frac{4{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}50{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000} = 3 + 0.5 = 3.5\ \text{years}
$$

#### Part (b): Discounted Payback Period

First, discount each cash flow at 10%:

$$
DCF_1 = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{1.10} = 90{,}909
$$

$$
DCF_2 = \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{(1.10)^2} = \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{1.21} = 1{,}23{,}967
$$

$$
DCF_3 = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{(1.10)^3} = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{1.331} = 75{,}131
$$

$$
DCF_4 = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{(1.10)^4} = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{1.4641} = 68{,}301
$$

| Year | Discounted CF (₹) | Cumulative Discounted CF (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 90,909 | 90,909 |
| 2 | 1,23,967 | 2,14,876 |
| 3 | 75,131 | 2,90,007 |
| 4 | 68,301 | 3,58,308 |

Note: Even after 4 years, the cumulative discounted cash flow (₹3,58,308) is less than the initial investment of ₹4,00,000. Therefore, the **project does not pay back on a discounted basis within 4 years**. The project should be **rejected under the Discounted Payback criterion** if the target payback is ≤ 4 years.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-Verification Note:** The regular Payback says "Accept" (3.5 years < 4 years), but the Discounted Payback reveals the truth — when time value of money is accounted for, the project actually destroys value. This is why KTU examiners heavily stress DCF methods over traditional ones.

---

### 3.4 Example 4: Accounting Rate of Return (ARR)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> A project has an initial investment of ₹6,00,000 with a scrap value of ₹1,00,000 after 5 years. Expected average annual accounting profit is ₹90,000. Compute ARR.

#### Average Investment Formula

$$
Average\ Investment = \frac{Initial\ Cost + Scrap\ Value}{2}
$$

$$
Average\ Investment = \frac{6{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000}{2} = 3{,}50{,}000
$$

#### ARR Calculation

$$
ARR = \frac{Average\ Annual\ Profit}{Average\ Investment} \times 100
$$

$$
ARR = \frac{90{,}000}{3{,}50{,}000} \times 100 = 25.71\%
$$

If the firm's target return is 20%, the project is **accepted**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Capital Budgeting Process — Block-Level Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Investment Opportunities] --> B[Forecast Cash Inflows and Outflows]
    B --> C[Apply Investment Criteria]
    C --> D{Criteria Satisfied}
    D -- Yes --> E[Accept the Project]
    D -- No --> F[Reject the Project]
    E --> G[Capital Allocation and Implementation]
    G --> H[Post Audit Performance Review]
    H --> I[Feedback to Identification Stage]
    I --> A
    F --> J[Project Abandoned or Deferred]
```

### 4.2 Classification of Investment Criteria

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Investment Criteria] --> B[DCF Methods]
    A --> C[Non DCF Methods]
    B --> B1[Net Present Value NPV]
    B --> B2[Internal Rate of Return IRR]
    B --> B3[Profitability Index PI]
    B --> B4[Discounted Payback Period]
    C --> C1[Payback Period]
    C --> C2[Accounting Rate of Return ARR]
```

### 4.3 Decision Logic Comparison Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Project Cash Flow Inputs] --> NPV[Compute NPV]
    Start --> IRR[Compute IRR]
    Start --> PI[Compute PI]
    Start --> PB[Compute Payback]
    NPV --> N1{NPV greater than or equal to 0}
    N1 -- Yes --> Accept1[Accept]
    N1 -- No --> Reject1[Reject]
    IRR --> I1{IRR greater than Cost of Capital}
    I1 -- Yes --> Accept2[Accept]
    I1 -- No --> Reject2[Reject]
    PI --> P1{PI greater than 1}
    P1 -- Yes --> Accept3[Accept]
    P1 -- No --> Reject3[Reject]
    PB --> PB1{Payback less than Target}
    PB1 -- Yes --> Accept4[Accept]
    PB1 -- No --> Reject4[Reject]
```

### 4.4 NPV Profile and IRR Graphical Interpretation

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Discount Rate 0 percent] --> B[NPV Maximum Positive]
    B --> C[NPV Decreases as Discount Rate Rises]
    C --> D[IRR Point NPV equals 0]
    D --> E[NPV becomes Negative]
    E --> F[Reject Region]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Manager's Insight:** The IRR is the exact point where the NPV curve crosses the horizontal axis. To the left of IRR, NPV is positive (project adds value); to the right, NPV is negative (project destroys value).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define Net Present Value (NPV) and state the decision rule for project acceptance.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (2 Marks):** Net Present Value (NPV) is the sum of the present values of all cash inflows and outflows of a project, discounted at the firm's cost of capital. Mathematically, $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+k)^t}$.
- **Decision Rule (1 Mark):** A project is **accepted if NPV ≥ 0** and **rejected if NPV < 0**. NPV ≥ 0 implies the project earns at least the required rate of return and adds to shareholder wealth.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*Distinguish between NPV and IRR. Mention any one limitation of IRR.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Difference (2 Marks):** NPV is an absolute measure of value addition expressed in monetary units, whereas IRR is a relative measure of return expressed as a percentage. NPV assumes reinvestment at the cost of capital, while IRR assumes reinvestment at the IRR itself.
- **Limitation of IRR (1 Mark):** IRR can give **multiple rates** in the case of non-conventional cash flows (where sign changes occur more than once), making the decision ambiguous.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

A company is considering two mutually exclusive projects. The cost of capital is **12%**. The cash flows are as follows:

| Year | Project A (₹) | Project B (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | -2,00,000 | -2,50,000 |
| 1 | 80,000 | 1,20,000 |
| 2 | 80,000 | 1,00,000 |
| 3 | 80,000 | 90,000 |
| 4 | 80,000 | 50,000 |

**Required:**
**(a)** Compute the NPV of both projects. Which project should be selected based on NPV? **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Compute the IRR of both projects and the Profitability Index. Which project is preferred and why? **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Solution to Question A:**

###### **Part (a) — NPV Calculation**

Using $PVAF_{(12\%, 4)} = \frac{1 - (1.12)^{-4}}{0.12}$:

Compute $(1.12)^4$:

$$
(1.12)^2 = 1.2544
$$

$$
(1.12)^4 = (1.2544)^2 = 1.5735
$$

Therefore:

$$
(1.12)^{-4} = \frac{1}{1.5735} = 0.6355
$$

$$
PVAF = \frac{1 - 0.6355}{0.12} = \frac{0.3645}{0.12} = 3.0375
$$

**For Project A:**

$$
PV_{A} = 80{,}000 \times 3.0375 = 2{,}43{,}000
$$

$$
NPV_A = 2{,}43{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 = ₹43{,}000
$$

**For Project B (Uneven cash flows — discount each year individually):**

$$
PV_1 = \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{1.12} = 1{,}07{,}143
$$

$$
PV_2 = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{(1.12)^2} = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{1.2544} = 79{,}719
$$

$$
PV_3 = \frac{90{,}000}{(1.12)^3} = \frac{90{,}000}{1.4049} = 64{,}061
$$

$$
PV_4 = \frac{50{,}000}{(1.12)^4} = \frac{50{,}000}{1.5735} = 31{,}777
$$

$$
Total\ PV_B = 1{,}07{,}143 + 79{,}719 + 64{,}061 + 31{,}777 = 2{,}82{,}700
$$

$$
NPV_B = 2{,}82{,}700 - 2{,}50{,}000 = ₹32{,}700
$$

**Decision:** Project A has higher NPV (₹43,000 > ₹32,700), so **Project A is preferred**.

---

###### **Part (b) — IRR and PI Calculation**

**For Project A (Annuity):**
Solve for IRR such that $80{,}000 \times PVAF_{(IRR, 4)} = 2{,}00{,}000$:

$$
PVAF_{(IRR, 4)} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{80{,}000} = 2.50
$$

Try **22%:**

$$
(1.22)^4 = 1.22 \times 1.22 = 1.4884
$$

$$
(1.22)^4 = 1.4884 \times 1.4884 = 2.2153
$$

$$
(1.22)^{-4} = 0.4514
$$

$$
PVAF_{22\%} = \frac{1 - 0.4514}{0.22} = \frac{0.5486}{0.22} = 2.4937
$$

Close to 2.50. Try **21.8%:**

$$
NPV_A^{21.8\%} = 80{,}000 \times 2.50 - 2{,}00{,}000 \approx 0
$$

So $IRR_A \approx 21.8\%$.

**For Project B (Trial 12% and 16%):**

$NPV_B$ at 12% = ₹32,700 (calculated above, positive)

Try **16%:**

$$
(1.16)^4 = (1.16)^2 \times (1.16)^2 = 1.3456 \times 1.3456 = 1.8106
$$

$$
(1.16)^{-4} = 0.5523
$$

$$
PV_1^{16\%} = \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{1.16} = 1{,}03{,}448
$$

$$
PV_2^{16\%} = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{1.3456} = 74{,}316
$$

$$
PV_3^{16\%} = \frac{90{,}000}{1.5609} = 57{,}658
$$

$$
PV_4^{16\%} = \frac{50{,}000}{1.8106} = 27{,}615
$$

$$
Total\ PV_B^{16\%} = 2{,}63{,}037
$$

$$
NPV_B^{16\%} = 2{,}63{,}037 - 2{,}50{,}000 = ₹13{,}037
$$

Try **18%:**

$$
(1.18)^4 = 1.4185 \times 1.4185 = 2.0114
$$

$$
(1.18)^{-4} = 0.4972
$$

$$
PV_1^{18\%} = 1{,}01{,}695
$$

$$
PV_2^{18\%} = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{1.3924} = 71{,}818
$$

$$
PV_3^{18\%} = \frac{90{,}000}{1.6430} = 54{,}778
$$

$$
PV_4^{18\%} = \frac{50{,}000}{1.9388 \text{ approx}} = 25{,}789
$$

Wait — let me recompute $(1.18)^3$:

$$
(1.18)^2 = 1.3924
$$

$$
(1.18)^3 = 1.3924 \times 1.18 = 1.6430
$$

$$
(1.18)^4 = 1.6430 \times 1.18 = 1.9388
$$

$$
PV_4^{18\%} = \frac{50{,}000}{1.9388} = 25{,}789
$$

$$
Total\ PV_B^{18\%} = 1{,}01{,}695 + 71{,}818 + 54{,}778 + 25{,}789 = 2{,}54{,}080
$$

$$
NPV_B^{18\%} = 2{,}54{,}080 - 2{,}50{,}000 = ₹4{,}080
$$

Try **19%:**

$$
(1.19)^2 = 1.4161
$$

$$
(1.19)^4 = 1.4161^2 = 2.0053
$$

$$
(1.19)^{-4} = 0.4987
$$

$$
PV_1 = \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{1.19} = 1{,}00{,}840
$$

$$
PV_2 = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{1.4161} = 70{,}617
$$

$$
PV_3 = \frac{90{,}000}{1.6852} = 53{,}406
$$

$$
PV_4 = \frac{50{,}000}{2.0053} = 24{,}933
$$

$$
Total = 1{,}00{,}840 + 70{,}617 + 53{,}406 + 24{,}933 = 2{,}49{,}796
$$

$$
NPV_B^{19\%} = -₹204
$$

Interpolating between 18% and 19%:

$$
IRR_B = 18\% + \frac{4{,}080}{4{,}080 + 204} \times 1\% = 18\% + 0.9523\% \approx 18.95\%
$$

**Profitability Index (PI):**

$$
PI_A = \frac{2{,}43{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} = 1.215
$$

$$
PI_B = \frac{2{,}82{,}700}{2{,}50{,}000} = 1.131
$$

**Conclusion:** Both criteria (NPV and PI) favor **Project A**. Moreover, IRR_A (≈21.8%) > IRR_B (≈18.95%), confirming Project A is financially superior. **Select Project A.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

A firm is evaluating a new machine with the following details:

- **Initial Investment:** ₹8,00,000
- **Scrap Value (end of Year 5):** ₹80,000
- **Expected Cash Inflows:** ₹2,50,000 per year (Years 1 to 5)
- **Cost of Capital:** 11%
- **Target Payback Period:** 3 years

**Required:**
**(a)** Calculate the Payback Period and Discounted Payback Period. Advise on acceptance. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Calculate the NPV, PI, and ARR (on average investment). Should the machine be purchased? **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Solution to Question B:**

###### **Part (a) — Payback and Discounted Payback**

**Payback Period:**

$$
Payback = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{2{,}50{,}000} = 3.2\ \text{years}
$$

Since **3.2 years > 3 years (target)**, the project **fails the simple payback test**.

**Discounted Payback at 11%:**

First, compute $PVAF_{(11\%, 5)}$:

$$
(1.11)^5 = 1.11 \times 1.11 \times 1.11 \times 1.11 \times 1.11
$$

$$
= 1.2321 \times 1.11 = 1.3676
$$

Wait, recompute:

$$
(1.11)^2 = 1.2321
$$

$$
(1.11)^4 = (1.2321)^2 = 1.5181
$$

$$
(1.11)^5 = 1.5181 \times 1.11 = 1.6851
$$

$$
(1.11)^{-5} = \frac{1}{1.6851} = 0.5935
$$

$$
PVAF = \frac{1 - 0.5935}{0.11} = \frac{0.4065}{0.11} = 3.6959
$$

$$
PV_{inflows} = 2{,}50{,}000 \times 3.6959 = 9{,}23{,}975
$$

Cumulative discounted cash inflow is ₹9,23,975 — but it never equals the ₹8,00,000 initial investment at a **single point** in years; we need to look at the year-wise discounted cash flows:

$$
DCF_1 = \frac{2{,}50{,}000}{1.11} = 2{,}25{,}225
$$

$$
DCF_2 = \frac{2{,}50{,}000}{1.2321} = 2{,}02{,}906
$$

$$
DCF_3 = \frac{2{,}50{,}000}{1.3676} = 1{,}82{,}799
$$

$$
DCF_4 = \frac{2{,}50{,}000}{1.5181} = 1{,}64{,}685
$$

$$
DCF_5 = \frac{2{,}50{,}000}{1.6851} = 1{,}48{,}366
$$

| Year | Discounted CF (₹) | Cumulative (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2,25,225 | 2,25,225 |
| 2 | 2,02,906 | 4,28,131 |
| 3 | 1,82,799 | 6,10,930 |
| 4 | 1,64,685 | 7,75,615 |
| 5 | 1,48,366 | 9,23,981 |

Cumulative crosses ₹8,00,000 between Year 4 and Year 5:

$$
Discounted\ Payback = 4 + \frac{8{,}00{,}000 - 7{,}75{,}615}{1{,}48{,}366} = 4 + 0.165 = 4.16\ \text{years}
$$

**Decision:** Discounted payback of 4.16 years > 3 years target. The project **fails both payback criteria**.

---

###### **Part (b) — NPV, PI, and ARR**

**NPV:**

Add salvage value's present value (if salvage occurs at end of Year 5):

$$
PV_{salvage} = \frac{80{,}000}{1.6851} = 47{,}476
$$

$$
Total\ PV = 9{,}23{,}975 + 47{,}476 = 9{,}71{,}451
$$

$$
NPV = 9{,}71{,}451 - 8{,}00{,}000 = ₹1{,}71{,}451
$$

**PI:**

$$
PI = \frac{9{,}71{,}451}{8{,}00{,}000} = 1.214
$$

**ARR:**

Assume average annual profit = Annual cash inflow − Annual depreciation:

$$
Depreciation = \frac{8{,}00{,}000 - 80{,}000}{5} = 1{,}44{,}000
$$

$$
Average\ Annual\ Profit = 2{,}50{,}000 - 1{,}44{,}000 = 1{,}06{,}000
$$

$$
Average\ Investment = \frac{8{,}00{,}000 + 80{,}000}{2} = 4{,}40{,}000
$$

$$
ARR = \frac{1{,}06{,}000}{4{,}40{,}000} \times 100 = 24.09\%
$$

**Decision:**
- NPV = **+₹1,71,451** (Positive → **Accept**)
- PI = **1.214** (> 1 → **Accept**)
- ARR = **24.09%** (Strong return → **Accept**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for Students:** Notice the **conflict between criteria** — Payback criteria reject the project, but DCF criteria accept it. This is a classic KTU-style question designed to test whether students understand that **NPV is the most reliable criterion**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the initial investment sign:** NPV calculation must subtract the *initial outlay* (Year 0) — students often omit it, losing 2–3 marks.
> 
> 2. **Confusing Payback vs. Discounted Payback:** Students compute the simple payback but call it "discounted payback" — examiners deduct full marks for the second part.
> 
> 3. **Incorrect IRR interpolation sign:** The interpolation formula $IRR = L\% + \frac{NPV_L}{NPV_L - NPV_H}(H\% - L\%)$ must use the **lower rate's positive NPV** in the numerator. Reversing it is a frequent error.
> 
> 4. **ARR — Average vs. Initial Investment:** KTU examiners explicitly look for which base the student has used. Always state the assumption clearly.
> 
> 5. **Inconsistent decimal places:** Round-off errors in PVAF tables (or compound calculations) can shift IRR by 1–2%. Show full working.
> 
> 6. **Decision rule confusion:** Writing "PI > 0" instead of "PI > 1" loses marks — the **threshold is strictly 1**, not 0.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Capital Budgeting Investment Criteria**
> 
> - **Primary Objective:** Wealth Maximization, not profit maximization.
> 
> - **Six Core Criteria:** NPV, IRR, PI, Payback, Discounted Payback, ARR.
> 
> - **NPV is the Gold Standard:** It directly measures value addition in monetary terms and is consistent with shareholder wealth maximization.
> 
> - **Decision Rules (must memorize):**
>   - NPV ≥ 0 → Accept
>   - IRR > k → Accept
>   - PI > 1 → Accept
>   - Payback < Target → Accept
>   - ARR > Target Return → Accept
> 
> - **DCF vs. Non-DCF:** KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes DCF methods (NPV, IRR) as they incorporate the time value of money. Non-DCF methods (Payback, ARR) have serious limitations and are generally supplementary.
> 
> - **IRR Limitations:** (i) Multiple IRRs in non-conventional cash flows; (ii) Unrealistic reinvestment assumption; (iii) May conflict with NPV in mutually exclusive projects — **always trust NPV in conflicts**.
> 
> - **PI Use Case:** Most useful under **capital rationing** (limited funds).
> 
> - **Payback Use Case:** Quick liquidity assessment; ignores profitability beyond payback period.
> 
> - **ARR Use Case:** Uses accounting profit (not cash flow) and average book value; favored for ratio analysis and performance reporting.
> 
> - **PVAF Formula to Memorize:** $PVAF = \frac{1 - (1+k)^{-n}}{k}$
> 
> - **Cash Flow vs. Profit:** Capital budgeting uses **cash flows**, not accounting profits. Depreciation is a non-cash item and must be added back to profit to derive cash flow (Indirect method).
> 
> - **Mutually Exclusive vs. Independent Projects:** NPV and PI can give different rankings for mutually exclusive projects. **NPV is the tie-breaker.**
> 
> - **Sunk Costs:** Ignore them in capital budgeting (they are past, irreversible costs).
> 
> - **Opportunity Cost:** Always include the opportunity cost of funds tied up in a project.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conventional and Discounted Cash Flow Methods - Net Present Value (NPV) and Internal Rate of Return (IRR) - Computation of NPV
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET602 - FINANCIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Conventional and Discounted Cash Flow Methods - Net Present Value (NPV) and Internal Rate of Return (IRR) - Computation of NPV

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Capital Budgeting — The Backbone of Engineering Economics

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** treatment of *Financial Engineering*, capital budgeting occupies a central position. Every engineering project — whether it is the installation of a CNC machine, the commissioning of a solar farm, or the launch of a new production line — requires a firm to commit a large lump sum today in anticipation of a stream of cash inflows over several future years. The fundamental question that confronts the financial manager is:

> *"Is this project worth investing in?"*

Two families of techniques have been developed by financial engineers to answer this question:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conventional (Non-Discounted) Cash Flow Methods** ignore the **time value of money (TVM)**. They treat a rupee received today as equal in value to a rupee received ten years from now. Example: **Payback Period** and **Accounting Rate of Return (ARR)**.
>
> **Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Methods** explicitly account for the **time value of money** by converting every future cash flow into its **equivalent present-day value** using a discount rate. Example: **Net Present Value (NPV)** and **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)**.

The **DCF methods** are considered *scientifically superior* and are universally accepted as the gold standard in modern financial engineering practice.

---

## 1.2 Net Present Value (NPV) — The Rigorous Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> The **Net Present Value (NPV)** of a project is the algebraic sum of the present values of all expected cash flows (both inflows and outflows) associated with the project, discounted at the firm's cost of capital (also called the *minimum attractive rate of return*, MARR, or *hurdle rate*).

Mathematically, if a project generates $n$ cash flows $CF_0, CF_1, CF_2, \ldots, CF_n$ and the discount rate is $r$, then:

$$\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$$

The first cash flow $CF_0$ is conventionally **negative**, representing the initial investment outflow.

### 1.2.1 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Time-Machine of Money"

Imagine you have a magical **time machine** that can transport any future rupee back to today. The catch: the further into the future the rupee lies, the more **fuel (interest)** the machine consumes to bring it back. The discount rate $r$ is precisely this fuel cost per year.

- A rupee arriving **1 year from now** needs $(1+r)^1$ units of fuel → present value is $\dfrac{1}{(1+r)^1}$.
- A rupee arriving **5 years from now** needs $(1+r)^5$ units of fuel → present value is $\dfrac{1}{(1+r)^5}$.
- A rupee arriving **20 years from now** needs $(1+r)^{20}$ units of fuel → present value is $\dfrac{1}{(1+r)^{20}}$, which becomes almost negligible.

**NPV** is therefore the **net fuel-corrected value** of the entire project as if every cash flow had happened today. If this net value is **positive**, the project creates wealth; if **negative**, the project destroys wealth.

---

## 1.3 Internal Rate of Return (IRR) — The Break-Even Discount Rate

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> The **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** of a project is the discount rate $r^\*$ at which the Net Present Value of all cash flows equals exactly **zero**. In other words, it is the rate of return that equates the present value of cash inflows with the present value of cash outflows.

The defining equation is:

$$\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r^*)^t} = 0$$

By convention, $r^\* = \text{IRR}$ is the unique positive root of this polynomial equation.

### 1.3.1 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Built-In Rate of Return"

Think of IRR as the **effective interest rate built into the project itself**. If you were to lend the initial investment to the project at the IRR, the project would exactly repay the loan (principal + interest) through its future cash inflows — leaving nothing left over.

- If **IRR > Cost of Capital** → project earns more than the hurdle → **Accept**.
- If **IRR < Cost of Capital** → project earns less than required → **Reject**.

---

## 1.4 Decision Rules at a Glance

| Criterion | Decision Rule | Interpretation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **NPV > 0** | **Accept** the project | Project creates wealth above the cost of capital |
| **NPV = 0** | **Indifferent** (MARGINAL) | Project exactly recovers cost of capital |
| **NPV < 0** | **Reject** the project | Project destroys shareholder wealth |
| **IRR > r** | **Accept** the project | Return earned exceeds the minimum required |
| **IRR = r** | **Indifferent** (MARGINAL) | Return equals the minimum required |
| **IRR < r** | **Reject** the project | Return earned is below the minimum required |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For *independent projects* with conventional cash flows, **NPV and IRR yield identical accept/reject decisions**. For *mutually exclusive projects*, however, they may rank projects differently — a phenomenon known as the **NPV–IRR conflict** or **ranking conflict**, which the KTU 2024 syllabus treats in advanced modules.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Project Cash Flow Stream

A typical engineering capital project exhibits the following cash flow signature — known as a **conventional cash flow pattern**:

- A single large **negative** cash flow at $t = 0$ (the initial capital outlay).
- A sequence of **positive** cash flows at $t = 1, 2, \ldots, n$ (operating profits net of taxes, plus salvage value at the end).

Only **one sign change** occurs (negative → positive). This structure is critical because it guarantees a **unique, real, positive IRR**.

A **non-conventional cash flow stream** has multiple sign changes and can produce multiple IRRs (a problem addressed in Descartes' rule of signs and the Norstrom criterion).

---

## 2.2 The Time Value of Money — Foundation of All DCF Methods

The discount factor for one rupee received at time $t$ in the future, when the per-period discount rate is $r$, is:

$$\text{PV Factor} = \frac{1}{(1+r)^t} = (1+r)^{-t}$$

This single factor is the engine behind every DCF computation in financial engineering. The table below summarizes the standard present-value factors used throughout KTU examinations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet — Present Value Factors**

| Symbol | Name | Formula | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\text{PVIF}(r, n)$ | Present Value Interest Factor (Single Payment) | $(1+r)^{-n}$ | Discount a single lump sum received at end of period $n$ |
| $\text{PVIFA}(r, n)$ | Present Value Interest Factor of an Annuity | $\dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}$ | Discount an equal series of payments for $n$ periods |
| $\text{PVIFG}(r, g, n)$ | Present Value of a Growing Annuity | $\dfrac{1}{r - g}\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{1+g}{1+r}\right)^{n}\right]$ | Discount a series growing at constant rate $g$ |
| $\text{PV}_\infty$ | Present Value of a Perpetuity | $\dfrac{C}{r}$ | Discount an infinite series of equal payments $C$ |

> **CRITICAL TABLE RULE:** Notice how absolute values such as $\vert r-g \vert$ are avoided; we instead write the difference $r-g$ directly inside the formula column to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of NPV Computation

The NPV computation algorithm for a KTU-style problem proceeds as follows:

1. **Identify** the cash flow at each time period $t$, paying careful attention to the sign convention (outflows negative, inflows positive).
2. **Determine** the appropriate discount rate $r$. For a private firm this is typically the **Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)**. For a government project it is the **social discount rate**.
3. **Compute** the discount factor $(1+r)^{-t}$ for every $t$ from $0$ to $n$.
4. **Multiply** each cash flow $CF_t$ by its corresponding discount factor to obtain the **Present Value** $PV_t$.
5. **Sum** all the present values $PV_t$. The result is the **Net Present Value**.
6. **Apply** the decision rule: NPV $\geq 0$ means acceptance; NPV $< 0$ means rejection.

---

## 2.4 Step-by-Step Logic of IRR Computation

Because IRR is defined implicitly by $\text{NPV}(r^\*) = 0$, finding $r^\*$ requires solving a polynomial equation of degree $n$. The standard KTU techniques are:

| Method | When to Use | Algorithm |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Trial and Error (Bracketing)** | Any project | Guess $r$, compute NPV, adjust guess until NPV $\approx 0$. |
| **Linear Interpolation** | Projects with 1 sign change, after two bracketing guesses | $\text{IRR} \approx r_L + \dfrac{\text{NPV}_L}{\text{NPV}_L - \text{NPV}_H}(r_H - r_L)$ |
| **Analytical Formula (Payback ≤ 2 years)** | 2-period or 3-period projects | Direct algebraic solution of the polynomial |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility in Engineering:**
> - **NPV** is used in public-private partnership (PPP) project appraisal for highway and metro projects.
> - **IRR** is the metric preferred by infrastructure finance ministries and World Bank appraisals because it expresses profitability as an intuitive percentage.
> - In **renewable energy project finance**, both NPV and IRR are reported to lenders, who insist on minimum IRR thresholds before sanctioning debt.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the NPV Master Equation

### 3.1.1 Single-Period Case ($n = 1$)

Consider an investment of $I_0$ today that yields $CF_1$ at the end of year 1. The discount rate is $r$. The present value of the future cash flow is what an investor would willingly pay today to receive $CF_1$ next year, given the opportunity cost of capital $r$.

The relationship between present and future value for one period is:

$$FV = PV \cdot (1+r)$$

Solving for $PV$:

$$PV = \frac{FV}{1+r}$$

Substituting the project-specific values:

$$PV_1 = \frac{CF_1}{(1+r)^1}$$

The net present value is the present value of inflows minus the present value of outflows:

$$\text{NPV} = -I_0 + \frac{CF_1}{(1+r)^1}$$

---

### 3.1.2 Two-Period Case ($n = 2$)

Extend to two periods. The cash flow $CF_2$ is two years away, so it must be discounted twice:

$$PV_2 = \frac{CF_2}{(1+r)^2}$$

The total NPV is:

$$\text{NPV} = -I_0 + \frac{CF_1}{(1+r)^1} + \frac{CF_2}{(1+r)^2}$$

---

### 3.1.3 General $n$-Period Case — The Master Equation

By induction, for $n$ periods, every future cash flow is discounted by $(1+r)^t$ for its respective period $t$. Summing all discounted cash flows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV} &= \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} \\
&= CF_0 + \frac{CF_1}{(1+r)^1} + \frac{CF_2}{(1+r)^2} + \cdots + \frac{CF_n}{(1+r)^n}
\end{aligned}
$$

Because $CF_0 = -I_0$, the expansion becomes:

$$\text{NPV} = -I_0 + \frac{CF_1}{(1+r)^1} + \frac{CF_2}{(1+r)^2} + \cdots + \frac{CF_n}{(1+r)^n}$$

This is the **NPV Master Equation** — the single most important formula in financial engineering.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the IRR Equation

The IRR $r^\*$ is defined as the discount rate that sets NPV to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &= \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r^*)^t} \\
\Rightarrow \quad I_0 &= \frac{CF_1}{(1+r^*)^1} + \frac{CF_2}{(1+r^*)^2} + \cdots + \frac{CF_n}{(1+r^*)^n}
\end{aligned}
$$

In words: at the IRR, the **present value of future inflows exactly equals the initial outflow**.

---

## 3.3 Linear Interpolation Formula — A KTU Favourite

When direct algebraic solution is impossible (which is the case for $n \geq 3$), KTU examiners expect the **linear interpolation** technique. Suppose two discount rates $r_L$ (low) and $r_H$ (high) are chosen such that:

- $\text{NPV}(r_L) > 0$ (positive NPV at low rate)
- $\text{NPV}(r_H) < 0$ (negative NPV at high rate)

The IRR is approximated as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{IRR} &\approx r_L + \frac{\text{NPV}(r_L)}{\text{NPV}(r_L) - \text{NPV}(r_H)} \cdot (r_H - r_L)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Derivation:** Assume NPV varies linearly between $r_L$ and $r_H$. The straight line from $(r_L, \text{NPV}_L)$ to $(r_H, \text{NPV}_H)$ crosses zero at:

$$
r^* = r_L + \frac{0 - \text{NPV}_L}{\text{NPV}_H - \text{NPV}_L}(r_H - r_L)
$$

Simplifying:

$$r^* = r_L + \frac{\text{NPV}_L}{\text{NPV}_L - \text{NPV}_H}(r_H - r_L)$$

This is the linear interpolation formula.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — NPV Computation (Model Problem)

> **Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
> A firm is evaluating a proposal to purchase a CNC machine. The machine costs **₹ 5,00,000** today. It is expected to generate net cash inflows of **₹ 1,80,000** per year for **4 years**. At the end of year 4, the machine has a salvage value of **₹ 50,000**. The firm's cost of capital is **12%** per annum. Compute the NPV and advise whether to accept the project.

### Step 1 — Tabulate the Cash Flows

| Year $t$ | Cash Flow $CF_t$ (₹) | Type |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0 | $-5,00,000$ | Initial Outflow |
| 1 | $+1,80,000$ | Operating Inflow |
| 2 | $+1,80,000$ | Operating Inflow |
| 3 | $+1,80,000$ | Operating Inflow |
| 4 | $+1,80,000 + 50,000 = +2,30,000$ | Operating + Salvage |

### Step 2 — Compute the Discount Factors at 12%

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.12)^0 &= 1.0000 \\
(1.12)^1 &= 1.1200 \\
(1.12)^2 &= 1.2544 \\
(1.12)^3 &= 1.4049 \\
(1.12)^4 &= 1.5735
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Present Value of Each Cash Flow

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_0 &= \frac{-5,00,000}{1.0000} = -5,00,000.00 \\
PV_1 &= \frac{+1,80,000}{1.1200} = +1,60,714.29 \\
PV_2 &= \frac{+1,80,000}{1.2544} = +1,43,494.90 \\
PV_3 &= \frac{+1,80,000}{1.4049} = +1,28,120.44 \\
PV_4 &= \frac{+2,30,000}{1.5735} = +1,46,171.27
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Sum All Present Values to Obtain NPV

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV} &= -5,00,000.00 + 1,60,714.29 + 1,43,494.90 + 1,28,120.44 + 1,46,171.27 \\
&= +78,500.90 \;\; \text{(approximately ₹ 78,501)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Decision

Because **NPV = ₹ 78,501 > 0**, the project **adds wealth** to the firm and **should be accepted**.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — IRR Computation Using Linear Interpolation

Using the same project, let us find the IRR by linear interpolation.

### Step 1 — Try $r_L = 15\%$

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.15)^0 &= 1.0000 \\
(1.15)^1 &= 1.1500 \\
(1.15)^2 &= 1.3225 \\
(1.15)^3 &= 1.5209 \\
(1.15)^4 &= 1.7490
\end{aligned}
$$

Present values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_0 &= -5,00,000.00 \\
PV_1 &= 1,80,000 / 1.1500 = 1,56,521.74 \\
PV_2 &= 1,80,000 / 1.3225 = 1,36,105.86 \\
PV_3 &= 1,80,000 / 1.5209 = 1,18,352.05 \\
PV_4 &= 2,30,000 / 1.7490 = 1,31,503.72
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV}(15\%) &= -5,00,000 + 1,56,521.74 + 1,36,105.86 + 1,18,352.05 + 1,31,503.72 \\
&= +42,483.37
\end{aligned}
$$

Positive → IRR lies above 15%.

### Step 2 — Try $r_H = 18\%$

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.18)^1 &= 1.1800 \\
(1.18)^2 &= 1.3924 \\
(1.18)^3 &= 1.6430 \\
(1.18)^4 &= 1.9388
\end{aligned}
$$

Present values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_1 &= 1,80,000 / 1.1800 = 1,52,542.37 \\
PV_2 &= 1,80,000 / 1.3924 = 1,29,272.34 \\
PV_3 &= 1,80,000 / 1.6430 = 1,09,552.84 \\
PV_4 &= 2,30,000 / 1.9388 = 1,18,629.04
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV}(18\%) &= -5,00,000 + 1,52,542.37 + 1,29,272.34 + 1,09,552.84 + 1,18,629.04 \\
&= +9,996.59
\end{aligned}
$$

Still positive → IRR is above 18%.

### Step 3 — Try $r_H = 19\%$

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.19)^1 &= 1.1900 \\
(1.19)^2 &= 1.4161 \\
(1.19)^3 &= 1.6852 \\
(1.19)^4 &= 2.0053
\end{aligned}
$$

Present values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_1 &= 1,80,000 / 1.1900 = 1,51,260.50 \\
PV_2 &= 1,80,000 / 1.4161 = 1,27,109.66 \\
PV_3 &= 1,80,000 / 1.6852 = 1,06,814.00 \\
PV_4 &= 2,30,000 / 2.0053 = 1,14,694.31
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV}(19\%) &= -5,00,000 + 1,51,260.50 + 1,27,109.66 + 1,06,814.00 + 1,14,694.31 \\
&= -121.53 \;\; (\text{approximately } 0)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Linear Interpolation Between $r_L = 18\%$ and $r_H = 19\%$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{IRR} &\approx 18\% + \frac{\text{NPV}(18\%)}{\text{NPV}(18\%) - \text{NPV}(19\%)} \cdot (19\% - 18\%) \\
&\approx 18\% + \frac{+9,996.59}{9,996.59 - (-121.53)} \cdot 1\% \\
&\approx 18\% + \frac{9,996.59}{10,118.12} \cdot 1\% \\
&\approx 18\% + 0.988\% \\
&\approx 18.99\% \;\; \approx \; \mathbf{19\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Decision

Because **IRR $\approx 19\%$ > Cost of Capital = 12%**, the project **should be accepted**.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Decision Comparison

| Parameter | Value at $r = 12\%$ | Value at $r = 19\%$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| NPV | ₹ 78,501 (positive) | ≈ ₹ 0 |
| Project Verdict | **Accept** | **Indifferent** |
| Interpretation | Wealth created above 12% hurdle | Break-even rate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** The project earns an effective return of **19% per year** on the initial investment. Since this is **7 percentage points higher** than the 12% cost of capital, the project is highly attractive from a financial-engineering perspective.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cash Flow Timeline — The Conventional Project Pattern

The following Mermaid timeline captures the structure of a conventional engineering project cash flow stream, which is the canonical input for both NPV and IRR computations.

```mermaid
graph LR
    t0[T0: Initial Outlay - Negative CF0]
    t1[T1: Operating Inflow Positive CF1]
    t2[T2: Operating Inflow Positive CF2]
    t3[T3: Operating Inflow Positive CF3]
    tn[T n: Operating Inflow plus Salvage Positive CFn]
    t0 -->|Discount by 1 plus r to power 0| t1
    t1 -->|Discount by 1 plus r to power 1| t2
    t2 -->|Discount by 1 plus r to power 2| t3
    t3 -->|Discount by 1 plus r to power n minus 1| tn
```

---

## 4.2 NPV Computation Engine — Functional Architecture

This block diagram models the **computational pipeline** used by a financial engineer when evaluating a project using NPV.

```mermaid
graph TD
    inputA[Project Cash Flow Vector CF0 to CFn]
    inputB[Cost of Capital r]
    process1[Generate Discount Factors 1 plus r to power minus t]
    process2[Multiply CFt by Discount Factor]
    process3[Sum All Present Values]
    outputA[Output: Net Present Value NPV]
    decision{NPV greater than or equal to 0?}
    resultA[Accept Project - Creates Wealth]
    resultB[Reject Project - Destroys Wealth]
    inputA --> process2
    inputB --> process1
    process1 --> process2
    process2 --> process3
    process3 --> outputA
    outputA --> decision
    decision -->|Yes| resultA
    decision -->|No| resultB
```

---

## 4.3 IRR Search Algorithm — Bracketing and Bisection

The IRR is found by iteratively searching for the discount rate that makes NPV zero. The following flowchart captures the **bisection search loop** used in professional financial calculators.

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Start with Project Cash Flows]
    guess1[Guess Lower Rate rL]
    guess2[Guess Higher Rate rH]
    npvL[Compute NPV at rL]
    npvH[Compute NPV at rH]
    checkL{NPV at rL greater than 0?}
    checkH{NPV at rH less than 0?}
    interp[Apply Linear Interpolation Formula]
    refine[New Estimate rStar]
    converge{Absolute NPV at rStar less than Tolerance?}
    output[Output: IRR equals rStar]
    adjustL[Increase rL]
    adjustH[Decrease rH]
    start --> guess1
    start --> guess2
    guess1 --> npvL
    guess2 --> npvH
    npvL --> checkL
    npvH --> checkH
    checkL -->|Yes| interp
    checkL -->|No| adjustL
    checkH -->|Yes| interp
    checkH -->|No| adjustH
    adjustL --> guess1
    adjustH --> guess2
    interp --> refine
    refine --> converge
    converge -->|Yes| output
    converge -->|No| refine
```

---

## 4.4 NPV Profile — The Visual Map of NPV versus Discount Rate

The **NPV profile** is the most powerful visual tool in financial engineering. It plots NPV on the vertical axis against the discount rate on the horizontal axis. The point where the curve crosses the horizontal axis is the **IRR**.

```mermaid
graph LR
    axisY[Vertical Axis: NPV in Lakhs of Rupees]
    axisX[Horizontal Axis: Discount Rate r in Percent]
    curve[NPV Curve: Downward Sloping from Left to Right]
    irPoint[Intersection Point on X axis: This is the IRR]
    acceptRegion[Region to Left of IRR: NPV greater than 0, Accept]
    rejectRegion[Region to Right of IRR: NPV less than 0, Reject]
    axisY --> curve
    axisX --> curve
    curve --> irPoint
    irPoint --> acceptRegion
    irPoint --> rejectRegion
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The downward slope of the NPV curve reflects an inescapable truth — *the higher the discount rate demanded by the market, the lower the present value of future cash flows, and therefore the lower the NPV*. The IRR is the unique rate at which the curve kisses the horizontal axis.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Define Net Present Value (NPV). State the decision criterion based on NPV.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Net Present Value (NPV)** of a project is the algebraic sum of the present values of all expected cash flows associated with the project, discounted at the firm's cost of capital. Mathematically:

$$\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$$

**Decision Criterion:** A project should be **accepted** if its NPV is **positive (NPV > 0)**, indicating that the project generates value greater than the cost of capital. A project should be **rejected** if NPV is **negative (NPV < 0)**. If **NPV = 0**, the firm is indifferent — the project exactly recovers its cost of capital but creates no additional wealth.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Distinguish between Conventional and Discounted Cash Flow methods of capital budgeting.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Conventional (Non-Discounted) Methods | Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Methods |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Time Value of Money** | Ignored | Explicitly considered |
| **Discount Rate** | Not used | Required |
| **Examples** | Payback Period, Accounting Rate of Return | Net Present Value, Internal Rate of Return, Profitability Index |
| **Theoretical Soundness** | Weak — biased towards short-term projects | Strong — theoretically preferred |
| **Computational Effort** | Simple | More complex (requires discounting) |
| **Decision Reliability** | Approximate | Highly reliable |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part B questions carry an **internal choice**. Students must answer **either Question A or Question B**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of Net Present Value. Discuss its merits and limitations as a tool for project evaluation.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A company is considering two mutually exclusive projects, X and Y, with the following cash flows. The cost of capital is **10% per annum**. Compute the NPV of each project and advise which project should be selected.
>
> | Year | Project X (₹) | Project Y (₹) |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | 0 | $-50,000$ | $-50,000$ |
> | 1 | $+15,000$ | $+5,000$ |
> | 2 | $+20,000$ | $+15,000$ |
> | 3 | $+25,000$ | $+25,000$ |
> | 4 | $+15,000$ | $+30,000$ |

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Concept of NPV (7 Marks)

**Definition:** NPV is the difference between the present value of cash inflows and the present value of cash outflows over the life of a project, discounted at the cost of capital.

$$\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$$

**Merits of NPV:**
1. Considers the **time value of money** explicitly — every cash flow is converted to present value.
2. Uses **all cash flows** over the entire project life, not just those until payback.
3. Provides a **direct measure of wealth creation** in monetary units (rupees, dollars).
4. Aligns with the **objective of shareholder wealth maximization**.
5. The discount rate can be **risk-adjusted**, allowing incorporation of project-specific risk.

**Limitations of NPV:**
1. Requires an **accurate estimate of the discount rate**, which is difficult in practice.
2. Involves **estimation of future cash flows**, which are inherently uncertain.
3. **Not easily comparable** across projects of vastly different scales (Profitability Index resolves this).
4. The result is an **absolute monetary value**, not a percentage rate, making inter-firm comparison awkward.
5. **Ignores project flexibility** — the option to abandon, expand, or defer is not captured (Real Options resolve this).

#### Part (b) — NPV Computation (7 Marks)

**Discount factors at 10%:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.10)^0 &= 1.0000 \\
(1.10)^1 &= 1.1000 \\
(1.10)^2 &= 1.2100 \\
(1.10)^3 &= 1.3310 \\
(1.10)^4 &= 1.4641
\end{aligned}
$$

**NPV of Project X:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV}_X &= -50,000 + \frac{15,000}{1.10} + \frac{20,000}{1.21} + \frac{25,000}{1.331} + \frac{15,000}{1.4641} \\
&= -50,000 + 13,636.36 + 16,528.93 + 18,782.87 + 10,245.40 \\
&= \mathbf{₹ \; 9,193.56}
\end{aligned}
$$

**NPV of Project Y:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV}_Y &= -50,000 + \frac{5,000}{1.10} + \frac{15,000}{1.21} + \frac{25,000}{1.331} + \frac{30,000}{1.4641} \\
&= -50,000 + 4,545.45 + 12,396.69 + 18,782.87 + 20,490.79 \\
&= \mathbf{₹ \; 6,215.80}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:** Both projects have **positive NPV**, so both are individually acceptable. However, since the projects are **mutually exclusive**, select **Project X**, which has the **higher NPV (₹ 9,193.56 > ₹ 6,215.80)**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Define Internal Rate of Return (IRR). Explain the steps to compute IRR using the linear interpolation method. What are the limitations of IRR?
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A project requires an initial investment of **₹ 1,00,000** and generates annual cash inflows of **₹ 30,000** for **5 years**. Compute the IRR of the project using linear interpolation. Take trial rates of **15% and 18%**.

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Concept of IRR (7 Marks)

**Definition:** The Internal Rate of Return (IRR) is the discount rate at which the Net Present Value of a project equals zero. It is the rate of return that the project is expected to earn.

**Steps to compute IRR using linear interpolation:**

1. Choose two trial discount rates $r_L$ (low) and $r_H$ (high) such that $\text{NPV}(r_L) > 0$ and $\text{NPV}(r_H) < 0$.
2. Compute $\text{NPV}(r_L)$ and $\text{NPV}(r_H)$ using the standard NPV formula.
3. Apply the linear interpolation formula:

$$\text{IRR} \approx r_L + \frac{\text{NPV}(r_L)}{\text{NPV}(r_L) - \text{NPV}(r_H)} \cdot (r_H - r_L)$$

4. The result is an approximation; for higher accuracy, refine the trial rates and re-apply.

**Limitations of IRR:**
1. **Multiple IRRs** can arise for non-conventional cash flows (multiple sign changes), making interpretation ambiguous.
2. **No Reinvestment Assumption Clarity:** IRR implicitly assumes reinvestment at the IRR itself, which may be unrealistic.
