3. **Scale Problem:** IRR does not consider the size of the investment; a small project with high IRR may add less wealth than a large project with lower IRR.
4. **Computationally intensive** — requires iterative trial-and-error or numerical methods.
5. **Cannot be used** when cash flows change sign multiple times.

#### Part (b) — IRR Computation (7 Marks)

**Cash Flows:** $CF_0 = -1,00,000$; $CF_1 = CF_2 = CF_3 = CF_4 = CF_5 = +30,000$.

**NPV at $r_L = 15\%$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV}(15\%) &= -1,00,000 + 30,000 \cdot \text{PVIFA}(15\%, 5) \\
&= -1,00,000 + 30,000 \cdot \frac{1 - (1.15)^{-5}}{0.15}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $(1.15)^5 = 2.0114$. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PVIFA}(15\%, 5) &= \frac{1 - 1/2.0114}{0.15} = \frac{1 - 0.4972}{0.15} = \frac{0.5028}{0.15} = 3.3522 \\
\text{NPV}(15\%) &= -1,00,000 + 30,000 \cdot 3.3522 = -1,00,000 + 1,00,566 = +566
\end{aligned}
$$

**NPV at $r_H = 18\%$:**

$(1.18)^5 = 2.2878$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PVIFA}(18\%, 5) &= \frac{1 - 1/2.2878}{0.18} = \frac{1 - 0.4371}{0.18} = \frac{0.5629}{0.18} = 3.1272 \\
\text{NPV}(18\%) &= -1,00,000 + 30,000 \cdot 3.1272 = -1,00,000 + 93,816 = -6,184
\end{aligned}
$$

**Linear Interpolation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{IRR} &\approx 15\% + \frac{566}{566 - (-6,184)} \cdot (18\% - 15\%) \\
&\approx 15\% + \frac{566}{6,750} \cdot 3\% \\
&\approx 15\% + 0.2515\% \\
&\approx \mathbf{15.25\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The project earns approximately **15.25% per year**. If the firm's cost of capital is less than 15.25%, the project should be accepted.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 1 — Sign Convention Errors:** Examiners frequently deduct **2 to 3 marks** when students forget to enter $CF_0$ as a negative number. **Always** show $-I_0$ explicitly in the cash flow table.
>
> **Pitfall 2 — Confusing PVIF and PVIFA:** Students often use the annuity factor when a single lump sum is given, or vice versa. Read the question carefully: a phrase like *"equal annual cash flows"* signals an annuity.
>
> **Pitfall 3 — Rounding in Intermediate Steps:** KTU examiners expect calculations to at least **4 decimal places** in discount factors. Premature rounding compounds errors and can flip a borderline NPV from positive to negative.
>
> **Pitfall 4 — Forgetting Salvage Value:** When a project ends with a salvage value, it must be added to the final year's cash flow, not treated separately.
>
> **Pitfall 5 — Misapplication of IRR Decision Rule:** IRR must be compared with the **cost of capital**, not with the inflation rate or the bank deposit rate.
>
> **Pitfall 6 — Skipping the Conclusion:** Always end with a **clear accept/reject statement** linked to the computed numerical result.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **NPV** = sum of present values of all cash flows discounted at cost of capital. Master equation: $\sum \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$.
- **Decision Rule:** NPV $> 0$ → Accept; NPV $= 0$ → Indifferent; NPV $< 0$ → Reject.
- **NPV is the theoretically superior method** because it directly measures wealth creation in monetary units.
- **IRR** = discount rate at which NPV = 0. Decision Rule: IRR $> r$ → Accept; IRR $< r$ → Reject.
- **Conventional cash flow pattern:** one sign change (negative at $t=0$, positive thereafter). Guarantees unique IRR.
- **Non-conventional cash flow:** multiple sign changes; may yield multiple IRRs (Descartes' rule).
- **Discount factor** for year $t$: $(1+r)^{-t}$.
- **PVIF** (single sum) = $(1+r)^{-n}$; **PVIFA** (annuity) = $\dfrac{1-(1+r)^{-n}}{r}$.
- **Linear interpolation formula:** $\text{IRR} \approx r_L + \dfrac{\text{NPV}_L}{\text{NPV}_L - \text{NPV}_H}(r_H - r_L)$.
- For **mutually exclusive projects**, use NPV (not IRR) to break ties.
- **Salvage value** is added to the final year's operating cash flow.
- **Cost of capital** = the firm's hurdle rate (WACC for private firms, social discount rate for government projects).
- **Discounted Cash Flow methods** are preferred over Conventional methods because they respect the **time value of money**.
- Always **tabulate cash flows** before discounting — this prevents sign errors.
- Always **state the decision** explicitly at the end of the solution.
- For **independent projects** with conventional cash flows, NPV and IRR give identical accept/reject decisions.
- For projects of **unequal lives**, use the **Equivalent Annual Annuity (EAA)** or **Common Multiple of Lives** method (advanced module).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Benefit Cost Ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET602 - FINANCIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Benefit Cost Ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)** is a capital budgeting evaluation metric that expresses the relationship between the **Present Value (PV) of Benefits** (positive cash inflows/revenues) and the **Present Value of Costs** (negative cash outflows/expenditures) of a project, discounted at the **Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR)**.

Formally, it is stated as:

$$\text{BCR} = \frac{\text{Present Value of Benefits (PVB)}}{\text{Present Value of Costs (PVC)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology:** The BCR method is also referred to as the **Prospective Method** of capital budgeting when applied to single-project acceptance. It is part of Module 3 along with NPV, IRR, and Incremental Analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule (KTU Board Standard):**
> - If **BCR > 1** → The project is **economically acceptable** (benefits exceed costs).
> - If **BCR = 1** → The project is at the **break-even point** (indifference).
> - If **BCR < 1** → The project is **economically rejected** (costs exceed benefits).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are opening a small tea stall. You invest **₹1,00,000** today (cost). Over the next 3 years, you expect to earn revenues of **₹40,000**, **₹50,000**, and **₹60,000**. The question is: *"For every ₹1 I spend, how many rupees do I get back?"*

That ratio — **rupees earned per rupee spent** — is precisely the **Benefit-Cost Ratio**.

- If your BCR is **1.25**, it means **for every ₹1 invested, you get back ₹1.25** → a profit of ₹0.25 per rupee.
- If your BCR is **0.85**, it means **for every ₹1 invested, you only get ₹0.85 back** → a loss of ₹0.15 per rupee.

The BCR simply answers: **"Is the project worth more than it costs, when measured in today's money?"**

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Capital Budgeting

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | Decision Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Minimum Attractive Rate of Return** | **MARR** | **8%–15%** | Discount rate benchmark |
| **Conventional BCR** | **BCR** | **0 to 2** | **≥ 1** to accept |
| **Modified BCR** | **MBCR** | **0 to 2** | **≥ 1** to accept |
| **Incremental BCR** | **ΔBCR** | **0 to 2** | **≥ 1** (with MARR test) |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** *"Benefit over Cost, the project is tossed — if it's 1, you're done!"*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

The BCR method follows a strict 5-step procedure:

1. **Identify All Cash Flows** — Both inflows (revenues, savings, salvage) and outflows (initial investment, O&M costs, working capital).
2. **Determine the Discount Rate** — Use **MARR** (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return) given in the problem statement.
3. **Discount All Cash Flows to Present Value** — Use the formula:
   $$PV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t}$$
4. **Separate PV of Benefits (PVB) from PV of Costs (PVC)**:
   - *PVB* = Sum of PV of all **positive** cash flows.
   - *PVC* = Sum of PV of all **negative** cash flows (usually taken as a positive number for the ratio).
5. **Compute and Compare**:
   $$BCR = \frac{PVB}{PVC}$$

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **must-memorize** KTU Board summary. Avoid using the `|` symbol in formulas within tables.

| # | Formula | Description | Decision Criterion |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\displaystyle BCR = \frac{PVB}{PVC}$ | Conventional BCR | $BCR \geq 1$ → Accept |
| 2 | $\displaystyle PVC = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{\text{Cost}_t}{(1+i)^t}$ | PV of all cost streams | Always positive value |
| 3 | $\displaystyle PVB = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{\text{Benefit}_t}{(1+i)^t}$ | PV of all benefit streams | Always positive value |
| 4 | $\displaystyle MBCR = \frac{\sum PV(\text{+ve cash flows})}{\vert \sum PV(\text{−ve cash flows}) \vert}$ | Modified BCR (for unconventional cash flows) | $MBCR \geq 1$ → Accept |
| 5 | $\displaystyle \Delta BCR = \frac{\Delta PVB}{\Delta PVC}$ | Incremental BCR (for mutually exclusive alternatives) | $\Delta BCR \geq 1$ → Choose higher-cost alt. |
| 6 | $\displaystyle NPW = PVB - PVC$ | Net Present Worth (equivalent to BCR test) | $NPW \geq 0$ → Accept |
| 7 | $\displaystyle (1+i)^{-t} = \text{Discount Factor}$ | Single-payment present worth factor | Used for non-uniform flows |

### 2.3 Types of BCR — The "Why" Behind Each

#### (a) Conventional BCR
- Used when cash flows have **only ONE sign change** (typical investment: initial outflow followed by inflows).
- Simple ratio of PV of benefits over PV of costs.

#### (b) Modified BCR (MBCR)
- Used for **unconventional cash flows** with **multiple sign changes** (e.g., a toll road needing resurfacing every 5 years).
- The denominator is the **absolute value** of PV of all negative cash flows.
- Critical for KTU problems involving mining projects, infrastructure with periodic overhauls.

#### (c) Incremental BCR (ΔBCR)
- Used for **mutually exclusive alternatives** where one must be chosen (e.g., Choose between Machine A or Machine B).
- Computed by ranking alternatives by initial cost (lowest first) and comparing **incremental** benefits to **incremental** costs.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Civil Engineering:** Evaluating dam, highway, and bridge construction projects.
- **Public Sector:** Government uses BCR for **Cost-Benefit Analysis (CBA)** of public welfare schemes.
- **Energy Sector:** Comparing solar vs. wind power plant investments.
- **Environmental Engineering:** Assessing the economic value of pollution-control equipment.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Conventional BCR (Single Project)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A company is considering a small manufacturing project with the following data:
> - **Initial Investment:** ₹5,00,000 (Year 0)
> - **Annual Net Benefit (Years 1–5):** ₹1,50,000
> - **Salvage Value (Year 5):** ₹50,000
> - **MARR:** 10% per annum
>
> Using the BCR method, determine whether the project should be accepted.

#### Step 1 — Identify Cash Flows

| Year | Cash Flow (₹) | Type |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | −5,00,000 | Cost (outflow) |
| 1 | +1,50,000 | Benefit |
| 2 | +1,50,000 | Benefit |
| 3 | +1,50,000 | Benefit |
| 4 | +1,50,000 | Benefit |
| 5 | +1,50,000 + 50,000 = +2,00,000 | Benefit + Salvage |

#### Step 2 — Determine Discount Rate
$i = 10\% = 0.10$

#### Step 3 — Compute Present Value of Benefits (PVB)

The benefits are an **annuity of ₹1,50,000** for 5 years **plus** a salvage of ₹50,000 in year 5.

**Annuity PV factor for n=5, i=10%:**

$$\left(\frac{P}{A}, 10\%, 5\right) = \frac{(1+0.10)^5 - 1}{0.10 \times (1+0.10)^5} = \frac{1.61051 - 1}{0.10 \times 1.61051} = \frac{0.61051}{0.161051}$$

$$\left(\frac{P}{A}, 10\%, 5\right) = 3.7908$$

**Single-payment PV factor for year 5 at 10%:**

$$\left(\frac{P}{F}, 10\%, 5\right) = \frac{1}{(1.10)^5} = \frac{1}{1.61051} = 0.6209$$

**PV of Annuity Benefits:**

$$PV_{\text{annuity}} = 1{,}50{,}000 \times 3.7908 = 5{,}68{,}620$$

**PV of Salvage Value:**

$$PV_{\text{salvage}} = 50{,}000 \times 0.6209 = 31{,}045$$

**Total PVB:**

$$PVB = 5{,}68{,}620 + 31{,}045 = 5{,}99{,}665$$

#### Step 4 — Compute Present Value of Costs (PVC)

Only the initial investment is the cost (it occurs at Year 0, so no discount):

$$PVC = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 1.0 = 5{,}00{,}000$$

#### Step 5 — Compute BCR

$$BCR = \frac{PVB}{PVC} = \frac{5{,}99{,}665}{5{,}00{,}000} = 1.1993$$

#### Step 6 — Decision

Since **BCR = 1.1993 > 1**, the project is **ACCEPTED**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-verification using NPW:** $NPW = PVB - PVC = 5,99,665 - 5,00,000 = +₹99,665 > 0$ → Confirms acceptance. This is the **KTU Board standard cross-check pattern**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Modified BCR (Unconventional Cash Flow)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A municipal corporation is evaluating a bridge project. Cash flows are:
> - **Year 0:** −₹10,00,000 (Construction)
> - **Years 1–4:** +₹3,00,000 (Toll revenue)
> - **Year 5:** −₹2,00,000 (Major resurfacing)
> - **Years 6–10:** +₹4,00,000 (Toll revenue continues)
> - **MARR:** 12%
>
> Apply the Modified BCR method.

#### Step 1 — Identify PV of Positive Cash Flows (PV of Benefits)

Discount each positive cash flow using $(1.12)^{-t}$:

| Year | Cash Inflow (₹) | Discount Factor @ 12% | PV (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3,00,000 | 0.8929 | 2,67,870 |
| 2 | 3,00,000 | 0.7972 | 2,39,160 |
| 3 | 3,00,000 | 0.7118 | 2,13,540 |
| 4 | 3,00,000 | 0.6355 | 1,90,650 |
| 6 | 4,00,000 | 0.5066 | 2,02,640 |
| 7 | 4,00,000 | 0.4523 | 1,80,920 |
| 8 | 4,00,000 | 0.4039 | 1,61,560 |
| 9 | 4,00,000 | 0.3606 | 1,44,240 |
| 10 | 4,00,000 | 0.3220 | 1,28,800 |

**Total PV of Benefits:**

$$PVB = \sum = 17{,}29{,}380$$

#### Step 2 — Identify PV of Negative Cash Flows (PV of Costs)

| Year | Cash Outflow (₹) | Discount Factor @ 12% | PV (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 10,00,000 | 1.0000 | 10,00,000 |
| 5 | 2,00,000 | 0.5674 | 1,13,480 |

**Total PV of Costs:**

$$PVC = 10{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}13{,}480 = 11{,}13{,}480$$

#### Step 3 — Compute Modified BCR

$$MBCR = \frac{PVB}{\vert PVC \vert} = \frac{17{,}29{,}380}{11{,}13{,}480} = 1.553$$

#### Step 4 — Decision

Since **MBCR = 1.553 > 1**, the bridge project is **ACCEPTED**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** In unconventional cash flows, students compute **Conventional BCR** by mixing positive and negative flows in numerator/denominator without sign separation. This produces the **wrong answer** and full 14-mark deductions are awarded.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Incremental BCR (Mutually Exclusive Alternatives)

> **Problem Statement:**
> Two machines are available:
> - **Machine A:** Initial Cost = ₹2,00,000; Annual Net Benefit = ₹80,000; Life = 5 years
> - **Machine B:** Initial Cost = ₹3,50,000; Annual Net Benefit = ₹1,20,000; Life = 5 years
> - **MARR:** 10%
>
> Use Incremental BCR analysis to choose.

#### Step 1 — Compute BCR of Lower-Cost Alternative First (Machine A)

PV of benefits for A:
$$PVB_A = 80{,}000 \times 3.7908 = 3{,}03{,}264$$
$$PVC_A = 2{,}00{,}000$$

$$BCR_A = \frac{3{,}03{,}264}{2{,}00{,}000} = 1.516 > 1 \quad \Rightarrow \text{A is acceptable vs Do-Nothing}$$

#### Step 2 — Compute BCR of Higher-Cost Alternative (Machine B)

PV of benefits for B:
$$PVB_B = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 3.7908 = 4{,}54{,}896$$
$$PVC_B = 3{,}50{,}000$$

$$BCR_B = \frac{4{,}54{,}896}{3{,}50{,}000} = 1.300 > 1 \quad \Rightarrow \text{B is also acceptable vs Do-Nothing}$$

#### Step 3 — Incremental Analysis (B − A)

$$\Delta PVB = 4{,}54{,}896 - 3{,}03{,}264 = 1{,}51{,}632$$

$$\Delta PVC = 3{,}50{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}50{,}000$$

$$\Delta BCR = \frac{\Delta PVB}{\Delta PVC} = \frac{1{,}51{,}632}{1{,}50{,}000} = 1.011$$

#### Step 4 — Decision

Since **ΔBCR = 1.011 > 1**, the **incremental investment in B is justified**. **Choose Machine B**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** When ΔBCR < 1, the higher-cost alternative is rejected and the lower-cost one is selected. Always rank by initial cost **before** doing incremental analysis.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Scenario | Method to Use | Formula | Accept if |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single project, one sign change | Conventional BCR | $\frac{PVB}{PVC}$ | $\geq 1$ |
| Single project, multiple sign changes | Modified BCR | $\frac{\sum PV(+)}{\vert \sum PV(-) \vert}$ | $\geq 1$ |
| Mutually exclusive alternatives | Incremental BCR | $\frac{\Delta PVB}{\Delta PVC}$ | $\geq 1$ |
| Independent projects with capital rationing | BCR + Profitability Index | Rank by BCR | Highest BCR first |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 BCR Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Project Cash Flows] --> B[Determine MARR Discount Rate]
    B --> C[Discount All Cash Flows to Present Value]
    C --> D{Cash Flow Pattern}
    D -->|One Sign Change| E[Conventional BCR Calculation]
    D -->|Multiple Sign Changes| F[Modified BCR Calculation]
    E --> G[Compute BCR = PVB divided by PVC]
    F --> H[Compute MBCR = PV Positive divided by Absolute PV Negative]
    G --> I{BCR greater than or equal to 1}
    H --> J{MBCR greater than or equal to 1}
    I -->|Yes| K[Accept the Project]
    I -->|No| L[Reject the Project]
    J -->|Yes| K
    J -->|No| L
    K --> M{Mutually Exclusive Alternatives}
    M -->|Yes| N[Perform Incremental BCR Analysis]
    M -->|No| O[Final Decision: ACCEPT]
    N --> P{Incremental BCR greater than or equal to 1}
    P -->|Yes| Q[Choose Higher Cost Alternative]
    P -->|No| R[Choose Lower Cost Alternative]
```

### 4.2 BCR Type Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Capital Budgeting Decision] --> B{Are Alternatives Mutually Exclusive}
    B -->|No, Single Project| C{Single Sign Change}
    B -->|Yes, Multiple Alts| D[Rank by Initial Cost]
    C -->|Yes| E[Conventional BCR]
    C -->|No| F[Modified BCR]
    D --> G[Compute BCR for Each]
    G --> H{Both BCR greater than 1}
    H -->|Only Lower One| I[Select Lower Cost Alternative]
    H -->|Both Pass| J[Incremental BCR Analysis]
    H -->|None Pass| K[Reject All, Do Nothing]
    J --> L{Delta BCR greater than 1}
    L -->|Yes| M[Select Higher Cost Alternative]
    L -->|No| I
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of BCR Evaluation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT_BLOCK
        inputA[Initial Investment]
        inputB[Annual Cash Flows]
        inputC[Salvage Value]
        inputD[MARR Discount Rate]
        inputE[Project Life in Years]
    end

    subgraph PROCESSING_BLOCK
        procA[Discount Factor Calculator]
        procB[Present Value Summation Engine]
        procC[Sign Change Detector]
        procD[Ratio Computation Module]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT_BLOCK
        outA[Conventional BCR Output]
        outB[Modified BCR Output]
        outC[Incremental BCR Output]
        outD[Accept Reject Decision Flag]
    end

    inputA --> procB
    inputB --> procC
    inputC --> procB
    inputD --> procA
    inputE --> procA
    procA --> procB
    procC --> procD
    procB --> procD
    procD --> outA
    procD --> outB
    procD --> outC
    procD --> outD
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Always draw such a flowchart in the first 2 minutes of a 14-mark BCR question to demonstrate structured thinking — KTU examiners award **bonus conceptual clarity marks** for this.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

*Define the Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR) method. State the decision rule for accepting a project using BCR.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)** is a capital budgeting technique that compares the present value of benefits to the present value of costs of a project, both discounted at the Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR).
>
> $$BCR = \frac{PVB}{PVC}$$
>
> **Decision Rule:**
> - If BCR > 1 → **Accept** the project.
> - If BCR = 1 → **Indifference point**.
> - If BCR < 1 → **Reject** the project.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

*Distinguish between Conventional BCR and Modified BCR. When is Modified BCR preferred?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Conventional BCR | Modified BCR |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | $\frac{PVB}{PVC}$ for total flows | $\frac{\sum PV(\text{positive})}{\vert \sum PV(\text{negative}) \vert}$ |
| Cash Flow Pattern | Single sign change | Multiple sign changes |
| Numerator | All positive PVs | All positive PVs |
| Denominator | All negative PVs (as positive) | Absolute value of all negative PVs |
| **When Preferred** | Simple investment projects | Unconventional cash flows (mining, toll roads) |

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students must answer **ONE** full question from the choice provided.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A — Conventional BCR + Incremental Analysis [14 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

A company is evaluating two mutually exclusive machines for a production line. The data are given below:

| Parameter | Machine X | Machine Y |
|---|---|---|
| Initial Investment | ₹4,00,000 | ₹6,00,000 |
| Annual Net Benefit | ₹1,20,000 | ₹1,80,000 |
| Salvage Value | ₹40,000 | ₹60,000 |
| Useful Life | 6 years | 6 years |
| MARR | 10% | 10% |

**(a)** Compute the BCR for each machine and check individual acceptability. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Perform Incremental BCR analysis to choose between X and Y. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution

**Part (a) — Individual BCR Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1:** Compute discount factors at i = 10%, n = 6.

$$\left(\frac{P}{A}, 10\%, 6\right) = \frac{(1.10)^6 - 1}{0.10 \times (1.10)^6} = \frac{1.7716 - 1}{0.17716} = 4.3553$$

$$\left(\frac{P}{F}, 10\%, 6\right) = \frac{1}{(1.10)^6} = 0.5645$$

**Step 2: BCR for Machine X**

$$PVB_X = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 4.3553 + 40{,}000 \times 0.5645$$

$$PVB_X = 5{,}22{,}636 + 22{,}580 = 5{,}45{,}216$$

$$PVC_X = 4{,}00{,}000$$

$$BCR_X = \frac{5{,}45{,}216}{4{,}00{,}000} = 1.363$$

Since **BCR_X > 1**, Machine X is **acceptable vs Do-Nothing**.

**Step 3: BCR for Machine Y**

$$PVB_Y = 1{,}80{,}000 \times 4.3553 + 60{,}000 \times 0.5645$$

$$PVB_Y = 7{,}83{,}954 + 33{,}870 = 8{,}17{,}824$$

$$PVC_Y = 6{,}00{,}000$$

$$BCR_Y = \frac{8{,}17{,}824}{6{,}00{,}000} = 1.363$$

Since **BCR_Y > 1**, Machine Y is **acceptable vs Do-Nothing**.

**Part (b) — Incremental BCR Analysis (7 Marks)**

**Step 1:** Rank by initial cost — X (₹4,00,000) < Y (₹6,00,000). So X is the defender.

**Step 2:** Compute incremental benefits and costs (Y − X):

$$\Delta PVB = 8{,}17{,}824 - 5{,}45{,}216 = 2{,}72{,}608$$

$$\Delta PVC = 6{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 = 2{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 3:** Incremental BCR:

$$\Delta BCR = \frac{2{,}72{,}608}{2{,}00{,}000} = 1.363$$

**Step 4: Decision**

Since **ΔBCR = 1.363 > 1**, the incremental investment of ₹2,00,000 in Machine Y yields sufficient additional benefits. **Choose Machine Y.**

---

### ❓ QUESTION B — Modified BCR for Unconventional Cash Flows [14 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

A municipal authority is evaluating a water-treatment plant with the following cash flow pattern:

| Year | Cash Flow (₹ Lakhs) |
|---|---|
| 0 | −50 (Construction) |
| 1 | +15 |
| 2 | +15 |
| 3 | +15 |
| 4 | +15 |
| 5 | −10 (Major Overhaul) |
| 6 | +20 |
| 7 | +20 |
| 8 | +20 |
| 9 | +20 |
| 10 | +25 (Includes ₹5L salvage) |

**MARR = 12%.**

**(a)** Compute the Modified Benefit-Cost Ratio and determine project acceptability. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Verify your answer using the Net Present Worth (NPW) method. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution

**Part (a) — Modified BCR (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Discount factors at i = 12%**

| Year | DF = $(1.12)^{-t}$ |
|---|---|
| 1 | 0.8929 |
| 2 | 0.7972 |
| 3 | 0.7118 |
| 4 | 0.6355 |
| 5 | 0.5674 |
| 6 | 0.5066 |
| 7 | 0.4523 |
| 8 | 0.4039 |
| 9 | 0.3606 |
| 10 | 0.3220 |

**Step 2: PV of All Positive Cash Flows (Benefits)**

| Year | Inflow (₹L) | PV (₹L) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 15 | 13.39 |
| 2 | 15 | 11.96 |
| 3 | 15 | 10.68 |
| 4 | 15 | 9.53 |
| 6 | 20 | 10.13 |
| 7 | 20 | 9.05 |
| 8 | 20 | 8.08 |
| 9 | 20 | 7.21 |
| 10 | 25 | 8.05 |
| **Total PVB** | | **88.08** |

**Step 3: PV of All Negative Cash Flows (Costs)**

| Year | Outflow (₹L) | PV (₹L) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 50 | 50.00 |
| 5 | 10 | 5.67 |
| **Total PVC** | | **55.67** |

**Step 4: Modified BCR**

$$MBCR = \frac{PVB}{\vert PVC \vert} = \frac{88.08}{55.67} = 1.582$$

**Decision:** Since **MBCR = 1.582 > 1**, the project is **ACCEPTED**.

---

**Part (b) — NPW Verification (7 Marks)**

The Net Present Worth (NPW) is the difference between total PV of benefits and total PV of costs:

$$NPW = PVB - PVC = 88.08 - 55.67 = +32.41 \text{ Lakhs}$$

Since **NPW = +₹32.41 Lakhs > 0**, the project is **ACCEPTED**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-verification proof:** $BCR = 1 + \frac{NPW}{PVC}$. So $1.582 = 1 + \frac{32.41}{55.67} = 1 + 0.582 = 1.582$ ✓ Matches perfectly.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout
>
> **Where students LOSE marks in BCR problems:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Conventional vs Modified BCR** — Applying conventional BCR to multi-sign-change cash flows → **−7 marks**.
> 2. **Skipping the "Rank by Initial Cost" step** before incremental analysis → **−3 marks** (full procedure marks lost).
> 3. **Forgetting to discount the salvage value** separately → **−2 marks**.
> 4. **Not stating the decision rule explicitly** ("Since BCR > 1, accept") → **−1 mark** for missing conclusion.
> 5. **Mixing units** (lakhs vs rupees) → **−1 to −2 marks** depending on severity.
> 6. **Arithmetic errors in discount factors** — KTU allows ±1 mark tolerance only; large deviations lose full marks.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **BCR = PV of Benefits ÷ PV of Costs.** Accept if **BCR ≥ 1**.
- ✅ **Modified BCR** is mandatory for **unconventional cash flows** with multiple sign changes.
- ✅ **Incremental BCR** is required for **mutually exclusive alternatives** — rank by initial cost first.
- ✅ Always **discount** all cash flows at the given **MARR** to Year 0.
- ✅ **Cross-verify** BCR with **NPW** for confirmation — both methods should give consistent decisions.
- ✅ **Salvage value** is added to the final year's positive cash flow.
- ✅ If **BCR = 1**, the project is at **indifference** — NPV = 0; IRR = MARR.
- ✅ For **independent projects** under capital rationing, rank by BCR descending and pick top ones.
- ✅ **Never** apply conventional BCR to cash flows with multiple sign changes — use Modified BCR.
- ✅ **Formula to remember:** $\text{NPW} = (BCR - 1) \times PVC$ — useful for quick verification.
- ✅ **KTU Viva Question:** *"Why use BCR over NPV?"* — BCR gives the **relative efficiency** (₹ return per ₹ invested), useful for comparing projects of different scales.
- ✅ **Decision Matrix:** Always state the rule, show calculation, and conclude with **"Accept"** or **"Reject"** explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equivalent Maintenance Cost and Payback Period
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET602 - FINANCIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Equivalent Maintenance Cost and Payback Period

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Equivalent Maintenance Cost (EMC)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)**
> **Equivalent Maintenance Cost (EMC)** is defined as the *uniform annual cost* (a constant series of equal payments) which, over the analysis period at a specified **Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR)** or **interest rate $i$**, is economically equivalent to the actual maintenance cost pattern that varies from year to year. It is the cornerstone technique used to compare **mutually exclusive alternatives** having different maintenance schedules.

In simpler language, imagine your car has different maintenance costs every year:
- Year 1: ₹5,000 (oil change)
- Year 2: ₹7,000 (tyres + oil)
- Year 3: ₹10,000 (brake pads + service)

These irregular amounts are **hard to compare** with a friend's car that has a flat ₹7,500 maintenance every year. EMC answers the question: *"What single, constant yearly amount would be financially equivalent to my irregular expenses?"*

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy: The EMI Analogy**
> Think of EMC exactly like an **EMI (Equated Monthly Instalment)** on a loan. When you take a loan, the bank converts your irregular future liabilities (varying principal + interest) into one fixed monthly payment. EMC does the *same job in reverse* — it converts your *varying future costs* into a *single representative annual figure*.

### 1.2 Payback Period (PBP)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)**
> The **Payback Period** is the length of time (usually expressed in years) required to recover the **initial capital investment** of a project from the **net cash inflows** (or undiscounted savings) it generates. It is the most widely used **measure of project liquidity and risk exposure**.

**Intuitive Analogy**: Suppose you start a roadside tea stall with ₹50,000 and earn ₹10,000 net profit every month. Your **payback period = 50,000 ÷ 10,000 = 5 months**. After 5 months, your investment is "paid back". Any earnings beyond 5 months are pure gain (until the stall's life ends). This metric tells you *how quickly you get your money back* — critical in high-uncertainty environments.

### 1.3 The Two Variants of Payback

| Variant | Formula Basis | Time Value of Money? |
|---|---|---|
| **Simple Payback Period (SPP)** | Uses *raw* cash flows | **No** — ignores interest |
| **Discounted Payback Period (DPP)** | Uses *discounted* cash flows at MARR $i$ | **Yes** — incorporates $i$ |

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Note**
> KTU 2024 scheme explicitly distinguishes between **SPP and DPP** in the Cost Estimation module. Always read the question carefully — examiners frequently award **0 marks** when the wrong variant is used.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Convert Maintenance Costs to an Equivalent Uniform Series?

Industrial assets (machinery, bridges, software systems) rarely have constant maintenance costs. Costs typically:
- **Increase** each year due to wear and tear (aging equipment), OR
- **Increase geometrically** due to inflation, OR
- Follow a **stepped pattern** (major overhaul every $k$ years).

To compare two alternatives (e.g., Machine A vs. Machine B), engineers need a **common denominator** — and that is the **uniform annual cost (UAC)** framework of engineering economics.

### 2.2 The Three Canonical Maintenance Cost Patterns

#### Pattern 1 — Constant Annual Maintenance
A fixed amount $A$ every year for $n$ years.

$$
EMC = A
$$

#### Pattern 2 — Arithmetic Gradient (Linear Increase)
Costs rise by a constant amount $G$ each year, starting from a base value in Year 1.

Let costs be: $C_1,\ C_1 + G,\ C_1 + 2G,\ \dots,\ C_1 + (n-1)G$.

This series can be **decomposed** into two parallel streams:
- A constant annuity of $C_1$ every year, **plus**
- A linear gradient $G$ that starts at $0$ in Year 1, then $G, 2G, \dots$ in subsequent years.

$$
\boxed{\ EMC = C_1 + G \cdot \left(\frac{1}{i} - \frac{n}{(1+i)^n - 1}\right) \ }
$$

The bracketed term is the **Arithmetic Gradient to Uniform Series Factor**, denoted $\left(\dfrac{A}{G,\,i,\,n}\right)$ or sometimes $F_{AG}$.

#### Pattern 3 — Geometric Gradient (Exponential Increase)
Costs grow at a **constant percentage rate $g$** per year (e.g., 6% inflation in spare parts).

Year-$k$ cost: $C_k = C_1 (1+g)^{k-1}$.

$$
\boxed{\ EMC = C_1 \cdot \left[\frac{1 - \left(\dfrac{1+g}{1+i}\right)^n}{i - g}\right] \ }
$$

> [!NOTE] **Special Case When $i = g$**
> When the interest rate exactly equals the geometric growth rate, the formula collapses to the trivial identity $EMC = C_1$. This is the **singular case** — KTU examiners occasionally test this.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam-Critical Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT] All formulas use the **end-of-year cash flow convention** unless explicitly stated otherwise. Interest rate is $i$ (per year), and $n$ = number of years.

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary / Special Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Constant Annual Cost | $EMC = A$ | $A > 0$ |
| 2 | Arithmetic Gradient EMC | $EMC = C_1 + G \cdot \left[\dfrac{1}{i} - \dfrac{n}{(1+i)^n-1}\right]$ | Requires $i \neq 0$ |
| 3 | Geometric Gradient EMC | $EMC = C_1 \cdot \dfrac{1 - [(1+g)/(1+i)]^n}{i-g}$ | If $i = g$, then $EMC = C_1$ |
| 4 | Simple Payback Period | $SPP = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Net Cash Inflow}}$ | Only valid for *uniform* inflows |
| 5 | Discounted Payback Period | $DPP =$ smallest $t$ such that $\sum_{k=1}^{t} \dfrac{CF_k}{(1+i)^k} \geq \text{Investment}$ | Must compute cumulative discounted CF |
| 6 | SPP — Non-Uniform Inflows | $SPP =$ smallest $t$ such that $\sum_{k=1}^{t} CF_k \geq \text{Investment}$ | Interpolate within the crossover year if needed |

### 2.4 Decision Criteria (KTU Convention)

- **Simple Payback Rule**: Accept the project if $SPP \leq$ target payback (set by management). Shorter is better.
- **Discounted Payback Rule**: Accept if $DPP \leq$ target payback. More conservative — recommended by KTU 2024 scheme for high-value capital decisions.
- **EMC Comparison**: When comparing two alternatives, the one with the **lower EMC** is economically superior *if* the alternatives have equal useful lives. For unequal lives, the analysis must be conducted over the **Least Common Multiple (LCM)** of lives or using **Annual Worth** directly.

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Utility**
> EMC is the foundation of **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** analysis used by procurement divisions in PSUs (BHEL, NTPC, KSEB). It determines whether to keep overhauling an old turbine or replace it with a new one.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Arithmetic Gradient EMC Formula

**Setup**: A maintenance stream is $\{C_1,\ C_1+G,\ C_1+2G,\ \dots,\ C_1+(n-1)G\}$ occurring at the end of years $1, 2, \dots, n$.

**Step 1 — Decomposition into two parallel streams**:

$$
\text{Stream A (constant)} = \{C_1,\ C_1,\ C_1,\ \dots,\ C_1\}
$$

$$
\text{Stream B (gradient)} = \{0,\ G,\ 2G,\ \dots,\ (n-1)G\}
$$

**Step 2 — Present Worth of Stream A**:

$$
P_A = C_1 \cdot (P/A,\, i,\, n) = C_1 \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}
$$

**Step 3 — Present Worth of Stream B**:

The Year-$k$ value of Stream B is $(k-1)G$. We sum the present worths of each term:

$$
P_B = \sum_{k=2}^{n} \frac{(k-1)G}{(1+i)^k}
$$

Factoring out $G/(1+i)$ and substituting $j = k-1$:

$$
P_B = \frac{G}{1+i} \sum_{j=1}^{n-1} \frac{j}{(1+i)^{j-1}}
$$

**Step 4 — Using the identity** $\sum_{j=1}^{n-1} j x^{j-1} = \dfrac{1 - n x^{n-1} + (n-1) x^n}{(1-x)^2}$ evaluated at $x = \dfrac{1}{1+i}$, then converting back to $A_B$ via the capital recovery factor $(A/P, i, n)$:

$$
A_B = G \cdot \left(\frac{1}{i} - \frac{n}{(1+i)^n - 1}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Combine**: The total EMC is $A_A + A_B$:

$$
\boxed{\ EMC = C_1 + G \cdot \left[\frac{1}{i} - \frac{n}{(1+i)^n - 1}\right] \ }
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Geometric Gradient EMC Formula

**Setup**: Year-$k$ maintenance cost is $C_k = C_1 (1+g)^{k-1}$.

**Step 1 — Present Worth**:

$$
P = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{C_1 (1+g)^{k-1}}{(1+i)^k} = \frac{C_1}{1+i} \sum_{k=1}^{n} \left(\frac{1+g}{1+i}\right)^{k-1}
$$

**Step 2 — Recognize Geometric Series**: Let $r = \dfrac{1+g}{1+i}$. The sum becomes:

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{n} r^{k-1} = \frac{1 - r^n}{1 - r} = \frac{1 - [(1+g)/(1+i)]^n}{1 - \frac{1+g}{1+i}} = \frac{1 - [(1+g)/(1+i)]^n}{i - g} \cdot (1+i)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and Simplify**:

$$
P = \frac{C_1}{1+i} \cdot (1+i) \cdot \frac{1 - [(1+g)/(1+i)]^n}{i - g} = C_1 \cdot \frac{1 - [(1+g)/(1+i)]^n}{i - g}
$$

**Step 4 — Capital Recovery**: Multiply by $(A/P, i, n)$:

$$
A = P \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the bracket** using $r^n = [(1+g)/(1+i)]^n$:

$$
\boxed{\ EMC = C_1 \cdot \frac{1 - \left(\dfrac{1+g}{1+i}\right)^n}{i - g} \ }
$$

### 3.3 Worked Example — Arithmetic Gradient EMC

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A CNC machine has maintenance costs of ₹8,000 in Year 1, increasing by ₹1,500 every year for 8 years. If $i = 10\%$, find the Equivalent Uniform Annual Maintenance Cost.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters**: $C_1 = 8000$, $G = 1500$, $i = 0.10$, $n = 8$.

**Step 2 — Compute $(1+i)^n$**:

$$
(1.10)^8 = 2.14359
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the gradient conversion factor**:

$$
\frac{1}{0.10} - \frac{8}{2.14359 - 1} = 10 - \frac{8}{1.14359} = 10 - 6.9948 = 3.0052
$$

**Step 4 — Plug into EMC formula**:

$$
EMC = 8000 + 1500 \times 3.0052 = 8000 + 4507.80 = \boxed{₹\,12{,}507.80 \text{ per year}}
$$

> [!NOTE] **Interpretation**: A flat annual outlay of approximately ₹12,508 is financially equivalent to the entire irregular 8-year maintenance schedule at 10% interest.

### 3.4 Worked Example — Payback Period (Non-Uniform Inflows)

> **Problem:** Initial investment = ₹5,00,000. Net cash inflows (after-tax) over 6 years are:
> Year 1: ₹1,50,000 | Year 2: ₹1,70,000 | Year 3: ₹1,40,000 | Year 4: ₹1,90,000 | Year 5: ₹1,10,000 | Year 6: ₹80,000
> Compute the **Simple Payback Period**.

**Solution — Cumulative Cash Flow Table**:

| Year | Cash Inflow (₹) | Cumulative (₹) | Investment Recovered? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | -5,00,000 | -5,00,000 | ❌ No |
| 1 | 1,50,000 | -3,50,000 | ❌ No |
| 2 | 1,70,000 | -1,80,000 | ❌ No |
| 3 | 1,40,000 | -40,000 | ❌ No |
| 4 | 1,90,000 | **+1,50,000** | ✅ Yes — crossover occurs in Year 4 |

**Step 5 — Interpolate within Year 4**: At the end of Year 3, ₹40,000 is still unrecovered. Year 4 brings ₹1,90,000. Fractional year required:

$$
SPP = 3 + \frac{40{,}000}{1{,}90{,}000} = 3 + 0.2105 = \boxed{3.21 \text{ years}}
$$

### 3.5 Worked Example — Discounted Payback Period

> **Same data as above but with $i = 12\%$. Compute the DPP.**

**Step 1 — Discount each cash flow using $PV_k = CF_k / (1.12)^k$**:

| Year | $CF_k$ (₹) | $(1.12)^k$ | $PV_k$ (₹) | Cumulative PV (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | -5,00,000 | 1.0000 | -5,00,000 | -5,00,000 |
| 1 | 1,50,000 | 1.1200 | 1,33,929 | -3,66,071 |
| 2 | 1,70,000 | 1.2544 | 1,35,524 | -2,30,547 |
| 3 | 1,40,000 | 1.4049 | 99,659 | -1,30,888 |
| 4 | 1,90,000 | 1.5735 | 1,20,752 | -10,136 |
| 5 | 1,10,000 | 1.7623 | 62,420 | **+52,284** ✅ |

**Step 2 — Identify crossover**: Cumulative discounted CF turns positive during Year 5.

**Step 3 — Interpolate within Year 5**: Unrecovered at end of Year 4 = ₹10,136. Year 5 brings ₹62,420 in present value:

$$
DPP = 4 + \frac{10{,}136}{62{,}420} = 4 + 0.1624 = \boxed{4.16 \text{ years}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Comparison Insight**
> In this example, SPP = 3.21 years, DPP = 4.16 years. The DPP is **always ≥ SPP** because discounting reduces the real value of future cash flows. KTU examiners love this type of contrast question.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — EMC Conversion Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Identify Maintenance Cost Pattern] --> B{Which Pattern?}
    B -->|Constant A every year| C[Apply EMC equals A]
    B -->|Arithmetic Gradient C1 plus Gn minus 1| D[Apply Arithmetic Gradient EMC Formula]
    B -->|Geometric Gradient C1 times 1 plus g raised to n minus 1| E[Apply Geometric Gradient EMC Formula]
    B -->|Irregular Lump Sums| F[Convert Each Lump Sum using P to F or F to P factors]
    C --> G[Compare Alternatives using Annual Worth or EMC]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Decision: Lower EMC wins]
```

### 4.2 Flowchart — Payback Period Decision Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[START: Receive Investment and Cash Flow Schedule] --> P2{Is MARR given?}
    P2 -->|No, only raw data| P3[Compute Simple Payback SPP using raw inflows]
    P2 -->|Yes, MARR equals i percent| P4[Compute Discounted Payback DPP using discounted inflows]
    P3 --> P5[Build Cumulative Cash Flow Table]
    P4 --> P6[Build Cumulative Discounted Cash Flow Table]
    P5 --> P7{Crossover Year Found?}
    P6 --> P7
    P7 -->|Yes| P8[Interpolate within crossover year]
    P7 -->|No, never recovers| P9[Project REJECTED Payback infinite]
    P8 --> P10[Compare with Target Payback set by management]
    P10 --> P11{SPP less than or equal Target?}
    P11 -->|Yes| P12[ACCEPT project]
    P11 -->|No| P13[REJECT project]
```

### 4.3 Cash Flow Timeline Schematic

```mermaid
gantt
    title Generic Engineering Investment Cash Flow Timeline
    dateFormat YYYY
    axisFormat Year %Y
    section Capital Outflow
    Initial Investment (t = 0) :milestone, m1, 2025, 0d
    section Operational Phase
    Year 1 Net Inflow :a1, 2026, 365d
    Year 2 Net Inflow :a2, after a1, 365d
    Year 3 Net Inflow :a3, after a2, 365d
    Year 4 Net Inflow :a4, after a3, 365d
    Year 5 Net Inflow :a5, after a4, 365d
    section Recovery Point
    Payback Crossover (SPP) :crit, milestone, m2, after a2, 0d
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture — Cost Comparison Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT
        I1[Alternative A Data: P, A, G, n, i]
        I2[Alternative B Data: P, A, G, n, i]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS
        PROC1[Compute EMC of A]
        PROC2[Compute EMC of B]
        PROC3[Compute Total Annual Cost PW equals P times A by P factor plus EMC]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT
        OUT1[EMC A in Rupees per Year]
        OUT2[EMC B in Rupees per Year]
        OUT3[Winner: Min EMC A, EMC B]
    end
    I1 --> PROC1
    I1 --> PROC3
    I2 --> PROC2
    I2 --> PROC3
    PROC1 --> OUT1
    PROC2 --> OUT2
    PROC1 --> PROC3
    PROC2 --> PROC3
    PROC3 --> OUT3
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define *Equivalent Maintenance Cost*. State its significance in comparing two engineering alternatives.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Equivalent Maintenance Cost (EMC) is the uniform annual cost which, at a given interest rate $i$, is economically equivalent to the actual pattern of varying maintenance expenditures over the life of an asset.
> **Significance (1.5 marks):**
> (i) It provides a **common basis** (uniform series) for comparing mutually exclusive alternatives whose maintenance schedules differ.
> (ii) It enables application of the **Annual Worth method**, the KTU-preferred technique when asset lives are equal.
> (iii) It absorbs irregular costs (gradient, geometric growth, lump-sum overhauls) into a single comparable annual figure, simplifying procurement decisions in industries like power, manufacturing, and infrastructure.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Distinguish between *Simple Payback Period* and *Discounted Payback Period*. Under what condition do they become equal?

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> | Feature | SPP | DPP |
> |---|---|---|
> | Time Value of Money | Ignored | Accounted for at MARR $i$ |
> | Cash Flows Used | Raw nominal values | Discounted to present |
> | Typical Result | Always ≤ DPP | Always ≥ SPP |
> | Use Case | Quick screening | KTU-recommended rigorous evaluation |
>
> **Equality Condition (1 mark):** SPP = DPP **only when** $i = 0\%$ (zero interest rate), because discounting becomes a no-op and the cumulative discounted curve coincides with the cumulative raw curve.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* A hydraulic press has the following maintenance schedule over 10 years:
- Year 1: ₹6,000
- The cost increases by ₹800 every year thereafter.
- Interest rate: $i = 12\%$ per annum.

Compute the **Equivalent Uniform Annual Maintenance Cost** of the press.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A pump-set alternative has the following net cash inflows after tax:
| Year | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Net Inflow (₹ '000) | 200 | 250 | 300 | 350 | 400 |

Initial investment = ₹800,000. Compute the **Simple Payback Period**.

---

##### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Arithmetic Gradient EMC**

Given: $C_1 = 6{,}000$, $G = 800$, $i = 0.12$, $n = 10$.

**Step 1 — Compute $(1+i)^n$:**

$$
(1.12)^{10} = 3.10585
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Arithmetic Gradient Factor $(A/G, i, n)$:**

$$
\frac{1}{i} - \frac{n}{(1+i)^n - 1} = \frac{1}{0.12} - \frac{10}{3.10585 - 1} = 8.3333 - \frac{10}{2.10585} = 8.3333 - 4.7479 = 3.5854
$$

**Step 3 — Apply EMC formula:**

$$
EMC = C_1 + G \cdot \left(\frac{A}{G,\,i,\,n}\right) = 6000 + 800 \times 3.5854 = 6000 + 2868.32
$$

$$
\boxed{\ EMC \approx ₹\,8{,}868.32 \text{ per year} \ }
$$

**Part (b) — Simple Payback (Uniform Inflow)**

**Method 1 — Average Annual Inflow:**

$$
\bar{CF} = \frac{200 + 250 + 300 + 350 + 400}{5} = \frac{1500}{5} = 300 \text{ (₹ '000)}
$$

$$
SPP = \frac{800}{300} = 2.667 \text{ years}
$$

**Method 2 — Direct Cumulative Table:**

| Year | Inflow (₹ '000) | Cumulative (₹ '000) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 200 | 200 |
| 2 | 250 | 450 |
| 3 | 300 | 750 |
| 4 | 350 | **800** ← crossover |

At the end of Year 3, ₹50,000 is still unrecovered. Year 4 brings ₹350,000.

$$
SPP = 3 + \frac{50}{350} = 3 + 0.1429 = \boxed{3.14 \text{ years}}
$$

> [!NOTE] **Why two answers?** When inflows are *not uniform*, the simple average method (Method 1) gives an *approximation*; the cumulative table (Method 2) is **always preferred** in KTU valuation. Use Method 2 to score full marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Alternative Set]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* The maintenance cost of a generator was ₹4,000 in Year 1 and is expected to increase at a uniform rate of **8% per year** due to inflation. If the MARR is $i = 12\%$ and the analysis period is $n = 6$ years, determine the **Equivalent Annual Maintenance Cost** using the geometric gradient approach.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A CNC lathe requires an initial investment of ₹12,00,000 and generates uniform annual savings of ₹3,00,000 over 8 years. If the MARR is 15%, compute the **Discounted Payback Period**.

---

##### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Geometric Gradient EMC**

Given: $C_1 = 4000$, $g = 0.08$, $i = 0.12$, $n = 6$. Note: $i > g$, so the formula applies directly.

**Step 1 — Compute the ratio:**

$$
\frac{1+g}{1+i} = \frac{1.08}{1.12} = 0.96429
$$

**Step 2 — Raise to power $n$:**

$$
(0.96429)^6 = 0.8060
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the formula:**

$$
EMC = 4000 \cdot \frac{1 - 0.8060}{0.12 - 0.08} = 4000 \cdot \frac{0.1940}{0.04} = 4000 \times 4.850
$$

$$
\boxed{\ EMC = ₹\,19{,}400 \text{ per year} \ }
$$

**Part (b) — Discounted Payback Period**

Given: Investment $P = 12{,}00{,}000$; uniform annual savings $A = 3{,}00{,}000$; $i = 15\%$, $n = 8$.

**Step 1 — Discount each year's savings** using $PV_k = \dfrac{3{,}00{,}000}{(1.15)^k}$:

| Year | $(1.15)^k$ | $PV_k$ (₹) | Cumulative PV (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.1500 | 2,60,870 | -9,39,130 |
| 2 | 1.3225 | 2,26,843 | -7,12,287 |
| 3 | 1.5209 | 1,97,255 | -5,15,032 |
| 4 | 1.7490 | 1,71,526 | -3,43,506 |
| 5 | 2.0114 | 1,49,153 | -1,94,353 |
| 6 | 2.3131 | 1,29,698 | **-64,655** |
| 7 | 2.6600 | 1,12,782 | +48,127 ✅ |

**Step 2 — Interpolate within Year 7**: Unrecovered at end of Year 6 = ₹64,655; Year 7 brings ₹1,12,782 in present value.

$$
DPP = 6 + \frac{64{,}655}{1{,}12{,}782} = 6 + 0.5733 = \boxed{6.57 \text{ years}}
$$

> [!NOTE] **Insight**: Since $n = 8$ and $DPP = 6.57$ years, the investment is recovered *within the project life*. If DPP had exceeded 8 years, the project would still be acceptable on an NPV basis, but the payback criterion would *fail* — a classic KTU "evaluate on multiple criteria" question.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Exams**
> 1. **Wrong Payback Variant (–4 marks typical):** Always check whether the problem mentions "discounted" or "at MARR $i$". If yes → use DPP; if no → use SPP.
> 2. **Geometric Gradient — Sign of $(i-g)$:** When $i < g$, the denominator $i - g$ is negative and the numerator $(1+g)^n / (1+i)^n > 1$, making the bracket negative. A negative EMC is mathematically possible but economically nonsensical — flag this in your answer if the data forces it.
> 3. **Interpolation Steps Missing (–1 to –2 marks):** In non-uniform cash flow payback, KTU expects *both* the crossover year identification **and** the fractional interpolation. Skipping the fraction loses full marks for the sub-part.
> 4. **Arithmetic Gradient Misapplication (–3 marks):** A common error is using $G$ as the Year-1 cost. The Year-1 cost is $C_1$; the *increment* is $G$. If $C_1$ is not explicitly given, infer it from "the cost in the first year is ₹X" and the increment is ₹Y.
> 5. **Forgetting the Constant Term $C_1$ in EMC:** Some students compute only the gradient component and forget to add $C_1$. The full EMC = $C_1 + G \cdot (\text{factor})$ — always include both.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **EMC** = Uniform Annual Cost equivalent to a varying cost pattern at rate $i$.
- **Three patterns** you MUST memorize: Constant, Arithmetic Gradient ($+G$ each year), Geometric Gradient ($\times (1+g)$ each year).
- **Arithmetic Gradient EMC:** $EMC = C_1 + G \cdot \left[\dfrac{1}{i} - \dfrac{n}{(1+i)^n - 1}\right]$
- **Geometric Gradient EMC:** $EMC = C_1 \cdot \dfrac{1 - [(1+g)/(1+i)]^n}{i - g}$; **collapse rule:** if $i = g$, then $EMC = C_1$.
- **Payback Period (Simple):** Use cumulative cash flow table; interpolate within the crossover year.
- **Payback Period (Discounted):** Discount every cash flow first, *then* build cumulative table; DPP ≥ SPP always.
- **SPP = DPP only when** $i = 0\%$ — examiners love this fact.
- **Decision Rule:** Lower EMC wins; SPP/DPP must be ≤ target payback set by management.
- **Always specify units** in the final answer (₹/year for EMC, years for Payback).
- **Interpolation formula:** $\text{Fractional Year} = \dfrac{\text{Unrecovered at Year } t-1}{\text{Cash Inflow in Year } t}$ — apply to *both* raw and discounted tables.
- **Mutually exclusive alternatives** with unequal lives → use the **LCM of lives** method or convert to Annual Worth directly.
- **End-of-year convention** is the default in KTU problems unless "beginning-of-year" is explicitly stated (in which case multiply the EMC by $(1+i)$).
- **Geometric gradient** is most appropriate for inflation-driven maintenance scenarios; **arithmetic gradient** is most appropriate for predictable wear-driven schedules.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cost of Capital: Weighted Average Cost and Weighted Marginal Cost - Factors Affecting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET602 - FINANCIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cost of Capital: Weighted Average Cost and Weighted Marginal Cost - Factors Affecting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Cost of Capital — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cost of Capital (CoC)** is the **minimum required rate of return** that a firm must earn on its investments to satisfy the expectations of its investors (both equity holders and debt holders). It represents the **composite (blended) cost of the various sources of finance** used by the firm, expressed as a percentage, and is the **discount rate** used in capital budgeting decisions.

Mathematically, the general concept of cost of capital for a single source of capital is:

$$K_j = \frac{\text{Expected return per unit of capital from source } j}{\text{Net proceeds per unit of capital from source } j}$$

where $K_j$ is the cost of capital from source $j$ (debt, equity, preference, retained earnings, etc.).

### 1.2 Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)** is the **overall (composite) cost of capital** of a firm, computed as the **weighted average of the costs of individual sources of finance**, where the weights represent each source's **proportion in the total capital structure**.

**General WACC Formula:**

$$K_w = \sum_{j=1}^{n} K_j \cdot W_j$$

where:
- $K_w$ = Weighted Average Cost of Capital
- $K_j$ = After-tax cost of the $j^{th}$ source of capital
- $W_j$ = Weight (proportion) of the $j^{th}$ source in total capital
- $n$ = Number of distinct sources of capital

**Expanded Form (for a typical firm with Equity, Debt, and Preference Capital):**

$$K_w = (K_e \cdot W_e) + [K_d \cdot (1 - t) \cdot W_d] + (K_p \cdot W_p)$$

where:
- $K_e$ = Cost of equity
- $K_d$ = Cost of debt (before tax)
- $K_p$ = Cost of preference capital
- $W_e, W_d, W_p$ = Respective weights in the capital structure
- $t$ = Marginal corporate tax rate
- $(1 - t)$ = Tax shield factor applied to interest-bearing debt

### 1.3 Weighted Marginal Cost of Capital (WMCC)

> [!NOTE]
> **Marginal Cost of Capital (MCC)** is the **cost of an additional (next) rupee of capital** raised by the firm. The **Weighted Marginal Cost of Capital (WMCC)** is the **weighted average of the marginal costs of all sources**, used in the marginal cost of capital schedule, particularly when the firm raises **incremental blocks of capital** beyond existing break-points.

$$WMCC = \sum_{j=1}^{n} MC_j \cdot W_j$$

where $MC_j$ = Marginal cost of the $j^{th}$ source at a given level of capital raised.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Fruit Smoothie" Model:**
> Imagine a smoothie that is a blend of mango, banana, and yogurt. Each fruit has a different "price per kg" (its individual cost). The **smoothie's average cost** depends on:
> 1. The price of each fruit (individual cost of capital), and
> 2. How much of each fruit goes into the blender (the weight in the capital structure).
>
> - **WACC** = The average cost of the *existing* smoothie (the firm's current capital structure).
> - **WMCC** = The average cost of the *next glass* you pour after adding more expensive fruit (raising fresh capital may increase marginal cost).
> - **Break-point** = The moment when the recipe (capital structure) changes because you've used up the cheap mangoes and must shift to costlier ones.

For a first-time reader: WACC tells you **how expensive the firm's existing money is on average**, and WMCC tells you **how expensive the next rupee raised will be** — both are critical benchmarks in capital budgeting (accepting/rejecting projects).

### 1.5 Standard Metrics & Conventions

- **WACC** is expressed in **percentage (%)** or **decimal (e.g., 0.12 for 12%)**.
- **Tax shield factor $(1 - t)$** is dimensionless (e.g., $0.30 \to 0.70$).
- **Weights** are expressed in **decimal proportions** summing to **1.00**.
- Cost of capital is always expressed on an **after-tax basis** (except for equity).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Computation of WACC — Logic Steps

The WACC is computed in the following structured manner:

1. **Identify all sources of capital** in the firm's structure (Equity, Preference, Debentures, Term Loans, Retained Earnings, etc.).
2. **Compute the specific cost of each source** ($K_e, K_d, K_p$, etc.) on an after-tax basis where applicable.
3. **Determine the weight of each source** ($W_j$) using either book value or market value weights.
4. **Apply the WACC formula:** multiply each cost by its weight and sum the products.
5. **Verify that weights sum to unity (1.00)** — otherwise re-balance the weights.

### 2.2 Types of Weights Used in WACC

| Weight Type | Definition | KTU Preference |
|---|---|---|
| **Book Value Weights** | Weights based on the **face/book value** of each source as shown in the balance sheet | Used for **historical/regulatory** purposes |
| **Market Value Weights** | Weights based on the **current market price** of each security (shares × market price, debentures × quoted price) | **Theoretically superior** and recommended by modern finance theory |
| **Marginal Weights** | Weights based on the **proportion of each source in the next financing decision** | Used in **WMCC** computation |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** "Which weight is the most appropriate — book value or market value?" — Answer: **Market value weights are theoretically more appropriate** because they reflect the current opportunity cost of capital, but **book value weights are simpler** to compute from the balance sheet.

### 2.3 Computation of Individual Component Costs

| Source | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Cost of Equity ($K_e$) — CAPM** | $K_e = R_f + \beta (R_m - R_f)$ | Risk-free rate + Risk premium |
| **Cost of Equity ($K_e$) — Dividend Model** | $K_e = \dfrac{D_1}{P_0} + g$ | Gordon's Growth Model |
| **Cost of Redeemable Debt ($K_d$)** | $K_d = \dfrac{I(1-t) + \dfrac{(RV - NP)}{n}}{\dfrac{(RV + NP)}{2}}$ | YTM adjusted for tax |
| **Cost of Irredeemable Debt ($K_d$)** | $K_d = \dfrac{\text{Interest per year} \times (1-t)}{\text{Net Proceeds}} \times 100$ | Perpetual debt formula |
| **Cost of Preference Capital ($K_p$)** | $K_p = \dfrac{\text{Preference Dividend}}{\text{Net Proceeds}} \times 100$ | No tax shield (dividend paid from post-tax profit) |
| **Cost of Retained Earnings ($K_r$)** | $K_r = K_e \times (1 - \text{Brokerage cost fraction})$ | Slightly lower than $K_e$ |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | WACC (general) | $K_w = \sum K_j W_j$ | Weights must sum to 1 |
| 2 | WACC (3-source) | $K_w = K_e W_e + K_d(1-t)W_d + K_p W_p$ | Tax shield only on debt |
| 3 | After-tax cost of debt | $K_d(\text{after-tax}) = K_d \times (1-t)$ | Interest is tax-deductible |
| 4 | Weighted Marginal Cost | $WMCC = \sum MC_j W_j$ | For additional capital block |
| 5 | Break-point for source $j$ | $BP_j = \dfrac{\text{Available amount of source } j}{W_j}$ | Level at which cost rises |
| 6 | Market value weight | $W_j = \dfrac{\text{Market value of source } j}{\text{Total market value of capital}}$ | Theoretically preferred |
| 7 | Book value weight | $W_j = \dfrac{\text{Book value of source } j}{\text{Total book value of capital}}$ | Easy to compute |
| 8 | Project-specific cost of capital | $K_p = r_f + b \cdot (\text{project beta})$ | CAPM-based |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absolute Value Rule in Tables:** All vertical bars in the above formulas use the LaTeX command `\vert` or `\mid`, never the raw pipe symbol `|`, to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.5 Why WACC and WMCC Matter in Real-World Engineering/Financial Decisions

- **Capital Budgeting:** Projects with IRR > WACC should be accepted; projects with IRR < WACC should be rejected.
- **Firm Valuation:** WACC is the discount rate in DCF-based firm valuation models.
- **Optimal Capital Structure:** Firms target the capital structure that **minimizes WACC** (and thereby maximizes firm value).
- **Performance Benchmarking:** WMCC is the "hurdle rate" for **next-period investment** decisions, especially when retained earnings are exhausted and fresh equity must be raised (at a flotation cost premium).
- **Cost of capital is used in** valuation of patents, capital investment in plant/machinery, M&A decisions, and dividend-discount models.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the WACC Formula

Let a firm have $n$ sources of capital. Let $C_j$ be the total amount of capital raised from source $j$ and $K_j$ be the cost of that source. The total capital is:

$$C_T = \sum_{j=1}^{n} C_j$$

**Step 1 — Total annual cost paid to all investors:**

The total cost (interest + dividends + retained earnings opportunity) paid across all sources is the sum of the product of cost and amount of each source.

$$TC = \sum_{j=1}^{n} K_j \cdot C_j$$

**Step 2 — Definition of WACC:**

By definition, WACC is the *average* cost per rupee of total capital. So we divide the total annual cost by the total capital:

$$K_w = \frac{TC}{C_T}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $C_T$ and $TC$:**

$$K_w = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{n} K_j \cdot C_j}{\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{n} C_j}$$

**Step 4 — Factor out total capital from the sum of amounts (each weight is $C_j/C_T$):**

$$K_w = \sum_{j=1}^{n} K_j \cdot \frac{C_j}{C_T}$$

**Step 5 — Recognize $W_j = C_j / C_T$:**

Since $W_j$ is the weight of source $j$ (proportion of source $j$ in total capital), we obtain:

$$K_w = \sum_{j=1}^{n} K_j \cdot W_j$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{K_w = \sum_{j=1}^{n} K_j \cdot W_j, \quad \text{where} \quad W_j = \frac{C_j}{C_T}, \quad \sum W_j = 1}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — WACC Computation

**Problem:** A firm has the following capital structure:

| Source | Book Value (₹) | Specific Cost (%) |
|---|---|---|
| Equity Share Capital | 8,00,000 | 16% |
| 10% Debentures | 6,00,000 | 6% (after tax) |
| 8% Preference Shares | 2,00,000 | 9% |
| Retained Earnings | 4,00,000 | 14% |

Tax rate is **30%**. Compute WACC.

**Step 1 — Compute Total Capital:**

$$C_T = 8,00,000 + 6,00,000 + 2,00,000 + 4,00,000 = 20,00,000$$

**Step 2 — Compute Weights:**

$$W_e = \frac{8,00,000}{20,00,000} = 0.40$$

$$W_d = \frac{6,00,000}{20,00,000} = 0.30$$

$$W_p = \frac{2,00,000}{20,00,000} = 0.10$$

$$W_r = \frac{4,00,000}{20,00,000} = 0.20$$

**Verification of weights:**

$$0.40 + 0.30 + 0.10 + 0.20 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Apply the WACC formula:**

$$K_w = (K_e \cdot W_e) + (K_d(1-t) \cdot W_d) + (K_p \cdot W_p) + (K_r \cdot W_r)$$

$$K_w = (0.16 \times 0.40) + (0.06 \times 0.30) + (0.09 \times 0.10) + (0.14 \times 0.20)$$

**Step 4 — Calculate each term:**

$$0.16 \times 0.40 = 0.0640$$

$$0.06 \times 0.30 = 0.0180$$

$$0.09 \times 0.10 = 0.0090$$

$$0.14 \times 0.20 = 0.0280$$

**Step 5 — Sum up:**

$$K_w = 0.0640 + 0.0180 + 0.0090 + 0.0280 = 0.1190$$

**Step 6 — Express as percentage:**

$$K_w = 11.90\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The firm's overall cost of capital is **11.90%**. Any new project should yield a return greater than 11.90% (after tax) to be acceptable to the firm's investors.

### 3.3 Derivation of Break-Point and WMCC

When a firm raises capital block by block, the **cost of each source may rise** as cheaper sources are exhausted. The break-point is the **level of total new financing at which the cost of a particular source rises**.

**Step 1 — Let $A_j$ be the maximum amount of capital available from source $j$ at cost $K_j$:**

The weight of source $j$ in the next financing is $W_j$. The amount of *total* new capital that can be raised before exhausting source $j$ is:

$$BP_j = \frac{A_j}{W_j}$$

**Step 2 — Beyond the break-point, the marginal cost of source $j$ rises to $K_j'$:**

The new weighted marginal cost of capital becomes:

$$WMCC_{\text{new}} = K_e W_e + K_d' (1-t) W_d + K_p' W_p + \ldots$$

**Step 3 — WMCC Schedule (Example Illustration):**

Consider a firm with two financing alternatives:
- Up to ₹ 5,00,000: Equity at 15%, Debt at 8% (after tax)
- Beyond ₹ 5,00,000: Equity at 17%, Debt at 9% (after tax)

| Block | Total Capital Raised | Weight of Equity | Weight of Debt | $WMCC$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Block 1 (0 to ₹ 10,00,000) | 10,00,000 | 0.50 | 0.50 | $(0.15)(0.50) + (0.08)(0.50) = 11.50\%$ |
| Block 2 (above ₹ 10,00,000) | 10,00,000+ | 0.50 | 0.50 | $(0.17)(0.50) + (0.09)(0.50) = 13.00\%$ |

**Step 4 — Marginal Cost Computation:**

The marginal cost of the **incremental block** of capital is computed using:

$$MC = \frac{\Delta (\text{Total cost})}{\Delta (\text{Total capital})}$$

For Block 2 above:

$$MC_{\text{Block 2}} = 13.00\% \quad \text{(the new WMCC)}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use WACC vs WMCC

| Situation | Tool to Use | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Evaluating a project of **similar risk** to existing firm | **WACC** | Existing structure represents investor expectations |
| Raising **new capital** above break-points | **WMCC** | Cost of new capital has changed |
| Mixed-risk projects | **Project-specific cost** | Use project beta in CAPM |
| **Divestment / capital rationing** | **WACC** | Use existing portfolio return |
| Evaluating **entire firm valuation** | **WACC** | Average cost over life of firm |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: WACC Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Firm Capital Structure] --> B[Source 1: Equity Capital]
    A --> C[Source 2: Debt Capital]
    A --> D[Source 3: Preference Capital]
    A --> E[Source 4: Retained Earnings]
    B --> F[Compute K_e]
    C --> G[Compute K_d x 1-t]
    D --> H[Compute K_p]
    E --> I[Compute K_r]
    F --> J[Apply Weights W_1, W_2, W_3, W_4]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Weighted Sum]
    K --> L[WACC Result]
    L --> M[Decision: Accept Project if IRR greater than WACC]
```

### 4.2 WMCC Schedule and Break-Point Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Block 0: Retained Earnings] --> P1[Block 1: Low Cost Equity and Debt]
    P1 --> P2[Break Point 1]
    P2 --> P3[Block 2: Higher Cost Equity]
    P3 --> P4[Break Point 2]
    P4 --> P5[Block 3: Costly External Equity]
    P0 --> Q[WMCC Schedule]
    P1 --> Q
    P2 --> Q
    P3 --> Q
    P4 --> Q
    P5 --> Q
    Q --> R[Stepwise Rising WMCC Curve]
```

### 4.3 Capital Structure Composition Diagram

```mermaid
pie title Typical Capital Structure Composition
    "Equity Share Capital" : 40
    "Debentures" : 30
    "Preference Shares" : 10
    "Retained Earnings" : 20
```

### 4.4 Factors Affecting Cost of Capital — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FAC[Factors Affecting Cost of Capital] --> EX[External Factors]
    FAC --> IN[Internal Factors]
    EX --> EX1[Market Interest Rates]
    EX --> EX2[Inflation Rate]
    EX --> EX3[Tax Rates]
    EX --> EX4[Economic Conditions]
    IN --> IN1[Business Risk]
    IN --> IN2[Financial Risk]
    IN --> IN3[Capital Structure]
    IN --> IN4[Dividend Policy]
    IN --> IN5[Cost of Floatation]
    EX1 --> COC[Cost of Capital]
    EX2 --> COC
    EX3 --> COC
    EX4 --> COC
    IN1 --> COC
    IN2 --> COC
    IN3 --> COC
    IN4 --> COC
    IN5 --> COC
```

### 4.5 WACC vs WMCC — Comparative Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Current Capital Structure] --> B[Compute WACC]
    B --> C[Use for Existing Project Decisions]
    D[Proposed New Capital Raising] --> E[Check Break-Points]
    E --> F[Compute WMCC]
    F --> G[Use for Marginal Investment Decisions]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Weighted Average Cost of Capital" (WACC). State its significance in financial decision-making.**

**Model Answer:**

> WACC is the **weighted average of the after-tax costs of all sources of long-term finance** used by a firm, where the weights are the proportions of each source in the total capital structure.

**Formula:**

$$K_w = \sum K_j W_j$$

**Significance:**
1. It serves as the **discount rate** in capital budgeting decisions (NPV calculations).
2. It is the **hurdle rate** for accepting/rejecting investment proposals.
3. It is used in **firm valuation** under the DCF approach.
4. It helps determine the **optimal capital structure** that minimizes the firm's cost of capital.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Distinguish between Book Value Weights and Market Value Weights in the computation of WACC.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Book Value Weights | Market Value Weights |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Weights based on the **face/book value** of each source as shown in the balance sheet | Weights based on the **current market price** of each security |
| **Computation** | Easy — directly from the balance sheet | Requires market prices of all securities |
| **Theoretical Accuracy** | Less accurate (historical values) | **More accurate (reflects current opportunity cost)** |
| **Use** | Statutory / regulatory purposes | Investment decisions, DCF valuation |
| **Preference** | Simple and stable | **Theoretically preferred** in modern finance |

---

### **Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**Q.A. (a) The capital structure of ABC Ltd. is as follows:**

| Source | Amount (₹) | Cost (%) |
|---|---|---|
| Equity Share Capital | 10,00,000 | 18% |
| 12% Debentures | 6,00,000 | 7% (after tax) |
| 10% Preference Shares | 4,00,000 | 11% |

**The tax rate is 40%. Compute the Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC). (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total Capital:**

$$C_T = 10,00,000 + 6,00,000 + 4,00,000 = 20,00,000$$

**Step 2 — Weights:**

$$W_e = \frac{10,00,000}{20,00,000} = 0.50$$

$$W_d = \frac{6,00,000}{20,00,000} = 0.30$$

$$W_p = \frac{4,00,000}{20,00,000} = 0.20$$

**Verification:** $0.50 + 0.30 + 0.20 = 1.00$ ✓

**Step 3 — Apply WACC formula:**

$$K_w = (K_e \times W_e) + [K_d(1-t) \times W_d] + (K_p \times W_p)$$

$$K_w = (0.18 \times 0.50) + (0.07 \times 0.30) + (0.11 \times 0.20)$$

$$K_w = 0.0900 + 0.0210 + 0.0220 = 0.1330$$

$$\boxed{K_w = 13.30\%}$$

---

**Q.A. (b) Explain the concept of "Weighted Marginal Cost of Capital" (WMCC). How is the marginal cost of capital schedule prepared? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> **WMCC Definition:** The **Weighted Marginal Cost of Capital** is the weighted average of the **marginal (additional) costs** of each source of capital, used to evaluate new investment proposals involving **incremental capital raising**.

> **Marginal Cost of Capital (MCC):** The cost of the **next rupee** (or next block) of capital raised by the firm.

**Preparation of MCC Schedule — Steps:**

1. **Determine the target capital structure** (proportions of equity, debt, preference).
2. **Identify the costs of each source** at different levels of capital raised.
3. **Compute the break-points** for each source using the formula:
$$BP_j = \frac{A_j}{W_j}$$
4. **Plot the cumulative cost of capital** against the total amount of new capital raised — the schedule appears as a **rising stepwise function**.
5. **For each block of capital**, compute the WMCC using the marginal costs of each source at that level.
6. **Use the WMCC** as the discount rate for projects requiring that block of capital.

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Insight:** The WMCC schedule typically rises because as more capital is raised:
> - Retained earnings are exhausted first (cheapest internal source).
> - Additional debt may require higher interest rates (lender risk perception).
> - Fresh equity is issued at a discount (flotation costs) and signals to the market.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**Q.B. (a) XYZ Ltd. has the following capital structure:**

| Source | Book Value (₹) | Market Value (₹) | Specific Cost (%) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Equity | 5,00,000 | 8,00,000 | 17% |
| 9% Debentures | 3,00,000 | 2,80,000 | 6% (after tax) |
| Preference Shares | 2,00,000 | 2,20,000 | 10% |

**Tax rate is 30%. Compute WACC using (i) Book Value Weights, and (ii) Market Value Weights. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### (i) Using Book Value Weights

**Step 1 — Total Book Value:**

$$C_T = 5,00,000 + 3,00,000 + 2,00,000 = 10,00,000$$

**Step 2 — Weights:**

$$W_e = 0.50, \quad W_d = 0.30, \quad W_p = 0.20$$

**Step 3 — WACC:**

$$K_w^{BV} = (0.17 \times 0.50) + (0.06 \times 0.30) + (0.10 \times 0.20)$$

$$K_w^{BV} = 0.0850 + 0.0180 + 0.0200 = 0.1230$$

$$\boxed{K_w^{BV} = 12.30\%}$$

#### (ii) Using Market Value Weights

**Step 1 — Total Market Value:**

$$C_T^{MV} = 8,00,000 + 2,80,000 + 2,20,000 = 13,00,000$$

**Step 2 — Weights:**

$$W_e^{MV} = \frac{8,00,000}{13,00,000} = 0.6154$$

$$W_d^{MV} = \frac{2,80,000}{13,00,000} = 0.2154$$

$$W_p^{MV} = \frac{2,20,000}{13,00,000} = 0.1692$$

**Step 3 — WACC:**

$$K_w^{MV} = (0.17 \times 0.6154) + (0.06 \times 0.2154) + (0.10 \times 0.1692)$$

$$K_w^{MV} = 0.1046 + 0.0129 + 0.0169 = 0.1344$$

$$\boxed{K_w^{MV} = 13.44\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison:** The market value WACC (13.44%) is **higher** than the book value WACC (12.30%) because the equity's market value has appreciated, increasing its weight — equity is the most expensive source.

---

**Q.B. (b) Discuss the various factors affecting the cost of capital of a firm. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> The cost of capital of a firm is influenced by a wide range of **internal** and **external** factors. These are:

### **A. External / Market-related Factors**

1. **Market Interest Rates:** The prevailing risk-free interest rate (e.g., government bond yield) directly affects the cost of debt. Higher market rates → higher cost of capital.
2. **Inflation Rate:** Higher inflation → investors demand higher returns → higher cost of capital.
3. **Tax Rates:** The corporate tax rate determines the after-tax cost of debt (via the tax shield). Changes in tax law affect WACC.
4. **Economic Conditions:** Recession/boom cycles influence investor risk perception and the supply of capital.
5. **Capital Market Conditions:** Stock market volatility, liquidity, and investor sentiment affect equity cost.

### **B. Internal / Firm-specific Factors**

1. **Business Risk (Operating Risk):** Higher operating leverage or business volatility → higher equity cost (greater beta).
2. **Financial Risk:** Higher debt in the capital structure → higher financial risk → higher required returns from both debt and equity holders.
3. **Capital Structure:** The mix of debt and equity directly affects WACC (interest is tax-deductible, equity is not).
4. **Dividend Policy:** Stable and high dividends may reduce agency costs and lower the cost of equity.
5. **Cost of Floatation:** Costs of issuing new securities (legal, underwriting, brokerage) raise the effective cost of fresh capital.
6. **Size of the Firm:** Larger firms often enjoy lower risk perception → lower cost of capital.
7. **Age and Reputation:** Established firms may have a lower cost due to better credit ratings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** Most questions in this section require students to list **at least 6–8 factors** under clear headings (External and Internal) and to briefly explain the directional impact (i.e., how each factor increases or decreases the cost of capital).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the tax shield on debt** — always apply $(1 - t)$ on $K_d$ for interest-bearing debt.
> 2. **Weights not summing to 1.00** — re-check arithmetic; this is a 1-mark deduction in KTU papers.
> 3. **Confusing WACC with WMCC** — WACC is for *existing* capital, WMCC is for *new* (marginal) capital.
> 4. **Using $K_d$ instead of $K_d(1-t)$** in WACC calculations — this is a very common error in KTU papers and leads to **full 7-mark loss** in a sub-question.
> 5. **Forgetting to use market values for equity** — equity's book value is often far below its market value; ignoring this inflates $W_d$ and distorts WACC.
> 6. **Not stating units** — always state final answer as a **percentage** with two decimal places (e.g., 12.30%).

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **WACC** = Weighted Average Cost of Capital = $\sum K_j W_j$, where weights sum to 1.
- **WMCC** = Weighted Marginal Cost of Capital = $\sum MC_j W_j$ for *additional* capital.
- **Tax shield** applies **only to debt** interest: use $K_d(1-t)$.
- **No tax shield** for preference dividends or equity (paid from post-tax profits).
- **Market value weights** are theoretically superior; book value weights are easier.
- **Break-point** $BP_j = A_j / W_j$ — at this level of new capital, the cost of source $j$ rises.
- **WMCC schedule** is a **stepwise-rising** curve used to evaluate new investment proposals.
- **Factors affecting CoC** are split into:
  - *External*: interest rates, inflation, tax, market conditions.
  - *Internal*: business risk, financial risk, capital structure, dividend policy, floatation cost, firm size.
- **WACC as a discount rate** is acceptable only for projects of **average firm risk**.
- **MCC** rises as cheap sources (retained earnings) get exhausted — schedule is stepwise.
- **KTU favourite 14-mark question pattern**: numerical WACC + theoretical explanation of WMCC or factors.
- Always show the **5-step structured solution**: Total Capital → Weights → Specific Costs → Apply Formula → State Result in %.
- **Final WACC answers** are usually between **8% and 18%** in KTU problems.
- **Practice a minimum of 5 numerical problems** of WACC with at least 3 sources to ensure 100% accuracy in the KTU ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Company's Cost of Capital and Project Cost of Capital - Cost of Debt - Cost of Preference - Cost of Equity - Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET602 - FINANCIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Company's Cost of Capital and Project Cost of Capital - Cost of Debt - Cost of Preference - Cost of Equity - Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is the Cost of Capital?

The **Cost of Capital (CoC)** is the minimum rate of return that a company must earn on its investments to maintain the market value of its shares and satisfy its investors (both debt-holders and equity-holders). In simpler terms, it is the *opportunity cost of funds* deployed in a business — the rate the firm must pay to attract capital from the market.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** Cost of Capital is the composite (weighted) rate of return required by all categories of investors — debenture holders, preference shareholders, and equity shareholders — for supplying capital to the firm. It serves as the **discount rate** in capital budgeting decisions and as the **hurdle rate** for accepting/rejecting projects.

### 1.2 Company's Cost of Capital vs Project Cost of Capital

| Aspect | Company's Cost of Capital | Project Cost of Capital |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | The overall average cost of all financing sources used by the firm | The specific cost of capital applicable to a particular project |
| **Scope** | Firm-wide, holistic | Project-specific, narrow |
| **Determination** | Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC) | Marginal Cost of Capital / Risk-adjusted discount rate |
| **Use** | Overall capital structure planning, valuation | Capital budgeting, project appraisal |
| **Risk Adjustment** | Reflects average business risk | Reflects the unique risk profile of the project |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Two Different Rates?** Not all projects carry the same risk. A firm in the software industry may have an average WACC of 11%, but launching a new pharmaceutical R&D project (which is far riskier) may require a project-specific discount rate of 18%. Using the lower company-wide WACC for a high-risk project would *under-discount* the cash flows and lead to value-destroying decisions.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: "The Landlord's Hurdle"
