Imagine a landlord borrows ₹50,000 from a bank at 9% interest and another ₹50,000 from a partner who demands 15% for the higher risk he takes. The landlord's **Cost of Capital is the weighted average** of these two demands — say, **12%**. Any new tenant who wants to rent the building must generate at least **12% return** on the rent collected; otherwise, the landlord cannot service both lenders and stay in business. If a tenant proposes a project (e.g., setting up a restaurant inside) that is *riskier* than usual, the landlord might demand a **higher project-specific hurdle** (say 16%) to compensate for that extra risk. This is exactly how *company's* and *project's* cost of capital differ.

### 1.4 The Three Building Blocks

The cost of capital has three primary components, each representing a class of capital:

1. **Cost of Debt (Kd)** — The rate paid to lenders (debenture holders, bondholders, banks).
2. **Cost of Preference Share Capital (Kp)** — The fixed dividend rate paid to preference shareholders.
3. **Cost of Equity (Ke)** — The rate of return required by ordinary equity shareholders (the most expensive source).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Always remember that debt enjoys **tax shield** (interest is tax-deductible), whereas dividends (preference and equity) are paid out of **after-tax profits** and provide **no tax shield**. This single principle drives the *effective* cost calculations we will derive later.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Cost of Debt (Kd)

Debt can be **irredeemable** (perpetual) or **redeemable** (with a fixed maturity).

#### 2.1.1 Cost of Irredeemable (Perpetual) Debt

For perpetual debt, the cost is simply the annual interest expressed as a percentage of the *net proceeds* of the debt.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** We use *net proceeds* (not face value) because the firm actually receives net proceeds after flotation costs, brokerage, and underwriting discounts. The effective cost is higher than the coupon rate.

#### 2.1.2 Cost of Redeemable Debt

For redeemable debt, the firm must also account for the **capital gain or loss** at redemption (difference between redemption price and net proceeds). The cost is found by solving the **IRR equation** that equates the net proceeds today to the future stream of interest payments and the redemption price.

#### 2.1.3 Tax-Adjusted Cost of Debt

Because interest is a tax-deductible expense, the *effective* cost of debt to the firm is reduced by the tax shield. The corporate tax rate $T$ reduces the outflow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb:** In numerical problems, if the corporate tax rate is not mentioned, **assume it is given or use the standard 25–30%**. The after-tax cost of debt is the figure used in WACC calculations.

---

### 2.2 Cost of Preference Share Capital (Kp)

Preference shares carry a **fixed dividend** and are usually **non-participating** and **non-voting**. They are *hybrid* — they sit between debt and equity.

**Key characteristics:**
- Fixed dividend rate declared at the time of issue
- Dividends are **not tax-deductible** (paid out of after-tax profits)
- Usually redeemable after a specified period
- Rank above equity but below debt in liquidation

The cost of preference capital is the **perpetual dividend yield** based on net proceeds. Since dividends are not tax-deductible, **no tax adjustment is made** to Kp.

---

### 2.3 Cost of Equity Capital (Ke)

Equity is the **most expensive** source of finance because equity-holders bear the **residual risk** — they are paid last, after debt and preference obligations are met. There are three principal methods to estimate Ke:

#### 2.3.1 Dividend Growth Model (Gordon's Model)

This model assumes dividends grow at a constant rate $g$ forever. The current market price $P_0$ equals the present value of all future dividends.

**Key Assumptions:**
- Dividends grow at a constant rate $g$ perpetually
- Growth rate $g$ is less than the cost of equity $K_e$
- The firm has a stable dividend policy

#### 2.3.2 Earnings Yield Approach (Capitalisation Method)

In this approach, the cost of equity is the **earnings yield** — earnings per share divided by the market price. It is used when dividends are not paid or are irregular.

#### 2.3.3 Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)

The most theoretically rigorous approach, discussed in detail below.

---

### 2.4 Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)

CAPM, developed by **William Sharpe (1964)**, John Lintner, and Jan Mossin (building on Harry Markowitz's portfolio theory), describes the relationship between **systematic risk** and the **expected return** on an asset.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Concept:** CAPM is a single-period equilibrium model. It states that the expected return on any risky asset is the **risk-free rate** plus a **risk premium** proportional to the asset's **beta (β)**. The model assumes investors are rational, markets are efficient, and there is a **risk-free lending and borrowing rate**.

#### Components of CAPM:
- **Risk-free rate (Rf):** Return on a default-free asset (e.g., 10-year Government of India bond yield).
- **Market return (Rm):** Expected return on the overall market portfolio (e.g., Nifty 50).
- **Beta (β):** A measure of an asset's **systematic (non-diversifiable) risk** relative to the market. $\beta = 1$ means the asset moves with the market; $\beta > 1$ means more volatile; $\beta < 1$ means less volatile.

#### Key Assumptions of CAPM:
1. Investors are **risk-averse** and **rational**, seeking to maximise utility.
2. Single-period investment horizon.
3. **Homogeneous expectations** — all investors have the same view on expected returns and risks.
4. No **transaction costs** or **taxes**.
5. Assets are **divisible** and perfectly **marketable**.
6. Investors can **lend and borrow at the risk-free rate**.
7. The market portfolio (a value-weighted index of all risky assets) is observable.

#### CAPM Equation:

$$
K_e = R_f + \beta \times (R_m - R_f)
$$

The term $(R_m - R_f)$ is called the **Market Risk Premium (MRP)** — the extra return investors demand for moving from a risk-free asset to the market portfolio.

---

### 2.5 Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)

WACC is the overall cost of capital of the firm, computed as the weighted average of the costs of all sources of financing, where the weights are the **proportions of each source in the capital structure**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Weights Decision — Book Value vs Market Value:** KTU examiners prefer **market value weights** because they reflect the current opportunity cost to investors. Book value weights are acceptable as a simpler alternative.

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Variables / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cost of Irredeemable Debt (pre-tax) | $K_d = \dfrac{I}{NP} \times 100$ | $I$ = annual interest, $NP$ = net proceeds |
| 2 | After-tax Cost of Debt | $K_d(\text{after tax}) = K_d(1 - T)$ | $T$ = corporate tax rate |
| 3 | Cost of Redeemable Debt | $\text{Solve: } NP = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{I(1-T)}{(1+K_d)^t} + \dfrac{RV}{(1+K_d)^n}$ | $RV$ = redemption value, $n$ = years to redemption |
| 4 | Cost of Irredeemable Preference Shares | $K_p = \dfrac{D_p}{NP} \times 100$ | $D_p$ = annual preference dividend |
| 5 | Cost of Redeemable Preference Shares | $\text{Solve: } NP = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{D_p}{(1+K_p)^t} + \dfrac{RV}{(1+K_p)^n}$ | No tax adjustment for preference dividend |
| 6 | Cost of Equity — Gordon's Model | $K_e = \dfrac{D_1}{P_0} + g$ | $D_1$ = expected dividend next year, $g$ = constant growth rate |
| 7 | Cost of Equity — Earnings Yield | $K_e = \dfrac{EPS}{P_0}$ | Used when dividends are not paid |
| 8 | Cost of Equity — CAPM | $K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$ | $\beta$ = systematic risk measure |
| 9 | Weighted Average Cost of Capital | $WACC = w_d K_d(1-T) + w_p K_p + w_e K_e$ | $w_d + w_p + w_e = 1$ |
| 10 | Market Value Weight | $w_i = \dfrac{\text{Market Value of Source } i}{\text{Total Market Value of Firm}}$ | Reflects current opportunity cost |
| 11 | Project Cost of Capital | $K_{\text{project}} = R_f + \beta_{\text{project}} \times (R_m - R_f)$ | Uses project-specific $\beta$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Convention Note:** In all formulas, the symbol $K$ represents the cost (rate), and lowercase subscripts denote the source: $d$ = debt, $p$ = preference, $e$ = equity. Always carry units in **% per annum** unless specified otherwise.

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

While the topic is financial, the discipline of calculating cost of capital is foundational to:

- **Project Appraisal in Engineering Firms:** Engineering companies bidding on infrastructure projects (e.g., L&T, Afcons, BHEL) compute project-specific discount rates to evaluate long-gestation projects.
- **Valuation of Technology Firms:** Startups raising venture capital use CAPM-adjusted discount rates to value IP and intangible assets.
- **Public-Private Partnership (PPP) Models:** Government infrastructure projects (NHAI, metro rail) use WACC-based viability gap funding calculations.
- **R&D Investment Decisions:** Pharmaceutical and semiconductor firms use risk-adjusted project cost of capital to decide whether to invest in new R&D pipelines.

---

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: After-Tax Cost of Irredeemable Debt

Let the firm issue perpetual debt with face value $F$, annual coupon rate $c$, and net proceeds $NP$ (after flotation).

**Step 1: Annual interest paid (pre-tax outflow)**
The firm pays interest $I$ to debenture-holders every year.
$$
I = c \times F
$$

**Step 2: Tax shield from interest**
Since interest is an allowable expense under the Income Tax Act, the firm gets a tax saving of $T \times I$, where $T$ is the corporate tax rate.

**Step 3: Net annual outflow to the firm**
The actual cost to the firm is interest minus tax savings:
$$
\text{Net Interest Outflow} = I - T \times I = I(1 - T)
$$

**Step 4: Express as a percentage of net proceeds**
$$
K_d (\text{after-tax}) = \frac{I(1 - T)}{NP} = \frac{I}{NP} \times (1 - T) = K_d(\text{pre-tax}) \times (1 - T)
$$

**Final Result:**
$$
\boxed{K_d (\text{after-tax}) = K_d(\text{pre-tax}) \times (1 - T)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why divide by NP, not face value?** Because the firm actually receives only the net proceeds (after underwriting commission, broker discount, legal fees). The effective cost of capital is the return on the *cash actually received*, not the face value.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Cost of Equity via Gordon's Dividend Growth Model

**Assumption:** The firm pays a current dividend $D_0$ and is expected to grow dividends at a constant rate $g$ forever. The expected dividend in Year 1 is:
$$
D_1 = D_0(1 + g)
$$

The market price $P_0$ of the share equals the present value of the infinite stream of expected future dividends, discounted at the required rate of return $K_e$.

**Step 1: Write the present value of a growing perpetuity**
$$
P_0 = \frac{D_1}{1+K_e} + \frac{D_2}{(1+K_e)^2} + \frac{D_3}{(1+K_e)^3} + \ldots
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the growth pattern $D_t = D_0(1+g)^t$**
$$
P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{D_0(1+g)^t}{(1+K_e)^t} = \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} D_0 \left( \frac{1+g}{1+K_e} \right)^t
$$

**Step 3: Apply the infinite geometric series formula $\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} x^t = \frac{x}{1-x}$ for $\vert x \vert < 1$**
Let $x = \frac{1+g}{1+K_e}$. For convergence, we need $K_e > g$.
$$
P_0 = \frac{D_0 \cdot \frac{1+g}{1+K_e}}{1 - \frac{1+g}{1+K_e}} = \frac{D_0(1+g)}{1+K_e - (1+g)} = \frac{D_0(1+g)}{K_e - g}
$$

**Step 4: Solve for $K_e$**
$$
P_0 (K_e - g) = D_0(1+g)
$$
$$
K_e - g = \frac{D_0(1+g)}{P_0} = \frac{D_1}{P_0}
$$
$$
\boxed{K_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: CAPM Equation

CAPM rests on two key market equilibrium conditions:
1. All investors hold a combination of the **market portfolio** and the **risk-free asset** (Two-Fund Separation Theorem).
2. In equilibrium, the expected return on any asset equals the risk-free rate plus a risk premium proportional to its systematic risk (beta).

**Step 1: Define the beta of an asset**
Beta measures the covariance of the asset's return with the market's return, scaled by the market variance:
$$
\beta_i = \frac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_m)}{\text{Var}(R_m)}
$$

**Step 2: Express the risk premium of asset $i$ in terms of the market risk premium**
The market portfolio, by definition, has $\beta_m = 1$. For any other asset:
$$
\text{Risk Premium}_i = \beta_i \times \text{Risk Premium}_m
$$
$$
E(R_i) - R_f = \beta_i \times [E(R_m) - R_f]
$$

**Step 3: Rearrange**
$$
\boxed{E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i \times [E(R_m) - R_f]}
$$

This is the **Security Market Line (SML)** equation. The SML plots expected return on the y-axis against beta on the x-axis. Every security in equilibrium lies on the SML.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)

Let the firm have three sources of capital: debt, preference, and equity, with market values $V_d$, $V_p$, $V_e$. Total firm value:
$$
V = V_d + V_p + V_e
$$

The weights are:
$$
w_d = \frac{V_d}{V}, \quad w_p = \frac{V_p}{V}, \quad w_e = \frac{V_e}{V}
$$

By definition, $w_d + w_p + w_e = 1$.

**Step 1: Total expected return to all investors**
The firm must pay $K_d(1-T)$ to debt-holders (after tax), $K_p$ to preference shareholders, and $K_e$ to equity shareholders. The total expected return to all capital providers is:
$$
\text{Total Return} = V_d \cdot K_d(1-T) + V_p \cdot K_p + V_e \cdot K_e
$$

**Step 2: Express as a rate per unit of total firm value**
$$
\frac{\text{Total Return}}{V} = \frac{V_d \cdot K_d(1-T) + V_p \cdot K_p + V_e \cdot K_e}{V}
$$

$$
WACC = w_d K_d(1-T) + w_p K_p + w_e K_e
$$

**Final Result:**
$$
\boxed{WACC = w_d K_d(1-T) + w_p K_p + w_e K_e}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Notice that only the debt component is tax-adjusted. Preference and equity dividends are paid from after-tax profits, so there is no tax shield. Many students incorrectly multiply $K_p$ by $(1-T)$ — this loses marks.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example: Full WACC Computation

**Problem:**
A firm has the following capital structure (market values):
- Debt: ₹4,00,000, coupon rate 10%, net proceeds ₹3,80,000
- Preference shares: ₹1,00,000, dividend rate 8%, net proceeds ₹95,000
- Equity: ₹5,00,000, market price ₹100, expected dividend ₹10 next year, growth rate 5%
- Corporate tax rate: 30%

**Required:** Compute the WACC.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Cost of Debt (pre-tax)**
$$
K_d = \frac{I}{NP} = \frac{10\% \times 4,00,000}{3,80,000} = \frac{40,000}{3,80,000} = 10.526\%
$$

**Step 2: After-tax Cost of Debt**
$$
K_d(1 - T) = 10.526\% \times (1 - 0.30) = 10.526\% \times 0.70 = 7.368\%
$$

**Step 3: Cost of Preference Shares**
$$
K_p = \frac{D_p}{NP} = \frac{8\% \times 1,00,000}{95,000} = \frac{8,000}{95,000} = 8.421\%
$$

**Step 4: Cost of Equity (Gordon's Model)**
$$
K_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g = \frac{10}{100} + 0.05 = 0.10 + 0.05 = 15.0\%
$$

**Step 5: Compute Market Value Weights**
Total firm value: $V = 4,00,000 + 1,00,000 + 5,00,000 = 10,00,000$

$$
w_d = \frac{4,00,000}{10,00,000} = 0.40
$$

$$
w_p = \frac{1,00,000}{10,00,000} = 0.10
$$

$$
w_e = \frac{5,00,000}{10,00,000} = 0.50
$$

**Step 6: Compute WACC**
$$
WACC = (0.40 \times 7.368\%) + (0.10 \times 8.421\%) + (0.50 \times 15.0\%)
$$

$$
WACC = 2.947\% + 0.842\% + 7.50\%
$$

$$
\boxed{WACC = 11.29\%}
$$

---

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example: CAPM

**Problem:**
Given: $R_f = 7\%$, $R_m = 14\%$, $\beta = 1.2$. Find the cost of equity.

**Solution:**
$$
K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f) = 7\% + 1.2 \times (14\% - 7\%)
$$

$$
K_e = 7\% + 1.2 \times 7\% = 7\% + 8.4\%
$$

$$
\boxed{K_e = 15.4\%}
$$

---

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Cost Method

| Scenario | Recommended Method | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Mature, dividend-paying firm | Gordon's Model | Constant dividend growth assumption is realistic |
| Growth-stage startup with no dividends | CAPM or Earnings Yield | Dividends are zero or erratic; CAPM uses risk-based logic |
| Highly leveraged firm | Weighted Cost of Debt (Redeemable) | Need to account for capital gains/losses at redemption |
| Multinational engineering project | CAPM with project-specific beta | International projects carry country and currency risk |
| Govt. project with predictable cash flows | Cost of Debt only | Sovereign guarantee effectively eliminates equity risk |

---

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cost of Capital — Hierarchical Architecture

The following block diagram maps the **structural relationships** between the various components of cost of capital — from the top-level WACC down to the individual sub-components.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["FINANCIAL ENGINEERING - COST OF CAPITAL"] --> B["COMPANY COST OF CAPITAL"]
    A --> C["PROJECT COST OF CAPITAL"]
    B --> D["WACC - Weighted Average"]
    C --> D2["Project Specific Discount Rate"]
    D --> E["Cost of Debt Kd"]
    D --> F["Cost of Preference Kp"]
    D --> G["Cost of Equity Ke"]
    E --> E1["Irredeemable Debt"]
    E --> E2["Redeemable Debt"]
    E2 --> E3["Includes Capital Gain or Loss at Redemption"]
    E --> E4["After Tax Adjustment Kd 1 minus T"]
    F --> F1["Fixed Dividend Perpetual"]
    F --> F2["Redeemable Preference"]
    F --> F3["No Tax Shield"]
    G --> G1["Gordon Dividend Growth Model"]
    G --> G2["Earnings Yield Approach"]
    G --> G3["CAPM Model"]
    G3 --> G4["Risk Free Rate Rf"]
    G3 --> G5["Beta Systematic Risk"]
    G3 --> G6["Market Risk Premium Rm minus Rf"]
    D2 --> D2a["Risk Adjusted Rate"]
    D2 --> D2b["Project Beta Used"]
    D2 --> D2c["CAPM based Project Discounting"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Start at the top — Cost of Capital bifurcates into *Company* and *Project* cost. The company cost is operationalized through the WACC, which has three branches: Kd, Kp, and Ke. Each branch is further decomposed into its sub-methods. The Project Cost of Capital uses a project-specific CAPM-style rate.

---

### 4.2 Security Market Line (SML) — CAPM Visualization

The SML is the graphical representation of CAPM. The following flowchart shows how an asset's expected return is determined by its position relative to the SML.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0["Start: Asset i in Market"] --> A1["Determine Beta of Asset"]
    A1 --> A2{"Compare Beta to 1"}
    A2 -->|"Beta greater than 1"| B1["Asset is Aggressive"]
    A2 -->|"Beta less than 1"| B2["Asset is Defensive"]
    A2 -->|"Beta equals 1"| B3["Asset moves with Market"]
    B1 --> C1["Expected Return greater than Rm"]
    B2 --> C2["Expected Return less than Rm"]
    B3 --> C3["Expected Return equals Rm"]
    C1 --> D1["Lies ABOVE SML for equal beta"]
    C2 --> D2["Lies BELOW SML for equal beta"]
    C3 --> D3["Lies ON the SML"]
    D1 --> E1["Higher Risk Premium Required"]
    D2 --> E2["Lower Risk Premium Required"]
    D3 --> E3["Fairly Priced by Market"]
    E1 --> F1["Apply CAPM: Ke = Rf + Beta x MRP"]
    E2 --> F1
    E3 --> F1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway from SML:** In equilibrium, **all assets must lie on the SML**. Assets plotted above the SML are *undervalued* (offer higher return for their risk), and assets below the SML are *overvalued*. CAPM is the **arbitrage equilibrium condition** that drives market prices toward the SML.

---

### 4.3 WACC Computation — Sequential Workflow

This block diagram depicts the **stepwise procedure** for computing WACC in an exam setting.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1["Step 1: Identify Capital Structure Components"] --> S2["Step 2: Compute Cost of Each Source"]
    S2 --> S3a["Cost of Debt: Kd and Kd x 1 minus T"]
    S2 --> S3b["Cost of Preference: Kp = Dp by NP"]
    S2 --> S3c["Cost of Equity: Gordon or CAPM"]
    S3a --> S4["Step 3: Determine Market Value Weights"]
    S3b --> S4
    S3c --> S4
    S4 --> S5["Step 4: Verify Weights Sum to 1"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 5: Apply WACC Formula"]
    S6 --> S7["Step 6: Express WACC as Percentage per Annum"]
    S7 --> S8["Step 7: Use WACC as Discount Rate for Project Appraisal"]
```

---

### 4.4 Cost of Capital Components — Comparison Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph R1["Cost of Capital Sources"]
    K1["Kd - Debt - Lowest Cost"]
    K2["Kp - Preference - Medium Cost"]
    K3["Ke - Equity - Highest Cost"]
    end
    K1 --> M1["Tax Shield Available"]
    K2 --> M2["No Tax Shield - Fixed Dividend"]
    K3 --> M3["No Tax Shield - Residual Claim"]
    M1 --> N1["Kd x 1 minus T = Effective Rate"]
    M2 --> N2["Kp = Dp by NP"]
    M3 --> N3["Ke = D1 by P0 + g or CAPM"]
    N1 --> O1["Combine via WACC"]
    N2 --> O1
    N3 --> O1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Heuristic:** The diagram visually orders the costs from lowest (Debt) to highest (Equity). The tax-shield property of debt is the single most important factor that keeps its effective cost below the cost of preference or equity, despite debt being the *riskiest* claim in terms of legal obligation.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define Cost of Capital. Distinguish between Company's Cost of Capital and Project Cost of Capital.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Cost of Capital is the minimum rate of return that a firm must earn on its investments to satisfy all providers of capital (debenture-holders, preference shareholders, and equity shareholders) and to maintain the market value of its shares.

**Distinction (2 Marks):**

| Basis | Company Cost of Capital | Project Cost of Capital |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Overall weighted cost of all sources | Cost specific to a particular project |
| **Risk Basis** | Average business risk | Project-specific risk |
| **Application** | Firm-wide valuation, capital budgeting | Project-level NPV / IRR |
| **Method** | WACC | Risk-adjusted discount rate (CAPM with project β) |

**Conclusion:** The company cost is used for general decisions, but projects with risk different from the firm's average require a project-specific cost of capital.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*State the Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) and explain the meaning of each component.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**CAPM Equation (1.5 Marks):**
$$
K_e = R_f + \beta (R_m - R_f)
$$

**Components (1.5 Marks):**
- **$R_f$ (Risk-free rate):** Return on a default-free investment, typically the 10-year Government of India bond yield. Represents the time value of money.
- **$\beta$ (Beta):** A measure of systematic (non-diversifiable) risk. It captures the sensitivity of the asset's returns to market returns. A beta of 1.5 means the asset is 50% more volatile than the market.
- **$R_m$ (Expected market return):** The expected return on a broad market index (e.g., Nifty 50, BSE Sensex).
- **$R_m - R_f$ (Market Risk Premium):** The extra return demanded by investors for moving from a risk-free asset to a diversified market portfolio.

---

### PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

(a) *Explain the concept of Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC). How is it computed? Illustrate with a suitable example.* (7 Marks)

(b) *A company has the following capital structure (all values in ₹ Lakhs, market values):*
- *Equity Share Capital: ₹600 (current market price ₹120, expected dividend ₹12 next year, growth rate 6%)*
- *Preference Share Capital: ₹150 (dividend rate 9%, net proceeds ₹140 per ₹100 share)*
- *Debentures: ₹250 (coupon 11%, net proceeds ₹240 per ₹1,000 debenture)*

*Corporate tax rate is 30%. Compute the WACC and interpret the result.* (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Concept of WACC (3 Marks):**
WACC is the overall cost of capital of a firm, computed as the weighted average of the costs of various sources of finance (debt, preference, equity), where weights are the proportions of each source in the capital structure.

**Formula:**
$$
WACC = w_d K_d(1 - T) + w_p K_p + w_e K_e
$$

**Computation Procedure (2 Marks):**
1. Compute the cost of each source (Kd, Kp, Ke).
2. Determine the weights (market value preferred).
3. Apply the WACC formula.

**Illustration (2 Marks):** A firm with 40% debt at 8% after-tax, 10% preference at 9%, and 50% equity at 14% will have:
$$
WACC = (0.40)(8\%) + (0.10)(9\%) + (0.50)(14\%) = 3.2\% + 0.9\% + 7.0\% = 11.1\%
$$

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1: Cost of Equity (Gordon's Model)**
$$
K_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g = \frac{12}{120} + 0.06 = 0.10 + 0.06 = 16.0\%
$$

**Step 2: Cost of Preference Shares**
$$
K_p = \frac{D_p}{NP} = \frac{9\% \times 100}{140} = \frac{9}{140} = 6.43\%
$$

**Step 3: Cost of Debt (pre-tax)**
$$
K_d = \frac{I}{NP} = \frac{11\% \times 1,000}{240} = \frac{110}{240} = 45.83\%\text{ ???}
$$

*Correction (interpretation):* The debt issue at ₹240 per ₹1,000 face value is at a **deep discount**, reflecting distress. The effective pre-tax cost is unusually high. Let us verify:
$$
K_d = \frac{11}{240} = 4.58\%
$$

*Clarification:* ₹240 is the net proceeds per ₹100 face value (not ₹1,000). Re-reading: "net proceeds ₹240 per ₹1,000 debenture" means ₹240/₹1,000 = 24% of face. So the **coupon of 11% on face** translates to:
$$
K_d(\text{pre-tax}) = \frac{11\% \times 1,000}{240} = \frac{110}{240} = 45.83\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** The wording "₹240 per ₹1,000 debenture" is **ambiguous**. In KTU papers, this usually means ₹240 is received per ₹1,000 face value (i.e., issued at a heavy discount of 76%). Verify the wording carefully. The student must clearly state the assumed interpretation in the answer.

**Step 4: After-tax Cost of Debt**
$$
K_d(1 - T) = 45.83\% \times (1 - 0.30) = 45.83\% \times 0.70 = 32.08\%
$$

**Step 5: Market Value Weights**
Total value: $V = 600 + 150 + 250 = 1,000$ Lakhs

$$
w_e = \frac{600}{1,000} = 0.60, \quad w_p = \frac{150}{1,000} = 0.15, \quad w_d = \frac{250}{1,000} = 0.25
$$

**Step 6: Compute WACC**
$$
WACC = (0.25)(32.08\%) + (0.15)(6.43\%) + (0.60)(16.0\%)
$$

$$
WACC = 8.02\% + 0.96\% + 9.60\% = 18.58\%
$$

**Interpretation:**
The high WACC of 18.58% indicates the firm is extremely risky and should only undertake projects that promise returns significantly above this hurdle rate. The deep discount on debentures suggests the market perceives high default risk.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

(a) *What is CAPM? Discuss its assumptions and significance. How does it differ from the Dividend Growth Model?* (7 Marks)

(b) *For a stock, the risk-free rate is 6%, the market return is 13%, and the stock's beta is 1.4. The company has issued bonds at 9% (pre-tax) with a corporate tax rate of 25%. The capital structure is 60% equity and 40% debt (market value). Compute the WACC of the firm.* (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
CAPM, developed by William Sharpe (1964), is an equilibrium model that relates the expected return on an asset to its systematic risk (beta). The equation is:
$$
E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i (R_m - R_f)
$$

**Assumptions (3 Marks):**
1. Investors are **risk-averse** and **rational**, with a single-period horizon.
2. **Homogeneous expectations** about returns, risks, and correlations.
3. **Perfect capital markets**: No taxes, transaction costs, or restrictions.
4. **Risk-free borrowing and lending** at rate $R_f$.
5. All assets are **divisible** and **marketable**.
6. The **market portfolio** is observable and consists of all risky assets.

**Significance (1.5 Marks):**
- Provides a **theoretically sound** link between risk and required return.
- Helps in **performance evaluation** of portfolios (Jensen's Alpha).
- Forms the basis for **risk-adjusted discount rates** in capital budgeting.

**Difference from Dividend Growth Model (1 Mark):**

| Basis | CAPM | Dividend Growth Model |
|---|---|---|
| Input | Risk-free rate, beta, market return | Current dividend, growth rate, market price |
| Risk Considered | Systematic (beta) | Implicit in market price |
| Best Used For | Non-dividend / volatile firms | Stable, dividend-paying firms |

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Step 1: Cost of Equity using CAPM**
$$
K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f) = 6\% + 1.4 \times (13\% - 6\%) = 6\% + 1.4 \times 7\%
$$

$$
K_e = 6\% + 9.8\% = 15.8\%
$$

**Step 2: After-tax Cost of Debt**
$$
K_d(1 - T) = 9\% \times (1 - 0.25) = 9\% \times 0.75 = 6.75\%
$$

**Step 3: Market Value Weights**
$$
w_e = 0.60, \quad w_d = 0.40
$$

**Step 4: Compute WACC**
$$
WACC = (0.40)(6.75\%) + (0.60)(15.8\%) = 2.70\% + 9.48\%
$$

$$
\boxed{WACC = 12.18\%}
$$

**Interpretation:** The firm's overall cost of capital is 12.18%, which means the firm should accept only those projects whose IRR exceeds 12.18% to create value for shareholders.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 1. **Forgetting the tax shield on debt** — always multiply $K_d$ by $(1 - T)$. **(Loses 1-2 marks)**
> 2. **Applying tax shield to preference or equity dividends** — they are paid from after-tax profits. **(Loses 1 mark)**
> 3. **Using face value instead of net proceeds** — when flotation costs are given, you *must* use net proceeds. **(Loses 1 mark)**
> 4. **Not showing the IRR equation setup for redeemable debt** — examiners look for the equation $NP = \sum \frac{I(1-T)}{(1+K_d)^t} + \frac{RV}{(1+K_d)^n}$. **(Loses 2 marks)**
> 5. **Mismatching units** — expressing some components in decimal (0.10) and others in percentage (10%). Be consistent.
> 6. **In CAPM, writing beta × Rm instead of beta × (Rm - Rf)** — the risk premium is $R_m - R_f$, not $R_m$ alone. **(Loses 2 marks)**
> 7. **Not specifying book value vs market value weights** — state clearly which you are using.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Cost of Capital = Minimum required return** for the firm to satisfy all capital providers.
- **WACC** is the weighted average of $K_d(1-T)$, $K_p$, and $K_e$, weighted by market value proportions.
- **Cost of Debt is the cheapest** because of the **tax shield** (interest is tax-deductible).
- **Cost of Preference (Kp)** has **no tax shield**; preference dividends are paid from post-tax profits.
- **Cost of Equity (Ke)** is the **most expensive** because equity-holders bear residual risk.
- **Gordon's Model:** $K_e = \dfrac{D_1}{P_0} + g$ — works only when dividends grow at a constant rate.
- **CAPM:** $K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$ — uses systematic risk; works for all types of firms.
- **Beta > 1** → aggressive (more volatile than market); **Beta < 1** → defensive; **Beta = 0** → risk-free equivalent.
- **Project Cost of Capital** uses a **project-specific beta**; do *not* apply firm-wide WACC to high-risk or low-risk projects indiscriminately.
- **Market value weights** are preferred over book value weights as they reflect current opportunity cost.
- **Assumptions of CAPM** are idealised (no taxes, no transaction costs) — limitations must be acknowledged in long-answer questions.
- **Security Market Line (SML)** is the graphical depiction of CAPM; all assets must lie on SML in equilibrium.
- **Numerical problems** typically require showing the IRR equation setup for redeemable debt — practice this format.
- **WACC is the discount rate** used in NPV calculations for the firm's average-risk projects.
- **KTU Hot Tip:** Always state the **assumed tax rate** if not given; do not leave it blank.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Working Capital - Factors Affecting Capital Requirements
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET602 - FINANCIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Working Capital - Factors Affecting Capital Requirements

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Working Capital

In KTU 2024 Scheme Financial Engineering terminology, **Working Capital** refers to the capital required by an enterprise to finance the day-to-day operations of a business. It represents the firm's investment in short-term assets used to support its operational cycle.

There are two standard academic interpretations:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Foundation (KTU Board-Examiner Definition)**
>
> **Gross Working Capital (GWC):** The aggregate of all current assets of a firm, including cash, marketable securities, accounts receivable, inventory, and prepaid expenses.
>
> **Net Working Capital (NWC):** The excess of current assets over current liabilities. It represents the long-term funds used to finance current assets.

$$
GWC = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Current Assets}_i
$$

$$
NWC = \text{Current Assets} - \text{Current Liabilities}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Under the **Operating Cycle Concept**, working capital is more rigorously defined as the funds locked up from the procurement of raw materials to the realization of cash from sales.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are running a small **tea stall in Kerala**.

Every morning, you must purchase tea leaves, milk, sugar, and cups (raw materials → inventory). You boil the tea, serve it to customers (production → sales), and at the end of the day, you collect cash in your hand (receivables → cash realization).

During this entire journey:
- Your **money is locked up** in milk you bought at 6 AM but won't sell until 8 AM.
- You have given **credit** to a regular customer who pays only at month-end.
- You must keep some **change ready** to give back to customers.

This locked-up money — needed to keep the operations moving smoothly — is essentially **Working Capital**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** *Working Capital = The "rotating money" needed to keep the engine of business running between pay-in and pay-out.*

---

### 1.3 Types of Working Capital

| Type | Definition | Nature |
|---|---|---|
| **Permanent / Fixed Working Capital** | Minimum level of current assets maintained at all times to ensure uninterrupted operations | Long-term, stable |
| **Temporary / Variable Working Capital** | Additional working capital required to meet seasonal or cyclical demand surges | Short-term, fluctuating |
| **Gross Working Capital** | Total current assets | Quantitative |
| **Net Working Capital** | Current Assets − Current Liabilities | Qualitative (solvency indicator) |

---

### 1.4 Operating Cycle — The Backbone of Working Capital

The **Operating Cycle (OC)** is the average time interval between the acquisition of raw materials and the final cash realization from sales.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Every working capital decision must be anchored to the length and structure of the Operating Cycle. A longer cycle = higher working capital requirement.

The standard operating cycle consists of four sequential phases:

1. **Raw Material Storage Period (R)** — Time raw materials remain in inventory.
2. **Work-in-Progress Period (W)** — Time goods spend in production.
3. **Finished Goods Storage Period (F)** — Time finished goods wait before sale.
4. **Receivables / Debtors Collection Period (D)** — Time taken to collect cash from credit sales.

---

### 1.5 Standard Metrics in **Bold**

- **Operating Cycle = 365 days (maximum, full year)**
- **Current Ratio Benchmark = 2 : 1** (ideal liquidity indicator)
- **Quick Ratio Benchmark = 1 : 1** (acid-test liquidity)
- **Cash Conversion Cycle (CCC)** — Net time cash is tied up in operations

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Need for Working Capital

A business enterprise requires working capital because of the **time gap between expenditure and receipts**. Firms must pay for raw materials, wages, and overheads long before they receive cash from the sale of finished products.

The **theoretical rationale** rests on three pillars:

1. **Liquidity vs. Profitability Trade-off:** Excess working capital means idle funds (low return); inadequate working capital means operational breakdown (low revenue).
2. **The Matching Principle:** Working capital financing should match the maturity of the asset with the maturity of the source.
3. **Operating Cycle Synchronization:** Working capital requirement is a direct function of operating cycle duration.

---

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Factors Affecting Capital Requirements

> [!NOTE]
> **The Why:** Every factor alters either the *timing* or the *quantum* of cash outflows vs. cash inflows, thereby changing how much capital must remain locked in operations.

> [!NOTE]
> **The How:** Factors either **increase** the length of the operating cycle, **increase** per-unit cost, or create **uncertainty** in cash flows.

---

### 2.3 Comprehensive List of Factors Affecting Working Capital Requirements

The KTU 2024 Scheme recognizes **15 canonical factors** classified into **internal** and **external** categories.

#### A. Internal Factors

| # | Factor | Effect on Working Capital | Logic |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Nature / Type of Business** | Manufacturing: High; Trading: Moderate; Service: Low | Production-based businesses hold more inventory |
| 2 | **Size / Scale of Operations** | Larger firm → Higher WC (proportionate) | Higher turnover requires higher absolute current assets |
| 3 | **Production Cycle Duration** | Longer cycle → Higher WC | Money locked up in WIP for longer time |
| 4 | **Length of Operating Cycle** | Direct positive correlation | Each day added = one extra day of locked capital |
| 5 | **Business / Product Mix** | Diversified → Higher WC | Multiple product lines require varied inventory |
| 6 | **Credit Policy (Sales Terms)** | Liberal credit → Higher WC | Larger receivables portfolio tied up |
| 7 | **Credit Availment (Purchase Terms)** | Liberal credit obtained → Lower WC | Suppliers finance part of inventory |
| 8 | **Operating Efficiency** | Higher efficiency → Lower WC | Faster turnover, lower per-unit holding |
| 9 | **Dividend Policy** | Higher dividend payout → Higher WC need | Cash leaves the firm → reduces internal accruals |
| 10 | **Profit Margin / Profitability** | Higher margin → Lower WC need (per unit) | Less capital locked per rupee of sales |

#### B. External Factors

| # | Factor | Effect on Working Capital | Logic |
|---|---|---|---|
| 11 | **Seasonality of Operations** | Seasonal firms → Fluctuating WC | Peak season demands surge in inventory + receivables |
| 12 | **Business Cycle Fluctuations** | Boom → Higher WC; Recession → Lower WC | Demand and supply volumes vary cyclically |
| 13 | **Price Level Changes (Inflation)** | Rising prices → Higher WC | Same volume costs more; same cash buys less |
| 14 | **Taxation Policy** | Higher taxes → Higher WC | Cash outflow for tax reduces internal accruals |
| 15 | **Technology & Production Methods** | Advanced → Lower WC (often) | Faster cycles, leaner inventory |

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All symbols use \vert for absolute value to preserve markdown table syntax.**

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $NWC = CA - CL$ | Net Working Capital | ₹ / Rs. |
| 2 | $CR = \dfrac{CA}{CL}$ | Current Ratio (Ideal = 2:1) | Ratio |
| 3 | $QR = \dfrac{CA - \text{Inventory} - \text{Prepaid}}{CL}$ | Quick / Acid-Test Ratio (Ideal = 1:1) | Ratio |
| 4 | $OC = R + W + F + D$ | Operating Cycle | Days |
| 5 | $R = \dfrac{\text{Avg. RM Inventory}}{\text{RM Consumed per day}}$ | Raw Material Storage Period | Days |
| 6 | $W = \dfrac{\text{Avg. WIP Inventory}}{\text{Production Cost per day}}$ | Work-in-Progress Period | Days |
| 7 | $F = \dfrac{\text{Avg. FG Inventory}}{\text{COGS per day}}$ | Finished Goods Storage Period | Days |
| 8 | $D = \dfrac{\text{Avg. Debtors}}{\text{Credit Sales per day}}$ | Receivables Collection Period | Days |
| 9 | $CCC = OC - \text{Payable Deferral Period}$ | Cash Conversion Cycle | Days |
| 10 | $WC_{\text{required}} = \dfrac{OC}{365} \times \text{Annual Operating Cost}$ | Working Capital Estimate | ₹ / Rs. |
| 11 | $CA_{\text{Total}} = \dfrac{OC}{365} \times COGS$ | Total Current Assets (for given OC) | ₹ / Rs. |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering / Industry Utility

- **Manufacturing Plants (e.g., Steel, FMCG):** Working capital decisions determine plant liquidity and inventory pile-up risk.
- **Infrastructure / Construction Firms:** Long operating cycles mean WC is often the largest financing challenge.
- **IT & Service Sectors:** Operating cycles are short → minimal WC needed → mostly cash-based.
- **Banks & NBFCs:** Working capital loans are a primary lending product; understanding factors is critical for credit appraisal.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Operating Cycle to Working Capital Requirement

We derive the relationship between the operating cycle and the gross working capital needed.

**Step 1: Define the Annual Operating Outflow (COGS).**
Let the annual cost of goods sold (excluding depreciation) be $C_{\text{annual}}$, in ₹.

**Step 2: Compute Daily Operating Outflow.**
The firm spends $C_{\text{annual}}$ evenly across 365 operating days.

$$
C_{\text{daily}} = \frac{C_{\text{annual}}}{365}
$$

**Step 3: Interpret Operating Cycle as "Days of Lock-up".**
An operating cycle of $OC$ days means that, on average, one full $OC$ days of operating cost remains locked in current assets at any moment.

**Step 4: Express Gross Working Capital.**

$$
GWC = OC \times C_{\text{daily}}
$$

$$
GWC = \frac{OC \times C_{\text{annual}}}{365}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Multiply daily cost by number of days money is tied up → total locked-up capital.

---

### 3.2 Detailed Numerical Illustration

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example — KTU Board Style**
>
> A firm reports the following annual figures:
> - Raw Material Consumption: ₹ 36,50,000
> - WIP Production Cost: ₹ 54,75,000
> - Cost of Goods Sold (COGS): ₹ 73,00,000
> - Credit Sales: ₹ 1,09,50,000
> - Average RM Inventory: ₹ 3,00,000
> - Average WIP Inventory: ₹ 4,50,000
> - Average FG Inventory: ₹ 6,00,000
> - Average Debtors: ₹ 9,00,000
> - Average Creditors (RM): ₹ 2,00,000

**Step 1 — Raw Material Storage Period ($R$):**

$$
R = \frac{\text{Avg. RM Inventory}}{\text{RM consumed per day}} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{\left(36{,}50{,}000 / 365\right)}
$$

$$
R = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{10{,}000} = 30 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 2 — WIP Period ($W$):**

$$
W = \frac{4{,}50{,}000}{\left(54{,}75{,}000 / 365\right)} = \frac{4{,}50{,}000}{15{,}000} = 30 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 3 — Finished Goods Period ($F$):**

$$
F = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{\left(73{,}00{,}000 / 365\right)} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{20{,}000} = 30 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 4 — Receivables Period ($D$):**

$$
D = \frac{9{,}00{,}000}{\left(1{,}09{,}50{,}000 / 365\right)} = \frac{9{,}00{,}000}{30{,}000} = 30 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 5 — Payable Deferral Period ($P$):**

$$
P = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{\left(36{,}50{,}000 / 365\right)} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{10{,}000} = 20 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 6 — Operating Cycle ($OC$):**

$$
OC = R + W + F + D
$$

$$
OC = 30 + 30 + 30 + 30 = 120 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 7 — Cash Conversion Cycle ($CCC$):**

$$
CCC = OC - P = 120 - 20 = 100 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 8 — Working Capital Required ($WC$):**

$$
WC = \frac{OC}{365} \times COGS = \frac{120}{365} \times 73{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
WC = \frac{12 \times 73{,}000}{3.65} = 24{,}00{,}000 \text{ rupees}
$$

**Step 9 — Cash-Based WC (using $CCC$):**

$$
WC_{\text{cash}} = \frac{CCC}{365} \times COGS = \frac{100}{365} \times 73{,}00{,}000 = 20{,}00{,}000 \text{ rupees}
$$

**Step 10 — Interpretation:**
The firm effectively needs ₹ **20,00,000** in cash-based working capital, but ₹ **24,00,000** in total current asset investment. Suppliers fund the gap of ₹ 4,00,000.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Factor Impact Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix maps each factor to its operational phase impact:

| Factor | Affects $R$? | Affects $W$? | Affects $F$? | Affects $D$? | Affects $P$? |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Nature of Business | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | — |
| Production Cycle | — | ✔ | — | — | — |
| Credit Policy (Sales) | — | — | — | ✔ | — |
| Credit Availment (Purchase) | — | — | — | — | ✔ |
| Operating Efficiency | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | — |
| Seasonality | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | — |
| Inflation | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| Profitability | — | — | — | — | — |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Operating Cycle Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["CASH OUTFLOW"] --> nodeB["RAW MATERIAL PURCHASE"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["RAW MATERIAL INVENTORY"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["WORK IN PROGRESS"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["FINISHED GOODS INVENTORY"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["CREDIT SALES / DEBTORS"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["CASH INFLOW REALIZATION"]
    nodeG -.->|Reinvested| nodeA

    nodeC -.->|"R days"| nodeD
    nodeD -.->|"W days"| nodeE
    nodeE -.->|"F days"| nodeF
    nodeF -.->|"D days"| nodeG

    nodeH["SUPPLIERS CREDIT"] -.->|"P days credit"| nodeB
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dotted arrows represent *time delays* in days. The total journey from `nodeA` to `nodeG` equals the Operating Cycle (OC = R + W + F + D). The credit obtained from suppliers (`nodeH`) reduces the effective cash cycle to the Cash Conversion Cycle (CCC = OC − P).

---

### 4.2 Working Capital Decision Framework (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inputA["External Factors"] --> procA["Working Capital Requirement Engine"]
    inputB["Internal Factors"] --> procA
    inputC["Operating Cycle Length"] --> procA
    inputD["Operating Cost Volume"] --> procA

    procA --> outA["Permanent WC"]
    procA --> outB["Temporary WC"]
    procA --> outC["Gross Working Capital"]
    procA --> outD["Net Working Capital"]

    outC --> finance1["Short-term Financing"]
    outD --> finance2["Long-term Financing Decision"]

    finance1 --> toolA["Bank Overdraft"]
    finance1 --> toolB["Cash Credit"]
    finance1 --> toolC["Commercial Paper"]
    finance1 --> toolD["Trade Credit"]

    finance2 --> decisionA["Hedging Approach"]
    finance2 --> decisionB["Conservative Approach"]
    finance2 --> decisionC["Aggressive Approach"]
```

---

### 4.3 Factor-Impact Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    f1["Business Nature"] --> m1["Inventory Level"]
    f2["Production Cycle"] --> m2["WIP Duration"]
    f3["Credit Policy"] --> m3["Receivables Volume"]
    f4["Credit Availment"] --> m4["Payable Period"]
    f5["Inflation"] --> m5["Cost of Holding"]
    f6["Seasonality"] --> m6["Peak Demand Lockup"]
    f7["Operating Efficiency"] --> m7["Turnover Velocity"]
    f8["Dividend Policy"] --> m8["Retained Earnings"]

    m1 --> outX["Working Capital Requirement"]
    m2 --> outX
    m3 --> outX
    m4 --> outX
    m5 --> outX
    m6 --> outX
    m7 --> outX
    m8 --> outX
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. Define Working Capital. Distinguish between Gross Working Capital and Net Working Capital.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Working capital refers to the capital required to finance the day-to-day operations of a business.

- **Gross Working Capital** is the total of all current assets (cash, receivables, inventory).
- **Net Working Capital** is the excess of current assets over current liabilities and indicates the firm's liquidity position and the portion of long-term funds invested in current assets.

*Key Point:* Gross WC emphasizes *investment* in current assets; Net WC emphasizes *financing* quality.

---

**Q2. State any three internal factors that affect working capital requirements.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Nature of Business** — Manufacturing firms require more working capital than service firms.
2. **Length of Operating Cycle** — Longer cycles require more working capital.
3. **Credit Policy** — Liberal credit sales policy increases receivables and hence working capital requirement.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay / Numerical Questions (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – University Pattern, with Internal Choice]**

---

#### **Question A — (14 Marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Sample Pattern]`**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various **internal and external factors** affecting working capital requirements of a business enterprise. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A manufacturing company provides the following data:

| Item | Value (₹) |
|---|---|
| Average RM Inventory | 4,00,000 |
| Average WIP Inventory | 6,00,000 |
| Average FG Inventory | 5,00,000 |
| Average Debtors | 8,00,000 |
| Average Creditors | 3,00,000 |
| Annual RM Consumed | 48,00,000 |
| Annual Production Cost | 72,00,000 |
| Annual COGS | 96,00,000 |
| Annual Credit Sales | 1,20,00,000 |

Compute the **Operating Cycle**, **Cash Conversion Cycle**, and **Working Capital required**. *(7 Marks)*

---

##### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Factors Affecting Working Capital:**

**Internal Factors:**

1. **Nature / Type of Business** — Manufacturing units hold substantial raw materials, WIP, and finished goods → high WC. Trading firms hold less → moderate WC. Service firms hold almost no inventory → low WC.
2. **Size of Operations** — Larger firms require higher absolute WC, though proportion may remain constant or decline due to economies of scale.
3. **Production Cycle** — Longer production cycle = more time capital is locked in WIP → higher WC.
4. **Operating Cycle Length** — Direct positive correlation with WC.
5. **Credit Policy (Sales)** — Liberal credit terms lengthen receivables cycle → higher WC.
6. **Credit Availment (Purchases)** — Liberal credit from suppliers reduces net WC.
7. **Operating Efficiency** — Higher efficiency reduces holding periods, lowering WC.
8. **Dividend Policy** — High payouts reduce internal accruals → increased WC need.
9. **Profitability / Profit Margin** — Higher margins mean more cash generation → reduced WC need per unit.

**External Factors:**

10. **Seasonality** — Seasonal demand (e.g., apparel, agriculture) creates predictable WC surges.
11. **Business Cycle / Economic Conditions** — Boom → higher WC; Recession → lower WC.
12. **Price Level Changes (Inflation)** — Inflation increases replacement cost of inventory → higher WC.
13. **Taxation Policy** — High taxes drain cash → higher external WC financing needed.
14. **Technological Change** — Automation reduces inventory and processing time → lower WC.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Daily Cost Calculations:**

$$
\text{RM/day} = \frac{48{,}00{,}000}{365} = 13{,}150.68
$$

$$
\text{Production cost/day} = \frac{72{,}00{,}000}{365} = 19{,}726.03
$$

$$
\text{COGS/day} = \frac{96{,}00{,}000}{365} = 26{,}301.37
$$

$$
\text{Credit sales/day} = \frac{1{,}20{,}00{,}000}{365} = 32{,}876.71
$$

**Step 2 — Storage Periods:**

$$
R = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{13{,}150.68} = 30.42 \text{ days}
$$

$$
W = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{19{,}726.03} = 30.42 \text{ days}
$$

$$
F = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{26{,}301.37} = 19.01 \text{ days}
$$

$$
D = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{32{,}876.71} = 24.33 \text{ days}
$$

$$
P = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{13{,}150.68} = 22.81 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 3 — Operating Cycle:**

$$
OC = 30.42 + 30.42 + 19.01 + 24.33 = 104.18 \approx 104 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 4 — Cash Conversion Cycle:**

$$
CCC = OC - P = 104.18 - 22.81 = 81.37 \approx 81 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 5 — Working Capital Required (GWC basis):**

$$
WC = \frac{104}{365} \times 96{,}00{,}000 = 27{,}35{,}342 \text{ rupees}
$$

**Step 6 — Cash-Based WC:**

$$
WC_{\text{cash}} = \frac{81}{365} \times 96{,}00{,}000 = 21{,}30{,}411 \text{ rupees}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Students frequently **forget to deduct creditors (P)** when computing cash-based working capital. Always compute both GWC and cash-based WC separately.
> - Avoid mixing up *credit sales* with *total sales* in the denominator of $D$. If credit sales data is unavailable, total sales may be used with examiner approval.
> - Always show **daily cost calculations** as an explicit step — examiners award partial marks for this.

---

#### **Question B — (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**`[KTU University Exam – Sample Internal Choice]`**

**(a)** "Working capital is not a one-size-fits-all concept." Discuss this statement with reference to **gross vs. net working capital**, and explain how the choice between the two affects financial decision-making. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A firm has an Operating Cycle of **135 days** and a Payable Deferral Period of **30 days**. The annual cost of goods sold is ₹ **82,00,000**. Compute:

- (i) Cash Conversion Cycle
- (ii) Gross Working Capital
- (iii) Net Working Capital (assume current liabilities = ₹ 6,00,000)

*(7 Marks)*

---

##### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Conceptual Discussion:**

The statement emphasizes that working capital must be analyzed in both **quantitative** (gross) and **qualitative** (net) terms.

**Gross Working Capital (GWC):**
- Represents the total investment in current assets.
- Useful for planning the *quantum* of funds required.
- Focus: Adequacy of current assets for operational smoothness.

**Net Working Capital (NWC):**
- Represents the long-term funds committed to current assets.
- A positive NWC indicates liquidity; negative NWC indicates potential insolvency risk.
- Focus: Solvency and financial health assessment.

**Implications for Decision-Making:**
- A firm with high GWC but low NWC is relying heavily on short-term financing → risky.
- A firm with optimal NWC (~25–30% of total assets) balances liquidity and profitability.
- Strategic decisions such as **dividend payout, capital budgeting, and financing mix** depend on whether gross or net perspective is adopted.

**Conclusion:** Both views are complementary; prudent financial engineering integrates both.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Cash Conversion Cycle:**

$$
CCC = OC - P = 135 - 30 = 105 \text{ days}
$$

**(ii) Gross Working Capital:**

$$
GWC = \frac{OC}{365} \times COGS = \frac{135}{365} \times 82{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
GWC = 0.3699 \times 82{,}00{,}000 = 30{,}32{,}877 \text{ rupees}
$$

**(iii) Net Working Capital:**

$$
NWC = \text{Current Assets} - \text{Current Liabilities}
$$

Assuming GWC ≈ Current Assets (a standard simplification),

$$
NWC = 30{,}32{,}877 - 6{,}00{,}000 = 24{,}32{,}877 \text{ rupees}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (b):**
> - **Common Mistake:** Students calculate GWC by dividing by 360 instead of 365. **Always use 365** unless the question specifies banking year (360 days).
> - **Unit Error:** The answer must be in **rupees (₹)**, not lakhs or crores, unless specifically instructed.
> - **Logical Pitfall:** Do NOT compute NWC by subtracting OC from CCC. NWC requires *balance sheet* data, not just operating cycle data.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4**

- **Working Capital** = Capital locked in current assets for daily operations.
- **GWC** = Σ Current Assets; **NWC** = Current Assets − Current Liabilities.
- **Operating Cycle (OC)** = $R + W + F + D$.
- **Cash Conversion Cycle (CCC)** = $OC - P$ (Payable Deferral Period).
- **Working Capital Formula:** $WC = (OC / 365) \times COGS$.
- **15 Factors** are categorized into **9 Internal** and **6 External** — memorize with the **"Nature-Cycle-Credit-Inflation"** acronym.
- **Current Ratio** ideal = **2 : 1**; **Quick Ratio** ideal = **1 : 1**.
- A firm with **negative NWC** is technically solvent but operationally risky.
- **Inflation** universally *increases* WC requirement across all phases.
- **Operating efficiency** is the single most controllable factor for WC reduction.
- Always distinguish between **credit sales** vs. **total sales** when computing the receivables period.
- **Suppliers' credit (P)** reduces the *cash* burden of WC but not the *gross* WC.
- **Permanent WC** is financed by long-term sources; **Temporary WC** by short-term sources.
- In KTU numericals, **always show daily cost** as an intermediate step to earn full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cash Management - Models of Cash Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET602 - FINANCIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cash Management - Models of Cash Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cash Management** is the corporate process of collecting, managing, and disbursing cash in a manner that maximizes the firm's **liquidity** (ability to meet short-term obligations) while minimizing the **opportunity cost** of holding idle cash and the **transaction cost** of converting investments into cash.

Within Cash Management, the **Models of Cash Management** (also termed **Cash Optimization Models** or **Inventory-Theoretic Models of Cash**) are mathematical frameworks used to determine the **optimal cash balance** — the precise amount of cash a firm should hold at any point in time to minimize the total cost of cash management. These models treat cash just like inventory and apply **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** logic.

The three canonical KTU-board-tested models are:

| Model | Origin / Year | Assumes Variability? | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Baumol's Model** | William J. Baumol (1952) | Deterministic, Constant Demand | Predictable, stable cash outflows |
| **Miller-Orr Model** | Merton H. Miller & Daniel Orr (1966) | Stochastic, Random Demand | Uncertain, fluctuating cash flows |
| **Stone Model** | Bernell K. Stone (1972) | Stochastic, Time-Series (ARIMA) | Forecasting-based digital era firms |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Water Tank" Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine your firm's bank account is a **water tank**.
> - Every day, water **leaks out** (salaries, supplier payments = cash outflows).
> - To refill, you must call a **water-tanker** that charges a fixed delivery fee plus gives you less water (interest foregone = opportunity cost).
> - If you call the tanker **too often** (small refills) → delivery fees pile up (**transaction cost ↑**).
> - If you call it **too rarely** (huge refills) → the tank holds too much water, and you "lose" the interest it could have earned (**opportunity cost ↑**).
> - The **optimal refill size** is the one that **balances** the delivery charges against the lost interest.
> - Baumol solves this when the leak is **constant**; Miller-Orr solves it when the leak is **random** (some days big, some days small).

## 1.3 Why Cash Management Models Matter (Engineering Perspective)

Even though this is a finance course, the optimization logic is identical to **inventory control in production engineering** and **buffer management in industrial systems** — the same calculus a mechanical engineer uses for tool-replacement intervals or a civil engineer uses for reservoir sizing.

**Standard performance metrics used in the formulas:**

- **b** = Fixed transaction cost per conversion (₹) — *fixed cost of selling marketable securities or borrowing*
- **T** = Total annual cash demand (₹/year) — *annual need*
- **I** or **i** = Opportunity cost of holding cash (decimal, e.g., 0.08 = 8% p.a.)
- **C\*** = Optimal cash balance per transaction (₹)
- **σ²** = Variance of daily net cash flows (₹²)
- **L** = Lower control limit (₹)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Model 1 — Baumol's Model (All-Cash / EOQ Model)

### 2.1.1 Assumptions (board-favourite 2-mark question)

1. Firm uses cash at a **constant, predictable rate** (T per year).
2. Cash is **replenished instantly** by selling marketable securities.
3. There are **no cash receipts** during the period — only disbursements.
4. Transaction cost **b** and opportunity cost **i** are known & constant.
5. Firm holds **no safety stock** of cash.

### 2.1.2 Logic — The U-Shaped Total Cost

When the firm withdraws **C** every time it needs cash:

- **Number of conversions per year:** N = T / C
- **Average cash held:** C / 2
- **Transaction cost (TC₁):** b × (T / C) — *falls as C rises*
- **Opportunity cost (TC₂):** i × (C / 2) — *rises as C rises*
- **Total cost:** TC(C) = b(T/C) + i(C/2)

Set dTC/dC = 0 → minimum total cost.

### 2.1.3 Engineering Insight

The shape of TC(C) is a **hyperbola + a straight line → U-shaped curve**. The minimum lies where the slopes are equal in magnitude (the **economic balancing point**). This is mathematically identical to the **EOQ** derivation in Operations Research (Wilson’s formula).

## 2.2 Model 2 — Miller-Orr Model (Random Cash Flows)

### 2.2.1 Assumptions

1. Net cash flows are **random (normally distributed)** around a stable mean.
2. Daily variance σ² of net cash flow is known & constant.
3. Firm sets a **Lower Limit (L)** and lets cash fluctuate randomly.
4. When cash hits the **Upper Limit (U)**, the firm buys securities to bring balance back to the **Return Point (Z)**.

### 2.2.2 The Two Decision Variables

- **L (Lower Limit):** Usually set by management judgement or by the firm’s minimum operating cash cushion.
- **Z (Return Point / Optimal Balance):** Calculated from formula.

### 2.2.3 Engineering Insight

Miller-Orr is essentially a **stochastic control system with a two-sided deadband** — the same logic used in **thermostatic temperature control** and **PID controllers** in instrumentation engineering. The firm "corrects" only when the signal (cash) crosses the upper threshold.

## 2.3 Model 3 — Stone Model (Stochastic with Forecasting)

Stone’s model **generalises Miller-Orr** by using **time-series forecasting (ARIMA)** to predict future cash flows rather than assuming a simple random walk. It is rarely asked numerically in KTU but appears as a **2-mark conceptual question**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — MEMORISE THIS VERBATIM**

| Quantity | Baumol's Model | Miller-Orr Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Optimal Order Size (C\*)** | $C^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2bT}{i}}$ | $Z^* = \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{3b\sigma^2}{4i}} + L$ |
| **Average Cash Balance** | $C^*/2$ | $(Z^* + 3Z^* - 2L)/3 \;=\; \dfrac{4Z^* - L}{3}$ — board uses approx. $(Z^* - L)/2$ in simple cases |
| **Upper Control Limit (U)** | Not applicable | $U = 3Z^* - 2L$ |
| **Number of Transactions / Year** | $N = T/C^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{iT}{2b}}$ | Expected number of trades: $N = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{2Z^* - 2L}\cdot\dfrac{1}{b}$ (rare) |
| **Total Annual Cost (TC)** | $TC = \sqrt{2bTi}$ | $TC = \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{3b\sigma^2 i}{4}} + iL$ |
| **Critical variable** | $T$ (total demand) | $\sigma^2$ (variance) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition:** All formulas require $b>0$, $i>0$, $T>0$, $\sigma^2>0$. The optimal $C^*$ and $Z^*$ are **real, positive, and unique**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Treasury departments** of corporates (TCS, Infosys, Reliance) use variants of Miller-Orr in real-time cash positioning.
- **Bank sweep accounts** automatically implement the upper/lower limit logic.
- **Robotic inventory systems** in manufacturing warehouses use the **same mathematical structure** for spare-parts management.
- **Renewable energy battery storage sizing** in electrical engineering uses analogous trade-off between charging cycles (transaction cost) and stored energy opportunity cost.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Baumol's Optimal Cash Formula

### Step 1: Define total cost as a function of withdrawal size C

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC(C) &= \underbrace{\text{Transaction Cost}}_{b \cdot (T/C)} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{Opportunity Cost}}_{i \cdot (C/2)} \\[4pt]
TC(C) &= \frac{bT}{C} \;+\; \frac{iC}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The firm withdraws C, spends it uniformly, then withdraws again — so it holds an average balance of $C/2$. Every withdrawal costs a fixed fee b, and there are $T/C$ withdrawals in a year.

### Step 2: First-order condition for minimum

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d(TC)}{dC} &= -\frac{bT}{C^{2}} + \frac{i}{2} \;=\; 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The minimum of a U-shaped curve is where its slope is zero.

### Step 3: Solve for C*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{bT}{C^{2}} &= \frac{i}{2} \\[4pt]
C^{2} &= \frac{2bT}{i} \\[4pt]
C^{*} &= \sqrt{\frac{2bT}{i}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Cross-multiply and take the positive square root (C* must be non-negative).

### Step 4: Optimal number of transactions per year

$$
\begin{aligned}
N^{*} &= \frac{T}{C^{*}} = \frac{T}{\sqrt{2bT/i}} = \sqrt{\frac{iT}{2b}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Minimum total cost (substitute C* back)

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_{\min} &= \frac{bT}{\sqrt{2bT/i}} + \frac{i}{2}\sqrt{\frac{2bT}{i}} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{2bTi}\cdot\frac{1}{2} + \sqrt{2bTi}\cdot\frac{1}{2} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{2bTi}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** At optimum, transaction cost exactly equals opportunity cost. This is the **principle of marginal balance** that examiners love to test.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Miller-Orr Optimal Return Point

### Step 1: Set up the structure

The cash balance moves randomly between the lower limit **L** and the upper limit **U**, with a target return point **Z** such that the firm buys/sells securities to bring the balance to Z whenever U or L is hit.

Expected cash balance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[\text{Cash}] &= L + \frac{Z - L}{3} \;=\; \frac{2L + Z}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Components of total cost per day

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC &= \underbrace{\frac{b \cdot \sigma^{2}}{2\,(Z-L)^{2}}}_{\text{Expected transactions per day (in b)}/\text{day}} \;+\; \underbrace{i \cdot \left(L + \frac{Z - L}{3}\right)}_{\text{Opportunity cost per day}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Differentiate with respect to Z (treat L as fixed)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial TC}{\partial Z} &= \frac{b\sigma^{2}}{2}\cdot\left[ -\frac{2}{(Z-L)^{3}} \right] + \frac{i}{3} \;=\; 0 \\[6pt]
-\frac{b\sigma^{2}}{(Z-L)^{3}} + \frac{i}{3} &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Solve for (Z − L)

$$
\begin{aligned}
(Z-L)^{3} &= \frac{3b\sigma^{2}}{i} \\[4pt]
Z - L &= \sqrt[3]{\frac{3b\sigma^{2}}{i}} \\[4pt]
Z^{*} &= L + \sqrt[3]{\frac{3b\sigma^{2}}{i}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Express in the standard board-exam form

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU standard form uses **$4i$ in the denominator** instead of $i$, because the convention in the KTU textbook (Khan & Jain) absorbs a factor-of-4 in the daily interest rate. Be ready for both versions.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z^{*} &= L + \sqrt[3]{\frac{3b\sigma^{2}}{4i}} \\[6pt]
U^{*} &= 3Z^{*} - 2L
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — Baumol's Model (KTU Pattern)

**Question:** A firm has an annual cash demand of **T = ₹12,00,000**. The cost of converting marketable securities into cash is **b = ₹150 per transaction**. The opportunity cost of holding cash is **i = 12% p.a.** Find: (i) Optimal cash balance C*, (ii) Average cash balance, (iii) Number of transactions per year, (iv) Total cost.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C^{*} &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 150 \times 12{,}00{,}000}{0.12}} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{\frac{36{,}00{,}00{,}000}{0.12}} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{3 \times 10^{9}} \;=\; \sqrt{3}\times 10^{4.5} \\[4pt]
&= 1.732 \times 31{,}623 \;=\; ₹54{,}772 \text{ (approx.)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Average cash balance = $C^*/2 = \text{₹}27{,}386$

Number of transactions: $N^* = T/C^* = 12{,}00{,}000 / 54{,}772 \approx 21.9 \approx 22$ transactions/year

Total cost: $TC = \sqrt{2 \times 150 \times 12{,}00{,}000 \times 0.12} = \sqrt{4.32 \times 10^{7}} = ₹6{,}572$

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — Miller-Orr Model (KTU Pattern)

**Question:** A firm has a lower cash limit **L = ₹20,000**. The fixed cost of a securities transaction is **b = ₹50**. The daily variance of net cash flow is **σ² = ₹10,00,000** (per day). The daily interest rate is **i = 0.0004** (≈ 14.6% p.a.). Compute Z* and U*.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z^{*} &= L + \sqrt[3]{\frac{3b\sigma^{2}}{i}} \\[4pt]
&= 20{,}000 + \sqrt[3]{\frac{3 \times 50 \times 10{,}00{,}000}{0.0004}} \\[4pt]
&= 20{,}000 + \sqrt[3]{\frac{1{,}50{,}00{,}00{,}000}{0.0004}} \\[4pt]
&= 20{,}000 + \sqrt[3]{3.75 \times 10^{13}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt[3]{3.75 \times 10^{13}} &= \sqrt[3]{37.5} \times 10^{4} \approx 3.357 \times 10{,}000 = 33{,}570 \\[4pt]
Z^{*} &= 20{,}000 + 33{,}570 = ₹53{,}570
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
U^{*} &= 3Z^{*} - 2L = 3(53{,}570) - 2(20{,}000) \\[4pt]
&= 1{,}60{,}710 - 40{,}000 = ₹1{,}20{,}710
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Whenever the cash balance touches ₹1,20,710, the firm should immediately buy securities to bring it down to ₹53,570. Whenever it falls to ₹20,000, the firm sells securities to bring it back to ₹53,570.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Model

| Situation | Use | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Salaries paid on a fixed monthly date | **Baumol** | Predictable, constant outflow |
| Daily collection from thousands of customers | **Miller-Orr** | Highly variable inflows/outflows |
| Multinational firm with multiple currencies | **Stone / simulation** | Need forecasting + correlation |
| Small proprietorship, low tech | **Baumol** | Simple to apply |
| Listed corporate treasury | **Miller-Orr** | Daily cash positions tracked |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Baumol's Model — Cash Balance vs Time (Sawtooth)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["Start: Cash = C star"] --> nodeB["Cash declines uniformly to ZERO"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Trigger: Sell marketable securities"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Cash restored to C star instantly"]
    nodeD --> nodeB
    style nodeA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style nodeB fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    style nodeC fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    style nodeD fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000
```

**Block-Level Functional Architecture of Cash Management**

```mermaid
graph TD
    input1["Daily Cash Outflows T"] --> bal["Running Cash Balance C t"]
    input2["Marketable Securities Pool"] --> bal
    bal --> check{"Compare C t with Upper Limit and Lower Limit"}
    check -->|"C t equals Upper Limit"| sell["SELL securities worth U minus Z"]
    check -->|"C t equals Lower Limit"| buy["BUY securities worth Z minus L"]
    check -->|"Lower Limit less than C t less than Upper Limit"| wait["No action Hold steady"]
    sell --> bal
    buy --> bal
    wait --> bal
    style input1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000
    style input2 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000
    style bal fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style check fill:#ffd966,stroke:#806000
    style sell fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000
    style buy fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#274e13
    style wait fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#404040
```

## 4.2 Miller-Orr Control Band — Schematic

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph band["MILLER ORR CONTROL BAND"]
        direction LR
        nodeU["UPPER LIMIT U = 3Z minus 2L"]
        nodeZ["RETURN POINT Z star"]
        nodeL["LOWER LIMIT L = fixed"]
    end
    nodeU --> nodeZ
    nodeZ --> nodeL
    style nodeU fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    style nodeZ fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    nodeL fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#274e13
    style nodeL fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#274e13
```

## 4.3 Total Cost Curve — U-Shape (Baumol)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["Low C: High Transaction Cost"] --> nodeB["Minimum TC at C star"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["High C: High Opportunity Cost"]
    nodeA -."dTC by dC less than zero".-> nodeB
    nodeB -."dTC by dC greater than zero".-> nodeC
    style nodeA fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    style nodeB fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    style nodeC fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
```

**Reading:** As C (withdrawal size) increases from very small to very large, Total Cost first falls (transaction cost dominates) and then rises (opportunity cost dominates). The bottom of the U is the optimal C*.

---

## 4.4 Comparative Model Architecture (Mermaid)
