<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State the assumptions of Baumol's model of cash management.**

**Model Answer:**
1. Cash is needed at a **constant, predictable rate** throughout the planning period.
2. The firm holds **no cash inflows** — only outflows.
3. Cash is replenished **instantaneously** by selling marketable securities.
4. Transaction cost per conversion (b) and opportunity cost (i) are **known and constant**.
5. There is **no safety stock** of cash held by the firm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cognitive Level:** Remember (L1). **CO Mapping:** CO4 — Apply working-capital management models.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Baumol's Model and Miller-Orr Model.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Baumol's Model | Miller-Orr Model |
|---|---|---|
| Year / Author | 1952, William Baumol | 1966, Miller & Orr |
| Nature of cash flow | Deterministic, constant rate | Stochastic, random walk |
| Number of limits | One (single withdrawal C*) | Two (L and U) + return point Z |
| Key input | Annual cash demand T | Variance of daily net cash flow σ² |
| Shape of cash balance | Sawtooth | Random walk between L and U |
| Limitation | Unrealistic in volatile environments | Requires L to be fixed by management |
| Real-world use | Stable salary/payment cycles | Treasury desks, large corporates |

**Cognitive Level:** Understand (L2). **CO Mapping:** CO4.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — Module-Internal Choice Pattern

### **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**Question A:**
**(a)** Derive the **Baumol's optimal cash balance formula** $C^* = \sqrt{2bT/i}$. State clearly each step and the meaning of every symbol. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** XYZ Ltd. has annual cash requirements of **₹18,00,000**. The cost of converting marketable securities into cash is **₹200 per transaction**. The firm can earn **10% per annum** on its marketable securities. Calculate:
1. Optimal cash balance (C*)
2. Average cash balance
3. Number of transactions per year
4. Total cost of cash management

**(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Total cost function**
$$TC(C) = \frac{bT}{C} + \frac{iC}{2}$$
where $b$ = fixed cost per transaction, $T$ = total annual demand, $i$ = opportunity cost rate, $C$ = cash withdrawn per transaction.

**Step 2 — First derivative**
$$\frac{dTC}{dC} = -\frac{bT}{C^{2}} + \frac{i}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Set to zero for minimum**
$$-\frac{bT}{C^{2}} + \frac{i}{2} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; C^{2} = \frac{2bT}{i}$$

**Step 4 — Take positive root**
$$C^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2bT}{i}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back to obtain minimum TC**
$$TC_{\min} = \sqrt{2bTi}$$

**Step 6 — Number of transactions per year**
$$N^{*} = \frac{T}{C^{*}} = \sqrt{\frac{iT}{2b}}$$

**Note:** At optimum, transaction cost = opportunity cost = $TC_{\min}/2$.

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

Given: $T = 18{,}00{,}000$, $b = 200$, $i = 0.10$.

**(i) Optimal cash balance C\***
$$C^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 200 \times 18{,}00{,}000}{0.10}} = \sqrt{7.2 \times 10^{10}} \approx ₹2{,}68{,}328$$

**(ii) Average cash balance**
$$\bar{C} = \frac{C^{*}}{2} = ₹1{,}34{,}164$$

**(iii) Number of transactions per year**
$$N^{*} = \frac{18{,}00{,}000}{2{,}68{,}328} \approx 6.71 \;\Rightarrow\; 7 \text{ transactions/year}$$

**(iv) Total annual cost**
$$TC = \sqrt{2 \times 200 \times 18{,}00{,}000 \times 0.10} = \sqrt{7.2 \times 10^{9}} = ₹84{,}852.81 \approx ₹84{,}853$$

**Cognitive Level:** Apply / Analyse (L3-L4). **CO Mapping:** CO4.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice

### **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative]**

**Question B:**
**(a)** Explain the **Miller-Orr model of cash management** with a neat diagram. Derive the formula for the **optimal return point Z\***. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A firm has a **lower cash limit L = ₹30,000**. The cost of buying/selling securities is **₹60 per transaction**. The daily variance of net cash flow is **σ² = ₹4,00,000**. The daily interest rate is **i = 0.0003**. Calculate:
1. Optimal return point Z*
2. Upper control limit U*
3. Expected number of transactions per day.

**(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Explanation + Derivation (7 marks)

**Explanation:** Miller-Orr model is a **stochastic cash management model** that allows net cash flows to be **random and normally distributed** with mean zero and variance σ². The firm sets a **Lower Limit (L)** (the minimum operating cash) and an **Upper Limit (U)**. The cash balance fluctuates randomly; when it hits U, the firm purchases securities worth (U − Z) to bring the balance down to the return point Z. When it falls to L, the firm sells securities worth (Z − L) to bring it back to Z.

**Derivation of Z\***
$$TC(Z) = \frac{b\,\sigma^{2}}{2(Z-L)^{2}} + i\left(L + \frac{Z-L}{3}\right)$$

$$\frac{dTC}{dZ} = -\frac{b\sigma^{2}}{(Z-L)^{3}} + \frac{i}{3} = 0$$

$$(Z-L)^{3} = \frac{3b\sigma^{2}}{i} \;\Rightarrow\; Z^{*} = L + \sqrt[3]{\frac{3b\sigma^{2}}{i}}$$

In KTU standard form: $Z^{*} = L + \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{3b\sigma^{2}}{4i}}$ and $U^{*} = 3Z^{*} - 2L$.

**Diagram:** (refer to the Mermaid Control Band in Section 4.2)

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

Given: $L = 30{,}000$, $b = 60$, $\sigma^{2} = 4{,}00{,}000$, $i = 0.0003$.

**(i) Optimal return point Z\***
$$Z^{*} = L + \sqrt[3]{\frac{3b\sigma^{2}}{i}} = 30{,}000 + \sqrt[3]{\frac{3 \times 60 \times 4{,}00{,}000}{0.0003}}$$

$$= 30{,}000 + \sqrt[3]{\frac{7.2 \times 10^{7}}{0.0003}} = 30{,}000 + \sqrt[3]{2.4 \times 10^{11}}$$

$$\sqrt[3]{2.4 \times 10^{11}} = \sqrt[3]{240} \times 10^{3} \approx 6.214 \times 1{,}000 = 6{,}214$$

$$Z^{*} \approx ₹36{,}214$$

**(ii) Upper control limit U\***
$$U^{*} = 3Z^{*} - 2L = 3(36{,}214) - 2(30{,}000) = 1{,}08{,}642 - 60{,}000 = ₹48{,}642$$

**(iii) Expected number of transactions per day**

Expected number of upward crossings of U per unit time:
$$E[N] \approx \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2(Z^{*}-L)^{2}} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{2 \times (6{,}214)^{2}} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{7.72 \times 10^{7}} \approx 0.00518 \text{ transactions/day}$$

$$\approx 0.005 \times 250 \text{ working days} \approx 1.3 \text{ transactions/year}$$

**Cognitive Level:** Apply / Analyse (L3-L4). **CO Mapping:** CO4.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Wrong unit of i** — Board often quotes i as an annual %. Convert to decimal **before** substituting (12% = 0.12, not 12). Failure to do so is the #1 cause of incorrect numerical answers.
> 2. **Cube-root vs Square-root confusion** — Baumol uses √ (square root), Miller-Orr uses ∛ (cube root). Mixing them is an instant 0.
> 3. **Forgetting to add L** in Miller-Orr Z* formula — many students write only the cube-root term and lose 2 marks.
> 4. **Upper limit formula U = 3Z − 2L** — must be written in this exact form; examiners do not give partial credit for 3Z − 2L written without showing the derivation link.
> 5. **Number of transactions** — examiners expect an **integer** rounded **up** (because you cannot make 0.7 of a transaction). Writing fractional N loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Confusing transaction cost b with total transaction cost** — b is the cost **per** transaction, not the annual transaction cost. The annual figure is $bT/C$.
> 7. **Not stating units in the final answer** (e.g., ₹) — small deduction but cumulatively matters.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Cash Management Models**

- [x] **Baumol's formula:** $C^* = \sqrt{2bT/i}$ — derived from balancing transaction cost $\frac{bT}{C}$ vs opportunity cost $\frac{iC}{2}$.
- [x] **Average cash balance in Baumol:** $C^*/2$ — the firm oscillates between $C^*$ and 0.
- [x] **Number of transactions:** $N^* = T/C^* = \sqrt{iT/(2b)}$.
- [x] **Minimum total cost:** $TC_{\min} = \sqrt{2bTi}$ — and at optimum, transaction cost = opportunity cost.
- [x] **Miller-Orr Z\*:** $Z^* = L + \sqrt[3]{3b\sigma^2/4i}$ (KTU textbook form).
- [x] **Miller-Orr U\*:** $U^* = 3Z^* - 2L$.
- [x] **Lower limit L** in Miller-Orr is a **managerial/management-decision variable** — not a formula output.
- [x] **Key input** in Baumol is the **mean** T; in Miller-Orr it is the **variance σ²**.
- [x] **Stone's model** extends Miller-Orr with **time-series forecasting**; rarely asked numerically.
- [x] **Opportunity cost of cash** = interest foregone on marketable securities, expressed in **decimal** (e.g., 0.10 for 10%).
- [x] **Transaction cost b** is **per conversion**, irrespective of size.
- [x] All answers must carry **currency units (₹)** and a clear **2-decimal** final figure for KTU valuation.
- [x] **Common trap:** When asked "average cash balance" in Miller-Orr, use the expected balance formula $\frac{4Z^* - L}{3}$, not simply $Z^*/2$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operating Cycle and Cash Cycle Calculations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIET602 - FINANCIAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Operating Cycle and Cash Cycle Calculations ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Operating Cycle (OC)** is the total time duration elapsed between the **acquisition of raw materials/inventory** and the **final collection of cash** from the sale of finished goods on credit. It is the sum of the **Inventory Holding Period** and the **Accounts Receivable Collection Period**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cash Cycle (CC)** — also widely termed as the **Cash Conversion Cycle (CCC)** — is the net time interval between the **outflow of cash to suppliers** (for raw materials/payables) and the **inflow of cash from customers** (collections from receivables). Mathematically, **Cash Cycle = Operating Cycle − Accounts Payable Period**. The Operating Cycle represents the *gross* operational timeline, while the Cash Cycle represents the *net* financial timeline — the actual duration for which the firm's working capital is blocked in day-to-day operations. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **"The Mango Trader's Story" — An Intuitive Walkthrough** Imagine you are a mango trader in Kerala during the Onam season. **Day 1 — Procurement:** You go to the wholesale market and buy **100 kg of mangoes** for ₹10,000. The wholesaler says: *"Pay me after 30 days."* — This 30-day credit you receive from your supplier is the **Accounts Payable Period**. **Day 1–7 — Holding:** You store the mangoes in your shop, ripen them, sort them, and prepare them for sale. The 7 days you hold them is the **Inventory Period** (you've bought inventory but haven't sold yet). **Day 7 — Sale:** You sell all mangoes to retail customers on credit: *"Pay me in 20 days."* — The 20 days you wait for your customer's payment is the **Accounts Receivable Period**. **Day 27 — Cash Inflow:** Your customers pay you on Day 27. - **Operating Cycle** = Inventory Period + AR Period = 7 + 20 = **27 days** - **Cash Cycle** = Operating Cycle − AP Period = 27 − 30 = **−3 days** A **negative Cash Cycle** is a financial manager's dream! It means your suppliers fund your entire business operation — you receive cash from customers *before* you must pay your suppliers. This is exactly how giants like Amazon and Apple operate. | Standard Metric | KTU Defined Value | Description | |---|---|---| | **Days in a Financial Year** | **365** | Standard banking/financial year for daily conversions | | **Operating Cycle** | **Days** | Time from inventory acquisition to cash collection | | **Cash Cycle** | **Days** | Net time firm's cash remains tied up in operations | | **Inventory Turnover Ratio** | **Times/Year** | How many times inventory is sold and replenished annually | --- ### 1.3 KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlights > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Mapping:** This topic falls under **Module 4 — Working Capital Management**, specifically addressing the **quantitative measurement of working capital cycles**. It is a **high-weightage topic** appearing frequently in KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE), typically as a 7 or 14-mark analytical problem in Part B. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Four Building Blocks of Working Capital Cycles Working Capital Management revolves around four critical time periods. Each represents a *delay* in the cash flow cycle, and the goal of financial engineering is to **minimize these delays optimally** without disrupting operations. #### Block 1 — Inventory Holding Period (IHP) - **What it measures:** Average number of days inventory is held before being sold. - **Why it matters:** Longer holding = higher storage cost, obsolescence risk, insurance, and blocked capital. - **How it works:** Compare the **Average Inventory** with **Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)** over the period. #### Block 2 — Accounts Receivable Period (ARP) / Average Collection Period - **What it measures:** Average number of days taken to collect cash from credit customers. - **Why it matters:** Directly impacts cash inflows; longer collection = greater bad-debt risk and liquidity strain. - **How it works:** Compare **Average Accounts Receivable** with **Net Credit Sales**. #### Block 3 — Accounts Payable Period (APP) / Average Payment Period - **What it measures:** Average number of days the firm takes to pay its suppliers. - **Why it matters:** This is the *only* favorable delay — longer payment period means the firm uses supplier credit as a free source of finance. - **How it works:** Compare **Average Accounts Payable** with **Net Credit Purchases**. #### Block 4 — The Synthesis (OC and CC) - **Operating Cycle** stitches together procurement → sale (inventory delay + receivables delay). - **Cash Cycle** adjusts OC for the *credit received* from suppliers (payables delay). --- ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Units | Standard Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Inventory Period** | $\text{IP} = \dfrac{\text{Average Inventory}}{\text{COGS}} \times 365$ | Days | Use **COGS**, not Sales | | 2 | **Inventory Period (Alt)** | $\text{IP} = \dfrac{365}{\text{Inventory Turnover Ratio}}$ | Days | ITR = COGS / Avg. Inventory | | 3 | **Receivable Period** | $\text{ARP} = \dfrac{\text{Average Accounts Receivable}}{\text{Net Credit Sales}} \times 365$ | Days | Use **Credit Sales** only | | 4 | **Payable Period** | $\text{APP} = \dfrac{\text{Average Accounts Payable}}{\text{Net Credit Purchases}} \times 365$ | Days | Use **Credit Purchases**, not total purchases | | 5 | **Operating Cycle** | $\text{OC} = \text{IP} + \text{ARP}$ | Days | Sum of two delays | | 6 | **Cash Cycle (CCC)** | $\text{CC} = \text{OC} - \text{APP} = \text{IP} + \text{ARP} - \text{APP}$ | Days | Net financial delay | | 7 | **Average Inventory** | $\dfrac{\text{Opening Inv.} + \text{Closing Inv.}}{2}$ | ₹ | Used unless monthly data given | | 8 | **Average AR** | $\dfrac{\text{Opening AR} + \text{Closing AR}}{2}$ | ₹ | Same averaging logic | | 9 | **Average AP** | $\dfrac{\text{Opening AP} + \text{Closing AP}}{2}$ | ₹ | Same averaging logic | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL KTU DISTINCTION:** > - Inventory uses **COGS** (cost basis). > - Receivables use **Credit Sales** (revenue basis). > - Payables use **Credit Purchases** (cost basis). > Mixing these bases is the **#1 cause of mark deduction** in KTU valuation. --- ### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Business | Industry | Typical OC | Typical CC | Strategic Implication | |---|---|---|---| | **Grocery / FMCG Retail** | 30–60 days | 5–20 days | High inventory turnover, tight margins | | **Heavy Manufacturing (e.g., Steel)** | 120–180 days | 60–90 days | Large WIP and finished goods | | **E-Commerce (e.g., Amazon)** | 25–40 days | **Negative** | Supplier credit > customer credit | | **Restaurants / QSR** | 5–10 days | **Negative** | Cash-on-delivery from customers | | **Construction / Real Estate** | 240+ days | 180+ days | Long project cycles, milestone payments | The Cash Cycle is a **leading indicator** of corporate liquidity health and is closely watched by credit rating agencies, banks, and treasury departments. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Cash Cycle Equation The Cash Conversion Cycle is derived as a *net time interval* using first-principles logic. **Step 1:** Define the total operational timeline (Operating Cycle). $$\text{Operating Cycle} = \text{Time from Inventory Acquisition to Cash Collection from Customers}$$ **Step 2:** Decompose the Operating Cycle into two sequential delays. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Operating Cycle} &= \underbrace{(\text{Days inventory is held})}_{\text{Inventory Period}} + \underbrace{(\text{Days to collect from customers})}_{\text{Receivables Period}} \\ \text{OC} &= \text{IP} + \text{ARP} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Recognize that the firm does NOT pay cash on Day 1 for inventory — it gets credit from suppliers for `APP` days. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Cash Cycle} &= \text{Total Operational Delay} - \text{Supplier Credit Delay} \\ \text{CC} &= \text{OC} - \text{APP} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Substitute the OC expression into the CC equation. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CC} &= (\text{IP} + \text{ARP}) - \text{APP} \\ \text{CC} &= \text{IP} + \text{ARP} - \text{APP} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5:** This is the **canonical Cash Conversion Cycle formula** universally used in treasury, working capital management, and KTU examinations. --- ### 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Problem (Comprehensive) > [!NOTE] > **KTU-Style Problem:** Given financial data for **M/s Kerala Electronics Ltd.** for FY 2023–24, compute the **Operating Cycle** and **Cash Cycle**. **Given Data:** | Item | Amount (₹) | |---|---| | Opening Inventory | 1,50,000 | | Closing Inventory | 2,50,000 | | Opening Accounts Receivable | 3,00,000 | | Closing Accounts Receivable | 4,00,000 | | Opening Accounts Payable | 1,80,000 | | Closing Accounts Payable | 2,20,000 | | Cost of Goods Sold (COGS) | 18,00,000 | | Net Credit Sales | 24,00,000 | | Net Credit Purchases | 15,00,000 | **Solution — Step-by-Step:** #### Step A: Compute the Averages $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Average Inventory} &= \frac{\text{Opening Inv.} + \text{Closing Inv.}}{2} = \frac{1,50,000 + 2,50,000}{2} = \frac{4,00,000}{2} = 2,00,000 \\ \text{Average AR} &= \frac{3,00,000 + 4,00,000}{2} = \frac{7,00,000}{2} = 3,50,000 \\ \text{Average AP} &= \frac{1,80,000 + 2,20,000}{2} = \frac{4,00,000}{2} = 2,00,000 \end{aligned} $$ #### Step B: Compute the Inventory Period $$ \begin{aligned} \text{IP} &= \frac{\text{Average Inventory}}{\text{COGS}} \times 365 = \frac{2,00,000}{18,00,000} \times 365 \\ &= 0.1111 \times 365 = 40.55 \text{ days} \end{aligned} $$ #### Step C: Compute the Receivables Period $$ \begin{aligned} \text{ARP} &= \frac{\text{Average AR}}{\text{Net Credit Sales}} \times 365 = \frac{3,50,000}{24,00,000} \times 365 \\ &= 0.14583 \times 365 = 53.23 \text{ days} \end{aligned} $$ #### Step D: Compute the Payables Period $$ \begin{aligned} \text{APP} &= \frac{\text{Average AP}}{\text{Net Credit Purchases}} \times 365 = \frac{2,00,000}{15,00,000} \times 365 \\ &= 0.13333 \times 365 = 48.67 \text{ days} \end{aligned} $$ #### Step E: Compute the Operating Cycle $$ \begin{aligned} \text{OC} &= \text{IP} + \text{ARP} = 40.55 + 53.23 = 93.78 \text{ days} \end{aligned} $$ #### Step F: Compute the Cash Conversion Cycle $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CC} &= \text{OC} - \text{APP} = 93.78 - 48.67 = 45.11 \text{ days} \\ \text{Alternatively: } \text{CC} &= \text{IP} + \text{ARP} - \text{APP} = 40.55 + 53.23 - 48.67 = 45.11 \text{ days} \end{aligned} $$ #### Final Analytical Interpretation > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result:** Kerala Electronics Ltd.'s cash is tied up for **~45 days** in operations. To finance this gap, the firm requires permanent working capital financing. Strategies to reduce CCC: (a) faster inventory turnover via JIT, (b) tighter credit policy for customers, (c) negotiating extended payable terms with suppliers. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Impact Analysis) | Strategy Lever | Effect on IP | Effect on ARP | Effect on APP | Net Effect on CCC | Risk | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Just-in-Time (JIT) Inventory** | ↓ Sharp | — | — | ↓ Decreases | Stock-out risk | | **Tight Credit Policy** | — | ↓ Decreases | — | ↓ Decreases | Loss of sales | | **Early Payment Discount to Suppliers** | — | — | ↓ Decreases | ↑ Increases | Cash strain | | **Negotiate Longer Credit Terms** | — | — | ↑ Increases | ↓ Decreases | Supplier goodwill | | **EOQ Optimization** | ↓ Modest | — | — | ↓ Decreases | Carrying cost trade-off | | **Factoring of Receivables** | — | ↓ Sharp | — | ↓ Decreases | Discount cost | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Cash Flow Timeline Diagram (Mermaid) The following schematic maps the precise time-flow of cash through the three operational phases, with each phase duration explicitly labeled. ```mermaid graph LR pointA["Day 0: Inventory Purchased
Cash NOT Paid Yet"] pointB["Day APP: Cash Paid to Supplier
Accounts Payable Settled"] pointC["Day IP: Inventory Sold
Goods Delivered to Customer"] pointD["Day IP plus ARP: Cash Received
Accounts Receivable Collected"] pointE["Firms Cash Cycle Duration
IP plus ARP minus APP"] pointA --> pointB pointB --> pointC pointC --> pointD pointD --> pointE ``` **Interpretation of the diagram:** - The arrow from **Day 0 → Day APP** represents the **Accounts Payable Period** (supplier credit received). - The arrow from **Day 0 → Day IP** represents the **Inventory Period** (holding delay). - The arrow from **Day IP → Day IP + ARP** represents the **Receivables Period** (customer collection delay). - The **Cash Cycle** is the residual gap the firm must self-finance. --- ### 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow of OC and CC Computation ```mermaid graph TD inputA["Raw Financial Data
COGS, Sales, Purchases"] inputB["Balance Sheet Averages
Inventory, AR, AP"] formula1["Compute IP
Avg Inv / COGS x 365"] formula2["Compute ARP
Avg AR / Sales x 365"] formula3["Compute APP
Avg AP / Purchases x 365"] compute1["Operating Cycle
IP plus ARP"] compute2["Cash Cycle
OC minus APP"] output1["OC Result in Days"] output2["CCC Result in Days"] decision{"Is CCC
Negative?"} pathA["Strong Liquidity
Supplier Funded"] pathB["Working Capital
Financing Needed"] inputA --> formula1 inputA --> formula2 inputA --> formula3 inputB --> formula1 inputB --> formula2 inputB --> formula3 formula1 --> compute1 formula2 --> compute1 compute1 --> compute2 formula3 --> compute2 compute2 --> output2 compute1 --> output1 output2 --> decision decision --> pathA decision --> pathB ``` **Architectural Reading Guide:** - **Layer 1 (Inputs):** Raw financial statements feed the engine. - **Layer 2 (Period Calculations):** Three independent formulas run in parallel. - **Layer 3 (Synthesis):** The outputs converge into the OC and CC equations. - **Layer 4 (Decision Node):** The CCC result triggers strategic interpretation — negative CCC = supplier-funded operations (Amazon-style), positive CCC = working capital financing needed. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define the Operating Cycle and Cash Cycle. State the formula for each.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Operating Cycle (OC)** is the average time interval between the acquisition of inventory and the final collection of cash from credit sales. It comprises the inventory holding period and the receivables collection period. > > $$\text{OC} = \text{Inventory Period} + \text{Receivables Period}$$ > > $$\text{OC} = \left(\frac{\text{Avg. Inventory}}{\text{COGS}} \times 365\right) + \left(\frac{\text{Avg. AR}}{\text{Credit Sales}} \times 365\right)$$ > > **Cash Cycle (CC)** or Cash Conversion Cycle (CCC) is the net time period for which the firm's cash remains tied up in operations. It is the Operating Cycle minus the payables period. > > $$\text{CCC} = \text{OC} - \text{Payables Period}$$ --- #### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Distinguish between Operating Cycle and Cash Cycle. Why is a shorter cash cycle preferred?** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > | Basis | Operating Cycle | Cash Cycle | > |---|---|---| > | **Definition** | Time from inventory purchase to cash collection | Time from cash outflow (to suppliers) to cash inflow (from customers) | > | **Formula** | IP + ARP | IP + ARP − APP | > | **Accounts Payable** | Not considered | Subtracted (supplier credit reduces financing need) | > | **Interpretation** | Gross operational timeline | Net financing requirement | > > **A shorter Cash Cycle is preferred because:** > (i) It reduces the duration for which working capital is locked in operations. > (ii) It lowers financing costs and improves liquidity. > (iii) A *negative* cash cycle indicates the firm is financed by its suppliers — a hallmark of strong working capital management (e.g., Amazon, Apple). --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 — Model Paper]` **M/s Trivandrum Steel Industries Ltd.** furnishes the following data for the year ending 31-Mar-2024: | Particulars | Amount (₹) | |---|---| | Opening Stock of Raw Materials | 80,000 | | Closing Stock of Raw Materials | 1,20,000 | | Opening Stock of WIP | 40,000 | | Closing Stock of WIP | 60,000 | | Opening Stock of Finished Goods | 1,00,000 | | Closing Stock of Finished Goods | 1,40,000 | | Opening Accounts Receivable | 2,00,000 | | Closing Accounts Receivable | 3,00,000 | | Opening Accounts Payable | 1,50,000 | | Closing Accounts Payable | 1,70,000 | | Raw Material Consumed | 8,00,000 | | Manufacturing Expenses | 4,00,000 | | Net Credit Sales | 20,00,000 | | Net Credit Purchases | 9,00,000 | **(a)** Calculate the **Operating Cycle** and **Cash Conversion Cycle** in days. (7 marks) **(b)** Comment on the working capital position and suggest **two strategies** to reduce the cash cycle. (7 marks) --- ##### **Solution to Question A — Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Step 1: Compute Total Average Inventory** Total Inventory = Raw Materials + WIP + Finished Goods $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Opening Total Inventory} &= 80{,}000 + 40{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 = 2{,}20{,}000 \\ \text{Closing Total Inventory} &= 1{,}20{,}000 + 60{,}000 + 1{,}40{,}000 = 3{,}20{,}000 \\ \text{Average Inventory} &= \frac{2{,}20{,}000 + 3{,}20{,}000}{2} = \frac{5{,}40{,}000}{2} = 2{,}70{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Compute Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)** COGS = Raw Material Consumed + Manufacturing Expenses + Opening FG − Closing FG (Note: WIP and RM movements are already absorbed into manufacturing; we use the standard simplified formula for KTU.) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{COGS} &= \text{RM Consumed} + \text{Mfg. Expenses} + \text{Op. FG} - \text{Cl. FG} \\ &= 8{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}40{,}000 = 11{,}60{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Compute Averages for AR and AP** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Average AR} &= \frac{2{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000}{2} = 2{,}50{,}000 \\ \text{Average AP} &= \frac{1{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}70{,}000}{2} = 1{,}60{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Compute the Three Periods** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Inventory Period (IP)} &= \frac{2{,}70{,}000}{11{,}60{,}000} \times 365 = 0.23276 \times 365 = 84.96 \text{ days} \\ \text{Receivables Period (ARP)} &= \frac{2{,}50{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000} \times 365 = 0.125 \times 365 = 45.63 \text{ days} \\ \text{Payables Period (APP)} &= \frac{1{,}60{,}000}{9{,}00{,}000} \times 365 = 0.17778 \times 365 = 64.89 \text{ days} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Operating Cycle and Cash Cycle** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Operating Cycle} &= 84.96 + 45.63 = 130.59 \text{ days} \\ \text{Cash Conversion Cycle} &= 130.59 - 64.89 = 65.70 \text{ days} \end{aligned} $$ --- ##### **Solution to Question A — Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Interpretation:** Trivandrum Steel Industries has an **Operating Cycle of ~131 days** and a **CCC of ~66 days**, indicating that the firm's cash remains blocked in operations for over two months. This demands significant working capital financing and exposes the firm to liquidity and interest-rate risks. **Two Strategies to Reduce the Cash Cycle:** **Strategy 1 — Adopt Just-in-Time (JIT) Inventory Management:** By synchronizing raw material procurement with production schedules, the firm can drastically reduce the Inventory Period (currently 85 days). A 50% reduction in IP alone would lower CCC by ~42 days. **Strategy 2 — Tighten Receivables Collection via Early Payment Discounts:** Offering a 2/10 net 30 scheme (2% discount if paid in 10 days, else due in 30 days) incentivizes customers to pay faster, reducing ARP from 46 days toward 25–30 days and improving cash inflow predictability. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **M/s Kochi Marine Exports Pvt. Ltd.** has submitted the following summarized data: | Item | ₹ | |---|---| | Average Inventory | 5,00,000 | | Average Accounts Receivable | 6,00,000 | | Average Accounts Payable | 3,00,000 | | Annual Credit Sales | 30,00,000 | | Annual Credit Purchases | 18,00,000 | | Cost of Goods Sold | 22,00,000 | **(a)** Compute the **Inventory Period, Receivables Period, Payables Period**, and the **Cash Conversion Cycle**. (7 marks) **(b)** If the firm manages to reduce its inventory period by **20%** and extend its payables period by **15%**, calculate the **new Cash Conversion Cycle** and the **savings in working capital** at an **interest rate of 12% per annum**. (7 marks) --- ##### **Solution to Question B — Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Step 1: Compute the Three Periods** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Inventory Period} &= \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{22{,}00{,}000} \times 365 = 0.22727 \times 365 = 82.95 \text{ days} \\ \text{Receivables Period} &= \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{30{,}00{,}000} \times 365 = 0.20 \times 365 = 73.00 \text{ days} \\ \text{Payables Period} &= \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{18{,}00{,}000} \times 365 = 0.16667 \times 365 = 60.83 \text{ days} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Operating Cycle** $$\text{OC} = 82.95 + 73.00 = 155.95 \text{ days}$$ **Step 3: Cash Conversion Cycle** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CCC} &= 155.95 - 60.83 = 95.12 \text{ days} \end{aligned} $$ --- ##### **Solution to Question B — Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Step 1: Compute the Revised Inventory Period (20% Reduction)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{New IP} &= 82.95 \times (1 - 0.20) = 82.95 \times 0.80 = 66.36 \text{ days} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Compute the Revised Payables Period (15% Increase)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{New APP} &= 60.83 \times (1 + 0.15) = 60.83 \times 1.15 = 69.95 \text{ days} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Compute the New Cash Conversion Cycle** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{New CCC} &= \text{New IP} + \text{ARP} - \text{New APP} \\ &= 66.36 + 73.00 - 69.95 = 69.41 \text{ days} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Compute the Reduction in CCC** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta \text{CCC} &= 95.12 - 69.41 = 25.71 \text{ days} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Compute the Working Capital Savings** Working capital is funded for the duration of CCC. The annual working capital requirement is computed based on the daily operating cost (COGS/365). $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Daily COGS} &= \frac{22{,}00{,}000}{365} = 6{,}027.40 \text{ per day} \\ \text{Savings in WC} &= \Delta \text{CCC} \times \text{Daily COGS} = 25.71 \times 6{,}027.40 = 1{,}54{,}965 \text{ (approximately ₹1,54,965)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6: Compute Annual Interest Savings at 12%** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Interest Savings} &= 1{,}54{,}965 \times 0.12 = 18{,}596 \text{ (approximately ₹18,596)} \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** Through these two operational improvements, Kochi Marine Exports can permanently reduce working capital blockage by ~₹1.55 lakhs and save ~₹18,596 annually in financing costs. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS IN KTU EXAMS:** > > **Pitfall 1 — Mixing Sales with COGS:** Many students erroneously use **Net Sales** instead of **COGS** in the Inventory Period formula. This inflates IP artificially because Sales > COGS due to the gross profit margin. Always remember: **Inventory is valued at cost, not at selling price.** > > **Pitfall 2 — Using Total Purchases instead of Credit Purchases:** The Payables Period must use *only* **Credit Purchases**. If a portion of purchases is on cash, including it inflates APP incorrectly. > > **Pitfall 3 — Forgetting to Average Opening & Closing Balances:** When both opening and closing balances are given (as in most KTU problems), **averaging is mandatory**. Using only the closing balance will lose 1–2 marks. > > **Pitfall 4 — Sign Convention in Cash Cycle:** A **negative CCC** is mathematically valid and financially *favorable* — it indicates supplier-financed operations. Do not "correct" it to positive; it is a deliberate outcome. > > **Pitfall 5 — Not Showing Intermediate Calculations:** KTU valuators award step marks. Always show: averages → period → OC → CCC. Skipping straight to the answer forfeits process marks. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Operating Cycle and Cash Cycle** - **Operating Cycle (OC)** = **Inventory Period (IP) + Receivables Period (ARP)** - **Cash Conversion Cycle (CCC)** = **OC − Payables Period (APP)** = **IP + ARP − APP** - **Inventory Period** uses **COGS** as denominator; **never use Sales**. - **Receivables Period** uses **Credit Sales** as denominator; **never use Total Sales**. - **Payables Period** uses **Credit Purchases** as denominator. - Standard financial year uses **365 days** unless the problem specifies otherwise. - **Average Balance** = (Opening + Closing) / 2, used for Inventory, AR, and AP. - **Negative CCC** = financially superior; supplier credit exceeds customer credit lag. - **Reducing CCC** improves liquidity, lowers financing costs, and increases ROE. - **OC measures operational efficiency**; **CCC measures financial efficiency**. - **Three levers to reduce CCC:** Faster inventory turnover + faster receivables collection + extended payable terms. - **KTU signature trap:** Always show *all three periods* individually before computing OC and CCC. - **Real-world benchmarks:** Amazon ≈ −25 days; Apple ≈ −30 days; Walmart ≈ 5 days; Manufacturing ≈ 60–90 days. - **Cash Cycle = Net working capital financing duration.** The firm must arrange funds (short-term borrowings, commercial paper, bank lines) for this exact period. - **The CCC directly feeds into the Working Capital formula:** `WC = (CCC / 365) × Annual Operating Cost`.

topics.title: Capital Structure - Net Income Approach
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET602 - FINANCIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Capital Structure - Net Income Approach

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — CAPITAL STRUCTURE: NET INCOME (NI) APPROACH

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> *Capital structure refers to the mix of debt and equity that a firm uses to finance its overall operations and growth. The Net Income Approach (NI Approach) is one of the traditional theories of capital structure, propounded by **David Durand (1952)**, which attempts to establish a relationship between the firm's value, cost of capital, and the degree of financial leverage.*

### Formal Definition

The **Net Income Approach (NI Approach)** is a capital structure theory that asserts that:

1. The cost of debt $(K_d)$ is **constant** irrespective of the degree of financial leverage.
2. The cost of equity $(K_e)$ is also **constant** irrespective of the degree of financial leverage.
3. There is **perfect correlation** between the use of debt and the market's perception of the firm's risk — investors are indifferent to the source of financing at constant $K_e$.

Under these assumptions, the approach concludes that a firm can **increase its total market value (V)** and **decrease its overall cost of capital ($K_o$)** continuously by employing more and more debt in its capital structure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical Definition:**
> The market value of equity $(S)$ is computed by capitalizing the net income (earnings available to equity shareholders) at the constant equity capitalization rate $K_e$:
> $$S = \frac{NI}{K_e}$$
> where $NI = EBIT - I$ (Earnings Before Interest and Taxes minus Interest).
> The **Total Market Value of the Firm (V)** is therefore:
> $$V = S + D$$
> The **Weighted Average Cost of Capital ($K_o$)** is:
> $$K_o = \left(\frac{S}{V}\right) K_e + \left(\frac{D}{V}\right) K_d$$

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Pizza Restaurant" Analogy:**
> Imagine you own a pizza shop. You have two ways to raise money: (a) take a bank loan at a fixed 10% interest, or (b) bring in new partners who want a share of the profits.
> - The **bank loan** is like **debt** — the bank doesn't care if your shop earns more or less; it just wants its 10%.
> - **Partners** are like **equity holders** — they demand a higher return (say, 15%) because they take more risk.
> - The **Net Income Approach** says: *"Since debt is always cheaper (10% < 15%), keep adding more and more debt to finance your shop. Your overall cost of capital will fall, and the value of your shop will rise — infinitely!"*
> - Of course, in reality, beyond a point, lenders will refuse or charge more, and partners will demand higher returns — this is the limitation that the **MM Approach** and **Traditional Approach** later corrected.

### Key Constant Parameters (in Bold)

| Parameter | Symbol | Behaviour under NI Approach |
|---|---|---|
| Cost of Debt | $K_d$ | **Constant** |
| Cost of Equity | $K_e$ | **Constant** |
| Earnings Before Interest & Tax | EBIT | Constant (assumed) |
| Interest (I) | $I$ | Increases with debt |
| Net Income | NI | Decreases with debt |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Assumptions of the Net Income Approach

The NI Approach is built on the following **rigid set of assumptions**:

1. **Perfect Capital Markets**: Investors have complete information, no transaction costs, and securities are infinitely divisible.
2. **Constant Business Risk**: The firm's operating risk (EBIT) is unaffected by its financing decisions.
3. **Constant $K_d$ and $K_e$**: Both the cost of debt and the cost of equity are independent of the capital structure.
4. **No Corporate Taxes**: In the original Durand formulation, taxes are ignored (the MM model with taxes later refined this).
5. **All Earnings are Distributed**: 100% of net income is paid out as dividends, eliminating retained earnings complications.
6. **Perpetual Life**: The firm has an indefinite life, allowing perpetual capitalization of earnings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The NI Approach is essentially the **opposite** of the Net Operating Income (NOI) Approach. While NOI says $K_o$ is constant regardless of leverage, NI says $K_o$ decreases monotonically with leverage.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Flow

- **Step 1:** Start with a given level of EBIT (operating profit).
- **Step 2:** Decide on a level of debt $D$. Compute Interest $I = K_d \times D$.
- **Step 3:** Calculate Net Income $NI = EBIT - I$.
- **Step 4:** Capitalize NI at constant $K_e$ to get Equity value $S = NI / K_e$.
- **Step 5:** Total Value $V = S + D$.
- **Step 6:** Compute Weighted Average Cost of Capital $K_o = (S/V)K_e + (D/V)K_d$.
- **Step 7:** Repeat for varying $D$. Plot $V$ vs $D$ (rising) and $K_o$ vs $D$ (falling).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these formulas — every KTU Numerical is solved using these!**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I = K_d \times D$ | Interest expense on debt | ₹ (Currency) |
| 2 | $NI = EBIT - I$ | Net Income available to equity | ₹ |
| 3 | $S = \dfrac{NI}{K_e}$ | Market value of equity (capitalization) | ₹ |
| 4 | $V = S + D$ | Total market value of firm | ₹ |
| 5 | $K_o = \left(\dfrac{S}{V}\right) K_e + \left(\dfrac{D}{V}\right) K_d$ | Weighted Average Cost of Capital | % (Dimensionless) |
| 6 | $K_o = \dfrac{EBIT}{V}$ | Simplified (since $EBIT/V = NI/S + I/D$ share-weighted) | % |
| 7 | $D/V$ | Debt-to-Value ratio (leverage) | Ratio (0 to 1) |
| 8 | $S/V$ | Equity-to-Value ratio | Ratio (0 to 1) |

> [!TIP]
> **CRITICAL NOTATION RULE:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` for absolute value or conditional dividers in markdown tables to prevent syntax breakage. Example: Use $\vert K_e \vert$ instead of $|K_e|$.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Management

- **Capital Budgeting Decisions**: Engineering firms (especially infrastructure) use this approach to evaluate whether issuing corporate bonds (debt) versus fresh equity to finance a new plant maximizes shareholder wealth.
- **Project Finance**: EPC (Engineering, Procurement, Construction) companies structure debt-equity mixes for large contracts based on such valuation logic.
- **Startup Valuation**: Tech and engineering startups use NI logic to assess the impact of VC funding (debt-like instruments) vs founder equity dilution.
- **Working Capital Management**: Short-term debt decisions in manufacturing firms rely on this approach for funding inventory and receivables.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Value of Equity ($S$)

By definition of the equity capitalization rate:

$$K_e = \frac{NI}{S}$$

Rearranging for $S$:

$$\boxed{S = \frac{NI}{K_e}}$$

Substituting $NI = EBIT - I$:

$$S = \frac{EBIT - I}{K_e}$$

Since $I = K_d \cdot D$:

$$\boxed{S = \frac{EBIT - K_d \cdot D}{K_e}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Total Value of Firm ($V$)

Total value is the sum of market value of equity and market value of debt:

$$V = S + D$$

Substituting $S$:

$$V = \frac{EBIT - K_d \cdot D}{K_e} + D$$

Taking common denominator $K_e$:

$$V = \frac{EBIT - K_d \cdot D + K_e \cdot D}{K_e}$$

$$V = \frac{EBIT + D(K_e - K_d)}{K_e}$$

Since $K_e > K_d$ (equity is always costlier than debt):

$$\boxed{V = \frac{EBIT}{K_e} + \frac{D(K_e - K_d)}{K_e}}$$

**Interpretation:**
- The first term $\dfrac{EBIT}{K_e}$ is the **unlevered value of the firm** (value if all-equity financed).
- The second term $\dfrac{D(K_e - K_d)}{K_e}$ is the **premium from leverage**, which is **positive** because $K_e > K_d$.

This derivation confirms the central thesis: **$V$ increases linearly with $D$**.

## 3.3 Derivation of Weighted Average Cost of Capital ($K_o$)

By definition:

$$K_o = \frac{\text{Total Cost}}{\text{Total Value}} = \frac{I + NI}{V}$$

$$K_o = \frac{K_d \cdot D + K_e \cdot S}{V}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $V$:

$$\boxed{K_o = \left(\frac{D}{V}\right) K_d + \left(\frac{S}{V}\right) K_e}$$

**Alternative derivation** using $EBIT$:

$$K_o = \frac{EBIT}{V}$$

Proof:

$$\frac{EBIT}{V} = \frac{NI + I}{V} = \frac{K_e S + K_d D}{V} = \frac{S}{V} K_e + \frac{D}{V} K_d = K_o$$

This identity holds universally and is often used in **KTU numericals** to cross-check answers.

## 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

> **Problem:**
> A firm has EBIT of ₹ 5,00,000. The cost of debt ($K_d$) is 10% and the cost of equity ($K_e$) is 15%. Calculate the value of the firm, cost of capital, and equity value under three financing plans: ₹ 0, ₹ 5,00,000, and ₹ 10,00,000 of debt.

### Plan 1: All-Equity (D = ₹ 0)

$$S = \frac{EBIT - 0}{K_e} = \frac{5,00,000}{0.15} = ₹\, 33,33,333$$

$$V = S + D = 33,33,333 + 0 = ₹\, 33,33,333$$

$$K_o = \frac{EBIT}{V} = \frac{5,00,000}{33,33,333} = 15.00\%$$

### Plan 2: Moderate Debt (D = ₹ 5,00,000)

$$I = K_d \times D = 0.10 \times 5,00,000 = ₹\, 50,000$$

$$NI = EBIT - I = 5,00,000 - 50,000 = ₹\, 4,50,000$$

$$S = \frac{NI}{K_e} = \frac{4,50,000}{0.15} = ₹\, 30,00,000$$

$$V = S + D = 30,00,000 + 5,00,000 = ₹\, 35,00,000$$

$$K_o = \frac{EBIT}{V} = \frac{5,00,000}{35,00,000} = 14.29\%$$

### Plan 3: High Debt (D = ₹ 10,00,000)

$$I = K_d \times D = 0.10 \times 10,00,000 = ₹\, 1,00,000$$

$$NI = EBIT - I = 5,00,000 - 1,00,000 = ₹\, 4,00,000$$

$$S = \frac{NI}{K_e} = \frac{4,00,000}{0.15} = ₹\, 26,66,667$$

$$V = S + D = 26,66,667 + 10,00,000 = ₹\, 36,66,667$$

$$K_o = \frac{EBIT}{V} = \frac{5,00,000}{36,66,667} = 13.64\%$$

### Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Plan | Debt (₹) | Equity (₹) | Total Value (₹) | $K_o$ (%) | Net Income (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 33,33,333 | 33,33,333 | 15.00 | 5,00,000 |
| 2 | 5,00,000 | 30,00,000 | 35,00,000 | 14.29 | 4,50,000 |
| 3 | 10,00,000 | 26,66,667 | 36,66,667 | 13.64 | 4,00,000 |

> [!TIP]
> **Observation Table:** As debt increases from 0 to ₹10,00,000:
> - $V$ increases from ₹33.33L to ₹36.67L (firm value rises)
> - $K_o$ decreases from 15% to 13.64% (cost of capital falls)
>
> This **linear monotonic relationship** is the defining feature of the NI Approach.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: NI Approach Computational Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Given EBIT, Kd, Ke] --> stepB[Select Debt Level D]
    stepB --> stepC[Compute Interest I = Kd x D]
    stepC --> stepD[Compute Net Income NI = EBIT - I]
    stepD --> stepE[Compute Equity Value S = NI / Ke]
    stepE --> stepF[Compute Total Value V = S + D]
    stepF --> stepG[Compute Cost of Capital Ko = EBIT / V]
    stepG --> stepH{More Debt Levels?}
    stepH -- Yes --> stepB
    stepH -- No --> stepI[Plot V vs D and Ko vs D]
    stepI --> stepJ[V increases linearly with D]
    stepI --> stepK[Ko decreases linearly with D]
    stepJ --> endA[Conclusion: Maximize Debt]
    stepK --> endA
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Capital Structure Decision Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[EBIT Input] --> procA[Net Income NI Computation]
    inputB[Cost of Debt Kd CONSTANT] --> procA
    inputC[Cost of Equity Ke CONSTANT] --> procA
    procA --> capA[Capitalize at Ke]
    capA --> valA[Equity Value S]
    inputD[Debt Level D] --> valB[Debt Component D]
    valA --> sumA[Sum Component V]
    valB --> sumA
    sumA --> outputA[Total Firm Value V]
    sumA --> outputB[Overall Cost Ko]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Behavioural Comparison Chart

```mermaid
graph LR
    nidir[Net Income Approach Direction] --> directionA[V INCREASES with Debt]
    nidir --> directionB[Ko DECREASES with Debt]
    nidir --> directionC[Ke REMAINS CONSTANT]
    nidir --> directionD[Kd REMAINS CONSTANT]
    nidir --> conclusionR[RECOMMENDATION: MAXIMIZE DEBT]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid is used here:** Financial concepts do not always have visualizable physical structures. The Mermaid block diagrams above represent the **functional architecture** of how financial inputs flow through the NI Approach logic to produce valuation outputs — making the methodology visually intuitive for engineering students who are accustomed to process flow diagrams.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**State the assumptions of the Net Income Approach of capital structure.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Net Income Approach (propounded by **David Durand**) rests on the following key assumptions:

1. **Constant Cost of Debt ($K_d$)**: The cost of debt remains unchanged regardless of the proportion of debt in the capital structure.
2. **Constant Cost of Equity ($K_e$)**: The cost of equity also remains unchanged irrespective of leverage — investors do not perceive additional financial risk.
3. **Constant EBIT**: The firm's operating earnings (EBIT) are unaffected by financing decisions, implying constant business risk.
4. **No Taxes**: The original model ignores corporate taxation.
5. **100% Payout Ratio**: All net income is distributed as dividends; retained earnings are zero.
6. **Perpetual Life**: The firm is valued as a going concern with infinite cash flows.

**Conclusion of NI Approach**: Since debt is cheaper than equity and both costs are constant, the firm should maximize debt to minimize $K_o$ and maximize $V$.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"According to the Net Income Approach, value of the firm is maximum at 100% debt." Comment.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The statement is **absolutely correct** within the framework of the Net Income Approach.

The mathematical justification is as follows:

$$V = S + D = \frac{EBIT - K_d \cdot D}{K_e} + D$$

$$V = \frac{EBIT}{K_e} + \frac{D(K_e - K_d)}{K_e}$$

Since $K_e > K_d$ (equity is always more expensive than debt), the term $(K_e - K_d)$ is **positive**. Therefore, $V$ is a **linear, monotonically increasing function of $D$**.

As $D \to$ maximum (theoretically 100%), $V \to$ maximum.

Simultaneously, $K_o$ reaches its minimum value:

$$K_o = \frac{EBIT}{V} \rightarrow \text{minimum when } V \rightarrow \text{maximum}$$

Hence, the **optimal capital structure under NI Approach** is theoretically **100% debt** — though, in practice, this is unattainable due to lender reluctance and bankruptcy risks.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern:** Each Part B question contains two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Two alternative questions (A and B) are provided below.

---

### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** A firm is considering two financing plans. EBIT is ₹ 6,00,000. Cost of equity ($K_e$) is 16% and cost of debt ($K_d$) is 10%. Compute the value of the firm and overall cost of capital under both plans.

| Plan | Debt (₹) | Interest Rate |
|---|---|---|
| Plan I | 10,00,000 | 10% |
| Plan II | 15,00,000 | 10% |

**(b)** Explain why the Net Income Approach suggests 100% debt financing. List three practical limitations.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**Given:** EBIT = ₹ 6,00,000; $K_e$ = 16%; $K_d$ = 10%

**Plan I: D = ₹ 10,00,000**

Step 1: Interest $I = K_d \times D$

$$I = 0.10 \times 10,00,000 = ₹\, 1,00,000$$

Step 2: Net Income $NI = EBIT - I$

$$NI = 6,00,000 - 1,00,000 = ₹\, 5,00,000$$

Step 3: Equity Value $S = NI / K_e$

$$S = \frac{5,00,000}{0.16} = ₹\, 31,25,000$$

Step 4: Total Firm Value $V = S + D$

$$V = 31,25,000 + 10,00,000 = ₹\, 41,25,000$$

Step 5: Overall Cost of Capital $K_o = EBIT / V$

$$K_o = \frac{6,00,000}{41,25,000} \times 100 = 14.55\%$$

---

**Plan II: D = ₹ 15,00,000**

Step 1: Interest $I = K_d \times D$

$$I = 0.10 \times 15,00,000 = ₹\, 1,50,000$$

Step 2: Net Income $NI = EBIT - I$

$$NI = 6,00,000 - 1,50,000 = ₹\, 4,50,000$$

Step 3: Equity Value $S = NI / K_e$

$$S = \frac{4,50,000}{0.16} = ₹\, 28,12,500$$

Step 4: Total Firm Value $V = S + D$

$$V = 28,12,500 + 15,00,000 = ₹\, 43,12,500$$

Step 5: Overall Cost of Capital $K_o = EBIT / V$

$$K_o = \frac{6,00,000}{43,12,500} \times 100 = 13.91\%$$

---

#### Comparative Table (1 mark)

| Plan | Debt (₹) | Equity (₹) | Total Value (₹) | $K_o$ (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Plan I | 10,00,000 | 31,25,000 | 41,25,000 | 14.55 |
| Plan II | 15,00,000 | 28,12,500 | 43,12,500 | 13.91 |

**Conclusion:** Plan II (higher debt) yields higher firm value (₹ 43.125L vs ₹ 41.25L) and lower cost of capital (13.91% vs 14.55%), confirming the NI Approach hypothesis.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**Why NI Approach suggests 100% debt:**

The NI Approach mathematically proves that $V$ increases linearly with $D$ and $K_o$ decreases linearly with $D$. The slope of increase is determined by $(K_e - K_d)/K_e$, which is positive as long as debt is cheaper than equity. Hence, theoretically, the more debt, the better — until $D$ reaches 100% of the firm's value.

$$V = \frac{EBIT}{K_e} + \frac{D(K_e - K_d)}{K_e}$$

The second term is the **leverage premium**, which grows indefinitely with $D$.

**Three Practical Limitations:**

1. **Risk of Financial Distress**: Excessive debt increases the probability of bankruptcy. Lenders will refuse beyond a certain leverage, or charge higher $K_d$.
2. **Rising $K_e$**: In practice, equity shareholders demand higher returns ($K_e$ rises) as leverage increases, because their residual claim becomes riskier. This contradicts the constant $K_e$ assumption.
3. **Tax Shield Ignored**: The original NI Approach ignores the tax-deductibility of interest, which the MM-with-taxes model later addressed as a major value-creating element of debt.

---

### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** XYZ Ltd. has an EBIT of ₹ 8,00,000 and wants to raise ₹ 20,00,000 of additional capital. It can choose between:
- **Option 1**: All-equity at $K_e$ = 14%
- **Option 2**: Debt of ₹ 10,00,000 at $K_d$ = 9% and the rest as equity at $K_e$ = 14%

Ascertain the financing decision using the Net Income Approach.

**(b)** Differentiate between the Net Income Approach and the Net Operating Income Approach of capital structure.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**Given:** EBIT = ₹ 8,00,000; Total capital = ₹ 20,00,000; $K_d$ = 9%; $K_e$ = 14%

**Option 1: All-Equity Financing (D = ₹ 0)**

Step 1: Net Income $NI = EBIT - I = 8,00,000 - 0 = ₹\, 8,00,000$

Step 2: Equity Value $S = NI / K_e$

$$S = \frac{8,00,000}{0.14} = ₹\, 57,14,286$$

Step 3: Total Firm Value $V = S + D$

$$V = 57,14,286 + 0 = ₹\, 57,14,286$$

Step 4: Cost of Capital $K_o = EBIT / V$

$$K_o = \frac{8,00,000}{57,14,286} \times 100 = 14.00\%$$

---

**Option 2: Mixed Financing (D = ₹ 10,00,000; Equity = ₹ 10,00,000)**

Step 1: Interest $I = K_d \times D$

$$I = 0.09 \times 10,00,000 = ₹\, 90,000$$

Step 2: Net Income $NI = EBIT - I$

$$NI = 8,00,000 - 90,000 = ₹\, 7,10,000$$

Step 3: Equity Value $S = NI / K_e$

$$S = \frac{7,10,000}{0.14} = ₹\, 50,71,429$$

Step 4: Total Firm Value $V = S + D$

$$V = 50,71,429 + 10,00,000 = ₹\, 60,71,429$$

Step 5: Cost of Capital $K_o = EBIT / V$

$$K_o = \frac{8,00,000}{60,71,429} \times 100 = 13.18\%$$

---

#### Comparative Analysis

| Option | Debt (₹) | Equity (₹) | Total Value (₹) | $K_o$ (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (All-Equity) | 0 | 57,14,286 | 57,14,286 | 14.00 |
| 2 (Mixed) | 10,00,000 | 50,71,429 | 60,71,429 | 13.18 |

**Decision:** Option 2 (mixed financing) is preferable because it yields:
- **Higher Firm Value** by ₹ 3,57,143 (₹ 60.71L vs ₹ 57.14L)
- **Lower Cost of Capital** by 0.82% (13.18% vs 14.00%)

This validates the NI Approach recommendation to use debt wherever possible.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**Differences between NI Approach and NOI Approach:**

| Basis | Net Income (NI) Approach | Net Operating Income (NOI) Approach |
|---|---|---|
| **Proposer** | David Durand (1952) | David Durand (1952) |
| **Cost of Debt ($K_d$)** | Constant | Constant |
| **Cost of Equity ($K_e$)** | Constant | Variable — increases with leverage |
| **Overall $K_o$** | Decreases with debt | Constant irrespective of capital structure |
| **Firm Value ($V$)** | Increases with debt | Independent of capital structure |
| **Optimal Structure** | Maximum debt (100%) | Irrelevant — any mix is optimal |
| **Logic** | Investors are indifferent to leverage | Investors demand higher $K_e$ as leverage rises, offsetting debt benefit |
| **Realism** | Less realistic (assumes $K_e$ constant) | More realistic than NI but contradicts MM |
| **Formula for $V$** | $V = \dfrac{EBIT}{K_e} + \dfrac{D(K_e - K_d)}{K_e}$ | $V = \dfrac{EBIT}{K_o}$ (constant $K_o$) |

**Conclusion:** The NOI Approach suggests capital structure decisions are **irrelevant** to firm value, while the NI Approach suggests **maximizing debt** is the best strategy.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $V = S + D$ step**: Students often compute only $S$ and forget to add $D$ for total firm value. This results in **2–3 marks lost** per question.
> 2. **Using $K_e$ on $NI$ correctly**: Equity value is $NI / K_e$ (NOT $EBIT / K_e$). Confusing the numerator costs **1 mark minimum**.
> 3. **Not using $K_o = EBIT / V$ shortcut**: While weighted average formula works, the $EBIT/V$ shortcut is faster and less error-prone in KTU exams with time pressure.
> 4. **Assuming taxes apply**: The **original** NI Approach is **without taxes**. If a question states "as per Modigliani-Miller with taxes", use a different framework.
> 5. **Confusing NI and NOI approaches**: They sound similar but yield **opposite conclusions**. Always verify the question's ask — "increase" or "decrease" of $V$ with debt.
> 6. **Rounding errors**: KTU typically allows ±0.01% tolerance. Always retain at least 2 decimal places until the final answer.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024:**

- ✅ **NI Approach** was proposed by **David Durand** in 1952.
- ✅ It is one of the **three classical theories** of capital structure (along with NOI and Traditional).
- ✅ **Both $K_d$ and $K_e$ are assumed constant** — this is the cornerstone assumption.
- ✅ **Formula Trifecta to memorize**:
  - $S = NI / K_e$
  - $V = S + D$
  - $K_o = (S/V)K_e + (D/V)K_d = EBIT / V$
- ✅ As debt $D$ increases → **$V$ rises linearly** and **$K_o$ falls linearly**.
- ✅ **Optimal structure under NI Approach** = **100% Debt** (theoretical).
- ✅ **NI vs NOI**: NI says $K_o$ falls with debt; NOI says $K_o$ is constant.
- ✅ **Practical Impossibility**: 100% debt is unattainable due to bankruptcy risk and rising $K_e$ in reality.
- ✅ The **identity $EBIT = K_o \cdot V$** is your fastest verification formula.
- ✅ Always check: **NI decreases** as debt rises (because interest eats into earnings), but **equity value $S$ also decreases** — yet total $V$ rises because cheaper debt compensates.
- ✅ **Levy Premium Term**: $\dfrac{D(K_e - K_d)}{K_e}$ — this positive quantity drives the value increase with leverage.
- ✅ For KTU 2024 exams, questions are typically 14-mark computational problems requiring **2–3 financing plans** to be evaluated and compared.
- ✅ Always present answers in a **comparative table** for full marks.
- ✅ The NI Approach **ignores taxes** and **bankruptcy costs** — both are addressed in more advanced models (MM, Trade-Off Theory).

---

**END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — NET INCOME APPROACH**
*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech Syllabus | PCIET602 — Financial Engineering*

topics.title: Net Operating Income Approach
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET602 - FINANCIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Net Operating Income Approach

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Net Operating Income (NOI) Approach** is a classical capital structure theory propounded by **David Durand (1952)**. It argues that the **overall cost of capital ($K_o$) and the total market value of the firm ($V$) are completely independent of the degree of leverage (debt-equity mix) in the capital structure**. Because the cost of debt ($K_d$) is cheaper than the cost of equity ($K_e$), any benefit obtained by using more debt is exactly offset by an equivalent increase in the cost of equity, leaving the Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC) constant.

In this approach, the market value of the firm is computed by capitalizing the expected net operating income at the overall cost of capital:

$$V = \frac{NOI}{K_o}$$

The cost of equity is then derived as a residual (plug figure) such that the weighted average cost of capital remains fixed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The NOI Approach is one of the three pillars of capital structure theory (alongside MM Approach and Traditional Approach). It forms the conceptual antithesis of the Modigliani-Miller Net Income Approach and is a **frequently tested 14-mark problem topic** in KTU ESE.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are **mixing two ingredients to make a dish** — a cheap local spice and an expensive imported herb. As you increase the quantity of the cheap spice, you are forced to add more of the expensive herb just to balance the taste, because customers instantly sense when the dish is "too cheap." The **per-unit cost of the dish (WACC) stays exactly the same**, regardless of how you mix the two ingredients. The cheap spice (debt) and the expensive herb (equity) rebalance so perfectly that the overall "flavor cost" ($K_o$) is invariant.

- **Cheap Spice** = Debt ($K_d$, lower cost due to tax shield and fixed contractual obligation)
- **Expensive Herb** = Equity ($K_e$, higher cost due to greater financial risk borne by shareholders)
- **Cost of the Dish** = Overall Cost of Capital ($K_o$, constant)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Lenders demand more security as debt rises → shareholders perceive higher financial risk → $K_e$ rises **proportionately** → the weighted average $K_o$ does not budge.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Net Operating Income | $NOI$ | Currency units (₹) |
| Overall Cost of Capital | $K_o$ | Percentage (%) |
| Cost of Debt (Pre-tax) | $K_d$ | Percentage (%) |
| Cost of Equity | $K_e$ | Percentage (%) |
| Market Value of Firm | $V$ | Currency units (₹) |
| Market Value of Equity | $S$ | Currency units (₹) |
| Market Value of Debt | $D$ | Currency units (₹) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Assumptions of the NOI Approach

1. **Presence of Corporate Taxes is Ignored** (or assumed to be perfectly offset by personal taxes — the "irrelevance" result).
2. **Debt is not free of risk** — as leverage increases, $K_d$ may eventually rise as well, but in the basic Durand model $K_d$ is held constant.
3. **The weighted average cost of capital ($K_o$) remains constant** at all levels of debt-equity mix.
4. **All investors and firms have identical borrowing rates** (no pecking-order differentials).
5. **The cost of equity ($K_e$) is a positive linear function of the debt-equity ratio.**
6. **NOI is expected to be a constant (a perpetuity)**, and business risk is constant.

### 2.2 The Core 'Why' and 'How'

- **Why $K_o$ is constant:** Because the **financial risk of equity holders rises in lockstep** with the benefit of using cheaper debt. The market efficiently re-prices equity risk to neutralize any leverage advantage.
- **How $K_e$ is computed:** It is derived as a **plug variable** by solving the WACC identity:

$$K_o = \frac{D}{V} \cdot K_d + \frac{S}{V} \cdot K_e$$

Rearranging for $K_e$:

$$K_e = K_o + (K_o - K_d) \cdot \frac{D}{S}$$

This shows $K_e$ is a **linear, increasing function** of the leverage ratio $D/S$.

- **How $V$ is computed:** The market value of the firm is independent of leverage:

$$V = S + D = \frac{NOI}{K_o}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Board-Ready)

| # | Formula | Description / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V = \dfrac{NOI}{K_o}$ | Total market value of the firm (capitalization formula) |
| 2 | $S = V - D$ | Market value of equity (residually determined) |
| 3 | $K_o = \dfrac{NOI}{V}$ | Overall (weighted average) cost of capital |
| 4 | $K_o = \dfrac{D}{V} K_d + \dfrac{S}{V} K_e$ | WACC identity (equating two expressions) |
| 5 | $K_e = K_o + (K_o - K_d) \cdot \dfrac{D}{S}$ | Cost of equity as a linear function of leverage $D/S$ |
| 6 | $NOI = EBIT \quad (\text{in perpetuity assumption})$ | Operating profit before interest and taxes |
| 7 | $\dfrac{\partial K_o}{\partial (D/S)} = 0$ | Slope condition — NOI approach invariant |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder for Tables:** Always use $\vert$ or $\mid$ (e.g., $\vert K_o - K_d \vert$) instead of the raw vertical pipe to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Corporate Finance

- **Capital budgeting decisions** for engineering firms evaluating debt-financed infrastructure projects.
- **WACC estimation** in DCF valuation models for technology and process engineering companies.
- **Optimal capital structure determination** — NOI approach serves as the "do-nothing-change" baseline against which MM and Traditional theories are benchmarked.
- **Regulatory finance** — Public utility regulators use similar logic to determine allowed rate of return.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Market Value of the Firm ($V$)

**Starting Premise:** The market capitalizes the perpetual stream of net operating income at the overall cost of capital.

$$\text{Step 1: Define NOI} \implies NOI = \text{EBIT (constant perpetuity)}$$

$$\text{Step 2: Apply perpetuity capitalization} \implies V = \frac{\text{Expected NOI}}{K_o}$$

$$\text{Step 3: Explicitly state} \implies V = \frac{NOI}{K_o}$$

This is **independent of capital structure** because both $NOI$ and $K_o$ are exogenous to financing decisions.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cost of Equity as a Plug Figure

**Starting Premise:** WACC identity must hold at all leverage levels.

$$\text{Step 1: WACC identity} \implies K_o = \frac{D}{V} K_d + \frac{S}{V} K_e$$

$$\text{Step 2: Multiply both sides by } V \implies K_o \cdot V = D \cdot K_d + S \cdot K_e$$

$$\text{Step 3: Substitute } V = S + D \implies K_o(S + D) = D \cdot K_d + S \cdot K_e$$

$$\text{Step 4: Expand} \implies K_o \cdot S + K_o \cdot D = D \cdot K_d + S \cdot K_e$$

$$\text{Step 5: Isolate } S \cdot K_e \implies S \cdot K_e - K_o \cdot S = K_o \cdot D - D \cdot K_d$$

$$\text{Step 6: Factor} \implies S(K_e - K_o) = D(K_o - K_d)$$

$$\text{Step 7: Solve for } K_e \implies K_e = K_o + (K_o - K_d) \cdot \frac{D}{S}$$

This proves that $K_e$ is a **linear function of $D/S$** with slope $(K_o - K_d)$.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (14-Mark KTU Style)

**Problem Statement:** A firm expects perpetual Net Operating Income of **₹ 12,00,000** per year. The current overall cost of capital is **12%**, and the firm can borrow at **8%** per annum. Currently, the firm uses **₹ 15,00,000** of debt. Calculate:

(a) The total market value of the firm, the value of equity, and the current cost of equity.
(b) The cost of equity and the value of the firm if the firm increases debt to **₹ 25,00,000**.
(c) Comment on the result in the context of the **Net Operating Income Approach**.

#### Part (a) — Solution

**Step 1: Compute total firm value**

$$V = \frac{NOI}{K_o} = \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{0.12} = 1{,}00{,}00{,}000$$

$$\implies V = \text{₹ } 1,00,00,000$$

**Step 2: Compute equity value**

$$S = V - D = 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 - 15{,}00{,}000 = 85{,}00{,}000$$

$$\implies S = \text{₹ } 85,00,000$$

**Step 3: Compute cost of equity using the plug formula**

$$K_e = K_o + (K_o - K_d) \cdot \frac{D}{S}$$

$$K_e = 0.12 + (0.12 - 0.08) \cdot \frac{15{,}00{,}000}{85{,}00{,}000}$$

$$K_e = 0.12 + 0.04 \cdot 0.17647 = 0.12 + 0.007059$$

$$K_e \approx 0.12706 \text{ or } 12.71\%$$

#### Part (b) — Solution (Debt = ₹ 25,00,000)

**Step 1: Total firm value is invariant under NOI**

$$V = 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 \quad (\text{remains the same})$$

**Step 2: New equity value**

$$S_{\text{new}} = V - D_{\text{new}} = 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 - 25{,}00{,}000 = 75{,}00{,}000$$

$$\implies S_{\text{new}} = \text{₹ } 75,00,000$$

**Step 3: New cost of equity**

$$K_{e,\text{new}} = K_o + (K_o - K_d) \cdot \frac{D_{\text{new}}}{S_{\text{new}}}$$

$$K_{e,\text{new}} = 0.12 + 0.04 \cdot \frac{25{,}00{,}000}{75{,}00{,}000}$$

$$K_{e,\text{new}} = 0.12 + 0.04 \cdot 0.3333 = 0.12 + 0.01333$$

$$K_{e,\text{new}} \approx 0.13333 \text{ or } 13.33\%$$

#### Part (c) — Critical Interpretation

| Component | Before (D = 15L) | After (D = 25L) | Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Value ($V$) | ₹ 1,00,00,000 | ₹ 1,00,00,000 | **No change** |
| Cost of Equity ($K_e$) | 12.71% | 13.33% | ↑ by 62 bps |
| Cost of Debt ($K_d$) | 8% | 8% | No change |
| Overall $K_o$ | 12% | 12% | **No change** |

**Conclusion:** Increasing debt from ₹ 15L → ₹ 25L raised $K_e$ by exactly the amount needed to offset the benefit of cheaper debt. As a result, $V$ and $K_o$ remained constant — a perfect illustration of the **Net Operating Income Approach**.

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Leverage Level ($D/S$) | $K_d$ | $K_e$ | $K_o$ | $V$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Low (0.0) | 8% | 12% | 12% | ₹ 1.00 Cr |
| Moderate (0.18) | 8% | 12.71% | 12% | ₹ 1.00 Cr |
| High (0.33) | 8% | 13.33% | 12% | ₹ 1.00 Cr |
| Very High (1.00) | 8% | 16.00% | 12% | ₹ 1.00 Cr |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Capital Structure Flow Under the NOI Approach

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Firm Capital Structure Decision"] --> B["Choose Debt Level D"]
    A --> C["Choose Equity Level S"]
    B --> D["Determine Cost of Debt Kd (fixed at 8 percent)"]
    C --> E["Determine Cost of Equity Ke as plug variable"]
    D --> F["Compute Weighted Average Cost of Capital Ko"]
    E --> F
    F --> G{"Check Invariant: Ko equals 12 percent constant"}
    G -- Yes --> H["Total Firm Value V = NOI / Ko = Rs 1 Crore"]
    G -- No --> I["Recalculate Ke to restore Ko"]
    I --> E
    H --> J["Final Output: V unchanged irrespective of D or S"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Interaction Between Capital Components

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Inputs["Inputs Layer"]
        N1["NOI = Rs 12,00,000 (exogenous perpetuity)"]
        N2["Ko = 12 percent (fixed WACC)"]
        N3["Kd = 8 percent (fixed debt cost)"]
    end

    subgraph Compute["Computation Layer"]
        C1["V = NOI / Ko = Rs 1,00,00,000"]
        C2["S = V - D (residual equity)"]
        C3["Ke = Ko + Ko minus Kd times D/S"]
    end

    subgraph Output["Output Layer"]
        O1["Firm Value V = Rs 1,00,00,000 (constant)"]
        O2["Cost of Equity Ke (variable, rises with D/S)"]
        O3["Overall Cost Ko = 12 percent (invariant)"]
    end

    N1 --> C1
    N2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    N3 --> C3
    C2 --> O2
    C3 --> O2
    C1 --> O1
    C2 --> O3
    C3 --> O3
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Behavior of $K_e$, $K_d$, and $K_o$ vs. Leverage

```mermaid
graph TB
    P1["Debt-Equity Ratio D/S on X axis"] --> P2["Cost of Capital percent on Y axis"]
    P3["Kd = constant 8 percent flat line"] --> P2
    P4["Ke = rising line starting at Ko"] --> P2
    P5["Ko = horizontal 12 percent line"] --> P2
    P3 --- P4
    P3 --- P5
    P4 --- P5
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Schematic:** The horizontal $K_o$ line is the defining signature of the NOI Approach. It is "sandwiched" between the rising $K_e$ and flat $K_d$, demonstrating the exact rebalancing that keeps $K_o$ invariant.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define the Net Operating Income Approach of capital structure.**

**Model Answer:**
The Net Operating Income (NOI) Approach, propounded by **David Durand (1952)**, is a theory of capital structure which states that the **overall cost of capital ($K_o$) and the total market value of the firm ($V$) remain constant and are independent of the debt-equity mix**. Any reduction in weighted cost due to cheaper debt is exactly offset by a corresponding rise in the cost of equity, leaving $K_o$ invariant at all leverage levels. The total firm value is computed as $V = \dfrac{NOI}{K_o}$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] What is the cost of equity under the NOI Approach?**

**Model Answer:**
Under the NOI Approach, the cost of equity ($K_e$) is **determined residually** (as a plug variable) by the equation:

$$K_e = K_o + (K_o - K_d) \cdot \frac{D}{S}$$

It is a **positive linear function** of the debt-equity ratio $D/S$. As debt increases, the financial risk perceived by shareholders rises, and they demand a higher $K_e$ — exactly offsetting the benefit of cheaper debt and keeping $K_o$ unchanged.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Forgetting to keep $K_o$ constant** — students mistakenly allow $K_o$ to vary. Under NOI, it is **strictly invariant**.
> 2. **Confusing $NOI$ with $EBT$ or $EAT$** — $NOI$ is operating profit **before interest and taxes**, not after.
> 3. **Plug formula misapplication** — the formula $K_e = K_o + (K_o - K_d) \cdot \dfrac{D}{S}$ requires **$D/S$**, not $D/V$.
> 4. **Forgetting units** — present answers in **percentage and ₹** explicitly for full marks.

---

#### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**PS Inc.** expects a perpetual Net Operating Income of **₹ 6,00,000**. The firm has an overall cost of capital of **10%** and borrows at **6%**.

**(a)** Calculate the total value of the firm, the value of equity, and the cost of equity at the current debt level of **₹ 20,00,000**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the firm restructures its capital to have debt of **₹ 30,00,000**, determine the new value of equity, the new cost of equity, and verify that the NOI Approach holds. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Total firm value**

$$V = \frac{NOI}{K_o} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{0.10} = 60{,}00{,}000$$

$$\implies V = \text{₹ } 60,00,000$$

**Step 2: Equity value**

$$S = V - D = 60{,}00{,}000 - 20{,}00{,}000 = 40{,}00{,}000$$

$$\implies S = \text{₹ } 40,00{,}000$$

**Step 3: Cost of equity**

$$K_e = 0.10 + (0.10 - 0.06) \cdot \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{40{,}00{,}000}$$

$$K_e = 0.10 + 0.04 \cdot 0.50 = 0.10 + 0.02 = 0.12$$

$$\implies K_e = 12\%$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Total firm value is invariant**

$$V_{\text{new}} = 60{,}00{,}000 \quad (\text{unchanged by NOI hypothesis})$$

**Step 2: New equity value**

$$S_{\text{new}} = 60{,}00{,}000 - 30{,}00{,}000 = 30{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 3: New cost of equity**

$$K_{e,\text{new}} = 0.10 + 0.04 \cdot \frac{30{,}00{,}000}{30{,}00{,}000}$$

$$K_{e,\text{new}} = 0.10 + 0.04 \cdot 1.00 = 0.14$$

$$\implies K_{e,\text{new}} = 14\%$$

**Step 4: Verification of NOI hypothesis**

| Component | Before | After |
|---|---|---|
| $V$ | ₹ 60,00,000 | ₹ 60,00,000 ✓ unchanged |
| $K_o$ | 10% | 10% ✓ unchanged |
| $K_e$ | 12% | 14% ↑ rose by 2% |

**Conclusion:** Despite raising debt from ₹ 20L → ₹ 30L, both $V$ and $K_o$ remained at ₹ 60L and 10% respectively. The cost of equity rose from 12% → 14%, exactly offsetting the leverage benefit. This **verifies the NOI Approach**.

---

#### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Zenith Corp.** has perpetual Net Operating Income of **₹ 8,00,000**, an overall cost of capital of **11%**, and a pre-tax cost of debt of **7%**.

**(a)** Compute the firm's value, equity value, and $K_e$ for the existing debt of **₹ 18,00,000**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If leverage is increased such that debt becomes **₹ 28,00,000**, calculate the new cost of equity and the implied cost of capital. Show that the NOI approach leads to leverage irrelevance. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Total firm value**

$$V = \frac{NOI}{K_o} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{0.11} \approx 72{,}72{,}727$$

$$\implies V \approx \text{₹ } 72,72,727$$

**Step 2: Equity value**

$$S = 72{,}72{,}727 - 18{,}00{,}000 = 54{,}72{,}727$$

**Step 3: Cost of equity**

$$K_e = 0.11 + (0.11 - 0.07) \cdot \frac{18{,}00{,}000}{54{,}72{,}727}$$

$$K_e = 0.11 + 0.04 \cdot 0.32895 = 0.11 + 0.01316$$

$$\implies K_e \approx 12.32\%$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: $V$ remains invariant at ₹ 72,72,727**

**Step 2: New equity value**

$$S_{\text{new}} = 72{,}72{,}727 - 28{,}00{,}000 = 44{,}72{,}727$$

**Step 3: New cost of equity**

$$K_{e,\text{new}} = 0.11 + 0.04 \cdot \frac{28{,}00{,}000}{44{,}72{,}727}$$

$$K_{e,\text{new}} = 0.11 + 0.04 \cdot 0.62621 = 0.11 + 0.02505$$

$$\implies K_{e,\text{new}} \approx 13.51\%$$

**Step 4: Verification using WACC identity**

$$K_o = \frac{D}{V} K_d + \frac{S}{V} K_e$$

$$K_o = \frac{28{,}00{,}000}{72{,}72{,}727} \cdot 0.07 + \frac{44{,}72{,}727}{72{,}72{,}727} \cdot 0.1351$$

$$K_o = 0.3850 \cdot 0.07 + 0.6150 \cdot 0.1351$$

$$K_o = 0.02695 + 0.08307 = 0.11002 \approx 11\% \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** $K_o$ remains at **11%** despite a shift in debt from ₹ 18L → ₹ 28L, confirming **leverage irrelevance under the NOI Approach**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 📌 **NOI = EBIT (perpetual)** — capital structure theory assumes constant operating income stream.
- 📌 **$K_o$ is the single most important invariant** — it is fixed at all leverage levels under the NOI hypothesis.
- 📌 **$K_e$ is the plug variable** — solve for it from the WACC identity: $K_e = K_o + (K_o - K_d) \cdot \dfrac{D}{S}$.
- 📌 **$V = \dfrac{NOI}{K_o}$** — always independent of debt.
- 📌 **$V$ and $K_o$ together = irrelevance of capital structure** — the firm's financing decision does not affect its value.
- 📌 **Use $\mid$ or $\vert$ (not $\vert \vert$) in markdown tables** to prevent syntax breakage.
- 📌 **Always state $K_o$ as a percentage and $V$ in ₹** for full valuation marks.
- 📌 **Visualize the relationship:** $K_d$ flat, $K_e$ rising, $K_o$ horizontal — the "sandwich" pattern of the NOI Approach.
- 📌 **NOI Approach is the conceptual opposite of the Net Income (NI) Approach** — NI assumes $V$ changes with leverage, NOI assumes it does not.
- 📌 **Board Trivia:** David Durand (1952) first presented both NOI and NI approaches in his seminal paper *"Cost of Debt and Equity Funds for Business: Trends and Problems of Measurement."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Traditional Position
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET602 - FINANCIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Traditional Position

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Traditional Position (Vanilla Position):** In Financial Engineering, a *Traditional Position* refers to the **four standard, uncomplicated (plain-vanilla) option positions** that an investor can hold in the options market. These are the **Long Call, Long Put, Short Call, and Short Put** positions — the fundamental building blocks of all option strategies, distinguished from "exotic" or "non-traditional" positions by their linear, single-leg, European/American standardized structure.

The four traditional positions are based on the two elementary option contracts:

- **Call Option (C):** A contract that gives the holder the **right (but not the obligation)** to *buy* the underlying asset at the strike price $X$ on (or before) the expiration date.
- **Put Option (P):** A contract that gives the holder the **right (but not the obligation)** to *sell* the underlying asset at the strike price $X$ on (or before) the expiration date.

Each contract can be either *bought* (Long) or *sold/written* (Short), giving us the **four canonical traditional positions**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Traditional"?** The term *traditional* (or *vanilla*) is used to emphasize that these positions have no path-dependent, discontinuous, or multi-factor payoff features.
